Query: A farmer was bitten by a snake while harvesting. His leg is swelling fast and he says he can't see properly. What should I do before the ambulance comes?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling moving up the leg quickly
- **Blurred vision or drooping eyelids**
- Difficulty swallowing or speaking
- Bleeding from the bite or from the gums
- Weakness in the neck muscles

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Carry the child, keep them completely still, rush to CHC |
| Adult | Any | Immobilize the leg with a stick and cloth splint, call 108 |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor breathing closely, keep head tilted to the side if vomiting |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Use a **long stick and cloth** to tie a splint to the leg to keep it from moving
- Keep the bitten leg **below the level of the heart**
- Remove shoes or tight threads from the leg immediately
- **Do NOT** let the person walk; movement makes the poison spread
- **Do NOT** use herbal pastes or try to suck the poison out
- Call **108** for immediate transport to a hospital with ASV

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Snake bite occurs in the field"] --> B["Stop the person from walking or moving"]
  B --> C["Apply a splint using a stick and local cloth"]
  C --> D["Remove rings or tight clothing near the bite"]
  D --> E["Do NOT cut the wound or apply cow dung/herbs"]
  E --> F["Arrange for 108 ambulance or local transport"]
  F --> G["Stay with the patient and check breathing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any swelling | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Vision changes | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Pain only (no marks) | PHC for observation | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is first-aid only. Only Anti-Snake Venom (ASV) at a hospital can save a life. Do not waste time with local healers.

Query: A woman was bitten by a 'Kala Nag' (Cobra) on her hand. She is vomiting and her breathing is getting heavy. How can I help her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Shortness of breath or heavy gasping**
- Severe vomiting and stomach pain
- Drooling or inability to swallow saliva
- Cold, clammy skin
- Fainting or loss of consciousness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (18-60) | Female | Keep her sitting up if breathing is hard, immobilize arm |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral, lay her on her left side during transport |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of heart failure, rush to CHC immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If she is vomiting, **turn her to the side** so she doesn't choke
- **Remove bangles and rings** immediately before the hand swells
- Tie the arm to a piece of wood or bamboo to keep it still
- **Do NOT** give her water or any 'kadha' (herbal drink) to drink
- **Do NOT** tie a tight rope (tourniquet) as it can cause gangrene
- Note the time of the bite and the time vomiting started

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on hand with vomiting"] --> B["Remove all jewelry and bangles immediately"]
  B --> C["Turn patient to the side if they are vomiting"]
  C --> D["Keep the arm still and level with the chest"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything to drink or eat"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to a facility with oxygen and ASV"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Heavy breathing | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Vomiting | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Arm swelling | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Breathing difficulty indicates a neurotoxic bite. Every minute counts. Only a hospital can provide life-saving respiratory support.

Query: A child was sleeping on the floor and got bitten by a snake. The child is very sleepy and won't wake up properly. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme sleepiness or 'drunken' behavior**
- Small puncture marks with no pain (common in Krait bites)
- Difficulty opening the eyes
- Weakness in the legs and arms
- Shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Carry child to the vehicle, do not let them walk |
| Infant | Any | Emergency referral, watch for blue lips or nails |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the child **awake** by talking to them loudly
- Lay the child on their side (recovery position) if they are drifting off
- **Check the throat** to make sure no tongue-blockage or spit is stuck
- **Do NOT** wait for swelling; some venomous snakes don't cause swelling
- **Do NOT** apply any 'Jhad-phook' or local tantrik methods
- Rush to the nearest hospital that has a ventilator or AMBU bag

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child bitten while sleeping"] --> B["Keep the child awake and talking"]
  B --> C["Lay on side to keep airway clear"]
  C --> D["Check for tiny bite marks on the body"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply heat, cold, or herbal pastes"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital with pediatric emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Child is very sleepy | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Drooping eyelids | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Tiny marks, no pain | Refer for 24-hour observation | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Krait bites often happen at night and are painless but highly lethal. Drowsiness is a major warning sign. Refer immediately.

Query: A man was bitten by a thick snake with circles on its back. The wound is bleeding a lot and won't stop. How to stop the blood?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Continuous bleeding from the bite wound**
- Bleeding from the nose or old scars
- Blood in the urine or spit
- Large purple bruises appearing on the skin
- Severe pain and rapid swelling

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Any | Keep the limb still, do not apply pressure bandages |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Monitor for shock (cold skin, fast pulse) |
| Pregnant | Female | High risk of internal bleeding, rush to District Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply a **clean, dry cloth** lightly over the wound; do NOT press hard
- Keep the bitten part **very still** to prevent more bleeding
- **Do NOT** cut the wound to 'drain' the blood; this will make it worse
- **Do NOT** use a tourniquet as it can cause the limb to rot (gangrene)
- Reassure the patient; high blood pressure from fear increases bleeding
- Transport to a hospital that can perform blood clotting tests

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite wound is bleeding continuously"] --> B["Place a clean cloth lightly over the area"]
  B --> C["Immobilize the limb with a simple splint"]
  C --> D["Do NOT cut or squeeze the wound"]
  D --> E["Check for bleeding from gums or nose"]
  E --> F["Transfer to District Hospital for ASV and blood test"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Non-stop bleeding | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Blood in spit/urine | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Rapid swelling | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bleeding indicates a vasculotoxic bite (like Russell's Viper). It can cause kidney failure. Only a hospital can stop the internal damage.

Query: I am an ASHA worker. A villager was bitten on the neck while cleaning the cattle shed. He is dizzy. What is the first aid for a neck bite?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling around the neck or throat**
- Difficulty breathing or wheezing
- Change in voice or inability to speak
- Rapidly increasing dizziness
- Loss of consciousness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Keep the person sitting upright, do not let them lie flat |
| Adult | Any | Monitor airway constantly, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the patient **sitting upright** to help them breathe better
- **Do NOT** apply any bandages or pressure to the neck
- **Do NOT** give the patient anything to swallow (not even water)
- Encourage the patient to stay calm; panic will make breathing harder
- Arrange the fastest possible transport to a hospital with an ICU
- If they stop breathing, begin rescue breaths (mouth-to-mouth) if trained

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on the neck or trunk area"] --> B["Keep the patient sitting upright"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply any bandage or local pressure"]
  C --> D["Monitor for any swelling of the throat"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give water, food, or herbs"]
  E --> F["Rush to a hospital with ventilator support"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Neck bite | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Voice change | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites on the neck are extremely dangerous as swelling can block the airway. Do not wait for any other symptoms to appear.

Query: A young boy was bitten on the foot. The family has tied a very tight rope around his thigh. Should I leave it or remove it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **The foot below the rope is turning blue or black**
- No pulse can be felt in the foot
- Severe pain where the rope is tied
- Swelling is very large above the rope

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Keep the child calm, do not remove the rope yet |
| Adult | Any | Prepare for immediate transport before touching the rope |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT** remove the rope suddenly; this can release a 'burst' of poison
- Leave the rope in place **UNTIL** the patient is at the hospital and ASV is ready
- If the rope is so tight it is cutting the skin, loosen it slightly but do not remove
- Splint the leg to keep it still
- Reassure the family that the hospital is the only place for treatment
- Tell the doctor exactly what time the rope was tied

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Tight rope/tourniquet found on limb"] --> B["Do NOT remove the rope at home"]
  B --> C["Immobilize the leg with a stick and cloth"]
  C --> D["Check the color of the toes for blood flow"]
  D --> E["Transport to hospital immediately"]
  E --> F["Remove rope ONLY when doctor is ready with ASV"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue/Cold toes | Emergency - Hospital Referral | Never |
| Severe pain | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Rope is present | Refer to have it safely removed | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Removing a tight tourniquet at home can cause sudden death or heart failure. It must be done in a hospital under medical supervision.

Query: A woman was bitten on the finger. She is using a 'snake stone' and herbs. What should I tell her and how can I help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Time passing without medical treatment**
- Swelling spreading from the finger to the wrist
- Feeling faint or weak
- Nausea or headache

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Calmly explain that stones do not work, immobilize arm |
| Elderly | Female | High risk of delay, insist on hospital visit |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Gently tell the family** that 'snake stones' and herbs cannot reach the poison in the blood
- **Do NOT** waste time arguing; focus on getting her to the hospital
- Remove her rings and bangles immediately before the arm swells
- Use a **sling or cloth** to keep the arm still and close to the body
- Note the time the 'remedies' were applied so the doctor knows the delay
- Call **108** or arrange a local vehicle immediately

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient using local stones or herbs"] --> B["Explain that medicine is needed to stop the poison"]
  B --> C["Remove all rings and jewelry from the hand"]
  C --> D["Keep the hand still using a sling or cloth"]
  D --> E["Do NOT wash away the 'herbs' if it wastes time"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital without further delay"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Finger bite | Refer to CHC immediately | Never |
| Swelling at site | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Use of 'remedies' | Refer due to risk of delay | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Snake stones and herbal pastes are not effective against venom. Delaying hospital treatment is the leading cause of death in snake bites.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake and now he is fainted. He is breathing but he is not waking up. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconsciousness (cannot be woken up)**
- Shallow or slow breathing
- Snoring sounds while breathing
- Cold skin and weak pulse
- Seizures or shaking

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Recovery position (lay on side), clear the mouth |
| Adult | Any | Call 108 immediately, do not give anything by mouth |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the person on their **left side** with the top leg bent (Recovery Position)
- Tilt the head back slightly to keep the **airway open**
- Check the mouth for any vomit or spit and clear it with a cloth
- **Do NOT** pour water, milk, or medicine into his mouth; he will choke
- **Do NOT** slap or shake the person violently
- Keep the bitten limb still and low
- Rush to a hospital with an **ICU or Ventilator** facility

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient is unconscious after bite"] --> B["Lay patient on their left side (Recovery Position)"]
  B --> C["Tilt head back and clear mouth of spit"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything to drink or eat"]
  D --> E["Check if they are still breathing"]
  E --> F["Transport to ICU-equipped hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fainting/Unconscious | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Snoring/Heavy breathing | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Weak pulse | Emergency - Immediate Hospital | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Unconsciousness after a snake bite is a critical emergency. The venom is likely affecting the brain or the heart. Do not delay.

Query: A villager was bitten by a snake with a triangular head and has swelling, bleeding from the wound and is feeling dizzy. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling spreading above the bite site
- **Bleeding from wound or gums**
- **Drooping eyelids or blurred vision**
- Difficulty breathing
- Unconsciousness or confusion

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Keep calm, immobilize limb, rush to CHC immediately |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Immobilize limb below heart, remove jewelry, call 108 |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk — call 108 immediately, do not wait |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral, monitor fetal movement |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the person **still and calm** — movement spreads venom faster
- **Immobilize** the bitten limb and keep it **below heart level**
- Remove rings, bangles, tight clothing near the bite
- **Do NOT** cut the wound, suck the venom, or apply tourniquet
- **Do NOT** give food, water, or any home remedies
- Mark the edge of swelling with a pen and note the time
- Call **108** and transfer to nearest CHC with anti-venom

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Snake bite reported by ASHA worker"] --> B["Check breathing, pulse, and consciousness"]
  B --> C["Immobilize bitten limb below heart level"]
  C --> D["Remove tight clothing and jewelry near bite"]
  D --> E["Mark swelling boundary and note time of bite"]
  E --> F["Do NOT cut wound, suck venom, or tie tourniquet"]
  F --> G["Call 108 ambulance immediately"]
  G --> H["Transfer to CHC with anti-venom facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling beyond bite site | Refer immediately to CHC | Never |
| Bleeding from wound/gums | Emergency referral — call 108 | Not safe at home |
| Drooping eyelids | Emergency referral — call 108 | Not safe at home |
| Mild pain, no swelling | Refer to PHC within 1 hour | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This guidance is for first-aid support only. Always refer to a qualified health facility. Anti-venom must be given by trained medical staff only.

Query: A man was bitten on the leg. There is no swelling yet, but he has severe stomach pain and is sweating a lot. What does this mean?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe abdominal (stomach) pain**
- Excessive sweating and shivering
- Vomiting or feeling very sick
- Fast heart rate
- Difficulty speaking

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Immobilize the bitten leg, keep him lying down |
| Elderly | Any | Check for chest pain, monitor pulse closely |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This could be a sign of **systemic poisoning** even without swelling
- Keep him **lying down completely still**
- Do NOT give him anything to drink for the stomach pain
- Use a **folded cloth or stick** to make sure the leg doesn't move
- Note the time the stomach pain started
- Arrange immediate transport to a hospital with ASV

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with stomach pain and sweating"] --> B["Keep the patient lying down and calm"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any liquids or food"]
  C --> D["Immobilize the bitten leg immediately"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any drooping of eyelids"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for ASV treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stomach pain | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Excessive sweating | Immediate Referral | Never |
| No swelling | Still refer if bite is suspected | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Some venomous bites (like Krait or Viper) can cause internal pain and sweating before any skin changes appear. Always refer.

Query: A child was bitten by a snake while playing in the bushes. The area is red and has two clear holes. The child is crying and breathing fast. What first aid can I give?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Two distinct puncture marks (fang marks)**
- Rapid swelling around the holes
- Child becoming quiet or unusually sleepy
- Breathing getting faster or shallow
- Redness spreading up the limb

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Comfort the child, stop them from crying/moving, carry them |
| Toddler | Any | High danger - emergency hospital referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Calm the child down**; crying makes the heart pump venom faster
- **Do NOT** wash the bite with hot water; use plain water if needed, but don't scrub
- Tie a splint using a **scale or stick** to keep the foot/hand still
- Carry the child in your arms; do not let them walk to the car
- Call the hospital ahead to ensure they have **Pediatric ASV**
- Note the time of the bite

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child bitten with fang marks"] --> B["Calm the child and stop them from moving"]
  B --> C["Immobilize the limb using a splint and cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT suck the wound or use a knife"]
  D --> E["Carry the child to the transport vehicle"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital with ASV"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fang marks present | Emergency - Immediate Hospital | Never |
| Rapid breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Crying/Panic | Refer to keep child still and monitored | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Children are at higher risk because their body size is small. Even a small amount of venom can be very dangerous. Refer immediately.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake and he is saying he can't lift his head or neck. He looks like he is falling asleep. What is happening?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Neck muscle weakness (head dropping)**
- Extreme sleepiness (cannot stay awake)
- Slurred speech (talking like a drunk person)
- Drooping eyelids
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Keep the head supported, do not let it flop back |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of respiratory failure, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This is a sign the **venom is affecting the nerves** (Neurotoxic)
- Keep the patient sitting or lying on their side with the **head supported**
- Ensure the airway is clear; if he can't hold his head up, he may choke on spit
- **Do NOT** give any tea, coffee, or stimulants to wake him up
- **Do NOT** wait; this person needs a ventilator very soon
- Transport immediately to the largest hospital in the area

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient cannot lift head or has drooping eyelids"] --> B["Support the head and keep airway open"]
  B --> C["Lay patient on their side if they are drowsy"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything by mouth"]
  D --> E["Immobilize the bitten limb if not done already"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital with ICU/Ventilator facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Broken neck sign (head drop) | Emergency - Immediate ICU | Never |
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Drowsiness | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Broken neck' sign is a classic symptom of Krait or Cobra bite. It is a life-threatening emergency. Every second counts.

Query: A person was bitten on the arm and someone has applied a herbal paste and tied a cloth over it. The arm is swelling under the cloth. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling spreading rapidly under or above the bandage**
- The herbal paste is making the skin look burnt or irritated
- Numbness or tingling in the fingers
- Severe throbbing pain

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Do not remove the bandage suddenly if it is very tight |
| Adult | Any | Immobilize the whole arm to the chest with a sling |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If the cloth is **not too tight**, you can leave it and add a splint to keep the arm still
- If the cloth is **cutting into the skin**, loosen it slightly but do not take it off completely yet
- **Do NOT** spend time washing off the herbal paste if it delays transport
- Remove rings and watches from that arm immediately
- Keep the arm **at the level of the heart**, not higher or lower
- Get the patient to the hospital and tell the doctor about the paste

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with herbal paste and cloth bandage"] --> B["Remove jewelry from the arm immediately"]
  B --> C["Immobilize the arm against the chest with a sling"]
  C --> D["Do NOT scrub off the herbal paste at home"]
  D --> E["Monitor the swelling and pulse at the wrist"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC with ASV facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling under bandage | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Numbness in fingers | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Herbal paste used | Refer to prevent infection/delay | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Herbal pastes can cause skin infections and do nothing to stop the venom. The priority is getting to a doctor, not cleaning the wound.

Query: A farmer was bitten by a snake. He is now complaining of severe back pain and his urine is very dark, like tea. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dark brown or 'tea-colored' urine**
- Severe back or kidney pain
- Decreased amount of urine
- Bleeding from the gums or nose
- General body weakness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Keep the patient hydrated ONLY if they can swallow safely |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of kidney failure, immediate hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This is a sign of **kidney damage** from the venom (typical of Vipers)
- Keep the patient lying down and still
- **Do NOT** give any local medicines for the back pain
- If the patient is fully awake, you can give small sips of water, but hospital is priority
- Note when the last time they passed urine was and what color it was
- Transport to a hospital that has **Dialysis and ASV**

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with dark urine and back pain"] --> B["Note the time of the last urine and its color"]
  B --> C["Keep the patient lying down and calm"]
  C --> D["Immobilize the bitten limb if not done"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give herbal medicines for pain"]
  E --> F["Transport to District Hospital with Dialysis support"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dark urine | Emergency - District Hospital | Never |
| Back pain | Immediate Referral | Never |
| No urine for hours | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dark urine is a sign that the venom is breaking down blood cells and damaging the kidneys. This requires specialized hospital care.

Query: A woman was bitten on the toe. She is dizzy and says her tongue feels thick. She is finding it hard to speak. What first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Thick tongue or difficulty moving the tongue**
- Slurred or slow speech
- Drooling saliva
- Dizziness and feeling heavy
- Eyelids closing on their own

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Keep her sitting up, watch her breathing |
| Pregnant | Female | Emergency - call 108, lay on left side during transport |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Help her to sit in a comfortable position with **back support**
- Keep her **talking to you** to monitor her speech and consciousness
- **Do NOT** give her anything to eat or drink as she may choke
- Splint the foot and ankle to prevent movement
- Clear any saliva from her mouth with a clean cloth
- Rush to a hospital that has ASV and oxygen

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with thick tongue and slurred speech"] --> B["Keep the patient sitting upright and supported"]
  B --> C["Monitor breathing and speech every 5 minutes"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give water or food - risk of choking"]
  D --> E["Immobilize the foot and ankle with a splint"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital with ICU/Emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Dizziness | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Speech and tongue problems are signs of neurotoxic venom affecting the nerves. This can lead to breathing stop very quickly.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake on the thigh. He is in shock, his skin is cold and he is breathing very fast. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cold, clammy, or pale skin**
- Very fast or very weak pulse
- Rapid, shallow breathing
- Confusion or extreme anxiety
- Fainting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Lay them flat, raise the legs slightly (if bite is NOT on leg) |
| Elderly | Any | Keep them warm with a blanket, monitor breathing |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the person **flat on their back**
- If the bite is on the arm, you can raise the legs slightly to help blood flow to the brain
- **If the bite is on the leg, keep the leg FLAT and still**
- Cover the person with a **blanket or extra cloth** to keep them warm
- Reassure them constantly in a calm voice
- **Do NOT** give any liquid to drink
- Transport to the hospital immediately in a lying position

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient shows signs of shock - cold skin, fast breathing"] --> B["Lay the patient flat on their back"]
  B --> C["Cover them with a blanket to maintain warmth"]
  C --> D["Reassure the patient to keep their heart rate low"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any food or water"]
  E --> F["Transport in a lying position to the hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cold skin/Shock | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Fast breathing | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shock can be caused by the venom or extreme fear. Both are dangerous and need medical monitoring at a hospital.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake and he is now having a fit (seizure). His body is shaking and his teeth are clenched. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Body shaking or convulsions (fits)**
- Clenched jaw or teeth
- Foaming at the mouth
- Eyes rolling back
- Unconsciousness after the fit

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Move objects away from them, do not restrain |
| Child | Any | High danger - keep airway clear after the fit |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move any hard or sharp objects** away from the person so they don't hit them
- **Do NOT** put anything in their mouth (like a spoon or cloth) to stop teeth clenching
- **Do NOT** try to hold them down or stop the shaking
- Once the shaking stops, turn them onto their **side (recovery position)**
- Check if they are breathing after the fit
- Rush to a hospital that has anti-seizure medicine and ASV

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient is having a fit/convulsion"] --> B["Clear the area of hard or sharp objects"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put anything in the mouth"]
  C --> D["Wait for the shaking to stop naturally"]
  D --> E["Turn the patient onto their side once finished"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital for emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizures/Fits | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Foaming at mouth | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Clenched teeth | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fits after a snake bite mean the venom is affecting the brain or blood pressure. This is a very serious stage. Do not wait.

Query: A girl was bitten on her ankle. She says her chest feels tight and she is having trouble swallowing her own spit. Is this serious?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Tightness in the chest or throat**
- Inability to swallow saliva (drooling)
- Difficulty taking a deep breath
- Panic and fast heart rate
- Weakness in the neck

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Girl | Female | Keep her sitting up, tell her to breathe slowly |
| Adult | Any | Monitor for chest pain, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Yes, this is **very serious**; it means the breathing muscles are getting weak
- Keep the girl in a **sitting position**, leaning slightly forward
- Tell her to stay as calm as possible to save her energy for breathing
- **Do NOT** give her water to 'clear her throat' as she may choke
- Splint the ankle to stop any movement of the leg
- Call **108** and explain that she has breathing difficulty

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Tightness in chest and difficulty swallowing"] --> B["Keep the patient sitting upright and calm"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give anything by mouth to drink"]
  C --> D["Immobilize the bitten ankle with a splint"]
  D --> E["Note the time the chest tightness started"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital with ICU/Oxygen facilities"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest tightness | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Drooling saliva | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Trouble breathing | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Difficulty swallowing and chest tightness are early signs of respiratory failure. The patient may need a ventilator soon.

Query: A farmer was bitten on the hand by a snake he thinks was a Russell's Viper. The whole arm is swelling and he has severe joint pain. What can I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling spreading past the elbow**
- Severe, unbearable pain in the whole arm or joints
- Large blisters forming around the bite
- Darkening of the skin (turning purple or black)
- Bleeding from the gums

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Remove rings/watches, immobilize arm in a sling |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of tissue death, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Remove all **rings, bangles, and watches** immediately; the arm will swell like a balloon
- Keep the arm **at the level of the heart** (use a cloth sling)
- **Do NOT** apply ice or cold water to the swelling; it can damage the skin
- **Do NOT** squeeze or try to pop any blisters that form
- Mark the top edge of the swelling with a pen and write the time next to it
- Transport to a hospital that can handle tissue damage and has ASV

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with rapid swelling and severe pain"] --> B["Remove all rings and jewelry from the arm"]
  B --> C["Support the arm in a sling at heart level"]
  C --> D["Mark the swelling line with a pen and note time"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply ice or cut the skin"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital for ASV and pain management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling above elbow | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| Severe joint pain | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Blisters forming | Refer to prevent infection/rot | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Viper bites cause severe local damage (cytotoxic). If not treated with ASV, the person may lose their arm or suffer kidney failure.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake. He is not having any swelling or pain, but he is saying his vision is double and he can't see one person as one. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Double vision (seeing two of everything)**
- Blurred vision or inability to focus
- Drooping eyelids (eyes look half-closed)
- Dizziness and loss of balance
- Weakness in the neck

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Keep them sitting down, do not let them walk or drive |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor breathing, high risk of quick collapse |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This is a major sign of **neurotoxic poisoning** (often from a Krait)
- Keep the person **completely still**; movement will speed up the paralysis
- Reassure the person and tell them to keep their eyes closed to rest
- **Do NOT** wait for swelling or pain; Krait bites often have neither
- **Do NOT** give any eye drops or local medicine
- Transport immediately to a hospital with an ICU and ventilator

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient complains of double vision or blurred sight"] --> B["Check for drooping eyelids (sleepy look)"]
  B --> C["Keep the patient sitting and still"]
  C --> D["Immobilize the bitten limb immediately"]
  D --> E["Do NOT wait for pain or swelling to appear"]
  E --> F["Rush to a hospital with ventilator support"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Double vision | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Drooping eyelids | Emergency - Immediate Referral | Never |
| No pain/swelling | Still refer if vision changes | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Changes in vision are a sign that the venom is starting to paralyze the muscles. This is a life-threatening emergency. Do not delay.

Query: Doctor Saheb, a boy was bitten by a small green garden snake (Vine snake) while playing. There is no swelling, but he is crying. What is the first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Any swelling starting after 30 minutes
- Feeling very sleepy or dizzy
- Vomiting or stomach pain
- Difficulty opening the eyes

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Wash wound, keep them still, monitor at PHC for 4 hours |
| Adult | Any | Clean with soap, visit PHC for Tetanus injection |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor heart rate; fear can cause high BP |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the bite area with **running water and soap** for 10 minutes
- Keep the child **calm** — tell them they are safe to keep the heart rate low
- Do NOT apply any lime (chuna), chili, or herbal pastes to the wound
- Do NOT tie any tight threads or ropes around the limb
- Visit the nearest **Primary Health Centre (PHC)** for a Tetanus shot

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite from small green snake reported"] --> B["Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Check for swelling or fang marks every 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Calm the patient to prevent panic-induced shock"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for Tetanus injection and observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No swelling after 4 hours | Home care after PHC visit | After Tetanus shot and doctor check |
| Any redness spreading | Refer to CHC immediately | Not safe at home |
| Fang marks (two dots) | Refer to CHC to be safe | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even non-venomous bites can cause infection or Tetanus. Always get the wound cleaned by a medical professional.

Query: A farmer was bitten by a 'Dhaman' (Rat Snake) on his leg. It is bleeding quite a bit but he says it’s not a poisonous snake. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bleeding that does not stop after pressing for 5 minutes
- Wound looking deep or torn
- Swelling moving up the leg
- Fainting from fear (Shock)

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Press the wound with clean cloth to stop bleeding |
| Elderly | Any | Keep lying down; watch for dizziness |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Use a **clean cloth** to press firmly on the bite to stop the bleeding
- Wash the area with **soap and clean water** once bleeding slows
- Keep the leg **still**; do not let the farmer walk to the clinic
- Do NOT use any 'snake stones' or local mud on the open wound
- Take him to the **PHC** for wound dressing and a Tetanus injection

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Rat snake bite with bleeding leg"] --> B["Apply firm pressure with a clean cloth to stop bleeding"]
  B --> C["Wash the wound with soap and water once stable"]
  C --> D["Keep the leg still and supported"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for wound cleaning and Tetanus toxoid"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding stops quickly | PHC visit for cleaning | Monitor for infection at home |
| Deep tooth tears | Refer for stitches | Not safe at home |
| Swelling starts | Emergency referral to CHC | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rat snakes have many small teeth that can cause infection. Professional cleaning is necessary.

Query: A woman was bitten by a water snake (Pani wala saanp) while washing clothes. She is very scared and shivering. There is only a scratch. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Shivering that turns into shaking/fits
- Rapid heartbeat or chest pain
- Nausea or vomiting
- Blurred vision

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Give warm support, calm her down, clean the scratch |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate PHC visit to check fetal heart rate due to stress |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Reassure her** that most water snakes in the village are not venomous
- Wash the scratch with **soap and water**; do not scrub hard
- Let her sit in a quiet place and offer a drink of water (only if fully awake)
- Do NOT apply any oils or traditional 'Jhar-phook'
- Visit the **PHC** to ensure it wasn't a baby cobra or viper

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Water snake bite with minor scratch"] --> B["Calm the patient and sit them down"]
  B --> C["Wash the scratch with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Monitor for any unusual symptoms like dizziness"]
  D --> E["Visit PHC for Tetanus injection and observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Minor scratch, no swelling | PHC visit mandatory | Home care after observation |
| Severe panic/shivering | Refer for counseling/sedation | Not safe at home |
| Vomiting | Emergency referral to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fear can cause symptoms that look like poisoning. Always treat the patient with calm and refer for professional observation.

Query: A child was bitten by a small brown snake near the woodpile. The marks look like a 'U' shape, not two dots. Is this dangerous?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Any change in the bite mark (swelling/bruising)
- Drooping eyelids
- Difficulty breathing
- Pain spreading to the belly

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Wash area, keep the child still, watch for 4 hours |
| Adult | Any | Clean wound, get Tetanus shot at PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the 'U' shaped mark with **soap and water**; these are usually non-venomous teeth marks
- **Mark the area** with a pen to see if any swelling starts later
- Keep the limb **below the heart level** just in case
- Do NOT use a knife to cut the 'U' marks to make them bleed
- Take the child to the **PHC** for 4-hour observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["U-shaped bite marks reported"] --> B["Wash with soap and water immediately"]
  B --> C["Mark the bite area with a pen to monitor swelling"]
  C --> D["Keep the limb still and slightly low"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for 4-hour medical observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| U-shaped marks, no pain | PHC for 4 hours | Home care after doctor clears |
| Two clear fang dots (..) | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Swelling starts | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Identification can be wrong. Always monitor for 4 hours at a medical facility even if marks look non-venomous.

Query: A man was bitten by a snake while cleaning his cattle shed. He caught the snake and it looks like a Wolf Snake (Ghar-wala saanp). What should he do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Neck weakness (Head dropping)
- Difficulty speaking or swallowing
- Blurred vision
- Weakness in legs

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Clean wound, keep calm, refer to CHC |
| Elderly | Any | High risk — monitor breathing closely |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Caution:** Wolf Snakes look exactly like Kraits (deadly). **Treat as venomous** until a doctor confirms
- Wash the bite with **soap and water** gently
- Keep the person **completely still** and lying on their side
- Do NOT try to handle the snake further; leave it alone
- Do NOT give any food or water
- Rush to the **CHC** immediately for observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite by snake looking like a Wolf Snake"] --> B["Assume it is a Krait and keep patient still"]
  B --> C["Wash the wound gently with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything to eat or drink"]
  D --> E["Transport immediately to CHC with anti-venom (ASV)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Looks like Krait/Wolf Snake | Emergency CHC referral | Never — too risky |
| Any drowsiness | Emergency CHC referral | Never |
| No symptoms yet | Still refer for 24 hours | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Distinguishing Wolf Snakes from Kraits is very hard. Misidentification kills. Always refer to a hospital.

Query: A girl was bitten by a snake while picking fruits. There is no pain, just a little itch. Her grandmother wants to apply cow dung. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain starting after some time
- Redness or hot skin around the bite
- Fever or body ache
- Swelling of the glands in the armpit or groin

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | Wash with soap, prevent local remedies, visit PHC |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for infection or allergic reaction |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **STOP** the application of cow dung; it causes severe infection (Tetanus/Gangrene)
- Wash the area thoroughly with **clean water and soap**
- Keep the girl calm and explain that the wound must stay clean
- Do NOT scratch the area even if it itches
- Take her to the **PHC** for a clean dressing and Tetanus injection

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Minor bite with itching"] --> B["Prevent application of cow dung or mud"]
  B --> C["Wash the wound with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Observe for any late-starting pain or swelling"]
  D --> E["Visit PHC for cleaning and Tetanus prevention"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itching only | PHC for cleaning | Home care after cleaning |
| Pus or foul smell | Refer for antibiotics | Not safe at home |
| Cow dung already applied | Refer for deep cleaning | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Local remedies like cow dung are the leading cause of wound infections after snake bites. Keep the wound clean and dry.

Query: A boy was bitten through his jeans by a small snake. He has two tiny scratches. Should we ignore it since it didn't go deep?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Scratches becoming swollen or purple
- Vomiting or feeling sick
- Feeling very weak
- Dark colored urine

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Male | Clean the skin, monitor for 4 hours, visit PHC |
| Adult | Any | Clean with soap, check if Tetanus is up to date |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Never ignore** a bite; even small scratches can deliver venom if the snake is small
- Wash the skin with **soap and water** for 5-10 minutes
- Check the jeans for any wetness (venom) and avoid touching it with bare hands
- Do NOT suck on the scratches
- Go to the **PHC** for a professional check-up and observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite through clothing with minor scratches"] --> B["Wash the skin thoroughly with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Do NOT touch the clothing area where the bite hit"]
  C --> D["Monitor for 4 hours for any systemic symptoms"]
  D --> E["Visit PHC for medical clearance and Tetanus shot"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Tiny scratches through cloth | PHC for observation | After 4 hours of no symptoms |
| Any skin color change | Refer to CHC | Never at home |
| Dizziness | Emergency referral to CHC | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Clothing can protect, but 'dry bites' or partial bites from venomous snakes still need medical monitoring.

Query: A woman was bitten by a Banded Kukri snake. It is very painful and bleeding. She is crying in pain. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain that is so bad she can't move
- Swelling that moves beyond the joint
- Bleeding from other parts (gums/nose)
- Rapid swelling

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Clean wound, apply cool cloth for pain, refer to PHC |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral to manage pain and stress |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Kukri snakes have sharp teeth like knives; **clean the wound** with soap and water
- Apply a **cool, clean damp cloth** to the site to help with the pain
- Press with a clean cloth if bleeding is heavy
- Do NOT give aspirin or ibuprofen for pain at home (can increase bleeding)
- Take her to the **PHC** for proper pain relief and wound care

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Kukri snake bite with high pain and bleeding"] --> B["Apply pressure with clean cloth to stop bleeding"]
  B --> C["Wash with soap and water gently"]
  C --> D["Apply a cool cloth for local pain relief"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for wound care and observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe local pain | PHC for pain management | After doctor clears |
| Non-stop bleeding | Refer to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Fainting | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Kukri snakes are non-venomous but their bites are deep and painful. They require professional cleaning to prevent infection.

Query: An old man was bitten by a snake while sleeping. He is awake now and says it was a small house snake. Should he go to the hospital?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drowsiness or difficulty staying awake
- Drooping eyelids
- Difficulty breathing
- Muscle weakness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Male | High priority - transport to CHC for 24hr watch |
| Child | Any | Immediate transport for observation |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Bites while sleeping are very dangerous**; it could be a Krait (painless but deadly)
- Wash the area with **soap and water** but do not waste time
- Keep him **lying on his side** (Recovery position) during transport
- Do NOT wait for symptoms to appear
- Transport to the **CHC** immediately for 24-hour observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite while sleeping (unknown snake)"] --> B["Wash wound with soap and water quickly"]
  B --> C["Keep patient lying on their side"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any food or water"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC for 24-hour medical observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any bite while sleeping | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Small marks, no pain | Still refer for 24 hours | Never at home |
| Drowsiness | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Krait bites at night are often mistaken for non-venomous bites or insect stings. Always observe in a hospital for 24 hours.

Query: A boy was bitten on his finger by a baby snake in the fields. The finger is turning a little red. What is the step-by-step aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Redness spreading to the hand or wrist
- Swelling of the finger
- Numbness or 'pins and needles' feeling
- Nausea

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Remove rings/threads, wash wound, monitor at PHC |
| Adult | Any | Remove rings immediately, clean wound |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove any rings, strings, or threads** from the finger immediately
- Wash the finger with **soap and water**
- Keep the hand **still and at heart level**
- Do NOT use a tourniquet or tie the finger tightly
- Take the child to the **PHC** to ensure no venom was injected

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on finger by small snake"] --> B["Remove rings or threads from the finger immediately"]
  B --> C["Wash the finger with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Keep the hand still using a simple cloth sling"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for 4-hour observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Redness on finger | PHC visit mandatory | Home care after 4 hours |
| Swelling of hand | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Baby snake seen | Refer (babies can be venomous) | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Baby venomous snakes are born with venom. Never assume a small snake is harmless. Refer for observation.

Query: A man was bitten on the foot while walking in tall grass. He didn't see the snake. There is just one scratch and it's itching. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Itching turning into severe pain
- Swelling of the ankle
- Fang marks appearing later
- Weakness in the legs

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Clean with soap, mark the itch area, visit PHC |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for shock; use a stick for walking support |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the foot with **soap and water**
- **Mark the exact spot** of the scratch with a pen to watch for swelling
- Keep the foot **still**; do not let the person walk or run
- Do NOT apply any garlic, onion, or herbal pastes
- Go to the **PHC** for observation and a Tetanus shot

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unknown snake bite with itching scratch"] --> B["Wash the foot with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Mark the scratch area with a pen"]
  C --> D["Keep the foot still and do not walk"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for Tetanus and observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itching scratch | PHC for observation | After 4 hours of no symptoms |
| Swelling of ankle | Refer to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Difficulty walking | Refer to CHC | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
An unknown bite must be monitored as if it were venomous for at least 4 to 6 hours. Do not take risks.

Query: A child was bitten by a Trinket snake (Gehuan lookalike). The parents are panicking and want to give the child a 'ghutti'. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Child becoming very quiet or non-responsive
- Vomiting
- Swelling at the bite site
- Difficulty swallowing

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Calm parents, clean wound, transport to PHC |
| Infant | Any | High priority — immediate transport for watch |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT** allow the child to swallow 'ghutti' or any local medicine; if they vomit, they could choke
- Wash the bite with **soap and water**
- Reassure the parents and keep the child **distracted and calm**
- Do NOT tie any 'mantra' threads too tightly on the limb
- Take the child to the **PHC** for 4 hours of medical monitoring

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite by Trinket snake (looks like Gehuan)"] --> B["Prevent parents from giving oral liquids/medicines"]
  B --> C["Wash the bite area with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Keep the child still and monitor breathing"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for professional observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Looks like Cobra/Viper | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Child is crying/panicking | PHC for calming/watch | After doctor clears |
| Swelling starts | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Trinket snakes are harmless but look like venomous snakes. Fear can cause real symptoms. Always refer for observation.

Query: A woman was bitten by a snake while picking up a pile of bricks. She has a row of small puncture marks. What is the first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain spreading up the arm
- Bleeding from the marks that won't stop
- Skin turning blue or black around the marks
- Swelling of the neck glands

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Clean with soap, remove jewelry, visit PHC |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate PHC visit to avoid stress-related issues |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **A row of marks** usually means a non-venomous snake; wash with **soap and water**
- Remove all **bangles and rings** from that arm immediately
- Keep the arm **still in a sling** made from a dupatta or cloth
- Do NOT use a hot iron or coal to 'burn' the venom out
- Visit the **PHC** for wound cleaning and Tetanus toxoid injection

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Row of small puncture marks on hand"] --> B["Remove bangles and rings immediately"]
  B --> C["Wash the area with soap and water for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Place the arm in a cloth sling to keep it still"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for Tetanus and check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Many small tooth marks | PHC for cleaning | After doctor clears |
| Two fang marks (..) | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Pain is very high | Refer for pain relief | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even non-venomous snakes have bacteria in their mouths. A Tetanus shot and proper cleaning are required.

Query: A man was bitten by a Checkered Keelback (water snake) while fishing. He says it’s fine, but the wound is dirty from pond water. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pus or yellow fluid from the wound
- Fever or chills
- Increasing pain and redness
- Foul smell from the wound

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash thoroughly, monitor for 4 hours, visit PHC |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor closely; infection risk is higher |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound multiple times** with soap and clean, running water to remove pond germs
- Do NOT apply mud or pond weeds to the bite
- If available, apply a simple **Betadine/Antiseptic** solution after washing
- Keep the limb **still** for a few hours
- Visit the **PHC** for a Tetanus shot and potential antibiotics for the pond water infection

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Water snake bite with dirty water contact"] --> B["Wash with soap and clean water multiple times"]
  B --> C["Apply antiseptic if available in first aid kit"]
  C --> D["Observe for 4 hours for any systemic signs"]
  D --> E["Visit PHC for Tetanus and infection prevention"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dirty water contact | PHC for cleaning/Tetanus | After doctor clears |
| Red streaks on skin | Refer for antibiotics | Not safe at home |
| Fever starts | Refer to CHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Water snake bites are prone to infections from the water. Medical cleaning and Tetanus prevention are vital.

Query: A child has a snake bite with no marks, but the area is red and the child is very panicked. What is the doctor's advice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Hidden marks appearing later as dots or bruises
- Child becoming very pale or cold
- Crying that doesn't stop
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Calm with talk/toys, wash area, monitor at PHC |
| Infant | Any | Immediate transport to CHC for monitoring |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Calm the child**; panic can cause fast breathing and fainting
- Wash the red area with **soap and water** and look closely for any tiny breaks in the skin
- Do NOT give the child any sweets or 'energizing' drinks
- Keep the child **sitting still**; do not let them run around
- Take them to the **PHC** for 4-hour observation just in case it was a dry bite

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Red area with no clear bite marks"] --> B["Wash with soap and water and look for tiny holes"]
  B --> C["Calm the child and keep them sitting still"]
  C --> D["Monitor for 4 hours for any new symptoms"]
  D --> E["Visit PHC for medical check and Tetanus toxoid"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No marks, just redness | PHC for 4 hours | Home care after observation |
| Any marks appearing later | Refer to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Vomiting or lethargy | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Sometimes bite marks are very hard to see in children. Professional observation is always safer than assuming.

Query: A boy was bitten on the head while climbing a tree by a vine snake. He says his head feels heavy. Is it the poison or fear?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drooping eyelids
- Difficulty speaking
- Vomiting
- Neck stiffness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Male | Keep head still, do not apply pressure, rush to CHC |
| Adult | Any | Immediate transport for observation |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Bites on the head or neck are always High Risk**; do not guess if it is fear
- Wash the site gently with **soap and water**; do not press on the head
- Keep the boy **sitting upright** and calm
- Do NOT tie any bandages around the neck or head
- Transport to the **CHC** immediately for expert observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on the head or neck area"] --> B["Keep the patient sitting upright and calm"]
  B --> C["Wash the site gently with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Monitor breathing and speech closely"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC for immediate medical observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Head/Neck bite | Emergency CHC referral | Never — always refer |
| Feeling 'heavy head' | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| No symptoms | Still refer for 24 hours | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites near the brain or airway are emergencies regardless of the snake type. Always refer to a hospital.

Query: A man was bitten on the thigh by a snake. He wants to walk to the village clinic. Should I let him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain spreading quickly
- Feeling of fainting
- Rapid swelling of the thigh
- Nausea

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT let him walk; transport him sitting down |
| Elderly | Any | Use a cot or chair to carry him |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT let him walk**; muscle movement can spread venom if the snake was poisonous
- Wash the bite with **soap and water**
- Have him **sit or lie down** while you arrange a vehicle (cycle, rickshaw, or car)
- Do NOT use a tourniquet around the thigh
- Take him to the **PHC** for 4-hour observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on thigh - patient wants to walk"] --> B["Force the patient to sit or lie still"]
  B --> C["Wash the bite with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Arrange for transport where he doesn't use his leg"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for observation and Tetanus"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Thigh bite | PHC for 4 hours | After doctor clears |
| Leg weakness | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Walking was done | Refer for longer observation | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Walking after a bite is dangerous. Always transport the patient without them putting weight on the bitten limb.

Query: A woman was bitten by a non-venomous snake but she has a lot of bruising (blue-black marks) around the bite. Is this normal?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bruising spreading away from the bite
- Bleeding from the gums or nose
- Dark urine
- Severe pain

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Keep limb still, refer to CHC for blood tests |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of internal bleeding; rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Bruising can be a sign of Viper venom** (vasculotoxic); do not assume it is non-venomous
- Wash with **soap and water** gently; do NOT massage the bruise
- Keep the limb **completely still** with a splint
- Do NOT take any pain killers like aspirin
- Take her to the **CHC** or District Hospital for blood clotting tests

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite with blue-black bruising around marks"] --> B["Assume it could be a Viper bite and keep limb still"]
  B --> C["Wash gently with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply pressure or massage the bruise"]
  D --> E["Transport to hospital for blood tests and observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bruising/Blue skin | Emergency referral to CHC | Never at home |
| Bleeding gums | Emergency referral to CHC | Never at home |
| Local pain only | Still refer for observation | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bruising is a serious sign. It often means the blood is not clotting properly. Medical intervention is required immediately.

Query: A child was bitten on the toe. Someone said we should give him hot milk and salt. Is this okay?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Vomiting after drinking
- Swelling of the toe or foot
- Child becoming sleepy
- Breathing getting fast

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Do NOT give milk/salt, wash with soap, visit PHC |
| Infant | Any | Immediate transport to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT give anything by mouth**; hot milk and salt can cause vomiting and choking
- Wash the toe with **soap and water**
- Remove any strings or 'dhaga' from the toe
- Keep the foot **still** and do not let the child walk
- Take the child to the **PHC** for 4-hour observation

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on toe - local advice to give milk"] --> B["Refuse any oral food or liquids to the child"]
  B --> C["Wash the toe with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Keep the foot still and carry the child"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for 4-hour observation and Tetanus"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Toe bite | PHC for 4 hours | After doctor clears |
| Vomiting | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| Sleepiness | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Giving liquids to a snake bite victim is dangerous. If they lose consciousness or have a reaction, they could drown in the liquid.

Query: A farmer was bitten by a wolf snake on his arm. He says his arm feels itchy and there is a tiny bit of swelling. What is the doctor's step?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling spreading past the wrist
- Drooping eyelids
- Difficulty swallowing saliva
- Weakness in the neck

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Remove watch/rings, wash with soap, refer to CHC |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of Krait lookalike; immediate referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Warning:** Wolf snakes and Kraits are twins in looks. **Treat as Krait bite**
- Remove his **watch and any rings** from that arm immediately
- Wash with **soap and water**
- Put the arm in a **sling** and tell him not to move it at all
- Rush to the **CHC** for 24-hour observation; do not wait for the swelling to grow

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite by Wolf Snake (Krait lookalike)"] --> B["Remove watch and rings immediately"]
  B --> C["Wash with soap and water and apply sling"]
  C --> D["Keep patient calm and completely still"]
  D --> E["Transport to CHC immediately for 24-hour watch"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wolf Snake lookalike | Emergency CHC referral | Never — too dangerous |
| Tiny swelling | Emergency CHC referral | Never at home |
| No symptoms | Still refer for 24 hours | Never at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A Wolf Snake bite is often indistinguishable from a lethal Krait bite by a layman. Professional medical observation is the only safe path.

Query: A 4-year-old child was playing in the dark and a red scorpion stung his finger. He is crying a lot and now his mouth is watering too much. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Excessive drooling or frothing at the mouth**
- Fast or irregular heartbeat
- **Shaking of hands or legs (convulsions)**
- Difficulty breathing or blue lips
- Child becoming very sleepy or unconscious

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | **High Emergency.** Keep calm and rush to CHC immediately. |
| Adult (12-60) | Any | Monitor for sweating/vomiting; refer if pain spreads. |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Refer immediately; high risk of heart complications. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Clean the sting site with soap and water.
- Apply a **cold compress** or cloth dipped in cold water for 10 minutes.
- Keep the affected limb **still** and below heart level.
- Do NOT let the child swallow anything if they are drooling.
- **Rush to the nearest hospital** that has Prazosin or anti-scorpion serum.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child stung by scorpion"] --> B["Check for drooling or difficulty breathing"]
  B --> C["Clean sting site with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Apply cold cloth to the area for pain"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any food or water by mouth"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drooling/Frothing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Age under 5 years | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only mild pain at site | Visit PHC for observation | Monitor for 4 hours |
| Vomiting or Sweating | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Scorpion stings in children are life-threatening. Home remedies like matchstick heads or herbal pastes must NOT be used as they delay critical hospital care.

Query: A farmer was stung by a black scorpion while lifting stones. He has a lot of pain and says his vision is getting blurry. What is the first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blurred vision or roving eye movements**
- Severe sweating and cold skin
- High blood pressure or severe headache
- **Tightness in chest**
- Inability to swallow

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Male | Immobilize the arm/leg; transport to medical facility. |
| Adult (12-60) | Female | Check for pregnancy; if yes, urgent referral. |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High priority referral due to heart stress. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Make the person sit or lie down comfortably.
- **Immobilize** the stung limb using a splint or bandage (not tight).
- Use a cold pack on the site to reduce the burning sensation.
- Remove any rings or tight threads near the sting area.
- **Monitor heart rate**; if it feels very fast, tell the doctor upon arrival.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Farmer stung on limb"] --> B["Check for blurry vision or chest pain"]
  B --> C["Remove rings or tight clothing from limb"]
  C --> D["Immobilize the limb using a simple splint"]
  D --> E["Apply cold water compress to the sting"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital for Prazosin dose"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blurry vision | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Chest pain | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Localized burning | Refer to PHC for pain relief | Monitor for worsening |
| Heavy sweating | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is first aid only. Do not attempt to 'suck out' the venom or cut the skin. Immediate medical intervention is required for systemic symptoms.

Query: Ek mahila ko bichu ne kaat liya hai foot pe. She is sweating and breathing fast. Should I tie a cloth tightly above the sting?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Heavy sweating (soaked clothes)**
- Fast breathing or gasping for air
- Cold, clammy hands
- **Vomiting**
- Feeling of 'pins and needles' all over the body

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Female | Refer to hospital; monitor for shock. |
| Pregnant | Female | **Strict Emergency.** High risk for mother and baby. |
| Child | Any | Immediate referral to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **DO NOT tie a tight cloth (tourniquet)**; it can cause tissue damage.
- Keep the woman lying on her side if she feels like vomiting.
- Reassure her to keep her heart rate low.
- Apply a cool cloth to the foot.
- Get her to a hospital that can manage 'Scorpion Sting Syndrome'.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient sweating and gasping"] --> B["Lie patient on their side"] 
  B --> C["Do NOT tie a tight tourniquet"]
  C --> D["Keep the stung foot below heart level"]
  D --> E["Reassure the patient to keep them calm"]
  E --> F["Transfer immediately to CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sweating/Fast breathing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Pregnancy | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only pain at sting site | PHC visit recommended | Not safe to stay home |
| Fainting | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Local 'tantriks' or traditional healers should be avoided. Delay in scientific treatment (like Prazosin) is the main cause of death in scorpion stings.

Query: A person has no symptoms but a lot of pain after a scorpion sting 2 hours ago. Can we treat at home with local leaves?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain moving up the limb towards the body
- Sudden vomiting
- Shaking of the body
- Difficulty speaking

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Monitor closely for 4-6 hours. |
| Child | Any | **Never stay home.** Always refer. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Clean the wound with soap.
- Use **cold water/ice** for pain. Do NOT use hot water.
- Do NOT apply herbal pastes; they can cause infection.
- If the pain is unbearable or person becomes restless, go to PHC.
- Give paracetamol only if the person can swallow easily.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pain only, no other signs"] --> B["Clean wound with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress for 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Observe for 4 to 6 hours for new signs"]
  D --> E["If vomiting or sweating starts, GO TO HOSPITAL"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC for pain management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain spreading up | Refer to PHC/CHC | Not recommended |
| Vomiting starts | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Pain stays at site | PHC for pain relief | Observe for 6 hours |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Many scorpions in India are highly toxic. Observation in a medical facility is always safer than home care.

Query: A pregnant woman in the village got stung by a scorpion on her hand. She is very scared. What are the steps for me?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Abdominal pain or contractions**
- Sudden high blood pressure
- Blurry vision
- Breathlessness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | **Emergency.** Transfer to CHC with OBGYN support immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Calm the mother down; stress harms the fetus.
- Remove any bangles or rings from the hand immediately.
- Keep the hand still using a sling.
- Do NOT give any local medicine or 'kadha'.
- Transport her in a sitting position.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman stung"] --> B["Remove jewelry immediately"]
  B --> C["Calm the patient to lower heart rate"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use local herbal remedies"]
  D --> E["Arrange transport to CHC or District Hospital"]
  E --> F["Monitor for fetal movement and contractions"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any sting in pregnancy | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Hand swelling | Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Scorpion venom can induce premature labor. Hospital monitoring is mandatory.

Query: A teenage girl was stung by a scorpion while sleeping. She is vomiting continuously and her hands are cold. Is this serious?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Continuous vomiting**
- Cold and clammy skin (Shock sign)
- Weak and fast pulse
- Drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teen (12-18) | Female | Emergency referral. Continuous vomiting leads to dehydration and shock. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Turn her to the side to prevent choking on vomit.
- Do NOT give water or ORS if she is actively vomiting.
- Keep her warm with a blanket if skin is cold.
- Take her to the hospital for IV fluids and Prazosin.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Teenager vomiting after sting"] --> B["Turn patient on her side"]
  B --> C["Check if hands and feet are cold"]
  C --> D["Cover with a blanket to maintain warmth"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything to drink"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for IV fluids and medication"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Repeated vomiting | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Cold skin/Shock | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Systemic toxicity can develop quickly in teenagers. Do not wait for the vomiting to stop at home.

Query: An elderly man was stung by a big yellow scorpion. His heart is racing very fast and he has a bad headache. What should ASHA do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Racing heart (Palpitations)**
- Severe headache (High BP sign)
- Chest heaviness
- Confusion

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | **High Risk.** Heart failure is common in elderly after sting. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep him strictly in a resting position.
- Do NOT allow him to walk; use a stretcher or chair to move him.
- Apply cold packs to the sting site.
- Note if he is already a heart patient.
- Refer to a hospital with ECG facility.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly man with racing heart"] --> B["Force strict bed rest"]
```
