```mermaid
  B --> C["Do NOT let the patient walk"]
  C --> D["Check for history of heart disease/BP"]
  D --> E["Apply cold compress to sting area"]
  E --> F["Transfer to hospital with ECG facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Racing heart | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Severe headache | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Yellow scorpions (Mesobuthus) are the most dangerous in India. In the elderly, they cause rapid heart failure.

Query: A toddler was stung on the tongue because he picked up a scorpion. He is struggling to breathe. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of the tongue/throat**
- Gasping for air
- Blue fingernails or lips
- Total inability to swallow

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Toddler (1-3) | Any | **Critical Emergency.** Airway may close. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT put anything in the mouth.
- Keep the child upright to help them breathe.
- If the child stops breathing, start chest compressions (CPR) immediately.
- Rush to the nearest hospital with oxygen and ICU.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Toddler stung on tongue"] --> B["Check for airway blockage"]
  B --> C["Keep child in upright sitting position"]
  C --> D["Do NOT attempt to wash the tongue"]
  D --> E["Provide oxygen if available in ambulance"]
  E --> F["Rush to ICU-equipped hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Blue skin/lips | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stings to the face or mouth are much more dangerous than stings to the feet. Mortality is very high without immediate hospital care.

Query: A young man was stung 4 hours ago. He used a hot iron to burn the wound. Now it is infected and he is feeling dizzy. What next?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dizziness or fainting**
- Severe burn at the site
- Red streaks coming from the wound
- Pus or foul smell

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Male | Refer for both venom toxicity and burn wound care. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT apply anything more to the burn.
- Cover the area with a clean, dry cloth.
- Give water only if he is fully conscious.
- Refer to PHC for Tetanus toxoid and antibiotics.
- Monitor BP as dizziness could mean heart issues.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Burned sting site + Dizziness"] --> B["Cover burn with clean dry cloth"]
  B --> C["Check blood pressure and pulse"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply ointments or oils"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for wound cleaning and TT"]
  E --> F["Observe for 2 hours for venom signs"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Infected burn | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Burning or cutting the wound is harmful and does not stop the venom. It only adds the risk of infection and gangrene.

Query: A woman was stung on her belly while cleaning the house. She is having severe muscle cramps and her eyes are moving strangely. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Roving eye movements (eyes moving fast left and right)**
- Muscle twitching or severe cramps
- Drooling
- Slurred speech

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Female | Referral required. Eye and muscle signs mean nerve involvement. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep her in a dark, quiet room (light may trigger cramps).
- Do NOT restrain her if she twitches; just protect her from falling.
- Apply cold packs to the belly sting site.
- Transport her gently to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Eye roving and muscle cramps"] --> B["Place in a quiet, dark area"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to the belly sting"]
  C --> D["Protect from injury during twitching"]
  D --> E["Do NOT put anything in the mouth"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC/Medical College"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Roving eye movements | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Muscle twitching | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Neurological signs (eyes/muscles) indicate a severe sting. Only hospital-grade medication can stabilize these symptoms.

Query: Should I give Garlic or Onion paste to a person who got stung by a scorpion? They are in deep pain.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain spreading quickly to the whole body
- Restlessness
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Focus on pain relief, not pastes. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use Garlic/Onion pastes**; they cause skin irritation and mask symptoms.
- Wash the area with plain soap and water.
- Use **ICE or cold water** to numb the pain. This is the best local remedy.
- If the person is an adult and pain is only at the site, paracetamol can be given.
- If the pain doesn't reduce in 30 minutes, go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about herbal pastes"] --> B["Advise AGAINST using garlic/onion/herbal pastes"]
  B --> C["Wash site with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Apply ice or cold cloth for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Give paracetamol for pain if conscious"]
  E --> F["Monitor for 1 hour for sweating or vomiting"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe, unmanaged pain | Refer to PHC | Not at home |
| Any systemic signs | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Natural remedies often waste precious time. Cold water is the only 'home remedy' proven to help with scorpion pain.

Query: A man unconscious in the fields with a scorpion nearby. What is the emergency protocol?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconsciousness**
- No response to name or pain
- Shallow or no breathing
- Weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Highest Emergency.** ABC protocol (Airway, Breathing, Circulation). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Check if he is breathing. If not, start **CPR**.
- Turn the patient to the **Recovery Position** (on his side).
- Do NOT pour water into his mouth (he will choke).
- Search for the sting site (usually on hands or feet) to confirm.
- Call 108 and rush to a hospital with a ventilator.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious patient found"] --> B["Check for breathing and pulse"]
  B --> C["Place in side-lying recovery position"]
  C --> D["Clear mouth of any saliva or vomit"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything by mouth"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital with ventilator support"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconsciousness | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Shallow breathing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Unconsciousness after a sting usually indicates severe heart or lung failure caused by the venom. Only an ICU can save the patient.

Query: A child was stung and has 'pins and needles' feeling in his whole body. He is also very itchy. Is this an allergy or venom?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of lips or eyes (Allergy)**
- Hives or red itchy rashes everywhere
- Wheezing sound while breathing
- Pins and needles sensation

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Refer for both potential venom and anaphylaxis (allergy). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Check for breathing sounds. If wheezing, he needs oxygen.
- Keep the child still.
- Do NOT give any anti-allergy pills at home unless directed by a doctor over the phone.
- Rush to the PHC/CHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pins and needles + Itching"] --> B["Check for lip swelling or rashes"]
  B --> C["Listen for wheezing sounds"]
  C --> D["Keep child calm and sitting up"]
  D --> E["Do NOT delay for home remedies"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for adrenaline/steroids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lip/Face swelling | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Body-wide itching | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Some people are allergic to scorpion venom. This can kill faster than the venom itself. Treat as an emergency.

Query: A man was stung on the neck. He says he cannot swallow his spit. What should I do before the ambulance arrives?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Inability to swallow**
- Changing voice (hoarseness)
- Excess saliva buildup
- Feeling of 'lump' in the throat

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **High Risk.** Neck stings are closer to the brain and airway. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the patient sitting upright. Do NOT let them lie flat.
- Tell the patient to spit out saliva, do not try to swallow it.
- Apply cold cloth to the neck gently (do not press hard).
- Prepare for immediate transport.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stung on neck + cannot swallow"] --> B["Keep patient sitting upright"]
  B --> C["Encourage spitting out of saliva"]
  C --> D["Apply light cold compress to neck"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give food or water"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC with emergency airway gear"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Neck sting | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stings on the head, neck, or chest are always more dangerous than stings on limbs. Immediate referral is mandatory.

Query: The villager says the scorpion was white/translucent. Does the color matter for treatment?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pale scorpions (Yellow/Red/Translucent) are often highly toxic in India.
- Sweating
- High heart rate
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Treat all scorpions as potentially dangerous. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT waste time identifying the scorpion or catching it.
- If it is already killed, you can bring it in a container (do not touch with hands).
- Focus on the **patient’s symptoms**, not the scorpion’s color.
- If it was a 'Red' or 'Yellow' scorpion, the risk of heart issues is very high.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Scorpion sting (any color)"] --> B["Focus on symptoms, not color identification"]
  B --> C["If red/yellow scorpion, expect high toxicity"]
  C --> D["Check for sweating or racing heart"]
  D --> E["Clean wound and apply cold cloth"]
  E --> F["Refer if any symptom beyond local pain appears"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red/Yellow scorpion sting | Refer for 4-hour observation | Not recommended |
| Systemic signs (any color) | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In India, the *Mesobuthus tamulus* (Red Scorpion) is the most lethal. Treat any pale/red scorpion sting as a high-risk emergency.

Query: A person is having severe chest pain after a sting. They are also coughing up pinkish spit. What is this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Chest pain**
- **Pink, frothy (bubbly) spit**
- Severe breathlessness
- Blue lips

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult/Elderly | Any | **Critical Emergency.** This is fluid in the lungs (Pulmonary Edema). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the patient sitting strictly upright with legs hanging down if possible.
- This helps the patient breathe better.
- Do NOT let them lie flat.
- Provide oxygen if available.
- This is a medical emergency that needs Prazosin and Diuretics immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chest pain + Pink frothy spit"] --> B["Sit patient upright with legs down"]
  B --> C["Provide oxygen support if available"]
  C --> D["Do NOT allow patient to lie down"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any liquids by mouth"]
  E --> F["Rush to a hospital with ICU and Ventilator"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pink frothy spit | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Chest pain | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pink spit means the heart is failing and lungs are filling with fluid. Without hospital treatment, this is fatal.

Query: Is it okay to give the person a little bit of alcohol to reduce the pain of a scorpion sting?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drowsiness
- Slow breathing
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Strictly NO.** Alcohol makes venom effects worse. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Never give alcohol.** It thins the blood and can speed up venom spread.
- It also masks the danger signs (like confusion or drowsiness).
- If they have already taken it, inform the doctor immediately.
- Give plain water only if they are fully awake and not vomiting.
- Use ice for pain relief instead.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about alcohol for pain"] --> B["Strongly forbid alcohol use"]
  B --> C["Check if patient is already drowsy"]
  C --> D["Wash sting with soap and use ice"]
  D --> E["Monitor for vomiting and breathing rate"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for safe pain management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsiness from alcohol/venom | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Severe pain | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcohol can cause the patient to vomit and choke while they are already struggling with venom. It is extremely dangerous.

Query: A child was stung and now his eyes are rolling up and he is making a clicking sound with his throat. What do I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Eyes rolling up**
- **Clicking/clucking sounds in throat**
- Arching of the back
- Loss of consciousness

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Critical Emergency.** These are signs of severe nerve toxicity. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT put anything in the child's mouth (no spoons, no water).
- Clear the area so the child doesn't hit their head if they shake.
- Lay them on their side (Recovery Position).
- Rush to the nearest hospital with pediatric ICU.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Eyes rolling + clicking sounds"] --> B["Lay child on their side immediately"]
  B --> C["Clear mouth of saliva/secretions"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put anything in the mouth"]
  D --> E["Record duration of these signs for doctor"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC/District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eyes rolling up | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Clicking sounds | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
These signs mean the venom is affecting the brain and nerves. Only a hospital can provide the necessary anti-convulsant and anti-venom treatment.

Query: A man says he feels a 'cold' feeling spreading up his leg after a sting. His foot is also turning blue. What is the action?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Foot turning blue/purple**
- Loss of pulse in the foot
- Coldness spreading upward
- Total numbness (no feeling)

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Emergency.** This could be a blocked blood vessel or a very tight bandage. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Check for any tight threads or bandages and **remove them immediately**.
- Do NOT apply heat to the leg.
- Keep the leg at the level of the heart (not above, not far below).
- Rush to the hospital; he may lose the limb if blood flow is stopped.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Foot turning blue and cold"] --> B["Remove any tight clothing or bandages"]
  B --> C["Check for pulse behind the ankle"]
  C --> D["Keep leg at heart level"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply heat or massage leg"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Surgery unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue/Cold limb | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| No pulse in limb | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If blood flow is blocked (due to venom or a tight tie), the limb can die (gangrene) within hours. This is a surgical emergency.

Query: The patient was stung 24 hours ago. The pain is gone but the site is now a large black blister. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blackening of skin (Necrosis)**
- Fever and chills
- Foul smelling fluid from the site
- Large area of redness and heat

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Refer for wound management and infection control. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT pop or burst the blister.
- Cover it with a clean bandage.
- Check if the person has a fever.
- Refer to the PHC for cleaning and potentially antibiotics.
- Ask about Tetanus vaccination history.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Black blister 24 hours later"] --> B["Do NOT pop the blister"]
  B --> C["Cover with a sterile/clean bandage"]
  C --> D["Check for fever or body aches"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for wound care/antibiotics"]
  E --> F["Advise on daily dressing at the clinic"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Black skin/Blister | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Fever/Chills | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Some scorpion stings cause the skin to die locally. If not cleaned by a doctor, it can lead to a severe blood infection (Sepsis).

Query: A group of children disturbed a beehive in the orchard. One boy has hundreds of stings on his face and chest. He is swelling up like a balloon and crying. What should I do quickly?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Difficulty breathing or wheezing**
- Swelling of the tongue or throat
- **Faintness, dizziness, or passing out**
- Nausea, vomiting, or stomach cramps
- Hundreds of stings (Toxic load emergency)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | **High Emergency.** Multiple stings in kids are life-threatening. Rush to CHC. |
| Adult (12-60) | Any | Remove stingers, apply cold pack, monitor breathing for 1 hour. |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Emergency referral if more than 50 stings or heart history. |
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral to monitor for allergic shock (Anaphylaxis). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Scrape out stingers** immediately using a credit card or blunt knife. Do NOT use tweezers (pinching releases more venom).
- Wash all sting areas with soap and water.
- Apply **cold mud or a wet cloth** to reduce swelling and pain.
- Keep the person sitting up if they are struggling to breathe.
- Remove any jewelry or tight clothes before the area swells up.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person attacked by bee/wasp swarm"] --> B["Check for breathing difficulty or fainting"]
  B --> C["Scrape out all visible stingers with a flat edge"]
  C --> D["Wash stings with soap and apply cold compress"]
  D --> E["Remove rings and tight clothing immediately"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC if more than 50 stings or breathing is hard"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling in throat/mouth | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| More than 50-100 stings | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only local pain/redness | Monitor for 2 hours | Treat at home with ice/mud |
| Vomiting or Diarrhea | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Multiple bee stings can cause kidney failure or severe allergic shock. Early referral saves lives. Do not wait for swelling to go down on its own.

Query: A woman was attacked by wasps while cutting grass. She has many stings and says her throat feels 'tight'. I don't have medicine. What local thing can I use?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Tightness in chest or throat**
- Hoarse voice (voice changing)
- **Itchy rash (hives) all over the body**
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Female | If throat feels tight, this is a 'Refer Now' emergency. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Scrape away stingers** quickly with a fingernail or flat stick.
- **Local Remedy:** Apply a paste of **baking soda and water** or **clean cold mud** to the stings to soothe the burn.
- If she is wheezing, help her sit in a 'tripod' position (leaning forward on hands).
- Give her nothing to drink if she is having trouble swallowing.
- Transport her to the nearest hospital with oxygen and adrenaline (Epinephrine).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Multiple wasp stings reported"] --> B["Check for voice changes or throat tightness"]
  B --> C["Scrape out stingers - do NOT squeeze them"]
  C --> D["Apply baking soda paste or cold mud locally"]
  D --> E["Keep patient upright and sitting forward"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to hospital for adrenaline"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Throat tightness | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Hives/Rashes on body | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Pain at sting site only | PHC for observation | Monitor for 1 hour |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Throat tightness is a sign of Anaphylaxis, which can close the airway in minutes. Home remedies are only for pain, not for breathing issues.

Query: Old man was attacked by a swarm. He is dizzy and his skin is turning pale and cold. How to help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pale, cold, clammy skin (Shock)**
- Confusion or extreme weakness
- **Very low blood pressure**
- Slow or gasping breath

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Male | High risk of heart failure. Keep legs raised and rush to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the person flat on their back and **raise their legs** 1 foot high (helps blood reach the brain).
- Scrape off any visible stingers quickly.
- If they are conscious and can swallow, do NOT give tea or coffee; only sips of water.
- Cover with a blanket if they feel cold.
- Rush to a facility that can give IV fluids and emergency injections.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly man stung - pale and cold"] --> B["Lay flat and raise legs 12 inches"]
  B --> C["Scrape out stingers with a flat card"]
  C --> D["Keep the patient warm with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Check for pulse and breathing every 5 mins"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transport in leg-raised position"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness/Fainting | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Cold, clammy skin | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Local swelling only | Refer to PHC | Monitor at home after |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shock from bee venom is a medical emergency. Raising the legs is a temporary measure; they need immediate hospital care.

Query: A toddler got stung by 10 bees. He is vomiting and his stomach is hard. Is this normal?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Repeated vomiting**
- Stomach pain or diarrhea
- **Extreme sleepiness (lethargy)**
- Swelling around the eyes or lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | **Critical.** 10 stings on a small child is a massive dose of venom. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Remove stingers by scraping. Do not waste more than 30 seconds.
- Wash the skin with cold water.
- If the child is vomiting, turn them on their side (Recovery Position) so they don't choke.
- **Do NOT give 'Janum Ghunti' or any herbal syrups.**
- Carry the child to the hospital immediately. Watch for 'quiet' breathing (a sign of stopping).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Toddler with 10+ stings"] --> B["Scrape out stingers immediately"]
  B --> C["Check if child is vomiting or very sleepy"]
  C --> D["Place child on their side if vomiting"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything by mouth"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for pediatric emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting/Stomach pain | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Child is very sleepy | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Redness only | Refer to PHC | Not safe for toddlers |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bee venom is a toxin. In small children, it can damage the kidneys or cause the heart to stop. 10 stings is a serious emergency for a toddler.

Query: A villager says to apply kerosene or petrol on bee stings to stop the pain. Should ASHA allow this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain spreading quickly to the whole limb
- Numbness in the fingers or toes
- Heavy sweating

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | **Strict NO.** Kerosene is harmful. Use cold mud or baking soda. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT apply kerosene, petrol, or engine oil.** These can burn the skin and cause poisoning.
- Wash the sting site thoroughly with soap and water to remove any applied chemicals.
- Use **cold mud** or ice wrapped in cloth for pain. This is safer.
- If the person has more than 20 stings, go to the PHC for observation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about kerosene for stings"] --> B["Strongly forbid use of kerosene/petrol"]
  B --> C["Wash site with soap to remove chemicals"]
  C --> D["Apply cold mud or baking soda paste"]
  D --> E["Count the total number of stings"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if stings are more than 20"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin burns from kerosene | Refer to PHC | Never |
| 20+ stings (adult) | Refer to PHC | Monitor closely |
| Less than 20 stings, no allergy | Monitor for 1 hour | Treat at home with mud |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
ASHA must correct harmful local practices. Kerosene application does not help with venom and adds the risk of chemical burns.

Query: Should I use tweezers or a needle to take out bee stingers? The villager is offering a rusty needle.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Local swelling spreading rapidly
- Red streaks coming from sting site

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Focus on scraping. Do NOT squeeze or use rusty needles. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use tweezers or a needle.** Squeezing pushes more venom into the body.
- **Do NOT use a rusty needle.** This will cause Tetanus or infection.
- **Scrape** the stingers out quickly using the **edge of a credit card, a blunt knife, or a fingernail**.
- If you cannot remove them, go to the PHC for professional removal.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stingers still in the skin"] --> B["Do NOT use tweezers or rusty needles"]
  B --> C["Scrape with a card or fingernail"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress locally for pain"]
  D --> E["Monitor for signs of infection for 24h"]
  E --> F["If redness spreads, visit PHC"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Many stingers unremoved | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Only 1-2 unremoved stingers | Monitor for 4 hours | If pain increases, refer |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The speed of removal is important, but how you remove it matters. Scraping is the only correct way.

Query: A girl was stung near her eye by a bee. Her eye is swelling shut. Is there a local remedy to put in the eye?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling moving to the face or neck**
- Difficulty opening the jaw
- Wheezing sound while breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Teen | Female | **Emergency.** Eye and face stings risk closing the airway. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT put anything INSIDE the eye.** No mud, no milk, no local juices.
- Very carefully scrape the stinger away if it is on the eyelid. Do not press on the eyeball.
- Apply a **cold, wet cloth** over the closed eyelid. Do not press hard.
- Keep the girl sitting up to reduce facial swelling.
- Rush to the hospital where they can give steroids or anti-allergy medication.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Girl stung near eye"] --> B["Check breathing and check voice"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put any local remedy IN the eye"]
  C --> D["Apply cold wet cloth gently over eyelid"]
  D --> E["Keep patient in sitting upright position"]
  E --> F["Transfer immediately to CHC/District hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sting on eye/eyelid | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Facial swelling | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only redness on cheek | PHC for eye check | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stings on the face and neck are dangerous because the swelling can block the windpipe or damage the eye permanently. Local 'totkas' must be avoided.

Query: A family was attacked. One boy got many stings and is now having a seizure (shaking). What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Seizures (shaking, fitting)**
- Unconsciousness
- Foaming at the mouth
- Irregular breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Teen | Any | **Critical Emergency.** Seizure means the venom is affecting the brain. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the person on their side (**Recovery Position**).
- Do NOT put anything in the mouth (no spoon, no water).
- Clear the area of objects so they do not hit their head.
- Scrape off any obvious stingers ONLY when they stop shaking.
- Rush to the hospital with pediatric emergency care.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person having seizure after stings"] --> B["Place patient on their side immediately"]
  B --> C["Clear nearby objects to prevent injury"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put anything in the mouth"]
  D --> E["Record duration of seizure if possible"]
  E --> F["Transfer immediately to CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizure/Fitting | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Unconsciousness | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A seizure after a bee swarm attack can be from an extreme allergic reaction or direct toxicity to the brain. Hospital management is critical.

Query: A villager says that a wasp stinger can be removed with a paste made from matchstick heads. Is this good?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Redness at sting site turning blue/black
- Severe pain that doesn't reduce

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | **Strict NO.** Matchstick heads are poisonous. Use cold water. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT apply matchstick paste.** Phosphorus is a chemical and can burn the skin and cause poisoning.
- **Wash** the area thoroughly with soap and water to remove any applied paste.
- Scrape out any stinger if visible with a blunt edge (card).
- Use **cold mud** or baking soda paste for safe pain relief.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about matchstick paste for stings"] --> B["Strongly forbid use of matchstick paste"]
  B --> C["Wash area with soap to remove chemicals"]
  C --> D["Scrape out stinger with card/fingernail"]
  D --> E["Apply cold compress or cold mud"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if skin redness spreads"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Matchstick paste used | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Local pain and redness | PHC for observation | Monitor for 1 hour |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Matchstick heads contain phosphorus, which can cause severe skin burns and lead to chemical absorption into the bloodstream. It must not be used.

Query: A pregnant woman got stung by multiple wasps on her legs. She is worried about her baby. Are local medicines okay?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Abdominal pain or contractions**
- Sudden high blood pressure
- Blurry vision
- Breathlessness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | **Emergency.** Transfer to CHC with OBGYN support immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Calm the mother down; stress harms the fetus.
- Remove any bangles or rings from her hand immediately in case of generalized swelling.
- Scrape out stings quickly. Wash with cold soap and water.
- Do NOT give any local medicine, 'kadha', or anti-allergy pills at home.
- Transport her in a sitting or side-lying position.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman stung by wasps"] --> B["Remove jewelry immediately in case of swelling"]
  B --> C["Scrape out visible stingers and wash with soap"]
  C --> D["Calm the patient and check fetal movement"]
  D --> E["Arrange transport to CHC/District hospital"]
  E --> F["Monitor for contractions or abdominal pain"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple stings in pregnancy | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Any swelling above knees/arms | Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bee/wasp venom in pregnancy can cause systemic inflammation and potentially premature labor. Fetal monitoring in a hospital is required.

Query: An elderly man was stung by a swarm in the field. He is coughing and wheezing. Should ASHA treat at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wheezing (whistling sound) when breathing**
- Cough that won't stop
- **Tightness in chest**
- Sudden confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | **High Emergency.** Multiple stings are dangerous for heart/lungs. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep him strictly in a resting position. Do NOT let him walk.
- Sit him upright, leaning forward with arms on knees to help breathing.
- Scrape off stingers. Do NOT waste time identifying the bee/wasp.
- Refer to a hospital with oxygen facility.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly man wheezing after stings"] --> B["Force strict rest and sit patient upright"]
  B --> C["Check voice for changes"]
  C --> D["Scrape out any visible stingers"]
  D --> E["Call 108 or transfer immediately"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC/Hospital with oxygen"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wheezing/Chest pain | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Few stings, no breathing issues | PHC for observation | Not recommended for elderly |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Meteorological changes (asthma, heart issues) are common in elderly after a venom load. Breathing issues are a priority over sting counts.

Query: A toddler got stung by 20 bees. He is vomiting and has bad stomach pain. He is very sleepy.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Repeated vomiting**
- **Severe stomach cramps/pain**
- **Extreme sleepiness (lethargy)**
- Child becoming very quiet (stops crying)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | **Critical.** This is systemic venom toxicity. Emergency referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Scrape off stingers immediately. Do not squeeze.
- Wash stings with cold soap and water.
- If vomiting, turn on side (Recovery Position).
- Do NOT give ORS or water if they are actively vomiting.
- Carry the child to the hospital immediately. Watch for shallow breathing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Toddler with 20 stings"] --> B["Check breathing and check sleepiness"]
  B --> C["Scrape out stingers immediately"]
  C --> D["Place child on their side if vomiting"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything to drink"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for pediatric emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting/Stomach pain | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Extreme sleepiness | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| 1-2 stings, no vomiting | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In small children, 20 stings is a huge toxic load. It can cause heart, lung, and kidney damage. Immediate hospital care is needed.

Query: A man got attacked by a swarm and jumped into the village pond to escape. He has many stings. What is the priority?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Shivering heavily from the cold water**
- **Mud or water in lungs (coughing)**
- Multiple sting signs (breathing difficulty, dizziness)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Priority is preventing hypothermia (cold) and managing stings. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority:** Get him out of wet clothes and cover him with dry blankets. Hypothermia makes shock worse.
- Scrape out stings. The water might have already washed some off.
- Check his breathing and for signs of water inhalation (coughing water).
- Apply cold mud to remaining stings for pain relief (ensure mud is clean).
- If he has many stings, go to the PHC for observation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient stung and was in pond"] --> B["Remove wet clothes and wrap in dry blankets"]
  B --> C["Check breathing for water inhalation signs"]
  C --> D["Scrape out remaining stingers from skin"]
  D --> E["Apply clean cold mud for pain relief"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if stings are more than 30"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Persistent coughing water | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Multiple stings (>30) | Refer to PHC for observation | Monitor closely |
| 1-2 stings, no other issues | Monitor at home with blankets | Apply cold compress |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
ASHA must treat the whole person, not just the stings. The shock of the attack and the cold water can lead to heart stress. Referral is needed for multiple stings.

Query: A villager says that a bee swarm attack is a curse. They are waiting for the priest. Should ASHA wait too?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain spreading quickly to the whole limb
- Numbness in the fingers or toes
- Heavy sweating

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | **Strict NO.** A bee attack is a medical event. Speed is important. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority:** Advise the family that this is a medical issue that needs fast treatment. Delay can be fatal.
- Scrape out any visible stingers. Wash with cold soap and water.
- If they insist on the priest, they can do the rituals, but the medical treatment (scraping, going to PHC) must not wait.
- Use **cold mud** or baking soda paste for safe pain relief. This is more helpful than rituals for venom.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["ASHA finds family waiting for priest"] --> B["Advise family to seek immediate medical care"]
  B --> C["Scrape out stingers while they wait"]
  C --> D["Apply cold mud or baking soda paste locally"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for professional observation"]
  E --> F["Do NOT delay medical care for rituals"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple stings, family delays | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Few stings, family delays | PHC for observation | Not safe at home |
| Family cooperative, few stings | Monitor for 1 hour | Treat at home with mud |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
ASHA must correct harmful local beliefs when they put lives at risk. Be gentle but firm that medical action is the only thing that will stop the venom from harming the patient.

Query: Can I use 'haldi' (turmeric) paste for bee stings? Local elder says it is a good remedy.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain moving up the limb towards the body
- Restlessness
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Focus on scraping first. Use haldi only if cold mud is unavailable. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority:** Scrape out the stingers first. This is most important.
- **Haldi (Turmeric) is okay** for the *pain* and local swelling *after* stingers are removed. It is a good local remedy in rural areas.
- Make a paste of haldi with clean cold water.
- Do NOT use hot water. Hot water makes swelling worse.
- If the person has many stings, go to the PHC for observation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about haldi for stings"] --> B["Focus on scraping stingers first"]
  B --> C["Wash site with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Apply cold haldi paste locally for pain"]
  D --> E["Monitor for 1 hour for allergy symptoms"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if symptoms worsen or stings >20"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain unmanaged with haldi | Refer to PHC for pain | Never |
| Any systemic signs | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only local pain/swelling | Monitor for 1 hour | Treat at home with haldi |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Haldi is a local remedy that can help with local pain. However, it does not stop venom from spreading through the body and cannot treat systemic toxicity.

Query: The patient was stung 24 hours ago. The pain is better, but now they have large red, itchy hives all over their body. Should they go to PHC?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Red streaks coming from sting site
- Fever and chills
- Large red itchy hives spreading

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Refer.** This is a late allergic reaction (hives) or infection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Cover stings with a clean bandage.
- Check if the person has a fever.
- Make them comfortable in a dark quiet room.
- Do NOT apply any more local pastes, only use ice for pain.
- Refer to PHC for potential anti-allergy medicines (antihistamines) or antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient has late allergic hives 24h later"] --> B["Check for fever or signs of infection locally"]
  B --> C["Make patient comfortable in quiet room"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply more local pastes"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for antihistamines or antibiotics"]
  E --> F["Observe for other late reactions like fever"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Large spreading hives 24h | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Fever/Chills | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Only local pain/swelling | PHC for observation | Monitor at home after |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Late allergic reactions can happen up to a few days after multiple stings. Sepsis can also occur. Professional assessment is necessary.

Query: Is it okay to give the person a little bit of alcohol to reduce the pain of bee stings?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drowsiness
- Slow breathing
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Strictly NO.** Alcohol makes venom effects worse. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Never give alcohol.** It thins the blood and can speed up venom spread.
- It also masks the danger signs (like confusion or drowsiness).
- If they have already taken it, inform the doctor immediately.
- Give plain water only if they are fully awake and not vomiting.
- Use ice or cold mud for pain relief instead.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about alcohol for pain"] --> B["Strongly forbid alcohol use"]
  B --> C["Check if patient is already drowsy"]
  C --> D["Wash sting with soap and use ice/mud"]
  D --> E["Monitor for vomiting and breathing rate"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for safe pain management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsiness from alcohol/venom | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Severe pain | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Alcohol taken, few stings | Monitor closely for 1 hour | Apply cold mud |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcohol can cause the patient to vomit and choke while they are already struggling with venom. It is extremely dangerous and must not be used.

Query: A child was stung by wasps in the field. He has a few stings and is very scared and crying. Can ASHA treat at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain moving up the limb towards the body
- Restlessness
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Focus on calming first. Use cold mud or baking soda. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Calm the child first.** Reassure him he will be okay. Stress makes heart rate go up.
- Use **ICE or cold water** wrapped in cloth for pain. If unavailable, use **cold mud**.
- Scrape off any obvious stingers ONLY when they stop crying.
- If the pain doesn't reduce in 30 minutes, go to the PHC.
- Give paracetamol only if the child can swallow easily and you have it.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with few stings, scared"] --> B["Calm the child first and reassure family"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress locally for pain"]
  C --> D["Scrape out stingers after child is calmer"]
  D --> E["Monitor for 1 hour for new symptoms"]
  E --> F["If symptoms increase, GO TO PHC"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe pain unmanaged | Refer to PHC for pain | Never |
| Any systemic signs | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only local pain/redness | Monitor for 1 hour | Treat at home with mud/ice |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Most bee/wasp stings are painful but not life-threatening. ASHA must use local knowledge of cold mud or ice while monitoring carefully. Referral is for systemic signs or severe unmanaged pain.

Query: Should I apply the juice of raw onion on bee stings to stop the swelling? My village elder says it is a good remedy.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain moving up the limb towards the body
- Restlessness
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Focus on scraping first. Use raw onion juice only if cold mud is unavailable. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority:** Scrape out the stingers first. This is most important.
- **Raw onion juice is okay** for the *pain* and local swelling *after* stingers are removed. It is a good local remedy in rural areas.
- Apply the juice of a raw onion locally to the stings for pain relief.
- Do NOT apply the juice to the whole limb, only to stings.
- If the person has many stings, go to the PHC for observation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about raw onion juice for stings"] --> B["Focus on scraping stingers first"]
  B --> C["Wash site with soap and water"]
  C --> D["Apply fresh raw onion juice locally to stings"]
  D --> E["Monitor for 1 hour for allergy symptoms"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if symptoms worsen or stings >20"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain unmanaged with onion | Refer to PHC for pain | Never |
| Any systemic signs | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only local pain/swelling | Monitor for 1 hour | Treat at home with onion juice |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Raw onion juice is a local remedy that can help with local pain. However, it does not stop venom from spreading through the body and cannot treat systemic toxicity.

Query: A person has few bee stings and is having trouble speaking (slurred speech). Is this venom or fear?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Slurred or difficult speech**
- Changing voice (hoarseness)
- Swelling around neck or face
- Difficulty swallowing saliva

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **High Emergency.** Slurred speech is a neurological sign from venom. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT assume it is fear.** Slurred speech with any history of stings is dangerous.
- **Keep the patient sitting upright.** Do NOT let them lie flat, as it can worsen airway risk.
- Encourage them to spit out saliva, do not try to swallow it.
- Scrape out stinger if visible in neck/face area. Do not waste more than 30 seconds.
- Transport immediately to hospital with emergency airway and neurological support.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Slurred speech after few stings"] --> B["Keep patient sitting upright"]
  B --> C["Check voice and throat swelling"]
  C --> D["Encourage spitting out of saliva"]
  D --> E["Scrape out stinger in neck/face carefully"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to District hospital"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Changing voice/Swelling | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Only local pain/redness | PHC for observation | Monitor carefully |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Neurological signs like slurred speech indicate severe systemic venom toxicity affecting the nerves or brain. Immediate hospital management is critical.

Query: A street dog bit a small girl on her leg while she was walking to school. The wound is bleeding. The villagers want to put red chili powder and lime on it. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Deep puncture wounds or heavy bleeding
- Bite on the face, neck, or hands
- **Dog acting strange, foaming at the mouth, or biting without provocation**
- Dog is missing or dead after the bite
- Victim has difficulty swallowing or fear of water

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | **Immediate Priority.** Wash wound for 15 mins and rush to PHC for vaccine. |
| Adult (12-60) | Any | Wash wound thoroughly, check dog status, and visit PHC. |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk for infection; wash and refer for vaccine immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound immediately** with running water and soap for at least **15 minutes**. This is the best way to kill the virus.
- **Do NOT** apply chili powder, lime, soil, or any herbal pastes. These cause severe pain and infection.
- Leave the wound open. **Do NOT** tie a bandage or get stitches unless a doctor says so.
- Apply a simple antiseptic like povidone-iodine if available after washing.
- Identify the dog and note if it was a stray or a pet.
- Rush to the nearest PHC/CHC for the **Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV)**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dog bite incident reported"] --> B["Wash wound with running water and soap for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply chili, lime, or local pastes"]
  C --> D["Leave the wound open and do NOT bandage tightly"]
  D --> E["Identify if the dog is stray or shows strange behavior"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC immediately for Rabies Vaccine"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any skin break by dog | Refer to PHC for vaccine | Never safe at home |
| Bite on face or neck | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Bleeding that won't stop | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Scratch with no blood | PHC for vaccine advice | Wash for 15 minutes |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rabies is 100% fatal once symptoms start but 100% preventable with a vaccine. Never ignore a bite, even a small scratch.

Query: A farmer was bitten by his own pet dog. The dog is vaccinated but the bite is deep. Should I still send him for a shot?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Dog showing sudden change in behavior
- Wound looks deep or involves muscle
- Fever or headache starting in the patient

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Male | Wash for 15 mins. Refer to PHC even if pet is vaccinated. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the bite** with soap and running water for 15 full minutes.
- **Do NOT** apply any home remedies like oil or turmeric.
- Check the dog's vaccination card but **refer the patient anyway**. Doctors must decide if a booster is needed.
- Keep the dog under observation for 10 days to see if it gets sick or dies.
- Visit PHC for a Tetanus shot and Rabies assessment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pet dog bite reported"] --> B["Wash wound with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Verify dog's vaccination status and keep dog tied for 10 days"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any local pastes or bandages"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Tetanus and Rabies vaccine assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deep puncture wound | Refer to PHC/CHC | Never |
| Dog is vaccinated | Refer to PHC for record | Wash for 15 minutes |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even pet dog bites require medical evaluation. Rabies risk is lower with vaccinated pets, but other infections like Tetanus are still possible.

Query: A child was bitten on the cheek by a stray dog. It is a small bite but on the face. What is the emergency step?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bite on face, head, or neck (High Risk)**
- Shallow breathing
- Excessive drooling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | **Critical Emergency.** Face bites reach the brain faster. Rush to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash immediately** with soap and water for 15 minutes. Be gentle but thorough.
- **Do NOT** put any 'gharelu nuskhe' (home remedies) on the face.
- Do NOT cover the wound.
- Go directly to a **CHC or District Hospital** that has Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) in addition to the vaccine.
- This is a Category III bite and needs special medicine around the wound.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on face or head"] --> B["Wash gently with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply any local pastes or cover the wound"]
  C --> D["Keep child calm and do NOT give oral medicine"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC/District Hospital for Vaccine + RIG serum"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bite on face/head | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Any stray dog bite | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites on the face are dangerous because the distance to the brain is short. These wounds often require Immunoglobulin serum, not just the vaccine.

Query: A man was bitten 3 days ago and didn't tell anyone. Now the wound is red and he has a fever. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever and headache**
- Pain or tingling at the old bite site
- **Fear of wind (Aerophobia) or water (Hydrophobia)**
- Aggressive behavior or confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | **Strict Emergency.** Could be early Rabies or infection. Refer NOW. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Refer to the hospital immediately.** Delay has already happened.
- Wash the wound again if it looks dirty, but do not scrub hard.
- Keep the patient in a quiet, dark room while waiting for transport.
- Do NOT give water if he is struggling to drink.
- Tell the doctor exactly how many days ago the bite happened.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Old bite with fever/redness"] --> B["Keep patient in a quiet and dark area"]
  B --> C["Check if patient can drink water easily"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply local herbs to the old wound"]
  D --> E["Inform hospital about the 3-day delay"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC/District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever after bite | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Tingling at bite site | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Hydrophobia | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If symptoms of Rabies start, there is no cure. However, even a delayed vaccine can sometimes help if symptoms haven't started. Rush to medical care.

Query: Should I use a tight bandage to stop the bleeding from a dog bite? The villager is using a cloth tied very hard.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Limb turning blue or cold below the bandage
- Loss of sensation

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Do NOT tie tightly.** Loosen the cloth immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Loosen any tight ties.** Dog bite wounds must breathe to prevent the virus from nesting.
- If bleeding is heavy, apply **direct pressure** with a clean cloth, but do not tie it like a tourniquet.
- **Wash for 15 minutes** with soap while the water is running.
- **Do NOT** use coffee powder or spider webs to stop bleeding.
- Refer to PHC for wound care and vaccine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bleeding dog bite with tight tie"] --> B["Loosen the tight bandage or tourniquet"]
  B --> C["Apply direct pressure with clean cloth for bleeding"]
  C --> D["Wash wound with running water and soap for 15 minutes"]
  D --> E["Leave wound open and refer to PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Heavy bleeding | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Tight tie used | Refer to PHC | Loosen immediately |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Tying a dog bite wound tightly traps the virus and other bacteria (like gangrene) inside. Always keep the wound open after washing.

Query: A monkey bit a woman on the arm. Is the treatment same as a dog bite? She wants to apply neem paste.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Deep scratches or bites
- Monkey acting aggressive

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Same as dog bite. Wash for 15 mins and refer for ARV. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash with soap and water for 15 minutes.** Monkey bites also carry Rabies.
- **Do NOT** apply neem paste or any herbal juice. This hides the wound from the doctor.
- Monkeys can also carry other dangerous viruses; referral is mandatory.
- Go to the PHC for the same Rabies vaccine schedule as a dog bite.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Monkey bite or scratch"] --> B["Wash area with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply neem or local herbal pastes"]
  C --> D["Leave wound open and avoid bandages"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Anti-Rabies Vaccine schedule"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any monkey bite | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In India, monkeys, cats, and even bats can spread Rabies. The first aid and vaccine protocol remain the same as for a dog bite.

Query: A child was licked by a street dog on a fresh cut on his hand. No bite. Does he need a vaccine?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Lick on fresh wound or mucous membrane (eyes/mouth)
- Dog is sick or salivating excessively

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash and refer. Licks on open wounds are Category II risk. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the cut** with soap and water for 15 minutes immediately.
- Do NOT panic, but do not ignore it.
- **Refer to PHC.** A lick on a broken skin area is treated the same as a bite.
- The child will likely need the full vaccine course.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Lick on open wound by stray dog"] --> B["Wash the area with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply any chemicals or home remedies"]
  C --> D["Identify the dog if possible"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Rabies vaccine schedule"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lick on open wound | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Lick on intact skin | Wash with soap | Safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rabies virus is in the saliva. If that saliva touches a fresh cut, the risk is real. Always wash and seek a vaccine.

Query: The PHC is closed. The boy was bitten by a mad dog. Can I wait until tomorrow morning?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Mad dog (biting everyone, foaming)
- Multiple bites
- Bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Do NOT wait.** Find the nearest 24-hour facility or CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start washing NOW.** Don't wait for the hospital. Wash for 15-20 minutes with soap.
- If the PHC is closed, go to the District Hospital or a private clinic that has the vaccine.
- Every hour of delay increases the risk.
- Ensure the boy does not get any local 'jhad-phook' (traditional healing) which wastes time.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite by mad dog + local PHC closed"] --> B["Wash wound for 20 minutes with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Search for nearest open CHC or District Hospital"]
  C --> D["Do NOT wait for morning; transport immediately"]
  D --> E["Carry any ID/health card of the patient"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Mad dog bite | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rabies is an emergency. If the closest center is closed, travel further. The first dose must be given as soon as possible.

Query: A person was bitten and we applied 'Gobar' (cow dung) as a ritual. Now the wound is green. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pus or foul smell from wound**
- Swelling and heat around the bite
- Fever starting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **High Infection Risk.** Wash immediately and refer. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the cow dung off immediately** with lots of soap and running water. Scrub gently but ensure all dung is gone.
- Cow dung carries Tetanus and other bacteria that cause rotting (Gangrene).
- Do NOT apply anything else.
- **Refer to CHC** for wound cleaning, Tetanus toxoid, and Rabies vaccine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wound covered in cow dung"] --> B["Thoroughly wash with soap and water until clean"]
  B --> C["Check for signs of infection (pus/heat)"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any more local substances"]
  D --> E["Refer to CHC for Tetanus and Antibiotics"]
  E --> F["Start Rabies vaccine course at PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Gobar applied | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Fever/Pus | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Applying cow dung to a bite is extremely dangerous and can lead to amputation due to severe infection. Always prioritize soap and water.

Query: A cat scratched a lady's hand. It is bleeding slightly. She says it's just a cat, no need for a doctor. What should I say?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Cat is a stray or acting wild
- Redness spreading from the scratch

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash for 15 mins and refer. Cats also carry Rabies. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the scratch** with soap and water for 15 minutes.
- Explain to her that **cats carry Rabies** in their saliva and claws (if they lick them).
- Refer her to the PHC for the Rabies vaccine.
- If it's a pet cat, observe the cat for 10 days.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cat scratch with blood"] --> B["Wash area with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Explain Rabies risk from cats to the patient"]
  C --> D["Leave the scratch open"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Rabies vaccine schedule"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cat scratch with blood | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Pet cat, no blood | Wash with soap | Monitor cat for 10 days |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cat bites and scratches are often deeper than they look and carry a high risk of Rabies and bacterial infections. Do not ignore them.

Query: Can I give the patient milk or water after a dog bite? The village says to keep them hungry for 24 hours.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Patient cannot swallow
- Patient is afraid of water

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **No fasting needed.** Give food/water normally. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT keep the patient hungry.** Fasting does not help with Rabies and makes the patient weak.
- Give water and food normally *unless* they are having trouble swallowing (which is a late danger sign).
- Focus on **washing the wound** for 15 minutes.
- Refer to PHC for the vaccine immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Inquiry about food/fasting after bite"] --> B["Advise against fasting; give food/water normally"]
  B --> C["Wash the bite wound with soap for 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Check for any difficulty in swallowing"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Rabies vaccine immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |
| Healthy appetite | Refer to PHC for vaccine | Normal meals |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fasting is a myth and delays proper medical care. The only thing that matters is washing the wound and getting vaccinated.

Query: A dog bite wound is very deep and the bone is showing. Can I put turmeric and salt to stop infection?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Visible bone or tendon**
- Spurting blood
- Numbness in the limb

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Critical Emergency.** Do NOT put anything in the wound. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash gently** with running water for 15 minutes. Do not poke the bone.
- **Do NOT put turmeric, salt, or any powder.** This will damage the tissues and cause extreme pain.
- Cover with a clean, damp cloth *very loosely*.
- **Rush to the District Hospital.** This needs a surgeon and Immunoglobulin (RIG) immediately.
- This wound may need special stitches that only a doctor should do.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Deep bite with bone visible"] --> B["Wash gently with running water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply salt, turmeric, or any powder"]
  C --> D["Cover loosely with a clean damp cloth"]
  D --> E["Rush to District Hospital/Medical College"]
  E --> F["Inform doctor that it is a Category III bite"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bone/Deep tissue visible | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Deep bites carry the highest risk of Rabies and severe bacterial infection. They require immediate hospital care and surgical evaluation.

Query: A lady was bitten while she was sleeping by a bat. There are tiny puncture marks. Should I worry?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Tiny needle-like bite marks
- Bat found in the bedroom

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | **High Risk.** Bat bites are often ignored but carry Rabies. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area** with soap and water for 15 minutes immediately.
- Treat this exactly like a dog bite.
- **Refer to PHC** for the full Rabies vaccine course.
- Do not try to catch the bat.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bat bite or scratch"] --> B["Wash the area with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply any local pastes or bandages"]
  C --> D["Explain the Rabies risk of bats to the patient"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for Anti-Rabies Vaccine"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any bat bite/contact | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bat bites can be so small they are barely visible, but they are very high risk for Rabies. Always vaccinate.

Query: A dog bit a man's hand and he killed the dog in anger. What should we do with the dog and the man?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Dog was aggressive before being killed
- Multiple bites on the man's hands

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash man's wounds for 15 mins. Refer for vaccine. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the man's wounds** for 15-20 minutes with soap and water.
- **Do NOT** touch the dead dog's mouth or brain with bare hands.
- Burial: Wear gloves, put the dog in a deep pit away from water sources, and cover with lime and soil.
- **Refer the man to PHC immediately.** Since the dog is dead, he must take the full vaccine course.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dog bite + Dog killed"] --> B["Wash man's wound with soap and water for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT touch dead dog's head/saliva with bare hands"]
  C --> D["Bury dog in a deep pit with lime"]
  D --> E["Refer man to PHC for full Rabies vaccine course"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bite by a dead/killed dog | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If a dog is killed after a bite, we cannot observe it for 10 days. Therefore, the bitten person must receive the full Rabies vaccine course without exception.

Query: A child was bitten through her thick jeans. The skin is not broken but there is a red mark. What is the rule?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Any** tiny break in skin found later
- Bruising or swelling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash and examine closely. If no break, no vaccine, but watch. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area** with soap and water.
- **Examine the skin very carefully** under a bright light for any tiny scratches or blood.
- If the skin is **completely intact** (no break), a vaccine is usually not needed (Category I).
- If there is even a **tiny scratch**, refer to PHC for a vaccine.
- When in doubt, always refer to the PHC for a professional check.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite through clothing"] --> B["Wash the area with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Inspect skin under bright light for tiny breaks"]
  C --> D["If skin is broken, refer to PHC for vaccine"]
  D --> E["If skin is NOT broken, monitor for 24 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Tiny scratch found | Refer to PHC | Never |
| No skin break at all | No vaccine needed | Wash with soap |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Saliva can seep through clothing. If there is any doubt about a skin break, it is safer to get the vaccine.

Query: A man says he already had 2 shots of Rabies vaccine last year. He was bit again today. Does he need more?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Deep bite
- Dog acting mad

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash for 15 mins. Refer to PHC for 're-exposure' doses. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound** for 15 minutes with soap.
- Tell the man that **new bites need new shots**, but the schedule is shorter (usually only 2 doses).
- **Refer to PHC.** The doctor will check his old cards and give the re-exposure doses.
- Never assume old vaccines will protect against a new bite.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["New bite in previously vaccinated person"] --> B["Wash wound for 15 minutes with soap"]
  B --> C["Find previous vaccination card/records"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply local remedies"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for re-exposure vaccine doses"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| New bite (any) | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Protection from Rabies vaccines fades over time. A new bite always requires a medical consultation and usually 2 booster doses.

Query: A dog bit a boy on his hand. The boy is now very scared of loud noises and bright lights. Is this a sign?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fear of light (Photophobia) or loud noise**
- **Fear of water (Hydrophobia)**
- Spasms in the throat
- Extreme restlessness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Critical Emergency.** These are late signs of Rabies. Isolation needed. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Refer to the District Hospital immediately.**
- Keep the child in a **quiet, dark room** to prevent seizures.
- Do NOT force him to drink water or eat.
- **Do NOT touch his saliva.** Wear a mask and gloves if possible.
- Alert the hospital that you are bringing a suspected Rabies case so they can prepare isolation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite history + Fear of light/noise"] --> B["Isolate patient in a dark, quiet room"]
  B --> C["Do NOT force water or food on the patient"]
  C --> D["Protect yourself from patient's saliva"]
  D --> E["Emergency transport to District Hospital/Isolation ward"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fear of light/water | Emergency - Refer NOW | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Once these symptoms appear, the disease is almost always fatal. The priority is comfort for the patient and safety for the caregivers. Always vaccinate immediately after a bite to prevent this stage.

Query: A stray dog bit a man's leg and ran away. We cannot find the dog. Does he need the full 5 shots or just 1?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Dog cannot be found (Untraceable stray)
- Bite is unprovoked

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Wash for 15 mins. Refer for **full** vaccine course. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound** for 15 minutes with soap and water.
- Tell the man that because the dog cannot be observed, we must assume it was rabid.
- **Refer to PHC** for the full 4 or 5-dose vaccine schedule.
- He must not skip any doses even if the wound heals.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite by untraceable stray dog"] --> B["Wash wound for 15 minutes with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Explain why full vaccine course is needed"]
  C --> D["Give patient a schedule card or reminder"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for first dose of ARV immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Untraceable dog bite | Refer to PHC for full course | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If a dog cannot be monitored for 10 days, the law is to finish the entire vaccine course. Skipping shots is a life-threatening mistake.

Query: A girl was bitten and the ASHA gave her the first shot. She doesn't want to go for the second shot because 'it's just a small mark'. How to convince her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Thinking a small wound is 'safe'
- Missing vaccine dates

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teen | Female | Explain that Rabies doesn't care about wound size. Refer for 2nd dose. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Tell her the truth:** Rabies virus can hide in the body for months and then kill suddenly.
- Explain that the **first shot is not enough** to build long-term protection.
- Show her the schedule card and take her to the PHC today.
- If she is late by a day, she still needs to take it immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient refusing follow-up vaccine dose"] --> B["Explain that Rabies is 100% fatal without full course"]
  B --> C["Check vaccine card for the correct date"]
  C --> D["Take patient to PHC immediately for the missed dose"]
  D --> E["Re-schedule remaining doses and ensure compliance"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Missing a dose | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The Rabies vaccine works as a series. Missing even one dose can leave the person at risk of death. Complete the course.

Query: A dog bit a woman who is 7 months pregnant. The family says the vaccine will harm the baby. Is this true?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Family refusing vaccine for pregnant woman
- Bite on a pregnant woman

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | **Safe to vaccinate.** Rabies vaccine does not harm the baby. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound** for 15 minutes with soap.
- **Correct the misinformation:** The Rabies vaccine is safe during all stages of pregnancy. Rabies death is a much bigger risk.
- **Refer to PHC/CHC** for the vaccine immediately.
- If she dies of Rabies, the baby will also not survive. The vaccine saves both.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bite on pregnant woman"] --> B["Wash wound for 15 minutes with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Explain to family that the vaccine is safe for the baby"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply local herbs or chemicals"]
```
