- **If she is conscious**, try to give small sips of water; if not, **do not give anything by mouth**.
- **Call 108** or find a vehicle to take her to the CHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Aggressive behavior followed by collapse in heat"] --> B["Move to shade and remove extra clothes"]
  B --> C["Douse with water and fan vigorously"]
  C --> D["Apply ice packs to neck and armpits"]
  D --> E["Call 108 for emergency transport"]
  E --> F["Continue cooling until medical help arrives"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Agitation/Confusion | Emergency—Call 108 | Never |
| Collapse/Fainting | Emergency—Call 108 | Never |
| Burning hot skin | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Sudden behavior changes in the heat are signs of heat stroke affecting the brain. Immediate cooling is life-saving.

Query: A young boy has a nosebleed after playing in the hot sun. He is also complaining of a headache and feels very hot. How to stop the bleeding and help the heat?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Nosebleed (Epistaxis) in heat**
- **Headache**
- **Hot skin**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Male | Sit up, lean forward, pinch nose, move to shade |
| Adult | Any | Pinch nose, cold cloth on head, hydration |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the child to the shade** immediately.
- **Have the child sit up and lean forward slightly** (Do NOT tilt the head back).
- **Pinch the soft part of the nose** firmly for 10 full minutes.
- **Place a cold cloth or ice** on the bridge of the nose and the forehead.
- **Give ORS or cool water** once the bleeding stops.
- **Keep the child quiet and resting**; no blowing the nose for several hours.
- If bleeding doesn't stop after 20 minutes, **take to PHC**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed and headache from heat"] --> B["Move to shade and sit leaning forward"]
  B --> C["Pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply cold cloth to nose and forehead"]
  D --> E["Give ORS once bleeding stops"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if bleeding lasts >20 mins"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding >20 mins | Refer to PHC | Pinch nose for 10 mins |
| Repeated nosebleeds | Refer to PHC | Home care for single event |
| Fever/Confusion | Refer to PHC | Cooling and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heat can cause blood vessels in the nose to burst. Cooling the head and body is essential alongside stopping the bleed.

Query: A man is working in the field and says his vision is getting blurry and his head is very light. He is sweating very heavily. What should he do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blurry vision**
- **Lightheadedness/Dizziness**
- **Very heavy sweating** (Heat Exhaustion)
- **Fast pulse**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Stop work, drink ORS, rest in shade with feet up |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for fainting, refer if heartbeat is irregular |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately stop all work** and move to a cool, shaded spot.
- **Lie down flat and raise the feet** about 1 foot high.
- **Drink 1–2 glasses of ORS** or salted water slowly.
- **Loosen all tight clothing** (belt, shirt buttons).
- **Wipe the face and body** with a cool damp cloth.
- **Do NOT go back to work** in the sun for the rest of the day.
- If vision does not clear or fainting occurs, **refer to PHC**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Blurry vision and heavy sweating in heat"] --> B["Stop work and move to cool shade"]
  B --> C["Lie down flat and raise feet 1 foot"]
  C --> D["Drink ORS or salt-sugar solution slowly"]
  D --> E["Loosen clothes and wipe skin with cool water"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if vision stays blurry"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vision stays blurry | Refer to PHC | Rest and fluids first |
| Fainting | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Rapid pulse persists | Refer to PHC | Rest in shade |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heavy sweating and blurry vision are signs of heat exhaustion. Treat immediately to prevent collapse.

Query: An ASHA worker finds a person who has stopped sweating and their skin is very dry, but they are still talking. They are very thirsty. Is it okay to give them a lot of water at once?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stopped sweating (Red flag for Heat Stroke)**
- **Dry, hot skin**
- **Intense thirst**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Cool skin with water, give small sips of ORS, refer |
| Elderly | Any | High risk—aggressive cooling, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT give a large amount of water at once**; it can cause vomiting or other issues.
- **Give small, frequent sips of ORS** or salted water.
- **The priority is cooling the skin**: Pour water on the body and fan.
- **Apply wet sheets** over the person and keep them wet.
- **Move them to a cool place** immediately.
- Even if they are talking, the lack of sweat is a **medical emergency**. Take them to the **CHC/Hospital**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person stops sweating with hot skin"] --> B["Move to shade and begin cooling with water"]
  B --> C["Give small, frequent sips of ORS slowly"]
  C --> D["Fan vigorously and apply wet sheets"]
  D --> E["Monitor for confusion or drowsiness"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for help"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sweating has stopped | Emergency Referral | Initial cooling only |
| Hot, dry skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Extreme thirst | Refer if no relief | Small sips of ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
When the body stops sweating, it can no longer cool itself. This is an emergency state regardless of how the person feels.

Query: A villager is having a 'seizure' (shaking) in the sun. His body is boiling hot. People are trying to put a metal key in his hand to stop it. What should the ASHA worker do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Seizures/Shaking (Fits)**
- **Body is boiling hot (High fever)**
- **Unconsciousness**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Protect head, do NOT use keys/spoons, cool with water, call 108 |
| Child | Any | Protect head, immediate cooling, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT put a metal key, spoon, or anything in the mouth** or hand. It does not help.
- **Move the person to a safe, shaded area** away from sharp objects.
- **Protect the head** with a soft cloth or pillow.
- **Immediately start cooling the body** by dousing with water and fanning.
- **Call 108** for an ambulance immediately.
- **After the shaking stops**, turn the person onto their side (Recovery Position).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Seizure and boiling hot skin in heat"] --> B["Move to shade and protect the head"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put objects in hands or mouth"]
  C --> D["Douse body with water and fan vigorously"]
  D --> E["Wait for shaking to stop, then turn on side"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizure/Fits | Emergency—Call 108 | Never |
| High body heat | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Unconsciousness | Emergency—Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Seizures in the heat are a sign of critical brain overheating. Physical cooling and emergency transport are the only treatments.

Query: An ASHA worker is in a village with no electricity. A man is suffering from heat stroke. How to cool him without a fan?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Confusion/Drowsiness**
- **Very hot, dry skin**
- **No electricity/No fan available**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Move to breezy spot, use hand fan, pour water, call 108 |
| Elderly | Any | Aggressive hand-fanning, cool water drenching, referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the person to a breezy area**, like a shaded porch or under a large tree.
- **Strip off clothing** to the underwear.
- **Drench the person with water** from a well or bucket.
- **Use a hand fan (Bijna)**, a piece of cardboard, or a folded cloth to fan him vigorously.
- **Apply wet towels or sheets** and keep them wet by pouring more water.
- **Place cool mud packs** on the limbs (a local cooling method) if water is scarce.
- **Arrange immediate transport** to the nearest health facility.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Heat stroke with no electricity"] --> B["Move to a breezy, shaded location"]
  B --> C["Strip clothes and drench with water"]
  C --> D["Fan vigorously with a hand fan or cardboard"]
  D --> E["Keep sheets/clothes wet constantly"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC as soon as possible"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Heat stroke symptoms | Emergency Referral | Aggressive cooling first |
| No improvement | Emergency—Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In the absence of a fan, the breeze and constant wetness are your best tools. Do not stop fanning by hand.

Query: A farm worker is feeling very dizzy and weak after working in the sun since morning. He is sweating a lot, his skin is cold and clammy, and he says he feels like vomiting. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Heavy sweating with cold, pale, clammy skin**
- **Fast, weak pulse**
- **Muscle cramps and severe weakness**
- **Dizziness or fainting**
- **Nausea or vomiting**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Move to shade, loosen clothes, give ORS sips |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Rest in cool area, feet up, drink salted lassi/water |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk for heart stress - monitor closely, refer if weak |
| Pregnant | Female | Lay on left side in shade, give coconut water, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the person to a cool, shaded, or breezy spot** immediately.
- **Loosen or remove tight clothing** to allow skin to breathe.
- **Have them lie down** and raise their feet about 1 foot high.
- **Give small sips of ORS**, salted buttermilk (Chaas), or lemon water.
- **Apply cool, wet cloths** to the forehead, neck, and armpits.
- **Do NOT** let the person return to work in the sun for the rest of the day.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person feels dizzy and is sweating heavily"] --> B["Move to cool shade and loosen clothing"]
  B --> C["Lay them down and raise feet 12 inches"]
  C --> D["Give small sips of ORS or salted water"]
  D --> E["Apply cool wet cloths to the body"]
  E --> F["Monitor for confusion or stop in sweating"]
  F --> G["Refer to PHC if no improvement in 30 mins"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting and cannot drink | Refer for IV fluids | Not safe at home |
| Fainting or confusion | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Mild dizziness/cramps | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |
| Sweating stops | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heat exhaustion can quickly turn into life-threatening heat stroke. If the person stops sweating or becomes confused, call 108 immediately.

Query: A woman was walking home from the market in the afternoon. She is very pale, breathing fast, and has a bad headache. She is sweating so much her clothes are wet. How to help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin is very pale and wet with sweat**
- **Pounding headache**
- **Rapid breathing and fast heartbeat**
- **Extreme tiredness**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Female | Sit under a fan, sip cold water with salt, remove jewelry |
| Elderly (>60) | Female | Monitor for chest pain, move to coolest room |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop all walking** and sit her in a ventilated, dark room if possible.
- **Give her Aam Panna** (raw mango drink) or water with a pinch of salt.
- **Fan her vigorously** with a hand fan or electric fan.
- **Spray her with cool water** and wipe with a damp towel.
- **Gently massage cramped muscles** if she has leg or stomach pain.
- **Wait at least 1 hour** before allowing any more physical activity.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pale woman with headache and heavy sweat"] --> B["Stop all movement and sit in cool shade"]
  B --> C["Provide Aam Panna or salted water sips"]
  C --> D["Fan vigorously and use cool water spray"]
  D --> E["Loosen clothes and remove shoes/jewelry"]
  E --> F["Check if headache improves with rest"]
  F --> G["Refer to PHC if symptoms persist"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe headache continues | Refer to PHC | Cool cloth and rest |
| Skin becomes hot and dry | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Pale skin and fast pulse | If no relief after cooling | Rest and hydration |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dehydration from sweat needs immediate replacement. Do not give plain water only; always add a pinch of salt or ORS.

Query: A child was playing in the hot wind (Loo) and now has a mild fever, is very cranky, and won't eat. He is sweating and says his stomach hurts. What first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Crankiness/Irritability in children**
- **Stomach cramps or nausea**
- **Dark yellow urine** (sign of dehydration)
- **Sunken eyes or dry mouth**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Sponge with room-temp water, give ORS, no solid food |
| Infant | Any | Breastfeed frequently, move to cool room, seek doctor |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor urine color, high hydration need |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately bring the child indoors** to a cool area.
- **Undress the child** to their underwear.
- **Sponge the whole body** with room-temperature water.
- **Give ORS or Barley water (Jo ka pani)** every 10–15 minutes.
- **Avoid giving oily or heavy food** until the stomach pain stops.
- **Watch the urine color**: If it stays dark, they need more fluids.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with stomach pain and fever from heat"] --> B["Bring indoors and remove clothing"]
  B --> C["Sponge body with room-temp water"]
  C --> D["Give ORS or Barley water in small sips"]
  D --> E["Monitor urine color and alertness"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if child becomes drowsy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No urine for 6 hours | Refer to PHC | Give plenty of fluids |
| Child is very sleepy | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Mild stomach cramps | If persists after hydration | Rest and ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Children lose water faster than adults. Ensure they are drinking even if they say they aren't thirsty.

Query: A man collapsed while working in the field. His skin is pale and very wet. His pulse is weak. He can speak but is very confused. Is this heat stroke?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Heavy sweating with cold, pale, damp skin**
- **Confusion or disorientation**
- **Weak, fast pulse**
- Muscle weakness or leg cramps

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Cool place, feet up, give ORS if conscious, call 108 |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High heart risk - rapid cooling, immediate referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move to a shaded, ventilated area** immediately.
- **Lay flat and elevate feet** (raise legs by 1 foot).
- **Loosen or remove restrictive clothing**.
- **Apply cool wet cloths** to head, neck, and armpits.
- **If fully awake, give ORS or salted water** slowly in sips.
- **Do NOT return to sun** work for several hours.
- **If confusion increases, treat as heat stroke** (call 108).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person collapsed in heat with pale wet skin"] --> B["Move to cool shade and raise feet"]
  B --> C["Loosen clothes and apply cool wet cloths"]
  C --> D["Check if person can talk or understand"]
  D --> E["If conscious, give small sips of ORS slowly"]
  E --> F["Monitor mental state carefully"]
  F --> G["If confusion increases, call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsy or severe confusion | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Fainting without recovery | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief with rest/hydration | Shade + Fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Confusion means brain stress. This needs close watching and professional medical help if it worsens.

Query: A teenage girl is complaining of very severe leg cramps after being in the sun all day at a village fair. She is sweating and feels very dizzy. What should I give her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe, painful muscle cramps**
- **Heavy sweating**
- Dizziness or lightheadedness
- Pale or flushed skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager (12-18) | Female | Sit in shade, stretch gently, drink salted fluids |
| Child (<12) | Any | Move to cool area, ORS, gentle stretch |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop all walking** and sit her in a cool, ventilated area.
- **Give ORS or salted Lemon water (Nimbu Pani)** immediately.
- **Gently stretch the cramped muscles**: For leg cramps, push the toes toward the face while the leg is straight.
- **Massage the area** with a little oil after stretching.
- **Apply a cool, wet towel** to the cramped area.
- Wait at least 1 hour after cramps stop before allowing any activity.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Girl with severe leg cramps and dizziness after sun"] --> B["Sit in cool shade and stop walking"]
  B --> C["Provide ORS or salted Lemon water immediately"]
  C --> D["Gently stretch and massage cramped muscles"]
  D --> E["Apply cool wet towel to the area"]
  E --> F["Rest and monitor for worsening dizziness"]
  F --> G["Refer if cramps continue or worsen"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cramps don't stop | Refer to PHC | Gentle stretching and salt |
| Confusion or fainting | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Pale skin/sweat | If no relief in 30 mins | Shade and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Muscle cramps mean the body has lost too much salt and water. Drinking ORS or salted water is essential.

Query: A elderly man is feeling faint, his skin is very cool and pale. He's breathing fast. His grandson is fanning him but no other first aid. What's the plan?

## 🩺 What To Do Now

- **Help him lie down and raise his feet** 1 foot higher than his head.
- **Fan him vigorously** with a hand fan or electric fan.
- **Sponge his face, neck, and limbs** with cool water.
- **Loosen or remove restrictive clothing**.
- **Give small sips of ORS** or salted water **if he is fully awake**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly man feels faint with pale cool wet skin"] --> B["Move to cool shade and help lie down"]

```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care

Elderly people are high-risk. Faintness should be treated seriously and referral considered if there's no quick relief.

Query: A pregnant woman in her 7th month has severe headache, nausea, and her legs are cramping. She's been working in the afternoon sun. She is sweating. What remedies can be used?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe headache and nausea**
- **Leg cramps and tiredness**
- **Sweating**
- **Changes in baby movement (very important)**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Lay on left side in cool shade, give coconut water/ORS, refer |
| Adult | Any | Cool place, ORS, gentle stretch, monitor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move her to a cool, shaded, or ventilated spot** immediately.
- **Have her lie on her left side** (improves blood flow to the heart and baby).
- **Give her Coconut water (Nariyal Pani)** or ORS in small sips.
- **Fan her** and place cool wet cloths on her forehead.
- **Gently stretch her leg muscles** and encourage small sips of salted lassi.
- **Ask her to monitor the baby's movements**. If she's unsure, or movements are less, she needs immediate referral.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with headache nausea and cramps"] --> B["Move to cool shade immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay her on her left side and provide cool shade"]
  C --> D["Give Coconut water or ORS in small sips"]
  D --> E["Gently stretch and massage cramped muscles"]
  E --> F["Ask about and monitor baby movements carefully"]
  F --> G["If baby movements reduce or symptoms worsen, refer immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Reduced baby movements | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Severe headache continues | Refer to CHC/Doctor | Cool cloth and rest |
| Worsening cramps/nausea | Refer to CHC | Gentle stretch + fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pregnant women need extra care. Baby movements are the critical sign to monitor after cooling and hydration.

Query: A boy who plays football has dark yellow urine, feels very tired, and is sweating a lot. He has a slight headache. What is the best drink for him in the village?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dark yellow urine (sign of dehydration)**
- Extreme tiredness or weakness
- **Heavy sweating**
- Slight headache

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Male | Cool place, give ORS, monitor urine color |
| Teenager (12-18) | Male | Rest in shade, salted drinks, monitor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the boy to a shaded, breezy area** immediately.
- **Give him ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution)**; 1 cup every 15 minutes.
- **Local remedies**: **Aam Panna** (raw mango drink), **Chaas** (buttermilk with salt), or **Lemon water (Nimbu Pani)** with a pinch of salt.
- **Avoid giving soda or soft drinks**. Plain water is okay but adding salt is better.
- **Encourage him to rest** for the rest of the day.
- Watch the next few urinations. If it doesn't get pale, he needs more fluids.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Football boy with dark urine and tiredness"] --> B["Move to cool shade and stop playing"]
  B --> C["Give ORS Aam Panna or Chaas in small sips"]
  C --> D["Fan vigorously and use cool water sponge"]
  D --> E["Encourage rest for the rest of the day"]
  E --> F["Monitor urine color until it becomes pale"]
  F --> G["Refer if no improvement or child becomes drowsy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Urine is dark red/orange | Refer to PHC | Monitor and give fluids |
| Extreme drowsiness/sleepy | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Tiredness improves | Monitor closely | Home care with fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dark urine is a warning sign of water and salt loss. Hydration (with salt) and rest are critical for recovery.

Query: A teenager at a village fair suddenly feels dizzy and faints. Her skin is cold and pale, she is sweating. She is conscious now but very weak. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden fainting followed by recovery**
- **Skin is cold, pale, and wet with sweat**
- Extreme weakness or fatigue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager (12-18) | Female | Sit up slowly, move to shade, give ORS, monitor |
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool place, ORS sips, watch alertness |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the person to a shaded, ventilated area** immediately.
- **Lay flat and elevate feet** (raise legs by 1 foot) if they are still lying down.
- **Loosen or remove restrictive clothing**.
- **Give ORS or salted Lemon water (Nimbu Pani)** slowly once fully awake.
- **Apply cool, wet cloths** to the forehead and neck.
- **Ask her to sit up slowly** after resting with feet elevated for 15-20 mins.
- Monitor for worsening weakness. If no quick relief, consider referral.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Teenager faints with pale cold wet skin in heat"] --> B["Move to cool shade and lay flat with feet up"]
  B --> C["Provide ORS or salted water slowly if awake"]
  C --> D["Fan vigorously and use cool water spray"]
  D --> E["Monitor mental state carefully"] 
  E --> F["Encourage slow movements when sitting up"]
  F --> G["If no improvement in 20 mins, refer to PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot be woken up | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Frequent fainting | Refer to PHC/CHC | Monitor after first episode |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting (Syncope) from heat exhaustion is temporary, but the person must be monitored for transition into heat stroke. Cooling and salt replacement are key.


## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cramps don't stop | Refer to PHC | Gentle stretching and salt |
| Confusion or fainting | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stomach cramps from heat exhaustion mean the body has lost too much water and salt. Rehydration is key.

Query: A child was playing in the hot wind (Loo) and now has pale skin, is very cranky, and won't eat. He is sweating and says his stomach hurts. What remedies can be used?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin is pale and wet with sweat**
- **Crankiness/Irritability in children**
- **Stomach cramps or nausea**
- **Heavy sweating**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Sponge with room-temp water, give ORS, monitor |
| Infant | Any | Breastfeed frequently, move to cool room, seek doctor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately bring the child indoors** to a cool area.
- **Undress the child** to their underwear.
- **Sponge the whole body** with room-temperature water.
- **Give ORS or Barley water (Jo ka pani)** every 10–15 minutes.
- Local remedies: **Aam Panna** (raw mango drink), **Chaas** (buttermilk with salt), or **coconut water** are all excellent choices.
- **Avoid giving solid or oily food** until the stomach pain stops.
- **Watch the urine color**: If it stays dark, they need more fluids.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with pale wet skin crankiness and stomach pain from Loo"] --> B["Bring indoors and remove clothing"]
  B --> C["Sponge body with room-temp water"]
  C --> D["Give ORS or Barley water in small sips"]
  D --> E["Monitor urine color and alertness"]
  E --> F["Refer if child becomes drowsy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Child is very sleepy | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Vomiting and cannot drink | Refer for IV fluids | Not safe at home |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Children lose water faster than adults. Ensure they are drinking even if they say they aren't thirsty.

Query: A teenage boy fainted while working in the field and is very confused now. His skin is pale and very wet. His pulse is weak. Is this heat stroke?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Heavy sweating with cold, pale, clammy skin**
- **Sudden fainting followed by confusion**
- **Confusion or disorientation**
- **Weak, fast pulse**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager (12-18) | Male | Cool place, feet up, give ORS if conscious, call 108 |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High heart risk - rapid cooling, immediate referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move to a shaded, ventilated area** immediately.
- **Lay flat and elevate feet** (raise legs by 1 foot).
- **Loosen or remove restrictive clothing**.
- **Apply cool wet cloths** to head, neck, and armpits.
- **Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution)**; 1 cup every 15 minutes **if fully awake**.
- Local remedies: **Aam Panna** (raw mango drink), **Chaas** (buttermilk with salt), or **coconut water** are all excellent choices.
- **Do NOT return to sun** work for several hours.
- **If confusion increases, treat as heat stroke** (call 108).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Teenager fainted in heat with pale wet skin and confusion"] --> B["Move to cool shade and lay flat with feet up"]
  B --> C["Loosen clothes and remove restrictive items"]
  C --> D["Check if person can talk or understand"]
  D --> E["If conscious, give small sips of ORS slowly"]
  E --> F["Monitor mental state carefully"]
  F --> G["If confusion increases, call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsy or severe confusion | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Fainting without recovery | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Confusion means brain stress. This needs close watching and professional medical help if it worsens.

Query: A child was playing in the afternoon heat. Now the child has a red, itchy rash all over the neck and chest, is very cranky, and is sweating a lot. What Remedies can ASHA work do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Red, itchy rash (heat rash)**
- Extreme tiredness or weakness
- **Crankiness/Irritability in children**
- Sunken eyes or dry mouth (severe dehydration)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool room, give ORS, monitor rash, loose cotton clothes |
| Infant | Any | Keep in shade, sponge with plain water, no heavy creams |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately bring the child indoors** to a cool, dry area.
- **Wipe the area gently** with a cool damp cloth.
- **Give ORS or Lemonade (Nimbu Pani)** with a pinch of salt.
- **Do NOT** use heavy creams, oily ointments, or strong soaps on the rash.
- Local Remedies: **Sandalwood paste (Chandan ka lep)** or **multani mitti** (Fuller's earth) on the rash can be helpful.
- **Wear loose, light-colored cotton clothing**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with red itchy heat rash and crankiness from heat"] --> B["Bring indoors to a cool, dry room"]
  B --> C["Gently wipe rash with cool damp cloth"]
  C --> D["Give ORS or Nimbu Pani in small sips"]
  D --> E["Do NOT use heavy creams, oily ointments on rash"]
  E --> F["Monitor for signs of severe dehydration"]
  F --> G["Refer if rash is infected or child becomes drowsy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rash looks infected or pus is seen | Refer to PHC/Doctor | Home care for simple rash |
| No urine for 6 hours | Refer to PHC | Give plenty of fluids |
| Mild simple rash | Monitor and keep dry | Home care with Chandan paste |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Children lose water faster than adults. Ensure they are drinking even if they say they aren't thirsty. Monitor urine color.

Query: A teenage girl has painful cramps in her arms, legs, and stomach after working in the sun for several hours. She is sweating heavily and feels dizzy. What Remedies and first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe, painful muscle cramps**
- **Heavy sweating**
- Dizziness or faintness
- Pale or flushed skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager (12-18) | Female | Sit in shade, stretch gently, drink salted fluids |
| Child (<12) | Any | Move to cool area, ORS, gentle stretch |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop all walking** and sit her in a cool, ventilated area.
- **Give ORS (Oral Rehydration Solution)**; 1 cup every 15 minutes.
- **Gently stretch the cramped muscles** and encourage small sips of salted lassi.
- **Gently massage the area** with a little oil after stretching.
- **Apply a cool, wet towel** to the cramped area.
- Wait at least 1 hour after cramps stop before allowing any activity.
- Local remedies: **Aam Panna** (raw mango drink), **Chaas** (buttermilk with salt), or **coconut water** are all excellent choices.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Girl with severe arm leg and stomach cramps from heat"] --> B["Sit in cool shade and stop walking"]
  B --> C["Provide ORS or salted Lemon water immediately"]
  C --> D["Gently stretch and massage cramped muscles"]
  D --> E["Apply cool wet towel to the area"]
  E --> F["Monitor carefully for worsening weakness"] 
  F --> G["Refer if no improvement in 30 mins"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cramps don't stop | Refer to PHC | Gentle stretching and salt |
| Confusion or fainting | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Mild pale wet skin | If no relief in 30 mins | Rest in shade + fluids |
| Chest pain | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe muscle cramps mean the body has lost too much salt and water. Drinking ORS or salted water is essential.

Query: A boy who was working in the field is having a nosebleed, complains of severe headache, nausea, and feels very dizzy. His face is pale and he is sweating heavily. What remedies for nosebleed and first aid in village?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Nosebleed following sun exposure**
- **Severe headache and nausea**
- Dizziness or faintness
- **Heavy sweating**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Male | Sit up slowly, lean forward, pinch nose, monitor |
| Teenager (12-18) | Male | Cool breezy place, pinch nose, ORS sips, monitor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit the boy down** in a shaded, ventilated area.
- **Do NOT** let him lie flat. **Have him lean forward** slightly.
- **Pinch the soft part of the nose** with fingers for 10 minutes.
- **Place a cool wet cloth** on his nose bridge and back of his neck.
- **Give ORS or Lemonade (Nimbu Pani)** with a pinch of salt slowly after bleeding stops.
- **Do NOT** return to sun work for several hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Boy with nosebleed headache and nausea from sun"] --> B["Sit down in cool shade and stop work"]
  B --> C["Pinch soft part of nose for 10 minutes, lean forward"]
  C --> D["Apply cool wet cloth on nose and neck"]
  D --> E["Give ORS Nimbu Pani in small sips if bleeding stops"]
  E --> F["Wait at least 1 hour before allowing any activity"]
  F --> G["Refer to PHC if nosebleed does not stop or child faints"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Nosebleed won't stop in 20 mins | Refer to PHC | Pinch nose for 10 mins |
| Repeated nosebleeds | Refer to PHC/CHC | Monitor after first episode |
| Fainting/Confusion | Emergency - Refer immediately | Never |
| Severe headache continues | Refer to PHC | Cool cloth and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Nosebleed in heat exhaustion can be temporary, but the boy must be monitored for other severe symptoms. Cooling and rehydration are critical for recovery.

Query: A teenager has been playing in the Loo. Now he has a red, itchy rash all over the neck and chest, is very cranky, and won't eat. He is sweating. What Remedies can ASHA work do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Red, itchy rash (heat rash)**
- Extreme tiredness or weakness
- **Crankiness/Irritability in children**
- Sunken eyes or dry mouth (severe dehydration)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool room, give ORS, monitor rash, loose cotton clothes |
| Infant | Any | Keep in shade, sponge with plain water, no heavy creams |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately bring the child indoors** to a cool, dry area.
- **Wipe the area gently** with a cool damp cloth.
- **Give ORS or Lemonade (Nimbu Pani)** with a pinch of salt.
- **Do NOT** use heavy creams, oily ointments, or strong soaps on the rash.
- Local Remedies: **Sandalwood paste (Chandan ka lep)** or **multani mitti** (Fuller's earth) on the rash can be helpful.
- **Wear loose, light-colored cotton clothing**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with red itchy heat rash and crankiness from Loo"] --> B["Bring indoors to a cool, dry room"]
  B --> C["Gently wipe rash with cool damp cloth"]
  C --> D["Give ORS or Nimbu Pani in small sips"]
  D --> E["Do NOT use heavy creams, oily ointments on rash"]
  E --> F["Monitor for signs of severe dehydration"]
  F --> G["Refer if rash is infected or child becomes drowsy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rash looks infected or pus is seen | Refer to PHC/Doctor | Home care for simple rash |
| No urine for 6 hours | Refer to PHC | Give plenty of fluids |
| Mild simple rash | Monitor and keep dry | Home care with Chandan paste |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Children lose water faster than adults. Ensure they are drinking even if they say they aren't thirsty. Monitor urine color.

Query: An old man was found outside in the snow. He is shivering uncontrollably, his hands are blue, and he is mumbling words that make no sense. How should I warm him up?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Uncontrolled shivering** or shivering that has suddenly stopped
- **Slurred speech** or mumbling (acting drunk)
- **Blue or gray skin** and cold touch
- Drowsiness or extreme exhaustion
- Slow, shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Skin-to-skin contact, wrap in dry woolens, give warm honey water |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Remove wet clothes, move to fire/heater, give warm sweet tea |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk — Move indoors immediately, wrap in several layers, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move the person indoors** or to a spot shielded from wind and snow.
- **Remove wet clothes gently**; do not rub the skin harshly.
- **Cover with dry blankets** and woolen hats. Focus on the chest, neck, and head.
- **Give warm, sweet drinks** like tea or ginger water (Adrak ki chai) only if they can swallow.
- **Use body heat**: If no heater, lie next to the person under blankets.
- **Do NOT** use direct boiling water or massage the limbs; it can damage the heart.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person found cold and shivering"] --> B["Move indoors and remove all wet clothing"]
  B --> C["Wrap head, neck, and chest in dry woolen blankets"]
  C --> D["Give warm sweet tea or ginger water if conscious"]
  D --> E["Use skin-to-skin contact if blankets are not enough"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC if shivering stops but skin stays cold"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shivering stops but still cold | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Confusion or slurred speech | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Mild shivering, alert | If no improvement in 30 mins | Warm blankets and sweet tea |
| Loss of consciousness | Immediate Referral | Keep warm during transport |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hypothermia is a silent killer in the hills. Always handle the person gently as their heart is fragile when very cold.

Query: A child fell into a cold mountain stream and is now shivering and very sleepy. We have a fire going, but he won't wake up properly. What is the first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme sleepiness** or hard to wake up
- **Shivering has stopped** despite being cold
- **Pale, cold skin**
- Very slow pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Remove wet clothes, wrap in warm dry shawl, skin-to-skin contact |
| Infant | Any | Warm the mother and keep baby against her chest, seek help |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove wet clothes immediately** and pat the body dry gently.
- **Wrap the child in pre-warmed dry blankets** or woolens.
- **Use 'Kangri' or warm water bottles** wrapped in towels (not directly on skin).
- **Keep the child awake** if possible; do not let them sleep if they are confused.
- **Skin-to-skin contact**: A warm adult should hold the child under the blankets.
- **Refer to the nearest hospital** immediately as the heart rate may drop.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child fell in cold water and is sleepy"] --> B["Gently remove wet clothes and pat dry"]
  B --> C["Wrap in dry shawls and cover the head"]
  C --> D["Adult provides body heat through skin-to-skin contact"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give water if the child is too sleepy"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately while keeping warm"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hard to wake up | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Shivering has stopped | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Mild shivering, alert | If shivering lasts >1 hour | Warm room and dry clothes |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal body temperature can continue to drop even after the person is pulled out of water. Keep fanning the fire and monitor breathing.

Query: A woman was stuck in a landslide area overnight in the cold. She is conscious but her hands and feet are stiff like wood and she is not shivering anymore. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stiff, rigid muscles** (stiff like wood)
- **Shivering has stopped** (Late stage)
- **Confusion or 'don't care' attitude**
- Low, slow heartbeat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Female | Gentle handling, warm blankets, do not rub limbs |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk — referral needed immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Handle her like glass**: Moving her roughly can trigger heart failure.
- **Move her to a warm room** but keep her lying flat.
- **Wrap the core (chest and belly)** first; do not focus on hands and feet yet.
- **Use warm (not hot) stones** or bottles wrapped in cloth around her armpits.
- **Avoid alcohol** or caffeinated coffee; give warm salt-sugar water or soup.
- **Transport her to CHC** in a lying position, covered in many layers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person was in cold overnight and is stiff"] --> B["Handle very gently and keep lying flat"]
  B --> C["Wrap the chest and head in many dry layers"]
  C --> D["Apply warm stones/bottles to armpits and groin"]
  D --> E["Do NOT rub or massage the stiff limbs"]
  E --> F["Transport to medical center immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stiff limbs/No shivering | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Irregular heartbeat | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Mild shivering, moving | If not better in 20 mins | Warm fluids and fire |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
When shivering stops but the person is still cold, the body has run out of energy to warm itself. This is a severe emergency.

Query: A trekker is acting very strange, he took off his jacket even though it is freezing outside. He says he is feeling hot. How do I help him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Paradoxical undressing** (taking off clothes in the cold)
- **Irrational behavior** or hallucinations
- **Clumsiness** (dropping things, stumbling)
- Cold skin despite saying they feel hot

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Forcefully put clothes back on, move to shelter |
| Teenager | Any | Calm the person, cover the head, give warm sweet tea |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately put his clothes back on** and cover him with a dry blanket.
- **Lead him to a sheltered area** away from the wind.
- **Give high-energy food** like jaggery (Gur), chocolate, or biscuits.
- **Give warm sweet tea** to boost energy and temperature.
- **Keep talking to him** to monitor his mental state.
- **Refer to a doctor** because 'feeling hot' in the snow is a sign of brain shutdown.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person acting strange and removing clothes in cold"] --> B["Immediately put dry clothes and blankets on them"]
  B --> C["Move to a wind-shielded shelter or indoor room"]
  C --> D["Give warm sweet tea and high-energy food like Gur"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of normal speech and behavior"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for evaluation of mental state"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Taking off clothes in cold | Refer to PHC | Warmth and food first |
| Hallucinations | Emergency Referral | Keep safe and warm |
| Stumbling/Clumsiness | If it persists after warming | Rest and sugar |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This behavior means the person's brain is not getting enough heat. They may think they are warm while they are actually freezing.

Query: A group of laborers worked in cold rain all day. One is shaking badly and his teeth are chattering. We only have a small hut. What is the best way to warm him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Violent shivering** and chattering teeth
- **Difficulty walking** or moving fingers
- **Very cold, white skin**
- Fast, shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Remove wet clothes, drink warm soup/tea, huddle together |
| Elderly | Any | Move to the warmest spot, multiple blankets, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove the wet work clothes** and give any dry cloth available.
- **Huddle together**: Use the body heat of other people to warm him.
- **Give warm salt-sugar water** or vegetable soup.
- **Build a small fire** safely away from the bedding but close enough for heat.
- **Keep the person moving** slightly if they can, to generate internal heat.
- **If he becomes quiet or stops shivering**, seek medical help immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person shaking from cold rain exposure"] --> B["Remove wet clothes and wrap in dry material"]
  B --> C["Give warm soup or tea with sugar"]
  C --> D["Huddle with others for shared body heat"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of normal temperature"]
  E --> F["Refer if shivering leads to exhaustion/drowsiness"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shivering turns into sleepiness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Limbs remain numb after warming | Refer to PHC | Warm water and massage (gentle) |
| Violent shivering | If not better in 1 hour | Dry clothes and hot tea |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Wet clothes in the wind cause the body to lose heat 25 times faster than dry clothes. Drying the person is the first priority.

Query: An ASHA worker finds an infant in a cold room. The baby is very quiet, the skin is red and cold to touch. The baby is not crying. What should she do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin is bright red and cold** (this is a bad sign in babies)
- **Very quiet** or limp (weakness)
- **No crying** or very weak cry
- Refusing to breastfeed

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC), wrap in woolens, refer |
| Child | Any | Warm fluids, dry clothes, cover head |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start Kangaroo Mother Care**: Place the baby naked against the mother's bare chest and cover both with a shawl.
- **Cover the baby's head** with a woolen cap (babies lose most heat from the head).
- **Try to breastfeed** if the baby is awake enough to suckle.
- **Do NOT** use a heater directly near the baby’s skin.
- **Refer to the PHC/Hospital** immediately; babies can die very quickly from cold.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant with red, cold skin and no crying"] --> B["Place baby skin-to-skin on mother's chest"]
  B --> C["Wrap both mother and baby in warm blankets"]
  C --> D["Put a woolen cap on the baby's head"]
  D --> E["Attempt breastfeeding if baby is alert"]
  E --> F["Refer to hospital immediately for neonatal care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red, cold skin in infant | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Limp/No crying | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Refusing milk | Refer to PHC | Keep warm and monitor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In infants, cold skin that looks bright red is a sign of severe cold stress. It is a medical emergency.

Query: A man got lost in the forest during a cold night. He is now stumbling and cannot use his fingers to hold a cup. He is very confused. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **The 'Umbles'**: Stumbling, fumbling, mumbling
- **Loss of coordination** in hands
- **Confusion** or answering slowly
- Skin is blue or very pale

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Assist with walking, move to heat, give high-sugar tea |
| Elderly | Any | Wrap in heavy woolens, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Help him sit or lie down** so he doesn't fall and get injured.
- **Wrap his hands and feet** in warm towels or woolens.
- **Give warm sweet tea or soup**. Add extra sugar or jaggery for energy.
- **Keep him in a warm, wind-free room**.
- **Do NOT** allow him to smoke or drink alcohol; it makes the body lose more heat.
- **Refer to PHC** if he remains confused after 30 minutes of warming.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person stumbling and fumbling from cold"] --> B["Move to a warm room and sit them down"]
  B --> C["Wrap chest and extremities in dry blankets"]
  C --> D["Give warm sweet tea or soup with jaggery"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of clear speech and movement"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if confusion does not improve"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion persists | Refer to PHC | Warmth and sugar first |
| Loss of coordination | If it lasts >45 mins | Warm room and rest |
| Blue fingers/toes | Refer to PHC | Warm towels and elevation |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Clumsiness is a sign that the nerves and brain are cooling down. Act fast to provide external heat.

Query: A villager says his feet are frozen and have no feeling after walking in the ice. He wants to put them in boiling water. Is this okay?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Numbness** (no feeling in feet)
- **Stiff, waxy skin**
- **Skin color is white or yellow-gray**
- Feet are hard to the touch

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | DO NOT use hot water, use lukewarm water, wrap in dry cloth |
| Child | Any | Warm between adult's hands, do not rub, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use boiling water or fire**; he cannot feel pain and will burn his skin.
- **Soak feet in lukewarm water** (body temperature, not hot).
- **Remove wet socks** gently. If stuck, soak them off in the water.
- **Do NOT rub or massage the feet**; this can cause tissue damage.
- **Pat dry gently** and wrap in a clean, dry cloth.
- **Refer to PHC** to check for frostbite or deep tissue damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Frozen, numb feet after ice exposure"] --> B["Remove wet socks gently using lukewarm water"]
  B --> C["Soak feet in lukewarm water for 20-30 minutes"]
  C --> D["Pat dry gently without rubbing or massaging"]
  D --> E["Wrap in clean, dry bandages or cotton cloth"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for wound and tissue check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin stays white/hard | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Blisters forming | Refer to PHC | Do not pop blisters |
| Numbness only | If no feeling in 1 hour | Lukewarm soak and dry |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Thawing frozen feet is very painful. Once thawed, do not allow the feet to freeze again.

Query: We found a hiker who is unconscious and his breathing is so slow I can barely see it. He is cold as ice. How do I help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconsciousness**
- **Extremely slow or shallow breathing**
- **Pulse is hard to find**
- Skin is cold and blue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Check for breathing, wrap in blankets, call 108, CPR if needed |
| Elderly | Any | Immediate emergency transport while warming |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for breathing** for at least 60 seconds (it may be very slow).
- **Handle very gently**: A sudden jolt can stop the heart of a cold person.
- **Wrap him in as many blankets as possible**, including the head.
- **Do NOT try to give food or water** to an unconscious person.
- **Call 108 immediately**. He needs a hospital to be warmed from the inside.
- **If breathing stops, start CPR** slowly and continue until help arrives.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious and very cold person"] --> B["Check breathing for a full 1 minute"]
  B --> C["Wrap head and body in multiple dry layers"]
  C --> D["Handle very gently to avoid heart shock"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything by mouth"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for emergency transport immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Breathing < 6 per min | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Blue skin/no pulse | Emergency — Call 108 | Perform CPR |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A cold person is not dead until they are 'warm and dead.' Keep warming them during transport even if they seem dead.

Query: A woman is shaking from the cold and is very pale. She says her chest hurts. What should I do for her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Chest pain** or tightness
- **Pale skin and shaking**
- Shortness of breath
- Extreme weakness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Female | Keep warm, sit upright, refer for heart check |
| Elderly | Any | High risk — immediate referral, monitor pulse |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move her to a warm place** and sit her in a comfortable position.
- **Cover her with warm blankets** and give her warm tea.
- **Do NOT ask her to walk**; she must rest to save her heart.
- **Loosen any tight clothing** around her neck and waist.
- **Ask if she has heart medicine** (like sorbitrate) and help her take it if prescribed.
- **Refer to the CHC immediately** to check for a heart attack triggered by the cold.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cold person with chest pain and shaking"] --> B["Move to warmth and keep in sitting position"]
  B --> C["Wrap in blankets and provide warm tea"]
  C --> D["Do NOT let the person walk or strain"]
  D --> E["Loosen tight clothes and monitor pulse"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for heart evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest pain in cold | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shortness of breath | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Pale skin/Weakness | Refer if it lasts after warming | Warmth and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cold weather makes the heart work much harder. Chest pain in the cold should always be treated as a heart emergency.

Query: A teenage girl was caught in a hailstorm. She is shivering and her fingernails are blue. She is awake but very slow to answer. How can I help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blue fingernails or lips**
- **Slow responses** (takes long to answer simple questions)
- **Shivering**
- Pale and cold skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager (12-18) | Female | Dry clothes, wrap in blankets, warm sweet drink |
| Child (<12) | Any | Skin-to-skin contact, honey water, cover head |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediately get her out of the wind** and into a dry place.
- **Check her clothes**; if damp, change them for dry ones.
- **Give her a hot drink with lots of sugar** or jaggery.
- **Wrap her in a blanket** and make sure her head and ears are covered.
- **Stay with her** and keep her talking to ensure she doesn't become drowsy.
- **If she becomes sleepy**, take her to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Girl with blue nails and slow speech from cold"] --> B["Move to dry shelter and change wet clothes"]
  B --> C["Wrap in blankets and cover head and ears"]
  C --> D["Give hot tea or milk with sugar/honey"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of alertness and pink color"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if drowsiness or confusion starts"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsiness/Sleepiness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Lips stay blue after warming | Refer to PHC | Warmth and sugar |
| Shivering and alert | If no relief in 30 mins | Dry clothes and heat |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Blue nails mean the body is pulling blood away from the hands to keep the heart warm. Warm the core first.

Query: An old lady is sitting near a cold window and is not moving. Her skin is very cold, she is not shivering, and she seems very confused about where she is. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No shivering but very cold skin** (Dangerous stage)
- **Disorientation** (not knowing where they are)
- **Slow movements** or no movement at all
- Weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Female | Move away from window, wrap in layers, call 108 |
| Adult | Any | Warm room, high-energy food, monitor carefully |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move her away from the cold window** to the warmest part of the house.
- **Wrap her in several layers** of dry woolens or blankets.
- **Apply gentle heat** like a warm water bottle (wrapped in cloth) near her belly.
- **Give warm soup or tea** slowly if she is awake.
- **Check her pulse** frequently.
- **Refer to the hospital**; elderly people can have 'quiet hypothermia' where they just fade away.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly woman confused and cold with no shivering"] --> B["Move to the warmest room in the house"]
  B --> C["Wrap in several dry layers and cover the head"]
  C --> D["Apply wrapped warm water bottle to the belly area"]
  D --> E["Give small sips of warm soup if alert"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately as this is serious"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confused/Disoriented | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Skin cold, no shivering | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Mild cold, alert | PHC visit if no change | Warm room and food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In the elderly, the body often fails to shiver even when it is dangerously cold. Don't wait for shivering to start treatment.

Query: A man has been shivering for two hours after his boat capsized in a lake. Now he is starting to vomit and is very weak. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting** after cold exposure
- **Severe weakness** and pale skin
- **Violent shivering**
- Dizziness when sitting up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Dry clothes, lie on side (if vomiting), warm blankets |
| Child | Any | Immediate warming, monitor breathing, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove wet clothes** and dry him off quickly but gently.
- **If vomiting, place him on his side** so he doesn't choke.
- **Do NOT give food or water** until the vomiting stops.
- **Wrap him in thick blankets** and cover his head.
- **Use a heater or fire** to warm the room, but keep him at a safe distance.
- **Refer to PHC** because vomiting in hypothermia is a sign of internal stress.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person shivering and vomiting after cold water"] --> B["Remove wet clothes and dry skin gently"]
  B --> C["Place on side (Recovery Position) if vomiting"]
  C --> D["Wrap in thick blankets and keep head covered"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give oral fluids while vomiting"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for observation and IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting from cold | Refer to PHC | Dry clothes and side-lying |
| Shivering and alert | If no relief in 30 mins | Warmth and blankets |
| Severe weakness | Refer to PHC | Rest and warmth |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Vomiting makes dehydration worse. Once vomiting stops and he is alert, give small sips of warm salt-sugar water.

Query: A child is shivering and crying because his ears and nose are white and hard. He says they hurt a lot. How can I help him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **White, hard skin on ears or nose**
- **Severe pain** followed by numbness
- **Blisters** starting to form
- Skin feels 'frozen'

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Warm with hands, do not rub, use lukewarm water |
| Infant | Any | Warm against mother's body, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cover the white areas with your warm hands** (do not rub!).
- **Use lukewarm water** on a cloth and press it gently to the ears/nose.
- **Move the child to a warm room**.
- **Give warm milk or honey water** to warm the body from inside.
- **Do NOT use a hair dryer or fire** directly on the white skin; it will burn.
- **Refer to the PHC** if the skin stays white or turns black/blue.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with white, hard ears and nose"] --> B["Cover white areas with warm, dry hands"]
  B --> C["Move to a warm room and give warm milk"]
  C --> D["Apply lukewarm (not hot) wet cloth gently"]
  D --> E["Do NOT rub, massage, or use direct fire"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if color does not return"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin stays white/hard | Refer to PHC | Warm hands and lukewarm cloth |
| Blisters/Black spots | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Pain returns with color | Monitor carefully | Home care for mild cases |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Frostbite (frozen skin) often happens with hypothermia. Always treat the whole body's warmth first.

Query: An ASHA worker finds a man in a cold shed who is breathing very fast and is very agitated. He is shivering but won't let anyone touch him. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Agitation or anger** (unusual behavior)
- **Fast breathing and shivering**
- Cold skin
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Calm the person, move to warmth, give jaggery/tea |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for heart strain, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Speak calmly** and try to lead him to a warm place.
- **Offer warm sweet tea or Gur (jaggery)**; the sugar will help his brain.
- **Once he calms down, wrap him in blankets**.
- **Ensure he is away from the cold wind**.
- **Check if he has been drinking alcohol**, as this can mask the cold.
- **Refer to PHC** if his behavior remains strange or if he becomes drowsy.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cold person who is agitated and shivering"] --> B["Speak calmly and offer warm sweet tea/jaggery"]
  B --> C["Move person to a warm, quiet shelter"]
  C --> D["Wrap in dry blankets once they permit touch"]
  D --> E["Monitor for change to drowsiness or calm state"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for mental and thermal check"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Continued agitation | Refer to PHC | Calm environment and sugar |
| Drowsiness starts | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering and alert | If not better in 30 mins | Warmth and food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Agitation can be an early sign of the brain being affected by the cold. It can quickly lead to unconsciousness.

Query: A woman was found in a mountain pass. She is shivering, her pulse is very fast, and she is crying saying she cannot feel her legs. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Numbness in legs** (cannot feel them)
- **Fast pulse and shivering**
- **Crying/Distress**
- Cold, pale skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Female | Warm room, elevate legs slightly, dry blankets |
| Elderly | Any | High risk — immediate referral, monitor pulse |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move her to a warm place** and remove any wet socks or shoes.
- **Wrap her legs in warm, dry woolens** (do not massage them!).
- **Give her a warm, sweet drink** like honey water or ginger tea.
- **Elevate her legs slightly** using a pillow or rolled blanket.
- **Keep her head covered** with a woolen cap or scarf.
- **Refer to the PHC** to check for nerve damage from the cold.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person shivering with numb legs in cold"] --> B["Move to warmth and remove wet footwear"]
  B --> C["Wrap legs and whole body in dry woolens"]
  C --> D["Give warm sweet tea and elevate legs slightly"]
  D --> E["Do NOT rub or apply direct heat to legs"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if numbness persists"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Legs stay numb >1 hour | Refer to PHC | Warm blankets and rest |
| Skin turns dark or black | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Crying and shivering | If not calmed by warmth | Warmth and support |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Numbness is a sign that the body is protecting the heart by cutting off blood to the legs. Warm the chest first.

Query: A baby was born in a cold village at night. The baby is cold and blue. There is no hospital nearby. How to keep the baby alive?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blue skin and cold touch**
- **No crying** or weak grunting
- **Slow breathing**
- Limp body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Newborn | Any | Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC), wrap head, seek help |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wipe the baby dry immediately** with a warm soft cloth.
- **Place the baby skin-to-skin** on the mother’s or father’s chest (Kangaroo Care).
- **Cover both with many blankets** and put a cap on the baby’s head.
- **Cover the feet with socks**.
- **Ensure the room is warm** (use a small heater or fire but far away).
- **Refer to the nearest Neonatal unit** as soon as possible.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Newborn baby cold and blue at night"] --> B["Wipe baby dry gently with a warm cloth"]
  B --> C["Place baby skin-to-skin on parent's chest"]
  C --> D["Put cap on head and socks on feet"]
  D --> E["Wrap parent and baby together in blankets"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC immediately for emergency care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue/Cold newborn | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Grunting/Slow breath | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Alert and feeding | Monitor temperature | KMC and warm room |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Newborns lose heat incredibly fast. Kangaroo Mother Care is the most effective life-saving tool in rural areas.

Query: A man drank a lot of local alcohol (Tharra) and fell asleep outside in the cold. He won't wake up and his skin is very cold. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconscious and won't wake up**
- **Alcohol smell** (alcohol masks cold symptoms)
- **Very cold skin**
- Shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Position on side, wrap in blankets, call 108 |
| Elderly | Any | High risk — immediate referral |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move him indoors gently**.
- **Place him on his side** (Recovery Position) so he doesn't choke if he vomits.
- **Remove any wet clothes** and wrap him in thick dry blankets.
- **Do NOT try to give him more alcohol** or even water if he is unconscious.
- **Monitor his breathing**; alcohol and cold together can stop breathing.
- **Call 108 immediately**; he needs a hospital to be safely warmed.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious person after alcohol and cold"] --> B["Move indoors and place on side (Recovery Position)"]
  B --> C["Wrap in thick blankets and check breathing"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything by mouth to swallow"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing and pulse constantly"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for immediate hospital transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Slow breathing | Emergency — Call 108 | Never |
| Awake but confused | Refer to PHC | Warmth and monitoring |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcohol dilates blood vessels, causing the body to lose heat even faster while making the person feel 'warm.' This is extremely dangerous.

Query: A woman is shivering and her face is swollen after being in the cold wind. She is very weak. What should I do for her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swollen face or hands** (Cold-induced swelling)
- **Shivering**
- **Extreme weakness**
- Pale or patchy skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Female | Warm room, dry blankets, warm sweet tea |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for heart strain, refer immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move her to a warm, wind-free room**.
- **Apply dry, warm towels** gently to her face (do not rub).
- **Give her warm tea or soup** with sugar or jaggery.
- **Wrap her in blankets** and cover her head.
- **Encourage her to rest** and not move around much.
- **Refer to the PHC** if the swelling does not go down after she warms up.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person shivering with swollen face from cold"] --> B["Move to warm, dry shelter and wrap in blankets"]
  B --> C["Apply warm (not hot) dry towels to the face"]
  C --> D["Give warm sweet tea or ginger water"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of energy and normal skin"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if swelling persists after 1 hour"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling persists >1 hour | Refer to PHC | Warmth and rest |
| Shortness of breath | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild shivering | If no change in 30 mins | Warm blankets and tea |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Face swelling can be a sign of a reaction to extreme cold. If her breathing changes, it is an emergency.

Query: A man is in the cold and says he is seeing things that aren't there. He is shivering and very pale. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Hallucinations** (seeing things)
- **Shivering**
- **Very pale skin**
- Confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Male | Calm the person, move to warmth, give jaggery/tea |
| Elderly | Any | High risk — immediate referral, monitor pulse |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Talk to him gently** and lead him away from the cold.
- **Provide a warm blanket** and cover his head.
- **Give him warm sweet tea or soup**; the energy will help his brain function.
- **Keep the room dimly lit and quiet** to help him calm down.
- **Check if he has a fever**, as cold plus infection can cause hallucinations.
- **Refer to PHC** immediately because his brain is under severe stress.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cold person having hallucinations and shivering"] --> B["Speak calmly and guide to a warm shelter"]
  B --> C["Wrap in blankets and give warm sweet tea"]
  C --> D["Stay with the person and monitor speech"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or injury"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for mental and thermal care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hallucinations | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion/Drowsiness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild shivering, alert | If not better in 30 mins | Warmth and food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hallucinations in the cold mean the brain is failing to maintain temperature. This must be treated as a severe emergency.

Query: A child was pulled out of the flood water. He is not breathing and his skin is blue. What should I do immediately?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Not breathing or gasping for air**
- **Blue or pale lips and skin**
- Unconscious and no response to shouting
- Cold skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Give 5 rescue breaths, then start 30 chest compressions |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Start chest compressions immediately, call for help |
| Infant (<1) | Any | Use two fingers for compressions, gentle puffs for breaths |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for response**: Tap the shoulders and shout.
- **Open airway**: Tilt the head back slightly and lift the chin.
- **Check breathing**: Look at the chest for 10 seconds.
- **Start CPR**: If not breathing, push hard and fast in the center of the chest.
- **Drain water**: If the person vomits, turn them onto their side immediately.
- **Keep warm**: Remove wet clothes and wrap in a dry blanket or saree once stable.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person pulled from flood water and unconscious"] --> B["Check for response and breathing for 10 seconds"]
  B --> C["If not breathing, start 30 chest compressions"]
  C --> D["Give 2 rescue breaths and continue CPR cycle"]
  D --> E["If person starts breathing, place on their side"]
  E --> F["Remove wet clothes and wrap in dry blankets"]
  F --> G["Transfer to hospital immediately for lung check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Was unconscious at any point | Always refer to hospital | Never treat at home |
| Coughing up frothy spit | Emergency — Call 108 | Not safe at home |
| Awake but swallowed water | Refer for 24-hour observation | Never wait at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even if the person seems fine after being pulled out, they can develop "secondary drowning" later. Always refer to a doctor.

Query: A farmer has a deep cut on his leg from a submerged tin sheet. The water is very dirty and the wound is full of mud. How do I clean it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Red streaks moving up the leg**
- **Pus or foul smell from the wound**
- High fever and shivering
- Severe swelling and heat around the cut

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Wash with boiled cooled water, apply Neem paste, refer for Tetanus |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High infection risk, clean and refer immediately |
| Child | Any | Clean gently, cover with clean cloth, seek PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the wound** under clean, running water (boiled and cooled is best) for 10 minutes.
- **Remove mud**: Use a clean cloth to gently dab away dirt. Do not scrub.
- **Natural Antiseptic**: Apply a paste of fresh Neem leaves or Turmeric (Haldi) to help fight germs.
- **Cover**: Wrap with a clean, dry cotton cloth or bandage.
- **Elevate**: Keep the injured leg raised to reduce swelling.
- **Tetanus**: Ensure the patient gets a Tetanus toxoid injection at the PHC within 24 hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wound caused by dirty flood debris"] --> B["Wash with boiled and cooled water for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply Turmeric or Neem paste to the area"]
  C --> D["Cover with a clean dry bandage or cloth"]
  D --> E["Elevate the limb to reduce swelling"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Tetanus shot and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wound is very deep/gaping | Refer for stitches | Never |
| Fever and chills | Emergency Referral | Not safe at home |
| Mild scrape, clean | Monitor at home after cleaning | Clean and apply Haldi |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Flood water contains sewage and animal waste. Any wound, no matter how small, can become life-threateningly infected.

Query: A man touched a fallen wire in the water and collapsed. He is still in the water. How do I save him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconscious and still near the wire**
- **Charred or burnt skin**
- No pulse or irregular heartbeat
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Disconnect power first, use dry wood to move him, check heart |
| All Ages | Any | NEVER touch the person with bare hands if they are in contact |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop!** Do not touch the person or the water until the power is off.
- **Separate**: Use a long, dry bamboo stick or dry wooden plank to move the wire away or pull the person out.
- **Check Breathing**: Once in a dry spot, check if they are breathing.
- **Start CPR**: If no pulse or breathing, start chest compressions immediately.
- **Cover Burns**: Use a dry, clean cloth to cover any visible burn marks.
- **Refer**: All electric shock victims must go to the hospital, even if they look fine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person shocked by wire in flood water"] --> B["Ensure power is off or use dry wood to separate"]
  B --> C["Move person to a dry, safe area"]
  C --> D["Check for pulse and breathing"]
```
