Fire injuries are common during seizures in rural areas. Never use local pastes on a fresh burn; they trap heat and worsen the damage.

Query: Someone has a fit and people are saying to make them smell a dirty shoe or onion. What is the ASHA's answer?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Struggling to breathe due to the smell
- Vomiting because of the smell

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Stop the smell; provide fresh air; explain the brain |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Shoe**: Dirty shoes and onions do NOT stop a seizure. They only block the person's airway.
- **Explain**: Tell the family, "The brain is like a machine that has short-circuited. It needs air to cool down, not a bad smell."
- **Circle of Air**: Make a wide circle around the person. Use a hand fan.
- **Time it**: Most seizures stop in 2-3 minutes regardless of what you smell.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Crowd using onions or dirty shoes to stop a fit"] --> B["Firmly ask them to stop and take the items away"]
  B --> C["Explain that the patient needs oxygen to recover"]
  C --> D["Ask the crowd to move back to allow air flow"]
  D --> E["Cushion the head and wait for the fit to end"]
  E --> F["Ensure person is on their side when fit stops"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fits that don't stop | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Confusion > 20 mins | Refer to PHC | Space and air |
| Regular known patient | Follow up with ASHA | Home rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Using irritants like onions can trigger vomiting or lung irritation. Fresh air is the best first aid.

Query: A child has a very high fever and has started shaking and rolling his eyes. Should I give him cold water to drink?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fever > 102°F
- Stiff neck (cannot touch chin to chest)
- Shaking lasts more than 5 minutes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Under 5) | Any | Tepid sponge; turn to side; **NO drinking** |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Drinking**: Never give liquids (water or medicine) while a child is shaking or unconscious. They will choke.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Wipe the child's body with a cloth soaked in **normal tap water** (not ice) to lower the fever.
- **Side Position**: Turn the child on their side so they don't choke on spit.
- **Refer**: Every child with a fever-fit (Febrile Seizure) needs to see a doctor immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with high fever begins to shake"] --> B["Lay child on their side on a soft surface"]
  B --> C["Wipe body with tap water to lower the heat"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put water or medicine in the mouth"]
  D --> E["Wait for shaking to stop and then rush to PHC"]
  E --> F["Check for stiff neck or vomiting during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Fit | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Stiff neck | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| High fever only | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponge |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A fever-fit can be a sign of 'Meningitis' (Brain Fever). It must be evaluated by a doctor to save the child's life.

Query: An old man had a fit and now one side of his face is drooping and he cannot speak clearly. Is this normal after a fit?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Face drooping on one side
- Weakness in one arm or leg
- Unable to speak or slurred speech

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Check for stroke; do not give pills; call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Stroke**: Use the FAST test (Face, Arm, Speech, Time). If any are wrong, it is a stroke.
- **Zero by Mouth**: Do not give water, tea, or BP medicine. Choking risk is very high.
- **Keep Lying Down**: Do not let him walk or sit up.
- **Refer Immediately**: A stroke requires hospital care within the first few hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person has a fit or collapses"] --> B["Check for face drooping or one-sided weakness"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any liquids or tablets by mouth"]
  C --> D["Note the exact time the symptoms started"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and transport to a District Hospital"]
  E --> F["Keep the person lying flat during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One-sided weakness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Quick recovery | Refer to PHC for ECG | Not safe to ignore |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In older people, a seizure can be the first sign of a stroke. Delaying hospital care can lead to permanent paralysis.

Query: A young girl is jerking but she is not unconscious. She is just shaking her right arm and smacking her lips. What is this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- The shaking spreads to the whole body
- She stops recognizing people
- Episode lasts more than 10 minutes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Youth | Any | Stay with her; guide her to sit; do not pull the arm |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stay Calm**: This is a 'Focal' fit. She is awake but the brain is misfiring.
- **Do Not Pull**: Do not try to hold the shaking arm still. You may dislocate her shoulder.
- **Guide to Sit**: Gently help her sit on the floor so she doesn't fall.
- **Note the Behavior**: Note if she smacks her lips or plucks at her clothes. Tell the doctor this.
- **Refer**: She needs a brain checkup at the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person is jerking one limb while still awake"] --> B["Gently guide the person to sit or lie down"]
  B --> C["Do NOT use force to stop the limb from shaking"]
  C --> D["Note the time and what the movements look like"]
  D --> E["Wait for the movements to stop naturally"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC or Neurologist for evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lip smacking/Staring | Refer to PHC | Guide to safety |
| Spreads to whole body | Refer to PHC | Standard first aid |
| Frequent episodes | Refer to Specialist | Monitor at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Partial seizures are often missed or called 'acting out'. They are medical events that need medication to prevent bigger fits.

Query: A man had a seizure and bit his tongue. There is a lot of blood in his mouth. Should I stitch it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Heavy bleeding that doesn't stop with pressure
- Large piece of tongue is hanging or cut off
- Difficulty breathing due to blood

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Press with clean cloth; turn on side; refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Side Position**: Turn him on his side immediately so the blood drains out of his mouth and not into his lungs.
- **Pressure**: Once shaking stops, use a clean cloth to press gently on the tongue to stop bleeding.
- **No Stitches at Home**: Never try to stitch or use home powders (like turmeric or lime) inside the mouth.
- **Cold Water**: If he is awake, he can rinse with cold water.
- **Refer**: Tongue bites can get infected easily.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person bites tongue and bleeds during a fit"] --> B["Turn the person on their side to drain blood"]
  B --> C["Wait for shaking to stop before touching the mouth"]
  C --> D["Apply pressure to the bite with a clean cloth"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any powders or pastes to the wound"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for wound cleaning and tetanus"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deep cut on tongue | Refer to PHC | Pressure with cloth |
| Bleeding stops | Refer to PHC | Cold water rinse |
| Swelling of tongue | Emergency - PHC | Side position |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Choking on blood is a major risk during a seizure. Always prioritize the side-lying position over stopping the bleeding.

Query: A child has a fit every time he gets angry or cries. The parents are beating him to make him stop. How do I help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Turning blue while crying
- Passing out after a crying spell

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Under 3) | Any | Stop the beating; stay calm; refer to Pediatrician |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **STOP the Beating**: Violence makes the child more upset and worsens the spell. It is a medical issue, not bad behavior.
- **Calm Down**: This is often a 'Breath Holding Spell'. Stay calm and the child will wake up in 30 seconds.
- **Safety**: Lay the child flat on the floor so they don't hit their head.
- **Refer**: The child needs to be checked for anemia (low iron), which often causes these spells.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child passes out or jerks after crying/anger"] --> B["Tell parents to stop hitting or shouting at child"]
  B --> C["Lay the child flat on the floor to prevent injury"]
  C --> D["Wait for the child to start breathing and wake up"]
  D --> E["Provide reassurance and do not show panic"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC to check for low iron (anemia)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue color during cry | Refer to PHC | Lay flat; stay calm |
| Jerking after crying | Refer to PHC | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Breath-holding spells are scary but usually harmless. However, beating the child is child abuse and can cause permanent brain injury.

Query: A man is jerking and foam is coming from his mouth. People are pouring water on his face to wake him. Is this correct?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Gasping for air
- Foam is tinged with blood
- Person is not waking up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | STOP the water; wipe foam; turn to side |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **STOP the Water**: Pouring water on the face or mouth of an unconscious person causes them to inhale water into their lungs. This causes pneumonia.
- **Wipe the Foam**: Use a clean cloth to wipe the foam from the *outside* of the mouth.
- **Side Position**: Turn the person on their side so the foam/spit can flow out safely.
- **Wait**: The fit will stop when the brain is ready. Water will not speed it up.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Foam at mouth during a violent seizure"] --> B["Stop anyone from pouring water on the person"]
  B --> C["Wipe foam from the lips with a clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Turn the whole body onto the side (recovery position)"]
  D --> E["Ensure the mouth is pointed slightly downward"]
  E --> F["Wait for shaking to stop and person to breathe"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Water inhaled/Coughing | Emergency - PHC | Side position |
| Persistent foaming | Refer to PHC | Wipe mouth |
| Alert after fit | Home care is okay | Let them rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Foaming is just saliva being mixed with air by the shaking jaw. It is not poisonous and does not need to be 'washed away' while the person is unconscious.

Query: A woman had a fit and passed urine in her clothes. The family is very ashamed and won't take her to the hospital. How to counsel?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Shame leads to hiding the patient
- Skipping medicines to hide the disease

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Counsel privacy; explain medical cause; refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Medical Fact**: Explain that passing urine is a normal medical sign that the brain lost control for a moment. It is like a sneeze—you can't help it.
- **Privacy**: Help the woman change in a private room. Cover her with a dry shawl.
- **Dignity**: Tell the family, "This is a disease of the nerves, not a matter of family honor."
- **Refer**: She needs regular medicine so she doesn't have fits in public again.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person passes urine during a seizure"] --> B["Cover them immediately with a shawl or sheet"]
  B --> C["Help them to a private room once shaking stops"]
  C --> D["Explain that this is a normal part of the illness"]
  D --> E["Encourage family to continue medical treatment"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for daily epilepsy medication"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| First-time wetting | Refer to PHC | Private change |
| Known patient | Visit PHC for meds | Keep clean and dry |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stigma kills more people than seizures. Hiding the patient leads to missed doses and more dangerous, prolonged fits.

Query: A man is having fits every 10 minutes and he is not waking up between them. Is this a 'big' fit?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Status Epilepticus**: Multiple fits without waking up
- Body temperature rising very high
- Face turning dark blue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | **Emergency** - Call 108; keep airway clear; do not wait |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **This is a Life-Threatening Emergency**: Brain damage starts if the fit doesn't stop.
- **Clear Airway**: Keep him on his side. Wipe away foam.
- **Cooling**: If his body is very hot from the constant shaking, use a damp cloth to cool him down.
- **Transport**: Do not wait for the fit to 'finish'. Start moving him to the hospital immediately.
- **Medical History**: Grab his medicine strip to show the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Multiple seizures without waking up in between"] --> B["Consider this a life-threatening brain emergency"]
  B --> C["Keep the patient on their side to prevent choking"]
  C --> D["Gather all current medicine strips and records"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and transport in an ambulance immediately"]
  E --> F["Do NOT delay transport for any home treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Continuous fits | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Not waking up | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Very high body heat | Emergency - Immediate | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Status Epilepticus is a medical emergency that home remedies cannot fix. Only intravenous (IV) medication can stop these seizures.

Query: A child has started jerking only on the right side of his body. Is this also a seizure?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- One side of the body stops moving after fit
- Confusion after the jerking
- Headache or vomiting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Keep safe; do not restrain; refer to Specialist |
| Adult | Any | Check for history of head injury |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Yes, it is a 'Focal' Seizure**: Any rhythmic jerking of one part of the body is a seizure.
- **Do Not Pull the Limb**: Do not try to hold the arm or leg still; you could cause a dislocation.
- **Observe**: Note if the jerking spreads to the rest of the body.
- **Check for Weakness**: After it stops, see if the right side is weaker than the left.
- **Refer**: This requires a brain scan (CT/MRI) to see why only one side is affected.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Jerking starts in only one part of the body"] --> B["Do NOT try to hold the shaking limb still"]
  B --> C["Note which part of the body started jerking first"]
  C --> D["Check if the jerking spreads to other parts"]
  D --> E["Monitor for weakness in that limb after it stops"]
  E --> F["Refer to a Pediatrician or Neurologist at the CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One-sided jerking | Refer to Specialist | Not safe at home |
| Post-fit weakness | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Spreads to whole body | Refer to PHC | Standard first aid |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
One-sided seizures often point to a specific spot in the brain that needs checking by a doctor. It is not just 'weakness' or a muscle twitch.

Query: Should I give a person salt or lemon during a fit to make them wake up faster?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Coughing after salt/lemon is given
- Vomiting and choking
- Breathing becomes fast and noisy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT give salt/lemon; turn on side; wait for natural recovery |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop All Feeding**: Salt, lemon, or water will go into the windpipe during a fit.
- **Brain vs Stomach**: A seizure is in the brain; nothing you put in the stomach will stop it.
- **Let them Recover**: The brain will wake up when it is ready. You cannot 'force' it awake.
- **Side Position**: Keep them on their side to allow saliva to drain.
- **Reassure**: When they wake, tell them they had a fit and are safe now.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Family wants to use salt/lemon to wake the patient"] --> B["Stop them and explain the risk of choking/pneumonia"]
  B --> C["Ensure the patient is in the recovery side position"]
  C --> D["Stay with the person until they wake naturally"]
  D --> E["Wipe the mouth of any excess saliva or foam"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if they are still drowsy after 30 mins"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Choked on salt/lemon | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Drowsy for 20 mins | Monitor closely | Let them sleep |
| Fully alert | Visit PHC tomorrow | Home rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Forcing anything into the mouth during a seizure is the #1 cause of 'Aspiration Pneumonia' (lung infection), which can be more deadly than the seizure itself.

Query: A boy had a fit and now he is crying and saying he is very ashamed. How should I counsel him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Thoughts of self-harm
- Refusing to take medication
- Withdrawing from school or work

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Youth | Any | Reassure it is a medical condition; encourage normal life |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Medical, Not Moral**: Tell him a fit is like a cough or a fever; it is not his fault and not a sin.
- **School/Work**: Encourage him to continue his activities but avoid swimming alone or heights.
- **Medication**: Explain that taking the pill every day will keep the fits away so he can live normally.
- **Family Support**: Tell the family not to hide him in a room. Social life is important for brain health.
- **Refer**: If he is very depressed, take him to the PHC counselor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient feels shame or depression after a seizure"] --> B["Explain that epilepsy is a treatable brain condition"]
  B --> C["Encourage the patient to resume school or work safely"]
  C --> D["Advise against swimming or climbing until fits stop"]
  D --> E["Stress the importance of taking daily medicine"]
  E --> F["Refer to a counselor if the person is very sad/withdrawn"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Suicidal thoughts | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Not taking meds | Refer to ASHA/PHC | Supervise daily pill |
| Feeling shy | Home counseling | Reassurance |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stigma is the biggest burden for epilepsy patients in India. Education and regular medication allow most people to lead completely normal lives.

Query: What if the person is wearing tight clothes like a necktie or a tight blouse during a seizure?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Face turning red or blue
- Veins in the neck bulging
- Struggling to take air in

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Loosen all tight clothing immediately; do not pull hard |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Loosen the Neck**: Open the top buttons of the shirt or loosen the tie/dupatta.
- **Waistband**: Loosen the belt or drawstring of the salwar/petticoat.
- **Remove Glasses**: Take off spectacles so they don't break and hurt the eyes.
- **Do Not Strip**: Just loosen what is tight. Maintain the person's dignity.
- **Check Breathing**: Ensure nothing is restricting the chest from expanding.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Seizure starts while wearing tight clothing/accessories"] --> B["Gently loosen buttons and ties around the neck"]
  B --> C["Loosen the belt or waistband to allow easy breathing"]
  C --> D["Remove glasses and keep them in a safe place"]
  D --> E["Ensure the chest and stomach can move freely"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if breathing stays difficult after fit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Noisy breathing | Refer for checkup | Loosen clothes |
| Quick recovery | Home care is okay | Rest |
| Blue face | Emergency - Call 108 | Oxygen needed |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Tight clothing can restrict the airway during the heavy breathing phase of a seizure. Always ensure the patient has 'room to breathe'.

Query: A person has a fit and passes urine in their clothes. Should I change them immediately while they are shaking?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Persistent unconsciousness
- Cold skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Wait for fit to stop; cover with a dry cloth; maintain dignity |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wait**: Do not try to undress or move a person while they are violently shaking; you may cause injury.
- **Dignity**: Cover the person with a shawl or bedsheet to hide the wetness from the crowd.
- **Post-Fit Care**: Once they are awake and aware, then help them change into dry clothes.
- **Check for Injury**: Often, passing urine means it was a true 'Grand Mal' seizure.
- **Refer**: If this is a new symptom in their fits, let the doctor know.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient passes urine or feces during a seizure"] --> B["Do NOT try to change their clothes during the shaking"]
  B --> C["Cover the person with a sheet or shawl for privacy"]
  C --> D["Wait for the shaking to stop and the person to wake"]
  D --> E["Help them change in a private area once they are alert"]
  E --> F["Inform the doctor about the loss of bladder control"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Loss of bladder control | Refer to PHC | Standard first aid |
| Known epilepsy | Visit doctor | Home rest |
| New symptom | Refer to Specialist | Record it |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Involuntary urination is a common and normal part of a major seizure. It is not a sign of 'weakness' and should be handled with privacy and respect.

Query: A young man was working in the fields under the hot sun and suddenly fell down. He is not talking or opening his eyes. What should I do first?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- No breathing or gasping for air
- Very high body temperature (feels hot to touch)
- Seizures or jerking of limbs
- Bleeding from the head or ears
- Not waking up after 1 minute

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Check breathing, lay on side, keep cool |
| Adult | Any | Move to shade, lift legs 12 inches, loosen belt |
| Elderly | Any | Check pulse, immediate referral to CHC |
| Pregnant | Female | Lay on LEFT side only, call 108 immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Breathing**: Listen for breath and watch the chest. If not breathing, start CPR.
- **Move to Shade**: Carry the person to a cool, airy spot immediately.
- **Lift the Legs**: Prop the feet up on a bag or rolled saree to help blood reach the brain.
- **Cool the Body**: Pour normal tap water on the skin and fan the person vigorously.
- **No Water by Mouth**: Do NOT pour water into the mouth of an unconscious person; they will choke.
- **Smell Onions?**: Do NOT use onions or shoes. It blocks fresh air.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person falls and is unresponsive"] --> B["Check for breathing and pulse"]
  B --> C["Move person to a cool shaded area with good air flow"]
  C --> D["Loosen tight clothes and lift legs 12 inches high"]
  D --> E["Wipe body with wet cloth and fan them"]
  E --> F["Do NOT give any liquids or food by mouth"]
  F --> G["Call 108 if they do not wake up within 2 minutes"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Faints and wakes up in 1 min | Visit PHC today | Rest in shade, drink ORS |
| Remains unconscious > 2 mins | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| High fever + Unconscious | Emergency - CHC | Tepid sponging on way |
| Head injury from fall | Refer to CHC | Clean wound |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Unconsciousness can be due to heat, low sugar, or heart issues. Never leave an unconscious person alone. If breathing stops, start chest compressions immediately.

Query: A woman has fainted in the temple. People are saying a ghost touched her. She is breathing but her hands are cold. How to help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pale or blue skin
- Weak or very fast pulse
- Vomiting while unconscious
- Confusion after waking up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Loosen saree/blouse, lift legs, sprinkle water on face |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Clear the Crowd**: Ask people to move back. She needs fresh air, not 'mantras'.
- **Check Airway**: Ensure her neck is not bent. Tilt the head back slightly.
- **Lift Legs**: Use a bench or chair to keep her legs higher than her heart.
- **Wait**: Most simple faints resolve in 30-60 seconds once the person is lying flat.
- **Recovery Position**: If she doesn't wake but is breathing, turn her onto her side.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Woman faints in a crowded place"] --> B["Ask the crowd to step back and provide air"]
  B --> C["Lay her flat on her back and lift her legs"]
  C --> D["Loosen tight clothing around neck and waist"]
  D --> E["Sprinkle a little water on the face and neck"]
  E --> F["Wait for 1 minute for her to regain consciousness"]
  F --> G["If she remains asleep, turn her to the side and call 108"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wakes up alert | Home rest and water | ORS or Lemon water |
| Repeated fainting | Refer to PHC for blood test | Keep lying down |
| Chest pain before fainting | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting is often due to low blood pressure or lack of air. It is a physical condition, not spiritual. Always check for breathing first.

Query: An old man who has sugar (diabetes) has become 'thanda' (cold) and is not waking up. He was sweating a lot before this. What do I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Cold, clammy skin
- Rapid, shallow breathing
- Seizure-like movements

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Apply sugar paste inside cheek ONLY if breathing; rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sugar Paste**: If he is semi-conscious, rub a thick paste of sugar or honey on the **inside of his cheek**. Do NOT pour sugar water down the throat.
- **Keep Warm**: Cover him with a blanket if he feels cold.
- **Left Side**: Lay him on his side to prevent choking.
- **Check Pulse**: If no pulse is felt at the neck, start chest compressions.
- **Emergency Move**: This is likely low sugar. He needs a glucose bottle at the hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Diabetic patient found cold and unconscious"] --> B["Rub sugar paste or honey inside the cheek gently"]
  B --> C["Lay them on their side in recovery position"]
  C --> D["Do NOT force them to drink any liquid"]
  D --> E["Cover with a blanket and check for breathing"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cold and unresponsive | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Shaking but awake | Give sweet tea/biscuit | Monitor at home |
| No breathing | Emergency - CPR and 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Low blood sugar (Hypoglycemia) kills faster than high blood sugar. Every minute counts. Get them to a doctor even if they wake up after the sugar paste.

Query: A child fell from a tree and is now unconscious. He has a bump on his head. Should I pour water on him to wake him up?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fluid or blood from nose or ears
- Unequal size of eye pupils
- Vomiting more than once
- Weakness in arms or legs

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Keep neck still, do NOT move him much, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Move the Neck**: If he fell, his neck bone might be broken. Keep his head and neck completely still.
- **No Water**: Do not pour water on the face. If he is unconscious, he might breathe it into his lungs.
- **Check Breathing**: If he is breathing, stay with him and wait for help.
- **Stop Bleeding**: If there is a wound, press a clean cloth on it gently.
- **Immediate Referral**: Head injuries in children are very dangerous.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child unconscious after a fall from height"] --> B["Keep the head and neck aligned and still"]
  B --> C["Do NOT pour water on the face or shake the child"]
  C --> D["Check for breathing and pulse every minute"]
  D --> E["Cover with a blanket to keep body warm"]
  E --> F["Transport on a flat hard board to the hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any fall + Unconscious | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Bleeding from ear/nose | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Bump on head, but alert | Refer to PHC | Observation |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Moving a person with a neck injury can cause permanent paralysis. Use a wooden plank or a flat surface to carry the child if help is far.

Query: A man was found unconscious in a closed room where a coal fire (angithi) was burning. He is breathing very slowly. What first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Cherry red lips or skin
- Gasping or very slow breath
- Unconscious and cannot be woken

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Move to open air, check breath, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Fresh Air**: Drag the person out of the room into the open air immediately. Do not stay inside yourself.
- **Loosen Clothing**: Open the shirt to help the chest expand easily.
- **Check Airway**: Make sure his tongue is not blocking his throat.
- **Recovery Position**: Lay him on his side once in the open air.
- **Oxygen Needed**: He needs hospital oxygen. Do not try home remedies like milk or water.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person found in room with coal fire/smoke"] --> B["Move the person to fresh open air immediately"]
  B --> C["Check if they are breathing or gasping"]
  C --> D["Loosen all tight clothing around the neck"]
  D --> E["Turn onto their side in recovery position"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for emergency oxygen at the hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Smoke inhalation | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Slow breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Fresh air |
| Headache but awake | Refer to PHC | Fresh air |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Coal smoke has a poison (Carbon Monoxide) that you cannot see or smell. It makes the blood unable to carry oxygen. Only hospital treatment works.

Query: A girl fainted while standing in the school assembly. She is now awake but dizzy. What should I give her to eat?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Second fainting spell in the same day
- Pale tongue and nails (Anemia signs)
- Fast heartbeat even while resting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Youth | Female | Do NOT give food yet, give ORS or sugar-salt water |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Lying Down**: Don't let her sit up too fast. Keep her flat for 10 minutes.
- **Hydration**: Give her ORS or a mix of sugar and a pinch of salt in water.
- **Cool Down**: Move her to a fan or shade. Use a hand fan if no electricity.
- **No Heavy Food**: Do not give rotis or heavy snacks. It might make her vomit.
- **Refer**: If she faints often, she needs a blood test for iron (Hb check).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Student faints during assembly or standing"] --> B["Lay her flat and lift legs higher than heart"]
  B --> C["Move her to a cool, shaded place with a fan"]
  C --> D["Once fully awake, give small sips of ORS water"]
  D --> E["Keep her lying down for at least 15 minutes"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for hemoglobin and BP check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness stops | Visit PHC this week | ORS and rest |
| Faints again today | Refer to PHC today | Keep lying down |
| Pale skin/weakness | Refer for Hb test | Iron-rich food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting in young girls is common due to heat or anemia. Ensure she has had breakfast before coming to school.

Query: A woman who is 7 months pregnant has fainted in the field. How should I lay her down?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bleeding from the vagina
- Swelling on the face or hands
- Seizure (jerking movements)
- Severe headache after waking

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | **Left side only**, lift left leg slightly, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Left Side Position**: Never lay a pregnant woman flat on her back. It stops blood flow to the baby. Lay her on her **LEFT side**.
- **Lift the Leg**: You can place a small pillow or rolled cloth under her left leg.
- **Fresh Air**: Fan her and loosen her saree.
- **Emergency Referral**: Fainting in late pregnancy can be 'Eclampsia' or blood pressure issues. It is an emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman faints or loses consciousness"] --> B["Turn her onto her LEFT side immediately"]
  B --> C["Ensure she is not lying flat on her back"]
  C --> D["Loosen the saree and fan her for fresh air"]
  D --> E["Check for any bleeding or high fever"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to a hospital with maternity care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any faint in pregnancy | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Headache or blurry vision | Emergency - Refer now | Left side position |
| Swelling on face | Emergency - Refer now | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A faint in pregnancy is a sign that the mother or baby could be in danger. Do not wait for her to 'feel better' at home.

Query: An old lady fainted and her pulse is very slow. She was complaining of chest pain earlier. Is it a heart attack?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain in chest, left arm, or jaw
- Heavy sweating while cold
- Difficulty breathing
- Irregular or very slow pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Female | Do NOT walk her, keep lying down, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Absolute Rest**: Do not allow her to sit up, walk, or talk. Any movement strains the heart.
- **Loosen Clothes**: Ensure her neck and chest are free of tight clothes.
- **Position**: Keep her flat. If she is struggling to breathe, you can lift her upper body slightly with a pillow.
- **Check Pulse**: If the pulse stops, start CPR (chest compressions) immediately.
- **Immediate Referral**: She needs an ECG and oxygen at a hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person faints with chest pain history"] --> B["Lay them flat and ensure absolute rest"]
  B --> C["Loosen saree or shirt and provide fresh air"]
  C --> D["Monitor pulse and breathing every minute"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any food, water, or medicine"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately in a lying position"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest pain + Faint | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Sweating + Slow pulse | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Awake but weak | Refer to CHC | Absolute rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heart attacks in women and elderly can look like simple fainting or 'gas pain'. Never ignore a faint accompanied by sweating or chest discomfort.

Query: A man fainted and then had a small fit (seizure). He is now unconscious but breathing. Should I put a key in his hand?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Multiple fits without waking up
- Tongue bite bleeding
- Foam at the mouth
- Blue lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Side position, protect head, **NO keys** |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Keys/Metal**: Iron keys do NOT stop seizures. It is a dangerous myth. It can hurt the person.
- **Recovery Position**: Turn him on his side (Recovery Position). This prevents his tongue from blocking his breath.
- **Cushion the Head**: Place a folded cloth under his head.
- **Wipe Foam**: Gently wipe any foam or spit from the outside of his mouth.
- **Time it**: Note how long he was unconscious. If it's more than 5 minutes, call 108.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person is unconscious after a fit/seizure"] --> B["Turn them onto their side in recovery position"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put anything (keys, spoons) in their mouth/hand"]
  C --> D["Wipe foam from the outside of the mouth"]
  D --> E["Stay with them until they are fully awake"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC once they can speak and walk"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| First time seizure | Refer to PHC today | Recovery position |
| Fit lasts > 5 mins | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Wakes up confused | Refer to PHC | Keep in quiet room |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Most faints followed by a fit need a doctor's checkup to find the cause in the brain. Never use strong smells like shoes or onions.

Query: A worker fell into a deep pit/well and is now unconscious at the bottom. How should we pull him out?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Visible broken bones
- Not breathing
- Multiple people fainting in the same pit

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT enter without a mask; pull out horizontally; call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Gas**: Deep pits can have poisonous gas. Do not jump in without air or a rope.
- **Keep Body Flat**: When pulling him out, try to keep his back and neck straight. Use a board if possible.
- **Fresh Air**: Once out, lay him in an open area on his side.
- **Check for Injuries**: Look for bleeding or odd-angled limbs.
- **No Liquids**: Do not try to make him drink water to 'wake up'.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person found unconscious in a pit or well"] --> B["Assess for poisonous gas before entering"]
  B --> C["Pull the person out keeping the spine flat"]
  C --> D["Move to open air and check for breathing"]
  D --> E["Turn to side position if breathing"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to the nearest hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious in pit | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Fresh air |
| Alert but has pain | Refer to CHC for X-ray | Keep still |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Gas in wells (like Carbon Dioxide or Methane) can kill in seconds. Safety of the rescuer is the first priority. Use a wet cloth over your nose if you must enter.

Query: A child was playing and suddenly stopped and fell. He is not breathing and his heart is not beating. What is the ASHA's action?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- No chest movement (not breathing)
- No pulse at the neck
- Lips turning blue or black

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Emergency CPR**: Push on chest, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Call 108**: Tell someone to call the ambulance immediately.
- **Chest Compressions**: Place your hands in the center of the chest. Push hard and fast (100-120 times a minute). Push at least 2 inches deep.
- **Keep Going**: Do not stop until the child wakes up, help arrives, or you are too tired to continue.
- **Do Not Shake**: Shaking the child will not help. Only heart compressions can save him.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child is unconscious and not breathing"] --> B["Check for pulse and breath for 5 seconds"]
  B --> C["Start chest compressions (push hard and fast)"]
  C --> D["Tell another person to call 108 immediately"]
  D --> E["Continue compressions without stopping"]
  E --> F["Stop only if child moves or ambulance arrives"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Not breathing | Emergency - CPR now | Never |
| No pulse | Emergency - CPR now | Never |
| Gasping breath | Emergency - CPR now | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
CPR (Chest Compressions) is the only way to keep the brain alive when the heart stops. Do not waste time with water or rituals.

Query: A woman has fainted from extreme pain in her stomach. She is very pale and her hands are cold. What should I check?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Severe stomach pain before fainting
- Cold, sweaty skin (Shock signs)
- Missed period (Pregnancy risk)
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Lift legs, keep warm, call 108, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Bleeding**: Ask the family about her last period. This could be internal bleeding (Ectopic pregnancy).
- **Shock Position**: Lay her flat and lift her legs 12 inches high with pillows.
- **Keep Warm**: Cover her with a thick blanket.
- **Zero by Mouth**: Do not give water or painkillers. She may need surgery.
- **Fast Referral**: This is an internal emergency. Home care is not possible.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Woman faints with severe stomach pain"] --> B["Lay her flat and lift legs 12 inches high"]
  B --> C["Cover her with a blanket to prevent cold"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any water or medicine by mouth"]
  D --> E["Check pulse and breathing continuously"]
  E --> F["Rush to a hospital with surgery facility immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain + Faint | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Cold skin/Pale | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Missed period + Pain | Emergency - Immediate | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal bleeding can look like a simple faint but is a surgical emergency. Cold skin and pale face are signs the body is losing blood or in shock.

Query: A man fainted after being bitten by something. There is a small mark on his leg. He is drooling and his eyes are half-closed. What is the priority?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drooling or foaming at the mouth
- Difficulty breathing or swallowing
- Drooping eyelids
- Convulsions (jerking)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Lay on side, keep limb still, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Recovery Position**: Lay him on his side. This keeps his airway clear if he vomits or drools.
- **Do NOT Cut or Suck**: Do not use a blade on the bite mark or try to suck out poison.
- **Keep the Limb Still**: If it was a snake, moving the leg spreads the venom. Tie a loose bandage but do not stop blood flow.
- **Immediate Referral**: He needs anti-venom at the CHC. Don't go to local healers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious person with a bite mark"] --> B["Place in side-lying recovery position"]
  B --> C["Keep the bitten limb still and below heart level"]
  C --> D["Do NOT cut, suck, or tie a tight tourniquet"]
  D --> E["Wipe drool/foam from the mouth gently"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest CHC with anti-venom immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bite + Unconscious | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Drooping eyelids | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Poison from snakes or scorpions can stop breathing. The side position and fast transport to a hospital are the only things that save lives.

Query: A man fell from his cycle. He is unconscious and snoring. People are trying to give him water. How should I stop them?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Snoring or 'gurgling' sound (Airway block)
- Blood from head or nose
- Limbs at odd angles

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Stop water, turn head/body to side, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Water**: Explain to the crowd: "If you give water to an unconscious man, it goes into his lungs, and he will die from choking."
- **Clear the Airway**: The 'snoring' is his tongue blocking his throat. Turn his body as a whole to the side.
- **Check Head**: If there is a deep cut, press it with a clean cloth.
- **Wait for Help**: Do not move him more than once. Keep him on his side until help arrives.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person unconscious and snoring after a fall"] --> B["Prevent anyone from giving water or food"]
  B --> C["Turn the whole body onto the side (log-roll)"]
  C --> D["Ensure the head is tilted slightly to let spit out"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing and pulse every 2 minutes"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for head injury transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Snoring/Gurgling | Emergency - Immediate | Side position |
| Head injury + Unconscious | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Snoring stops, wakes up | Refer to PHC | Observation |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Snoring in an unconscious person is a sign of a blocked airway. Never give liquids to someone who is not fully alert.

Query: An ASHA worker finds an infant who is not moving and is 'neela' (blue). The mother is crying. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Blue or gray skin/lips
- No movement or crying
- Limp body (like a rag doll)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1 yr) | Any | **Emergency CPR**: Push with 2 fingers, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Response**: Tap the bottom of the baby's foot. If no cry, start help.
- **Position**: Lay the baby on a firm flat surface.
- **Two-Finger Push**: Use two fingers in the center of the chest (just below the nipples). Push 1.5 inches deep, 100 times a minute.
- **Gentle Breaths**: If you can, give two tiny puffs of air into the nose and mouth together.
- **Keep Warm**: During transport, wrap the baby in a dry cloth but keep chest free for compressions.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant is limp, blue, and not crying"] --> B["Tap the foot to check for any response"]
  B --> C["If no response, start chest pushes with 2 fingers"]
  C --> D["Pushes should be fast and firm on the chest center"]
  D --> E["Wrap the rest of the body to keep it warm"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital while continuing pushes"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue/Limp infant | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| No breathing | Emergency - Immediate | CPR on the way |
| Gasping/Weak cry | Emergency - Refer now | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infants can stop breathing due to cold, infection, or choking. Quick chest compressions with two fingers are vital to save the baby's life.

Query: A man fainted and he smells of alcohol. People are laughing and saying 'let him sleep'. Should I ignore him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Cannot be woken by a loud shout
- Breathing is very slow (less than 8 times a minute)
- Skin is cold and blue
- Vomiting while unconscious

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Lay on side, check breathing, do NOT leave alone |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Ignore**: Alcohol poisoning or an injury while drunk can be fatal. He is not 'just sleeping'.
- **Side Position**: This is critical. If he vomits while on his back, he will die from choking. Turn him on his side.
- **Check for Injury**: Look for head bumps or blood; he may have fallen.
- **Check Breathing**: If he is breathing very slowly, he needs a hospital.
- **Stay with Him**: Monitor him until he can speak clearly.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person unconscious with smell of alcohol"] --> B["Immediately turn them onto their side"]
  B --> C["Check for any head injuries or bleeding from fall"]
  C --> D["Count how many breaths they take in 1 minute"]
  D --> E["If breathing is slow or blue skin, call 108"]
  E --> F["Do NOT leave them alone until they are awake"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Slow breathing (<8/min) | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Vomiting while asleep | Refer to PHC | Side position |
| Awake but confused | Stay with him | Home rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcohol can hide serious problems like a brain bleed or low sugar. An unconscious person, even if drunk, is a medical patient.

Query: A lady fainted after seeing blood. She is now awake but her heart is racing and she is breathing fast. What home remedy?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fainting again after sitting up
- Numbness in hands or feet
- Chest tightness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Calm her down, slow breathing, give water |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Slow Breathing**: Ask her to breathe in through her nose and out through her mouth slowly. "Breathe with me."
- **Lie Flat**: Keep her lying down until her heart feels normal. Do not rush her to stand.
- **Small Sips**: Give her a little water or sugar water once she is fully alert.
- **Reassurance**: Tell her she is safe and the fainting is over.
- **No Strong Smells**: Do not use smelling salts or ammonia.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person wakes up anxious/fast heart after faint"] --> B["Keep them lying flat on their back"]
  B --> C["Instruct them to take slow, deep breaths"]
  C --> D["Reassure them and talk in a calm voice"]
  D --> E["Give small sips of water once fully alert"]
  E --> F["If heart stays fast for 20 mins, refer to PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Anxiety/Fast breath | Home care - Calming | Slow breathing |
| Heart racing > 20 mins | Refer to PHC | Quiet rest |
| Alert and feels okay | Home rest | Reassurance |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is often a 'Vasovagal' faint. It is harmless but requires the person to lie flat to allow the heart and brain to sync again.

Query: An old man fainted while passing urine. He is now conscious but very weak. What is the ASHA's advice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fainting while coughing or urinating
- History of heart disease
- Dizziness when standing up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Male | Check BP, keep hydrated, refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit with Support**: When he needs to use the toilet next, someone should stay with him or he should sit down to urinate.
- **Hydration**: Ensure he is drinking enough water throughout the day.
- **Slow Movements**: Tell him to sit on the bed for 2 minutes before standing up.
- **Check BP**: If you have a BP machine, check it while he is lying and then standing.
- **Refer**: He needs to be checked for prostate issues or heart rhythm problems.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person faints during urination/coughing"] --> B["Ensure they are lying flat and resting"]
  B --> C["Give water or ORS to improve hydration"]
  C --> D["Advise them to sit while urinating in the future"]
  D --> E["Encourage slow standing from a sleeping position"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for blood pressure and heart check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Faints during strain | Refer to PHC this week | Sit while urinating |
| Low BP when standing | Refer to PHC | Drink more fluids |
| Chest pain + Faint | Emergency - Immediate | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting while straining (Syncope) can be a sign of underlying health issues in the elderly. It should always be investigated by a doctor.

Query: A child was found unconscious near a pesticide bottle in the field. He is breathing but very sweaty. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pinpoint (very small) eye pupils
- Excessive drooling or sweating
- Vomiting and diarrhea
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash skin, turn on side, rush to CHC with bottle |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove Poison**: If his clothes have pesticide, remove them and wash his skin with soap and water immediately.
- **Recovery Position**: Lay him on his side so he doesn't choke on drool or vomit.
- **Do NOT Induce Vomit**: Never try to make an unconscious person vomit. It will go into the lungs.
- **Save the Bottle**: Take the pesticide bottle or label to the hospital so the doctor knows the antidote.
- **Call 108**: This is a severe poisoning emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious child found near pesticide/poison"] --> B["Remove contaminated clothes and wash skin"]
  B --> C["Place child on their side in recovery position"]
  C --> D["Do NOT try to make the child vomit"]
  D --> E["Identify the poison and take the bottle with you"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Poisoning + Unconscious | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Excessive drooling | Emergency - Immediate | Side position |
| Small pupils | Emergency - Immediate | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pesticide poisoning is very common and very deadly. Fast washing of the skin and fast transport to a hospital are the only ways to save the child.

Query: A man fainted and his mouth is twisted to one side. He cannot move his right arm. What is the first thing to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Twisted or drooping face
- One-sided weakness (arm or leg)
- Slurred or no speech
- Severe headache

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT give water, keep flat, call 108 immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check the Time**: Note exactly what time he fainted or the face twisted. This is vital for the doctor.
- **No Liquids**: Stroke patients cannot swallow safely. Water will cause choking.
- **Keep Calm**: Speak to him. Even if he can't talk, he might hear you.
- **Lie Flat**: Keep him lying on his back or slightly on his side if he is drooling.
- **Rush to CHC**: A stroke needs hospital care within 3-4 hours to prevent permanent damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious or weak with twisted face (Stroke)"] --> B["Note the exact time symptoms started"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food, water, or aspirin"]
  C --> D["Keep the person lying flat and still"]
  D --> E["Call 108 or arrange fastest transport to CHC"]
  E --> F["Monitor breathing until medical help arrives"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Face droop/Weakness | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Sudden severe headache | Emergency - Immediate | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A stroke is a brain attack. Every minute lost is brain lost. Do not try any home remedies or wait for him to wake up.

Query: A woman in the village has a sudden, terrible headache like a thunderclap. She says it's the worst pain of her life. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Sudden "thunderclap" pain (worst ever)
- Fainting or confusion
- Stiff neck (cannot touch chin to chest)
- Weakness in one side of the body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------| 
| Adult | Any | Lay down in a dark room, keep head still, call 108 |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of stroke; do not give water; transport to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Absolute Rest**: Keep the person lying flat and still. Do not let them walk.
- **Dark Room**: Close the windows and turn off lights to reduce eye strain.
- **No Food/Water**: Do not give anything to drink in case they lose consciousness.
- **Cold Compress**: Place a wet, cool cloth on the forehead for comfort.
- **Monitor**: Check if they can still speak clearly and move both arms.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient reports sudden worst headache ever"] --> B["Lay patient flat in a quiet dark room"]
  B --> C["Check for neck stiffness and speech clarity"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any tablets or liquids"]
  D --> E["Call 108 for emergency hospital transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sudden "thunderclap" pain | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Headache + Stiff neck | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Mild ache, no other signs | Refer to PHC tomorrow | Ginger tea and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A sudden, explosive headache can be a brain emergency. Do not wait for it to pass with home remedies.

Query: An old man has a bad headache and now his face is looking crooked and he can't lift his left arm. How can I help as an ASHA?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drooping face on one side
- Arm weakness or numbness
- Slurred or garbled speech
- Loss of vision in one eye

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Check time of onset, keep lying down, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check the Face**: Ask the person to smile. See if one side hangs down.
- **Check Arms**: Ask them to raise both arms. See if one arm drifts down.
- **Note the Time**: Write down the exact time the headache and weakness started.
- **Keep Airway Clear**: Turn them to the side if they start drooling or vomiting.
- **Zero by Mouth**: Do not give water, tea, or any pills.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache with one-sided face or arm weakness"] --> B["Record the exact time symptoms began"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give water, food, or aspirin"]
  C --> D["Keep the person lying flat and calm"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and rush to a hospital with CT scan"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One-sided weakness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Slurred speech | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Headache only | PHC for BP check | Rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Headache with weakness is a stroke until proven otherwise. Every minute saved protects the brain.

Query: A young girl has a severe headache and high fever. She says the light hurts her eyes and her neck is very tight. What first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- High fever with headache
- Cannot bend neck forward
- Small purple/red spots on skin
- Extreme sensitivity to light

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Youth | Any | Cover eyes, keep cool, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Neck**: Gently try to touch her chin to her chest. If she cries in pain or can't do it, it is serious.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Use normal tap water to wipe her body to lower the fever.
- **Dim Lights**: Keep her in a dark area or cover her eyes with a soft cloth.
- **Stay Calm**: Do not shake the patient or give strong traditional medicines.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache with high fever and stiff neck"] --> B["Check for skin rashes or purple spots"]
  B --> C["Tepid sponge the body to lower fever"]
  C --> D["Protect eyes from bright light with a cloth"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC immediately for brain fever check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stiff neck + Fever | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Light sensitivity | Emergency - Refer now | Dark room |
| Fever only, no neck pain | Refer to PHC | Sponging and ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This can be 'Meningitis' (brain fever). It is highly contagious and deadly if not treated with hospital injections immediately.

Query: A pregnant woman in her 8th month has a crushing headache and says her vision is blurry. Her feet are also very swollen. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Headache that won't go away
- Blurry vision or seeing 'stars'
- Swelling of face and hands
- Pain in the upper stomach

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Lay on LEFT side, call 108, do not wait |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Left Side**: Lay her on her left side to improve blood flow to the baby.
- **Check BP**: If you have a BP cuff, check it immediately. High BP is a major danger.
- **Darkness**: Keep her in a quiet, dark place to prevent seizures.
- **Watch for Fits**: Stay with her in case she starts jerking.
- **Urgent Move**: This is a threat to mother and baby. Go to a hospital with a labor room.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with headache and blurry vision"] --> B["Lay her on her left side immediately"]
  B --> C["Check BP and look for hand/face swelling"]
  C --> D["Keep her in a quiet, dark environment"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and transport to a Maternity Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pregnancy + Vision change | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Persistent headache | Emergency - Refer now | Left side rest |
| Mild swelling only | Refer to PHC today | Rest, reduce salt |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is 'Pre-eclampsia'. It can cause sudden death or seizures (fits) during pregnancy. Never ignore a headache in a pregnant woman.

Query: A villager has a headache on only one side of his head. He is vomiting and cannot look at the sun. He gets this often. How can I help him at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Headache lasting more than 3 days
- Vomiting that won't stop
- New type of pain never felt before

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Dark room, ginger paste, hydration |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Dark & Quiet**: This is likely a migraine. Move him to the darkest, quietest corner of the house.
- **Local Remedy**: Apply a paste of **Ginger or Sandalwood** on the forehead for a cooling effect.
- **Pressure**: Gently massage the temples or base of the neck.
- **Sip Ginger Water**: Small sips of ginger tea can help reduce nausea.
- **Cold Cloth**: Place a cold, wet cloth over the eyes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["One-sided headache with nausea and light sensitivity"] --> B["Move patient to a dark, silent room"]
  B --> C["Apply cool compress or ginger paste to forehead"]
  C --> D["Give small sips of water or ginger tea"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC if pain persists after 24 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Frequent recurring pain | Refer to PHC for plan | Dark room rest |
| Vomiting and dehydration | Refer to PHC for IV | Ginger tea |
| Pain relieved by rest | Not needed | Home care |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
While migraines can be managed at home, any 'new' or 'different' headache in a person over 50 should be checked by a doctor.

Query: A man fell from his tractor and hit his head. He was okay for an hour, but now has a massive headache and is getting sleepy. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Headache getting worse after a fall
- Drowsiness or difficulty waking up
- One pupil larger than the other
- Vomiting after head injury

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Keep head elevated, do not let sleep, rush to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Awake**: Talk to the person constantly. Do not let them fall into a deep sleep.
- **Head Position**: Keep the head slightly raised (30 degrees) using a pillow.
- **No Medicine**: Do not give any pain pills as they can hide worsening symptoms.
- **Check for Leaks**: Look for clear fluid or blood coming from ears or nose.
- **Immediate Referral**: He needs a brain scan immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache developing after a fall or head blow"] --> B["Keep the person awake and talking"]
  B --> C["Keep head slightly raised with a pillow"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any painkillers or water"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC or District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Injury + Worsening pain | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Sleepiness/Confusion | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Small bump, no pain | Observe for 24 hours | Ice pack |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A 'lucid interval' (feeling fine then getting worse) is a sign of internal bleeding in the skull. This is a surgical emergency.

Query: A person has a bad headache after working all day in the hot sun. They feel dizzy and thirsty. What is the ASHA's advice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Stop sweating but skin feels hot
- Confusion or fainting
- Very dark urine or no urine

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Move to shade, give ORS, cool the body |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Shade First**: Move the person to a cool, breezy area immediately.
- **Hydration**: Give small, frequent sips of **ORS** or Salt-Sugar-Lemon water.
- **Local Cooling**: Apply a wet cloth to the neck, armpits, and groin.
- **Buttermilk**: Give cold buttermilk or coconut water if available.
- **Rest**: Ensure they lie down with legs slightly raised.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache after working in extreme heat"] --> B["Move to a cool shaded area immediately"]
  B --> C["Provide small sips of ORS or cold water"]
  C --> D["Apply wet cloths to body for cooling"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC if vomiting or confusion begins"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Heat stroke | Emergency - CHC | Tepid sponging |
| Headache + Thirst | Home care | ORS and shade |
| No urine for 6 hours | Refer to PHC | Hydration |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heat exhaustion can quickly turn into heat stroke, which is fatal. If the person stops sweating but is still hot, it is an emergency.

Query: An elderly woman has a headache that hurts more when she chews food. Her temple feels tender to touch. What should I tell her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Sudden loss of vision
- Pain when chewing or talking
- Scalp feels very sore when brushing hair

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Female | Do not massage temples, refer to doctor today |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do Not Massage**: If the temple artery is inflamed, rubbing it can be harmful.
- **Soft Food**: Give her liquid or very soft food (khichdi) so she doesn't have to chew hard.
- **Eye Check**: Ask if her vision is blurry or if she sees double.
- **Refer Today**: This needs a blood test to prevent permanent blindness.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person with headache and jaw pain while eating"] --> B["Check for scalp tenderness or vision changes"]
  B --> C["Provide soft or liquid food only"]
  C --> D["Do NOT massage the head or temples"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC/CHC for a doctor's visit today"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain when chewing | Refer today | Soft food |
| Vision change | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| General mild ache | Refer to PHC this week | Rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In older people, this can be an inflammation of the blood vessels (Temporal Arteritis). It can cause sudden, irreversible blindness if not treated with steroids.

Query: A man has a headache and feels like his head is in a tight band. He is very stressed about his debt. Can we use home remedies?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain so bad he cannot work
- Using painkillers every single day

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Neck massage, warm bath, herbal tea |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Warm Compress**: Apply a warm cloth to the back of the neck and shoulders.
- **Herbal Tea**: Give a warm drink of **Tulsi and Ginger** or Chamomile if available.
- **Massage**: Gently massage the neck and shoulder muscles to release tension.
- **Breath Work**: Guide him to take 10 slow, deep breaths.
- **Avoid Tea/Coffee**: Excess caffeine can make tension headaches worse later.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dull headache feeling like a tight band"] --> B["Apply warm compress to neck and shoulders"]
  B --> C["Provide warm Tulsi tea and a quiet space"]
  C --> D["Practice deep breathing and gentle massage"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC if pain is daily or needs many pills"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Daily headache | Refer to PHC | Warm compress |
| Stress-related ache | Counsel and Home care | Relaxation |
| Sudden change in pain | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stress is a major cause of headaches. However, if he starts needing tablets every day, he must see a doctor to avoid 'rebound' headaches.

Query: A child is crying with a headache and has a very runny nose with yellow mucus. He says his face hurts under his eyes. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling around the eyes
- High fever with facial pain
- Headache when bending forward

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Steam inhalation, warm salt water gargle |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Steam Inhalation**: Have the child breathe in steam from a bowl of hot water (carefully!).
- **Warm Pack**: Place a warm cloth over the nose and cheeks.
- **Salt Water**: Use warm salt water drops (Nasoclear) for the nose.
- **Hydration**: Give plenty of warm water and soup.
- **Keep Head Up**: Let the child sleep with an extra pillow to help drainage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache with facial pain and yellow mucus"] --> B["Start steam inhalation twice a day"]
  B --> C["Apply warm compress to cheeks and forehead"]
  C --> D["Increase warm fluid intake and rest"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC if eye swelling or high fever starts"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eye swelling/Redness | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Thick mucus + Pain | Refer to PHC for meds | Steam and warmth |
| Mild cold + Headache | Home care | Steam/Gargle |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Sinus infections can sometimes spread to the eyes or brain. If you see any swelling around the eyes, rush to the hospital.

Query: A woman gets a headache every month just before her periods. She is very irritable. Is there a natural way to help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Headache that prevents all daily work
- Severe depression or mood changes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Balanced diet, rest, hydration |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Diet**: Advise her to eat less salt and more green leafy vegetables (Palak) during that week.
- **Hydration**: Drink 8-10 glasses of water. Dehydration makes hormonal headaches worse.
- **Rest**: Ensure 8 hours of sleep.
- **Jaggery and Sesame**: Traditional snacks like Til-Gud can help with iron and energy.
- **Light Exercise**: A short walk in the fresh air can reduce the pain.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Regular headache linked to menstrual cycle"] --> B["Advise lower salt intake and high hydration"]
  B --> C["Encourage 8 hours of sleep and stress relief"]
  C --> D["Use warm compress for any associated body ache"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC if pain is unmanageable every month"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Monthly pain | Refer to PHC for plan | Diet and Rest |
| Extreme mood swings | Refer to PHC | Counseling |
| Mild ache | Home care | Hydration |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hormonal headaches are common but if they are getting worse with age, a doctor should check for other underlying causes.

Query: A man has a headache and is seeing 'double' of everything. He looks confused. What is the ASHA's role?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Double vision or blurred vision
- Confusion or strange behavior
- Loss of balance when walking

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Do not let walk, keep in bed, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety First**: Help him lie down so he doesn't fall and hit his head.
- **Cover One Eye**: Sometimes covering one eye helps with the dizziness of double vision.
- **Stay with Him**: Monitor his breathing and see if he becomes more drowsy.
- **No Alcohol/Smokes**: Ensure no one gives him anything to 'relax' him.
- **Refer**: Brain pressure or nerve issues need hospital testing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache accompanied by double vision"] --> B["Help the patient lie down and remain still"]
  B --> C["Monitor for confusion or changes in speech"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any home remedies or medicines"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and transport to a District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Double vision | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency - Immediate | Never |
| Dizziness only | Refer to PHC today | Rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Double vision with a headache is a red flag for serious brain conditions. Home remedies have no role here.

Query: A person has a headache and their eyes are very red and painful. They say they see 'halos' or rainbows around light. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Severe eye pain with headache
- Nausea and vomiting with eye pain
- Hardness of the eyeball when touched
- Seeing halos around lights

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Do not rub eyes, rush to Eye Specialist/CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do Not Rub**: Rubbing the eye can increase the pressure inside.
- **Stop All Eye Drops**: Do not use any old home eye drops or 'Gulab Jal'.
- **Darkness**: Keep them in a dark room until you reach the hospital.
- **Urgent Referral**: This is an eye emergency (Glaucoma). Waiting can cause permanent blindness.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Headache with severe eye pain and red eyes"] --> B["Check if they see halos or rainbows around lights"]
  B --> C["Move patient to a dark room immediately"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put any drops or liquids in the eyes"]
  D --> E["Rush to an Eye Specialist or CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eye pain + Redness | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Halos around light | Emergency - Refer now | Never |
| Itchy eyes only | Refer to PHC | Cold water wash |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Acute glaucoma causes extreme pressure in the eye. It is often mistaken for a normal headache, but it can destroy vision within hours.

Query: A child has a headache after a long day of school and says they can't see the blackboard clearly. Is it a migraine?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Headache only on school days
- Squinting or rubbing eyes often
- Headache after reading

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Limit screen time, eye checkup, rest |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Rest Eyes**: No TV or mobile phone for the rest of the day.
- **Face Wash**: Wash the face and eyes with cool, clean water.
- **Distance Check**: Ask the child to read something far away to see if they struggle.
- **Good Light**: Ensure the child has bright light when studying at home.
- **Refer**: Take the child to an eye doctor for a vision test.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with headache and blurry distance vision"] --> B["Restrict mobile/TV use for the day"]
  B --> C["Wash eyes with cool water and provide rest"]
  C --> D["Observe if headache follows reading or writing"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC or Optometrist for eye testing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blurry vision | Refer to Optometrist | Eye rest |
