Query: An ASHA worker asks: A villager has been using a Chulha for 40 years and now is always short of breath. Today he is very blue.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blue or purple lips and tongue**
- **Swollen ankles and neck veins**
- Confusion or extreme sleepiness
- Coughing up thick green/yellow phlegm

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Avoid lying flat; needs oxygen and urgent PHC visit |
| Caregiver | Any | Switch to smokeless chulha if possible; refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit Up**: This is the most important step. They cannot breathe lying down.
- **Fresh Air**: Move them out of the kitchen and away from all smoke.
- **Hand Fan**: Use a fan to move air toward their face.
- **Warm Water**: Helps to clear the thick phlegm they often have.
- **Referral**: Chronic lung disease (COPD) needs oxygen and long-term medicine from the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chronic breathing trouble (Chulha user) turns blue"] --> B["Move patient to a completely smoke-free area"]
  B --> C["Sit the patient upright in a sturdy chair"]
  C --> D["Use a hand fan to provide a constant breeze"]
  D --> E["Check for confusion or swelling in legs"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for oxygen and nebulization"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue lips/tongue | **Emergency - Call 108** | Never |
| Swollen legs + Distress | Refer to CHC today | Keep sitting upright |
| Constant cough | Refer to PHC for medicine | Avoid smoke/dust |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Long-term smoke damage (COPD) makes the lungs very weak. A small infection can become a major breathing emergency quickly.

Query: A person has a 'barking' cough like a dog and is struggling to breathe at night. What do I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Whistling sound (Stridor) even when resting**
- **Drooling or inability to swallow water**
- Chest sucking in deep
- Blue nails

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | Calm the child; expose to cool air or steam |
| Adult | Any | Refer for throat examination; rare but serious |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool Night Air**: Sometimes taking the child out into the cool, damp night air helps open the throat.
- **Steam**: Run a hot shower or use a bowl of hot water (be careful of burns) for 10 minutes.
- **Hydration**: If they can swallow, give small sips of clear liquids.
- **Position**: Keep the child upright on your shoulder.
- **Referral**: If the breathing is fast or noisy between coughs, they need a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Barking cough with breathing trouble at night"] --> B["Take the patient into cool air or provide steam"]
  B --> C["Keep the patient calm and sitting upright"]
  C --> D["Check for high-pitched sound (Stridor)"]
  D --> E["If no improvement in 15 mins, refer to PHC"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC if lips turn blue or drooling starts"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Noisy breathing at rest | **Urgent Referral to CHC** | Cool air/Steam |
| Drooling | **Emergency - Call 108** | Never |
| Barking cough only | Refer to PHC tomorrow | Steam and liquids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Croup' (barking cough) usually gets worse at night. Always be ready to take the child to the hospital if they struggle to breathe.

Query: A 2-year-old child has a very high fever, is breathing very fast, and his chest is pulling in deep under his ribs. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Chest indrawing** (lower chest wall goes in when child breathes in)
- **Nasal flaring** (nostrils widen with every breath)
- Child is unable to drink or breastfeed
- Grunting sounds or extreme sleepiness
- Blue tint on lips or tongue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<2 months) | Any | **Emergency** - Keep warm, do not feed, rush to CHC |
| Child (2m - 5y) | Any | Count breaths for 1 minute; sit upright; refer if fast |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit the child up**: Hold the child in an upright position to help them breathe easier.
- **Keep warm**: Ensure the child is warm but not overheated.
- **Clear the nose**: If the nose is blocked, use a salt-water drop (home-made: pinch of salt in cup of boiled-cooled water) to clear it.
- **Honey (if >1 year)**: Give half a teaspoon of honey with ginger juice to soothe the throat.
- **Count Breaths**: If breaths are more than 50 per minute (for 2-12 months) or 40 per minute (for 1-5 years), it is an emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess breathing and chest movement"] --> B["Check if child can drink or breastfeed"]
  B --> C["Clear nose with saline drops if blocked"]
  C --> D["Count breaths for one full minute"]
  D --> E["Check for chest indrawing or blue lips"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC if danger signs are present"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest indrawing | **Emergency - Refer Now** | Never |
| Fast breathing | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Mild cough/No fast breath | Monitor at home | Tulsi, ginger, and honey |
| Unable to drink | **Emergency - Refer Now** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Respiratory infections in children can turn into severe pneumonia quickly. If breathing is fast, do not wait for home remedies to work; seek a doctor immediately.

Query: A baby is coughing a lot and making a whistling sound. The mother says the baby is too weak to cry. How can I help?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wheezing** (whistling sound when breathing out)
- **Extreme lethargy** (child cannot be woken up)
- **Convulsions** or fits
- Very fast, shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1 year) | Any | Check for 'grunting' sound; keep head elevated; refer |
| Child (1-5 years) | Any | Steam inhalation; check for chest indrawing; refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Positioning**: Keep the baby’s head higher than the feet.
- **Steam**: Let the child breathe in steam from a bowl of hot water (keep a safe distance to avoid burns).
- **Fluids**: Encourage frequent breastfeeding or small sips of warm water if older than 6 months.
- **Observation**: Watch the skin between the ribs; if it sucks in, the baby is struggling.
- **Referral**: If the child is too weak to cry, they need oxygen at a hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Observe child for whistling or grunting sounds"] --> B["Check alertness and ability to cry or feed"]
  B --> C["Provide steam in a safe, controlled way"]
  C --> D["Maintain head-up position to open airways"]
  D --> E["Monitor for chest retractions or skin color changes"]
  E --> F["Arrange urgent transport to hospital if weak or wheezing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Whistling sound | **Immediate Referral** | Never |
| Too weak to cry | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Simple cough | PHC visit if no improvement | Warm fluids and steam |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Wheezing in small children often requires nebulization which can only be done at a health center. Do not delay transport.

Query: A child has a barky cough that sounds like a dog. It gets worse at night. The mother is scared.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stridor** (high-pitched sound when breathing IN)
- **Drooling** or unable to swallow saliva
- Blue nails or lips
- Child leaning forward to breathe

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (1-5y) | Any | Calm the child; use moist air or cool night air |
| Caregiver | Female | Reassure mother; panic makes child's throat tighten |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool Air**: Take the child outside into the cool night air for 10 minutes; this often shrinks throat swelling.
- **Moist Air**: If cool air doesn't help, sit with the child in a steamy bathroom.
- **Calmness**: Keep the child quiet; crying makes the airway swell more.
- **No Finger in Mouth**: Do NOT try to see the throat with a finger or spoon; it can cause the throat to close shut.
- **Referral**: If the child makes a sound while breathing in even when quiet, go to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child has barking cough and distress"] --> B["Keep child calm and in upright lap position"]
  B --> C["Expose to cool night air or steam for 10 mins"]
  C --> D["Check for high-pitched noise during inhalation"]
  D --> E["Do NOT put any objects in the child's mouth"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC if breathing sound persists at rest"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Noise when breathing in | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Barking cough only | PHC visit in morning | Steam and cool air |
| Drooling/Cannot swallow | **Emergency - Call 108** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A 'barking' cough usually indicates swelling in the windpipe. It can get worse very quickly at night. Watch the child closely.

Query: A 4-year-old child has a bad cold and high fever. Can I give him ginger and tulsi? When should I take him to the doctor?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Breathing faster than 40 times a minute**
- **Chest sucking in** under the ribs
- Child stops playing or becomes very dull
- Fever that does not go down with sponging

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (1-5y) | Any | Sponge with room-temp water; give ginger-tulsi tea |
| Adult | Any | Check for fast breathing while child is calm |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Fever Control**: Sponge the forehead, stomach, and underarms with room-temperature water.
- **Local Remedy**: Crush 5 Tulsi leaves, mix with ginger juice and half a spoon of honey. Give 3 times a day.
- **Warm Fluids**: Give warm 'Dal-ka-paani' or rice water to keep the child strong.
- **Fresh Air**: Ensure the room is ventilated and not smoky from the kitchen fire.
- **Count Breaths**: Count for 60 seconds while the child is sleeping or quiet.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child has cough, cold, and fever"] --> B["Apply room-temperature water sponges for fever"]
  B --> C["Give Tulsi-Ginger-Honey mix for cough relief"]
  C --> D["Encourage intake of warm, light liquids"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing rate and chest movement"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if fever persists >2 days or breathing fast"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast breathing | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Fever for 3 days | Refer to PHC | Sponging for first 24 hrs |
| Mild cough/Active child | Monitor at home | Tulsi and ginger |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
While Tulsi and Ginger help with symptoms, they do not cure pneumonia. Always count the breathing rate to be sure.

Query: I am an ASHA. A 6-month-old baby is coughing and the skin around the neck and ribs is pulling in very hard. Is this urgent?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Neck and rib retractions** (skin pulls in tight with every breath)
- **Nasal flaring**
- **Cyanosis** (blue color on face or hands)
- Child is grunting (making a 'uh' sound with every breath)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (6m) | Any | **Emergency** - Stop feeding; keep upright; rush to CHC |
| Caregiver | Any | Arrange transport immediately; do not wait for the father |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Feeding**: Do not try to give milk or water; the baby might breathe it into the lungs.
- **Clear the Airway**: Ensure nothing is covering the baby’s nose or mouth.
- **Upright Position**: Hold the baby against your shoulder.
- **Oxygen**: If your village has a health sub-center with oxygen, stop there on the way to the CHC.
- **Referral**: This baby is in 'Respiratory Distress' and needs a doctor NOW.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant with neck/rib retractions"] --> B["Check for grunting and blue skin"] 
  B --> C["Stop all oral feeding immediately"]
  C --> D["Hold baby in an upright shoulder position"]
  D --> E["Identify nearest facility with oxygen"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rib/Neck pulling in | **Emergency - Refer Now** | Never |
| Grunting sound | **Emergency - Refer Now** | Never |
| Blue skin | **Call 108/Emergency** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Retractions mean the child is using every muscle just to get air. This is a sign of severe lung infection or blockage. Do not delay for home treatment.

Query: A child has been coughing for 2 weeks. He is losing weight and gets a fever every evening. What should I tell the mother?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Weight loss** in a short time
- **Night sweats** (waking up wet with sweat)
- Swollen lumps in the neck
- Coughing up blood (very rare in children but serious)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Screen for TB; check family history of cough |
| Adult | Any | Refer child for Sputum/Chest X-ray |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Nutrition**: Give protein-rich foods like roasted chana, eggs, or pulses (dal).
- **History**: Ask if anyone else in the house has had a cough for a long time.
- **Ventilation**: Keep windows open to let fresh air and sunlight into the house.
- **Referral**: A cough lasting more than 2 weeks must be checked for Tuberculosis (TB) at the PHC.
- **Support**: Tell the mother that TB is curable and the medicine is free at the government hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cough lasting more than 14 days"] --> B["Check for evening fever and weight loss"]
  B --> C["Ask about TB history in the family"]
  C --> D["Increase protein intake (eggs/dal/sattu)"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for TB screening/X-ray"]
  E --> F["Monitor family members for similar symptoms"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cough > 2 weeks | Refer to PHC | Never wait at home |
| Evening fever | Refer to PHC | Use paracetamol for relief |
| Weight loss | Refer to PHC | Give energy-dense foods |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chronic cough in children should never be ignored as 'just a cold'. Early testing for TB saves lives and prevents spread.

Query: A baby is breathing very fast and the chest is making a rattling sound. He has no fever. What could it be and what should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rattling in chest** (mucus sound)
- **Inability to suckle** at the breast
- Very fast breathing without fever
- Convulsions

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | Clear nose; keep upright; refer for chest check |
| Caregiver | Any | Observe for 'silent' pneumonia (no fever) |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Nose Clearance**: Blocked nose can cause rattling. Use saline drops.
- **Positioning**: Hold the baby upright on your chest.
- **Breastfeeding**: Continue breastfeeding frequently to keep the baby hydrated.
- **Steam**: A little steam in the room can help thin the chest mucus.
- **Referral**: Even without fever, fast breathing and rattling mean the lungs are affected. Take to the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Rattling chest sound in infant"] --> B["Check for fast breathing and nasal flaring"]
  B --> C["Use saline drops to clear nasal passage"]
  C --> D["Continue frequent breastfeeding sessions"]
  D --> E["Check for chest indrawing under the ribs"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for lung examination"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast breathing | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Rattling + No fever | Refer to PHC | Saline drops/Breastfeed |
| Simple nasal block | Monitor at home | Home-made saline drops |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pneumonia in infants can occur without a high fever. Fast breathing is the most reliable sign of danger.

Query: A child has a cold and the mother wants to know if she can give him 'Sitopaladi' or other herbs. He is breathing normally.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden fast breathing**
- **High fever with chills**
- Refusing to eat or play
- Blue lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (>1y) | Any | Safe to give herbal home remedies if no distress |
| Infant (<1y) | Any | Avoid heavy herbal powders; stick to ginger-honey |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safe Herbs**: Ginger, Tulsi, and a little honey are safe and helpful.
- **Fluids**: Give warm rice water, dal soup, or herbal tea (Kadha) using ginger and black pepper.
- **Steam**: 5 minutes of steam twice a day.
- **Observation**: The most important task is to count breaths once in the morning and once at night.
- **Warmth**: Keep the chest and feet warm.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child has mild cold and cough"] --> B["Verify breathing rate is normal (no distress)"]
  B --> C["Give warm ginger-tulsi-honey mix"]
  C --> D["Encourage warm liquids and rest"]
  D --> E["Use steam inhalation safely"]
  E --> F["Monitor for danger signs like fast breathing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Normal breathing | Monitor at home | Herbs and warm fluids |
| Fever > 101°F | Refer to PHC | Sponging and paracetamol |
| Difficulty breathing | **Emergency Referral** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Home remedies only treat the symptoms. If the child’s breathing becomes fast or the chest pulls in, stop home remedies and go to a doctor immediately.

Query: A child is gasping for air and his face is turning blue. He was eating peanuts just before this happened. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden gasping** or unable to breathe
- **Silent cough** (no sound coming out)
- Face or lips turning blue
- Hand clutching the throat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts |
| Child (>1y) | Any | Abdominal thrusts (Heimlich Maneuver) |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Back Blows**: Lay child face down on your lap; give 5 sharp blows between shoulder blades.
- **Abdominal Thrusts**: Stand behind child; wrap arms around waist; make a fist above belly button; pull in and up sharply.
- **Do NOT reach in**: Do not put your finger in the throat; you might push the peanut deeper.
- **Call 108**: Get someone to call for an ambulance while you do first aid.
- **Transport**: If the peanut doesn't come out, rush to the hospital immediately while continuing thrusts.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Sudden choking while eating"] --> B["Check if child can speak or cough loudly"]
  B --> C["Perform 5 back blows (if no sound)"]
  C --> D["Perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich)"]
  D --> E["Repeat until object comes out or child faints"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital immediately if object remains"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Choking/Blue face | **Emergency - Refer Now** | Perform first aid NOW |
| Persistent cough after choking | Refer to CHC | Watch for infection |
| Object comes out/No symptoms | Visit PHC for checkup | Monitor for 24 hours |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Choking is different from a respiratory infection. It is a total blockage and needs action in seconds. Peanuts are a common cause of lung infection if aspirated.

Query: A 3-month-old baby has a blocked nose and is struggling to breathe while breastfeeding. What can an ASHA do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unable to breastfeed**
- **Fast breathing** when nose is cleared
- **Nasal flaring**
- Grunting sounds

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (3m) | Any | Clear nose with saline; keep upright; monitor feeding |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not use oil drops in the nose; only saline |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Saline Drops**: Mix a pinch of salt in a cup of boiled-cooled water. Put 2 drops in each nostril before feeding.
- **Clear Mucus**: Use a soft clean cloth to wipe away loosened mucus.
- **Upright Feed**: Hold the baby more upright during breastfeeding.
- **Monitor**: Check if the baby can feed properly after the nose is cleared. If still struggling, it is a lung issue.
- **Referral**: If the baby is still breathing fast after the nose is clear, take to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant struggling to feed due to blocked nose"] --> B["Apply home-made saline drops to nostrils"]
  B --> C["Gently clear loosened mucus with soft cloth"]
  C --> D["Try feeding in an upright position"]
  D --> E["Re-assess breathing rate after nose is clear"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if breathing remains fast"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Still fast breathing after clearing nose | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Refusal to feed | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Easy breathing after saline drops | Monitor at home | Continue saline drops |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A blocked nose is common, but it can hide pneumonia. Always re-count the breathing rate after cleaning the nose.

Query: A child has a very noisy chest and a cough. The mother is applying hot oil on the chest. Is this okay?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Chest indrawing**
- **Blue lips**
- **Whistling sound** (wheeze)
- Fever with fast breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (1-5y) | Any | Stop hot oil if skin is red; count breaths; refer if noisy |
| Caregiver | Any | Advise warm (not hot) mustard oil with garlic only for comfort |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Burning**: Ensure the oil is not too hot; a child's skin burns easily. Do not rub too hard.
- **Steam**: Provide warm steam; it helps the chest better than oil.
- **Count Breaths**: This is more important than applying oil. Check if breathing is over 40/min.
- **Hydration**: Give warm water or soup.
- **Referral**: If the noise in the chest is loud and the child is breathing fast, oil will not help. Take to the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child has noisy chest and cough"] --> B["Ensure any oil used is only lukewarm (not hot)"]
  B --> C["Monitor for whistling sounds or wheezing"]
  C --> D["Perform 60-second breath count while child is quiet"]
  D --> E["Provide steam inhalation for comfort"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital if breathing is fast or noisy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Noisy whistling sound | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Chest indrawing | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Mild congestion/No fast breath | Monitor at home | Warm oil/Steam |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
External oil application does not reach the lungs. It may soothe a cough but will not treat pneumonia or asthma.

Query: What is the fastest way to check if a baby has pneumonia in the village?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Chest indrawing** (most important sign)
- **Breathing rate > 50** for babies under 1 year
- **Gritting/Grunting** sound
- **Head nodding** with every breath

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | Uncover chest; count breaths for 1 minute; check for indrawing |
| Adult | Any | Keep child calm during counting; do not count while crying |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The Look Test**: Look at the child’s bare chest. If the bottom part sinks in when the child breathes in, it is pneumonia.
- **The Count Test**: Count the number of times the chest rises in one minute. 
- **Listen**: Put your ear near the baby's mouth. If you hear a 'uh' sound when they breathe out, it is serious.
- **Feed Test**: Try to offer the breast or water. If the baby can't swallow or keep it down, it's a danger sign.
- **Immediate Referral**: If any of the above are positive, go to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Uncover child's chest to observe movement"] --> B["Check for lower chest wall sinking (Indrawing)"]
  B --> C["Count breaths for 1 full minute when child is calm"]
  C --> D["Listen for grunting or noisy exhaling"]
  D --> E["Offer drink or breast to check swallowing ability"]
  E --> F["If chest indrawing or fast breathing, refer immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest indrawing | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Fast breathing (50+ or 40+) | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Fever/Cough with normal breath | Refer to PHC | Monitor and keep warm |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Early detection of 'Fast Breathing' is the best way to prevent pneumonia death. Refer immediately if count is high.

Query: A toddler has a cough and a cold. Is it okay to keep him near the kitchen fire to keep him warm?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Worsening cough** near smoke
- **Fast breathing**
- **Watery eyes and runny nose** getting redder
- Lethargy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Toddler (1-3y) | Any | **Move away from smoke**; use blankets for warmth instead |
| Caregiver | Any | Keep child in a smoke-free, well-ventilated room |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Avoid Smoke**: Smoke from the chulha (fire) makes lung infections much worse. Move the child to a different room.
- **Warmth**: Use sweaters, caps, and socks to keep the child warm, not fire.
- **Fresh Air**: Open a window for a few minutes every hour to let in fresh air.
- **Hydration**: Give warm liquids frequently.
- **Count Breaths**: Monitor the breathing rate away from the smoke.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child has respiratory symptoms and is near fire"] --> B["Move child to a smoke-free, ventilated area"]
  B --> C["Use layered clothing (socks/caps) for warmth"]
  C --> D["Ensure room air is fresh but child is warm"]
  D --> E["Give warm fluids (dal paani/warm water)"]
  E --> F["Monitor for fast breathing or chest indrawing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Coughing more in smoke | Move to fresh air | Use blankets for warmth |
| Difficulty breathing | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Mild cold | Monitor at home | Fresh air and warm clothes |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Smoke inhalation is a major cause of worsening pneumonia in rural areas. Always prioritize clean air for a sick child.

Query: My 5-year-old child has a fever, sore throat, and is refusing to swallow anything. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Drooling** (cannot swallow spit)
- **Difficulty breathing**
- **High fever** (>102°F)
- Swollen lumps in the neck

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (5y) | Any | Give soft/liquid foods; salt-water gargle; refer if drooling |
| Adult | Any | Check for white spots on throat using a torch |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Salt-Water Gargle**: If the child can gargle, have them do it with warm salt water twice a day.
- **Soft Foods**: Give cold or room-temp soft foods like curd, mashed banana, or thin khichdi.
- **Fever Sponging**: Use room-temperature water to keep the fever down.
- **Observe**: Look into the throat with a light. If you see white patches, they need medicine from the PHC.
- **Referral**: If the child cannot even swallow water or is drooling, go to the hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child refuses to swallow due to sore throat"] --> B["Check for fever and look for throat patches"]
  B --> C["Perform warm salt-water gargles if child is able"]
  C --> D["Offer soft, non-spicy, room-temp foods"]
  D --> E["Monitor for drooling or breathing trouble"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if fever high or patches visible"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drooling/Cannot swallow | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| White spots in throat | Refer to PHC | Salt-water gargles |
| Mild pain/Can eat | Monitor at home | Soft foods and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A severe sore throat with high fever can be 'Strep throat' or 'Tonsillitis', which needs antibiotics. Do not wait more than 24 hours if swallowing is hard.

Query: How to do steam inhalation for a small child safely at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Burn marks** (if water spills)
- **Worsening distress** during steam
- Child turning blue
- Fast breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | 'Bathroom steam' only; do NOT use a bowl of hot water |
| Child (>1y) | Any | Use a bowl with mother holding the child; keep distance |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety First**: NEVER leave a child alone with hot water. 
- **The Towel Method**: Mother sits with child on her lap. Place a bowl of hot water on a stable table. Cover both with a towel, but leave an opening for air. Keep the child's face at least 1 foot away.
- **Bathroom Method**: Run hot water in a small bathroom to make it foggy. Sit with the child for 10-15 minutes.
- **Plain Water**: Do NOT add Vicks or oils for small children; they can irritate the lungs.
- **Frequency**: Do this 2-3 times a day for 5-10 minutes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Prepare hot water for steam inhalation"] --> B["Ensure child is held securely by an adult"]
  B --> C["Maintain safe distance from hot water bowl"]
  C --> D["Allow child to breathe moist air for 5-10 mins"]
  D --> E["Stop immediately if child is restless or burnt"]
  E --> F["Monitor breathing rate after steam session"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Breathing gets harder | **Urgent Referral** | Stop steam immediately |
| Congested/Normal breath | Monitor at home | Steam twice a day |
| Accidental burn | Refer to PHC | Run cold tap water on burn |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Steam helps loosen mucus but does not cure the infection. If the child is breathing fast (Pneumonia), steam will not help; they need medicine.

Query: ASHA worker reporting: A child has a very high fever (103°F) and is breathing 60 times a minute. Lips are pale.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Breathing 60+ times/min** (Very high risk)
- **Pale or blue lips**
- **Chest indrawing**
- Fever not going down

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Emergency** - Cold sponge; stop feeding; rush to hospital |
| Caregiver | Any | Call for ambulance/108; carry child upright |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Emergency Cooling**: Use cold water sponges on the body to drop the fever quickly while moving to the hospital.
- **Oxygen**: This child likely has very low oxygen. Find the nearest place with a cylinder.
- **No Oral Meds**: Do not try to give syrups or water; the risk of choking is too high with fast breathing.
- **Position**: Keep the child in a vertical position during transport.
- **Referral**: This is Severe Pneumonia. Go to a District Hospital or large CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with 60+ breaths/min and high fever"] --> B["Start cold sponging immediately to lower temp"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food, water, or syrups"]
  C --> D["Carry child in an upright, vertical position"]
  D --> E["Identify nearest hospital with ICU/Oxygen"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital via fastest transport available"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| 60+ breaths per minute | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Pale/Blue lips | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| High fever + Fast breath | **Immediate Emergency** | Cold sponging during transport |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A breathing rate of 60 is a critical warning. The child could stop breathing soon. Move immediately.

Query: A child is coughing and it sounds like there is water or a rattle in the chest. No fever. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast breathing**
- **Chest indrawing**
- **Unable to breastfeed/drink**
- Rattling that worsens when lying down

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | Refer for chest sound check; keep upright |
| Child (1-5y) | Any | Steam; count breaths; refer if noisy breathing |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Clear Nasal Path**: Blocked nose can make a rattling sound in the chest. Use saline drops.
- **Posture**: Do not let the child sleep flat. Use a small pillow under the mattress (for children >1 year) or hold the baby.
- **Count Breaths**: This is the only way to know if the 'rattle' is serious. 
- **Listen**: If the rattle is heard only when the child is awake and active, it might be mucus. If heard while sleeping, it's more serious.
- **Referral**: If you can feel the rattle with your hand on the child's back, go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Rattling sound in child's chest"] --> B["Check for nasal blockage and use saline"] 
  B --> C["Assess if rattle is felt on the back/chest"]
  C --> D["Perform 60-second breath count at rest"]
  D --> E["Keep child in an elevated sleeping position"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for lung sound assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rattle felt on back | Refer to PHC | Never treat at home |
| Fast breathing | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Rattle only when crying | Monitor at home | Clear nose/Steam |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Silent' pneumonia can have a rattle without fever. Always check the breathing rate as the primary guide.

Query: A child with a cold has suddenly developed a high-pitched whistling noise when breathing out. What is it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wheezing** (whistling when breathing out)
- **Chest pulling in**
- **Talking in short words** (cannot speak long sentences)
- Blue lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (any) | Any | **Immediate Referral** for nebulization |
| Caregiver | Any | Keep child calm; do not give hot drinks if struggling |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit Up**: Keep the child sitting and leaning slightly forward.
- **Fresh Air**: Ensure there is no smoke or incense in the room.
- **Inhaler**: If the child has a prescribed 'pump' (inhaler), use it immediately as told by the doctor.
- **Calmness**: Reassure the child. Crying makes wheezing worse.
- **Referral**: Whistling (wheezing) means the lung pipes are narrow. This needs a nebulizer machine at the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Whistling sound (wheeze) while breathing out"] --> B["Check for chest retractions and lip color"]
  B --> C["Place child in a sitting-forward position"]
  C --> D["Administer prescribed inhaler if available"]
  D --> E["Ensure room is free of smoke and dust triggers"]
  E --> F["Rush to PHC for nebulizer treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Whistling sound | **Urgent Referral to PHC** | Never |
| Shortness of breath | **Urgent Referral to PHC** | Sit upright |
| Mild cough/Active | Monitor at home | Avoid dust/smoke |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Wheezing is often an allergy or asthma. It can cause the child to stop breathing if the pipes close too much. Do not delay referral.

Query: What should a child with a respiratory infection eat and drink at home in the village?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unable to swallow water**
- **Vomiting everything**
- **Very dry mouth/No tears** (Dehydration)
- Fast breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<6m) | Any | Frequent breastfeeding only; refer if refusing |
| Child (>6m) | Any | Warm fluids; soft calorie-dense food; refer if vomiting |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Frequent Fluids**: Give warm water, dal soup, or rice water every hour.
- **Breastfeeding**: For infants, breastfeed more often than usual.
- **Calorie Dense**: Give mashed bananas, khichdi with a little ghee, or boiled eggs.
- **Honey/Ginger**: Use for cough relief (only if >1 year).
- **Avoid**: Do not give very cold drinks or very spicy food.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nutrition for child with lung infection"] --> B["Increase frequency of breastfeeding or warm fluids"]
  B --> C["Offer soft, calorie-rich foods (Khichdi/Banana)"]
  C --> D["Check for signs of dehydration (tears/urine)"]
  D --> E["Avoid cold/spicy foods that trigger coughing"]
  E --> F["Refer if child stops drinking or vomits everything"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Won't drink/Refuse breast | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Vomiting everything | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Eating less/Still active | Monitor at home | Frequent small feeds |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A sick child needs more energy to fight infection. If they stop eating or drinking entirely, they will get weak and the infection will win. Seek help if feeding stops.

Query: Is it okay to give 'Bal-Gutti' or 'Janam-Gutti' to a baby with a bad cough?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast breathing**
- **Chest indrawing**
- **Blue tint**
- **Dullness/Lethargy**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<6m) | Any | **Do NOT give Gutti**; stick to breastmilk and saline drops |
| Child (>6m) | Any | Prefer simple ginger juice/honey over complex Gutti |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Avoid Gutti in Infants**: Many 'Guttis' contain opium or honey (risky for <1yr) or heavy metals that can harm a tiny baby.
- **Saline Drops**: Use home-made saline for nose blocks instead.
- **Breastfeeding**: This is the best 'medicine' for an infant’s immunity.
- **Count Breaths**: Always count breaths before giving any home remedy.
- **Referral**: If the cough is bad enough to need Gutti, it is bad enough to need a PHC check-up.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cough in infant - Gutti requested"] --> B["Perform 60-second breath count check"] 
  B --> C["Assess for chest indrawing or feeding trouble"]
  C --> D["Advise frequent breastfeeding for hydration"]
  D --> E["Discourage use of Gutti for infants under 6m"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if cough is persistent or severe"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cough with fast breath | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Child under 6 months | Refer to PHC for any bad cough | Breastfeeding only |
| Mild cough/Older child | Refer if no improvement | Ginger and warm water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Many traditional Gutti medicines are not safe for the immature kidneys and lungs of a small baby. Stick to safe, proven local knowledge like ginger-tulsi for older children and breastmilk for infants.

Query: A young boy fell from a guava tree and hit his head on a stone. He was unconscious for a minute but is now awake and crying. There is a large bump. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Loss of consciousness** even for a few seconds
- **Repeated vomiting** (more than twice)
- Seizures or fitting
- Fluid or blood leaking from ears or nose
- Difficulty walking or speaking
- Extreme sleepiness or cannot be woken up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Keep child quiet, apply cold pack to bump, monitor for 24 hours |
| Adult | Any | Rest in a dark room, avoid mobile screens, check for confusion |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Breathing**: Ensure the person is breathing clearly.
- **Control Bleeding**: If there is a cut, press a clean cloth firmly on the wound for 10 minutes.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply a cloth soaked in cold water or an ice pack to the swelling to reduce the 'egg' bump.
- **Keep Calm**: Lay the person down with the head slightly raised.
- **No Solid Food**: Give only small sips of water; do not give a heavy meal in case they vomit.
- **Monitor**: Watch the person closely for the next 6 hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess if the person is conscious"] --> B["Check for heavy bleeding or visible skull damage"]
  B --> C["Apply firm pressure to bleeding wounds with clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress to swelling to reduce pain"]
  D --> E["Keep patient lying down with head slightly elevated"]
  E --> F["Monitor for vomiting or extreme sleepiness for 24 hours"]
  F --> G["Call 108 if danger signs appear"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any unconsciousness | **Immediate Referral** | Never |
| Repeated vomiting | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Mild headache/Bump | Monitor for 24 hours | Cold pack and rest |
| Seizures or fits | **Call 108 immediately** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Head injuries can be 'hidden'. A person might seem fine now but worsen later. Always keep a close eye for 24 hours. Do not give any painkiller medicine without a doctor's advice.

Query: A man got hit by a tractor. He is awake but he keeps asking the same question again and again. He doesn't remember the accident. Should I be worried?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Memory loss** (not remembering the event)
- **Confusion** (repeatedly asking the same thing)
- Slurred speech
- Unequal pupil size (one eye black circle larger than the other)
- Weakness in arms or legs

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (18-60) | Male/Female | High risk of internal injury; refer for observation |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Very high risk; refer to District Hospital immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Referral**: Repeated questioning is a sign of brain injury (concussion). Take him to the CHC.
- **Keep Awake**: Try to keep the person talking and conscious until you reach the doctor.
- **Immobilize Neck**: If the hit was hard, do not move the neck much; keep the head straight.
- **Do NOT give alcohol**: Some villagers give local liquor for pain; this is very dangerous for head injuries.
- **Note the Time**: Write down exactly when the accident happened and how long he was confused.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Recognize signs of confusion or memory loss"] --> B["Keep the person's head and neck still and straight"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food, water, or medicine"]
  C --> D["Check for weakness in hands or feet"]
  D --> E["Ask simple questions like 'What is your name?'"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to a facility with a CT scan"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Memory loss | **Refer immediately** | Not safe at home |
| Slurred speech | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Visible scalp cut only | Visit PHC for dressing | Clean with soap/water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Confusion after a hit to the head is a sign that the brain has been shaken. This needs professional medical checking to rule out internal bleeding.

Query: An elderly woman slipped in the bathroom and hit her head. She says she is fine, but she is on blood-thinning medicine for her heart. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Even a minor bump** in someone on blood thinners
- Headache that keeps getting worse
- Dizziness or loss of balance
- Bruising behind the ears or around eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Female | **High Priority** - Internal bleeding can happen slowly |
| Caregiver | Any | Collect her medicine list to show the doctor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Refer to CHC**: People on heart/blood medicines can bleed inside the head very easily, even without a big bump.
- **Check Pupils**: Use a torch to see if both black circles in the eyes shrink the same way.
- **Keep Still**: Let her lie down comfortably.
- **No Massage**: Do not rub the head with oil or balms; it won't help internal bleeding.
- **Transport**: Arrange for a vehicle to take her to a hospital with a doctor, even if she refuses.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person falls and hits head"] --> B["Check if they take heart or blood-thinning medicine"]
  B --> C["Inspect for bruising or swelling on the head"]
  C --> D["Monitor for changes in behavior or balance"]
  D --> E["Collect all current medications in a bag"]
  E --> F["Refer to hospital immediately regardless of symptoms"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| On blood thinners | **Always Refer** | Never wait at home |
| Dizziness/Balance loss | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| No symptoms/No meds | Monitor for 24 hours | Cold compress |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In elderly patients, symptoms of brain bleeding may take hours or even days to appear. A 'wait and watch' approach is dangerous if they are on medication.

Query: A baby fell from the cot. There is a soft swelling on the side of the head. The baby stopped crying after 5 minutes and is now trying to sleep. Is sleep okay?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Soft, 'boggy' swelling** (feels like a water balloon)
- **Bulging soft spot** (fontanelle) on top of the head
- High-pitched crying
- Vomiting
- **Cannot be woken up** from sleep

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1y) | Any | **High Risk** - Check the soft spot on head; refer if bulging |
| Parent | Any | Wake the baby every 2 hours to check alertness |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wake-up Check**: It is okay to let the baby sleep, but you must wake them up every 2 hours to see if they recognize the mother and can feed.
- **Observe Soft Spot**: Gently feel the top of the baby's head. If it feels tight or bulging, rush to the hospital.
- **Cold Compress**: Wrap a cold wet cloth in a thin towel and hold it to the swelling.
- **No Oil/Paste**: Do not apply turmeric paste or oil to the soft part of a baby's head.
- **Breastfeeding**: Try to breastfeed; if the baby refuses or vomits, go to the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant falls and develops head swelling"] --> B["Check the soft spot on top of the head for bulging"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to the swelling gently"]
  C --> D["Try to breastfeed to check for vomiting or refusal"]
  D --> E["Wake the baby every 2 hours to ensure alertness"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC if baby is dull or soft spot bulges"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bulging soft spot | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Won't wake up | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Small bump/Active baby | Monitor closely | Cold pack and feeding |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Babies have thin skulls. A 'boggy' or very soft swelling on the side of the head often means a skull fracture. Refer for a professional check-up.

Query: A boy was hit by a cricket ball on the temple (side of head). He was fine for 10 minutes but now he is becoming very sleepy and confused. What is happening?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **'Talk and Die' syndrome** (Fine at first, then suddenly worsens)
- **Increasing headache**
- **One pupil larger than the other**
- Confusion or drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Youth | Any | **Life-Threatening Emergency** - Rush to District Hospital |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not wait for a local clinic; go where there is a surgeon |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Transport**: This is a medical emergency. The hit on the temple can cause rapid bleeding inside.
- **Stay Vertical**: Keep the person sitting up or with the head high during transport.
- **No Food/Water**: They may need surgery soon; keep the stomach empty.
- **Keep Talking**: Keep the person engaged to monitor their consciousness level.
- **Call 108**: If an ambulance is available, call it now.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Hit to the temple followed by 'lucid interval'"] --> B["Recognize sudden onset of sleepiness or confusion"]
  B --> C["Check for unequal pupil sizes with a light"]
  C --> D["Keep the patient upright and awake"]
  D --> E["Stop all food and water intake immediately"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital with surgical facilities"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sudden sleepiness | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Unequal pupils | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Worsening headache | **Urgent Referral** | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A hit to the temple is the most dangerous head injury. Even if the person says they are 'okay' at first, sudden sleepiness later is a sign of a brain-bleed emergency.

Query: There is a deep cut on a man's forehead after a fall. It is bleeding a lot and I can see the white bone. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Visible bone** or brain matter
- Bleeding that doesn't stop with pressure
- Skull feels soft or 'sunken' at the wound
- Convulsions

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Stop bleeding; refer for stitches and tetanus shot |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not put turmeric or mud into a deep wound |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Direct Pressure**: Use a clean, thick cloth. Press directly on the cut for 10 full minutes without lifting.
- **Bandage**: Once bleeding slows, wrap a clean bandage firmly (but not so tight it stops circulation).
- **Head Elevation**: Keep the person sitting up to reduce blood flow to the head.
- **Cover Bone**: If bone is visible, do not push on it; cover gently with a sterile/clean damp cloth and refer.
- **Referral**: Any wound where bone is seen needs specialized cleaning and stitches to prevent brain infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Deep forehead cut with heavy bleeding"] --> B["Apply direct pressure with clean cloth for 10 mins"]
  B --> C["Check for visible bone or sunken skull areas"]
  C --> D["If bone is visible, cover gently without pressure"]
  D --> E["Bandage the wound and keep the head elevated"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for stitches and tetanus injection"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Visible bone | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Uncontrolled bleeding | **Emergency Referral** | Pressure during transport |
| Small cut (<1cm) | PHC for cleaning | Clean with soap and water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Deep head wounds can lead to 'Meningitis' (brain infection) if mud or traditional pastes are put inside. Use only clean water and cloth.

Query: A child fell and has a black eye and some bruising behind the ear. He didn't lose consciousness. Is it serious?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **'Raccoon Eyes'** (Dark bruising around both eyes)
- **'Battle's Sign'** (Bruising on the bone behind the ear)
- Clear fluid leaking from the nose
- Hearing loss

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Urgent Referral** - These are signs of a skull base fracture |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not let the child blow their nose |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Referral**: These specific bruises mean the bottom of the skull is broken. Go to the District Hospital.
- **Don't Plug**: If there is clear fluid or blood in the ear or nose, do NOT plug it with cotton. Let it drain onto a clean cloth.
- **No Nose Blowing**: Tell the child not to blow their nose; it can push air into the brain.
- **Quiet Rest**: Keep the child very still.
- **Monitor**: Watch for fever or neck stiffness over the next few hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify bruising behind ear or around both eyes"] --> B["Check for clear fluid leaking from nose or ear"]
  B --> C["Instruct patient NOT to blow their nose"]
  C --> D["Do NOT block fluid drainage with cotton plugs"]
  D --> E["Keep the patient lying still and quiet"]
  E --> F["Refer to District Hospital for skull base assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bruising behind ear | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Bruising around both eyes | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Clear fluid from nose | **Emergency Referral** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bruising that appears hours after a fall behind the ear or around the eyes is a very serious sign of a deep skull fracture, even if the child is awake.

Query: Can I give an adult Saridon or Disprin for a bad headache after they hit their head?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Headache getting worse** despite rest
- Vomiting
- Confusion
- Drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **NO ASPIRIN/DISPRIN** - It can increase internal bleeding |
| Caregiver | Any | Use only Paracetamol if absolutely necessary after 6 hours |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Aspirin**: Medicines like Disprin thin the blood. If there is a small bleed in the brain, these medicines will make it much bigger.
- **Rest**: The best medicine is a quiet, dark room and sleep (while being monitored).
- **Cold Pack**: Use a cold cloth for pain instead of medicine.
- **Wait 6 Hours**: Do not give any pain medicine for at least 6 hours to ensure no 'Danger Signs' appear.
- **Referral**: If the headache is so bad that they need strong medicine, they should see a doctor first.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient complains of post-injury headache"] --> B["Check for confusion, vomiting, or drowsiness"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to the head for pain relief"]
  C --> D["Strictly avoid Aspirin, Disprin, or Ibuprofen"]
  D --> E["Monitor for 6 hours without medication if possible"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if pain is severe or increasing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe/Worsening pain | **Refer to PHC** | Not safe at home |
| Mild ache/No other signs | Monitor at home | Rest and cold packs |
| Drowsiness after meds | **Emergency Referral** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Giving the wrong painkiller after a head injury can be fatal because it prevents blood from clotting inside the skull.

Query: A child hit his head and had a fit (convulsion) for 2 minutes. Now he is sleeping heavily. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Any seizure/fit** after a head injury
- **Post-fit coma** (cannot be woken up)
- Difficulty breathing
- Foaming at the mouth

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Emergency** - Turn on the side; do not put anything in mouth |
| Adult | Any | Call 108; refer to hospital with a neurologist |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Side Position**: Turn the child onto their left side (Recovery Position) so they don't choke on spit or vomit.
- **Clear Airway**: Ensure the tongue is not blocking the throat.
- **No Objects**: Do NOT put a spoon, key, or finger in the mouth during or after a fit.
- **Note Duration**: Remember how long the fit lasted.
- **Immediate Referral**: A fit after a head injury means the brain is irritated or bleeding. Go to the hospital NOW.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Seizure occurs after head injury"] --> B["Place patient in the side-lying recovery position"]
  B --> C["Ensure the airway is clear and no objects in mouth"]
  C --> D["Protect the head from further injury during movements"]
  D --> E["Note the duration and type of seizure"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizure/Fit | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Deep sleep after fit | **Immediate Emergency** | Side position/Transport |
| Foaming/Tongue bite | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Seizures after trauma are a sign of serious brain distress. The child needs anti-seizure medicine and a scan immediately.

Query: A person fell and has blood coming from one ear, but no cut on the outside. What is this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blood from the ear canal**
- Clear watery fluid from the ear
- Deafness or ringing sound
- Facial drooping (one side of face looks flat)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | **Urgent Referral** - Sign of internal skull fracture |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not clean inside the ear with ear-buds or cloth |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Don't Stop the Flow**: Let the blood or fluid leak out. Do not plug the ear.
- **Cover Loosely**: Place a sterile or very clean gauze/cloth over the ear and tape it lightly.
- **Lying Side**: Have the person lie down with the bleeding ear facing DOWN to help it drain.
- **Immediate Transport**: This indicates a fracture at the base of the skull.
- **Check Movement**: Ask them to show their teeth or smile to check for nerve damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Blood or clear fluid leaking from ear canal"] --> B["Lay patient with the affected ear facing downwards"]
  B --> C["Place a clean cloth loosely over the ear area"]
  C --> D["Do NOT plug the ear or clean inside it"]
  D --> E["Check for facial weakness or drooping"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to a District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Ear bleeding (internal) | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Clear fluid from ear | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Cut on the outer ear | PHC for cleaning | Clean with soap/water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal ear bleeding after a fall is never 'just a scratch'. It means the skull bone protecting the brain is cracked. Rush to the hospital.

Query: How to transport someone who is unconscious after a head injury to the hospital 10km away?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Choking on vomit**
- **Snoring-like breathing** (blocked airway)
- Neck pain or deformity
- Blue lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | **Recovery Position** - Protect the neck and airway |
| Caregiver | Any | Use a flat board or 'charpai' as a stretcher if possible |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Airway First**: Ensure the mouth is clear of vomit. Turn the whole body to the side (Recovery Position).
- **Neck Support**: If you suspect a neck injury, keep the head and neck in a straight line with the body. Use rolled-up clothes on either side of the head.
- **Flat Transport**: Keep the person lying down on a flat surface; do not try to make them sit in a chair or rickshaw if they are dull.
- **Monitor Breath**: Someone must sit with them and check if they are breathing every minute.
- **Speed**: Move steadily but avoid jerky movements that shake the head.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unconscious patient needs transport"] --> B["Clear mouth of any vomit or obstructions"]
  B --> C["Place in side-lying recovery position if no neck injury suspected"]
  C --> D["Stabilize head with rolled clothes on both sides"]
  D --> E["Carry on a flat, firm surface (stretcher/board)"]
  E --> F["Monitor breathing constantly during the journey"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | **Emergency Transport** | Never stay at home |
| Noisy breathing | **Emergency Transport** | Clear airway/Side position |
| Vomiting while asleep | **Emergency Transport** | Turn to side immediately |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The biggest risk during transport is the person choking on their own vomit. Always transport an unconscious person on their side.

Query: A man was hit on the head and now his pupils (black part of eyes) are different sizes. Is this a local eye problem?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unequal pupils** (one big, one small)
- Pupils that don't react to light
- Blurred or double vision
- Drooping eyelid

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Critical Emergency** - Brain pressure is very high |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not put any eye drops |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Torch Test**: Shine a light into both eyes. If one doesn't shrink, it's a brain emergency.
- **Immediate Referral**: This is a sign of 'Herniation' (brain being pushed down due to pressure). Rush to the hospital.
- **Elevate Head**: Keep the head raised at a 30-degree angle (use 2 pillows).
- **Stay Awake**: Talk to the person; do not let them drift into a deep sleep.
- **Oxygen**: If available at a local sub-center, give oxygen during transport.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Observe unequal pupil sizes after head hit"] --> B["Check alertness and ability to follow fingers"]
  B --> C["Elevate the head with pillows at a 30-degree angle"]
  C --> D["Do NOT allow the patient to sleep deeply"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any local eye treatments"]
  E --> F["Rush to a Tertiary Care Hospital (Medical College)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unequal pupils | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Double vision | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Red eyes only (no hit) | Visit PHC | Cold water wash |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Unequal pupils after a head injury are almost always a sign of a life-threatening brain bleed. This cannot be treated at a small clinic.

Query: A school boy hit his head during a match. He feels dizzy and says the lights are too bright. He wants to keep playing. Should I let him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sensitivity to light or noise**
- Dizziness or feeling 'in a fog'
- Slow response to questions
- Balance problems

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Youth/Student | Any | **Stop Playing Immediately** - Risk of 'Second Impact Syndrome' |
| Teacher/ASHA | Any | Remove from sun; rest in a cool, quiet place |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Strict Rest**: He must not play, run, or study for the rest of the day.
- **Quiet Environment**: Keep him in a dark, quiet room. No TV or mobile phones.
- **Observation**: Check him every 30 minutes for vomiting or confusion.
- **No Second Hit**: A second hit to the head before the first one heals can be fatal within minutes.
- **Referral**: If dizziness doesn't go away in 1 hour, take him to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child feels dizzy or light-sensitive after hit"] --> B["Remove immediately from sports or physical activity"]
  B --> C["Place in a quiet, dark, well-ventilated room"]
  C --> D["Monitor for nausea or worsening headache"]
  D --> E["Prevent any further head contact or jumping"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if symptoms persist beyond 1 hour"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness > 1 hour | Refer to PHC | Quiet rest |
| Light sensitivity | Refer to PHC | Dark room |
| Brief dizziness (<5m) | Monitor at home | No sports for 1 week |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even 'mild' concussions need brain rest. Returning to play too early can lead to permanent brain damage or sudden death if another hit occurs.

Query: Is it okay to use a 'potli' (warm compress) with salt or ajwain on a head bump?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bump that is soft/mushy**
- Headache worsening with heat
- Dizziness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | **Use COLD, not hot** - Heat increases swelling in the brain |
| Caregiver | Any | Use a cold wet cloth or ice wrapped in a towel |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cold Only**: For the first 48 hours, only use cold packs. This shrinks blood vessels and stops swelling.
- **Avoid Heat**: Warm 'potli' or hot oil massage will make the inflammation and swelling worse.
- **Gentle Pressure**: You can hold the cold pack with very light pressure.
- **Simple Herbs**: You can apply a paste of Sandalwood (Chandan) which is cooling, but only on the skin, NOT on a cut.
- **Monitor**: If the bump gets bigger than a lemon, see a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Visible swelling/bump on the head"] --> B["Check for any broken skin or leaking fluid"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress/ice pack for 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Strictly avoid any heat or hot oil massage"]
  D --> E["Repeat cold compress every 2 hours for 24 hours"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if bump is very large or soft"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Very large/soft bump | Refer to PHC | Cold pack only |
| Small 'egg' bump | Monitor at home | Cold pack and rest |
| Pain not going away | Refer to PHC | Cold pack/Paracetamol |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Local tradition often suggests heat for injuries, but for the head, heat is dangerous as it increases blood flow and pressure inside the skull.

Query: A drunk man fell and hit his head. He is sleeping and snoring loudly. He won't wake up. Is it just the alcohol?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cannot be woken up** (even with a pinch)
- **Snoring/Labored breathing**
- Vomiting while unconscious
- Head injury visible

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | **Urgent Referral** - Alcohol hides brain injury symptoms |
| Caregiver | Any | Assume it is a head injury, not just 'being drunk' |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Don't Assume**: Never assume someone is 'just sleeping it off'. Alcoholics are at very high risk for brain bleeds.
- **Side Position**: Put him in the Recovery Position immediately to prevent choking on vomit.
- **Pain Response**: Pinch the back of his arm hard. If he doesn't move, he is in a coma.
- **Check Breathing**: If snoring is very loud, his tongue might be blocking his air.
- **Referral**: Take him to the CHC. Doctors need to check if the drowsiness is from booze or a bleed.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Intoxicated person with head injury is sleeping"] --> B["Attempt to wake with loud voice and firm pinch"]
  B --> C["If no response, place immediately in side position"]
  C --> D["Check for scalp wounds or bruising behind ears"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing rate and snoring sounds"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Won't wake up | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Vomiting | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Awake/Talking | Monitor for 12 hours | Quiet rest/No more alcohol |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcohol thins the blood and makes the brain more likely to bleed after a fall. 'Sleeping it off' can result in death if there is an internal injury.

Query: A woman hit her head 2 days ago. Now she has a severe headache and her neck is very stiff. She has a mild fever. Is this related?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stiff neck** (cannot touch chin to chest)
- **High fever**
- Severe headache
- Sensitivity to light

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | **Emergency** - Could be Meningitis (brain infection) |
| Caregiver | Any | Do not give fever medicine and wait; go to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Referral**: Fever and stiff neck after an injury are signs of infection in the brain lining.
- **Neck Test**: Ask her to look down at her belly button. If it causes severe pain or she can't do it, go to the hospital.
- **Keep Cool**: Use a damp cloth on the forehead for the fever during transport.
- **Darkness**: Keep her eyes covered with a cloth if light hurts.
- **No Solid Food**: Keep her on sips of water until seen by a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fever and stiff neck developed after injury"] --> B["Check if patient can touch chin to chest"]
  B --> C["Assess for light sensitivity and severe headache"]
  C --> D["Apply cool damp cloth for fever relief"]
  D --> E["Shield eyes from bright light with a cloth"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for infection check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stiff neck + Fever | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Headache only | PHC visit | Rest/Cold pack |
| High fever/Confusion | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infections can enter the brain through small, unseen cracks in the skull after an injury. This is a life-threatening condition.

Query: A child fell and is now acting 'very quiet' and 'not himself'. He is not crying. Should I be worried?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Change in personality** (too quiet or too angry)
- Refusing to play
- Not recognizing parents
- Staring into space

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | **Refer for Observation** - Behavior change is a sign of brain stress |
| Parent | Any | Do not scold the child; keep them calm and resting |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Quiet Test**: Try to engage the child with their favorite toy or food. If they don't care, it's a sign of a head injury.
- **Count Breaths**: Check if their breathing is regular.
- **No Screen Time**: No mobile phones or TV; the light and noise will stress the brain more.
- **Dark Room**: Let them rest in a quiet, dark place.
- **Referral**: Take the child to a PHC. An ASHA should accompany them to explain the behavior change to the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child appears unusually quiet after head hit"] --> B["Check if child recognizes family or favorite toys"]
  B --> C["Monitor for staring spells or lack of response"]
  C --> D["Place child in a dark, quiet, screen-free room"]
  D --> E["Assess for vomiting or walking difficulty"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if child remains 'not himself'"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Personality change | **Urgent Referral** | Never |
| Refusing to eat | Refer to PHC | Monitor for 6 hours |
| Crying then playing | Monitor at home | Quiet rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In children, the brain doesn't always show symptoms like adults. Being 'too quiet' after a fall is often a sign of a concussion.

Query: A man was hit on the back of the head. Now he says he cannot see anything, even though his eyes look normal. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden blindness**
- Blurred or 'curtain' over vision
- Seeing flashes of light
- Pupil not reacting to torch light

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | **Emergency** - The back of the head controls vision |
| Caregiver | Any | Keep the patient calm; blindness causes great panic |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Referral**: A hit to the back of the head (occipital area) can damage the vision center. Go to a hospital with an Eye Specialist and a CT scan.
- **Panic Control**: Hold the person's hand and talk to them. Panic increases blood pressure, which can worsen internal bleeding.
- **Lying Down**: Keep the person lying flat with the head slightly raised.
- **No Eye Rubbing**: Do not let them rub their eyes.
- **Transport**: Use an ambulance or a car where they can lie down.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Loss of vision after hit to back of head"] --> B["Reassure and calm the patient to lower BP"]
  B --> C["Check if pupils react to torch light"]
  C --> D["Keep the patient lying still with head raised"]
  D --> E["Prevent the patient from rubbing their eyes"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for brain and eye scan"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vision loss | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Flashes of light | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Redness only | Visit PHC | Cold water wash |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Vision loss after a head injury is a sign of serious brain trauma. Even if it 'comes back', the person must be seen by a specialist immediately.

Query: An ASHA worker is at a home where a 10-year-old girl fell. She has clear water dripping from her nose like a tap. Is it just a cold?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Clear fluid from nose** that tastes salty/sweet
- Fluid increases when leaning forward
- Headache
- Metallic taste in mouth

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | **Emergency** - This is brain fluid (CSF) leaking |
| ASHA | Any | Do not let her sniff or blow her nose |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The Halo Test**: Drip some of the fluid onto a clean white cloth. If a yellow ring forms around a red spot, it is brain fluid.
- **No Sniffing**: Tell her NOT to sniff the fluid back in or blow her nose. This can cause a brain infection.
- **Head Up**: Keep her sitting completely upright to reduce the pressure and the leak.
- **Immediate Referral**: This is a life-threatening leak of the fluid that protects the brain. Go to the District Hospital.
- **Antibiotics**: She will need special medicines from the hospital to prevent Meningitis.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Clear fluid dripping from nose after injury"] --> B["Instruct patient NOT to blow or sniff nose"]
  B --> C["Check if fluid increases when leaning forward"]
  C --> D["Sit the patient completely upright"]
  D --> E["Collect a sample of fluid on a clean white cloth"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for skull base repair"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Clear fluid leak | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Salty taste in mouth | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Normal runny nose | Monitor at home | Steam/Warm water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Brain fluid (CSF) looks like water but its leak means there is a direct opening between the nose and the brain. Infection risk is extremely high.

Query: A man fell and is complaining of neck pain after hitting his head. His hands are feeling 'tingly' or numb. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Neck pain** after head injury
- **Numbness/Tingling** in hands or feet
- Electric shock-like feelings in the back
- Unable to move fingers or toes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Do NOT move the person** - Potential spine injury |
| Caregiver | Any | Use the 'Log Roll' technique only if they must be moved |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Freeze**: Tell the person not to move their head at all.
- **Stabilize**: Place heavy bags of sand, grain, or rolled-up thick blankets on both sides of the neck to keep the head from turning.
- **Transport on Board**: Only move them on a hard, flat board (like a door or wooden plank). Use 4 people to lift as one unit.
- **No Pillow**: Do not put a pillow under the head; keep the neck flat and straight.
- **Immediate Referral**: This is a spinal cord emergency. Go to a hospital with an Orthopedic Surgeon.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Neck pain and tingling in limbs after injury"] --> B["Tell patient to remain completely still"]
  B --> C["Stabilize neck using sandbags or rolled clothes"]
  C --> D["Prepare a hard, flat board for transport"]
  D --> E["Lift patient as one unit (no twisting/bending)"]
  E --> F["Rush to a surgical center or District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Tingling/Numbness | **Immediate Emergency** | Never |
| Neck stiffness/Pain | **Emergency Referral** | Never |
| Scalp pain only | Monitor for 24 hours | Cold pack/Rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A head injury often comes with a neck injury. Moving the neck can cause permanent paralysis (inability to move limbs forever). Keep the neck still!

Query: A boy was hit by a cricket ball on his head. He was dizzy and forgot where he was for a minute. Now he says he is fine but has a headache. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Repeated vomiting** (more than once)
- **Worsening headache** that does not go away
- Seizures or fits
- Weakness in arms or legs
- Slurred speech or confusion
- One pupil (black part of eye) larger than the other

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Stop all play; keep in a quiet room; monitor for 24 hours |
| Adult | Any | Rest in a dark room; avoid mobile/TV; no heavy work |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Physical Rest**: Make the person lie down immediately. No running or working.
- **Brain Rest**: Keep the person away from bright lights, loud noises, and mobile screens.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply a cold cloth to the site of the hit to reduce swelling.
- **No Medicine**: Do NOT give Aspirin or Disprin as they can increase internal bleeding.
- **Check Consciousness**: Wake the person every 2 hours during the first night to ensure they respond normally.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess if person had any loss of consciousness"] --> B["Place person in a quiet, dark room for rest"]
  B --> C["Check pupils for equal size and light reaction"]
  C --> D["Monitor for vomiting or increased confusion"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give painkillers or blood thinners"]
```
