| Decreased baby movement | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Hard/Sore stomach in pregnancy | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Placental separation can happen from minor trauma and cause hidden internal bleeding (abruption). Do not delay referral.

query: A person has fainted multiple times today without any visible injury. Their stool is very black and smelly. They are extremely weak.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Black, 'tarry', smelly stool (Melena)
- Vomiting dark or red blood
- Fainting when sitting or standing

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Lay flat, lift legs, transport immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Treat for Shock:** Since they fainted, lay them flat and elevate the legs. This helps the little remaining blood reach the brain.
- **No Food:** Do not give any food or water. They may choke.
- **Sample:** Ask the family to keep the stool sample to show the doctor.
- **Local Remedy:** Use no traditional powders or leaves; this is deep internal bleeding.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Black tarry stools with fainting"] --> B["Lay person flat and lift both legs"]
  B --> C["Check pulse and breathing continuously"]
  C --> D["Save a sample of the black stool for the doctor"]
  D --> E["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC with blood transfusion facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Black, smelly stools | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fainting/Weakness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Black stools are a classic sign of hidden bleeding in the upper stomach or intestine. It is an emergency.

query: A child fainted after getting hit by a cricket ball in the left belly. They woke up, but now they are very quiet and want to sleep.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fainting after belly trauma
- Extreme sleepiness (cannot be woken easily)
- Child is unusually pale or quiet

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Lay flat, raise legs, cover with blanket, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Pulse:** Check the pulse at the wrist or neck. If it is fast (over 110) or weak, suspect internal bleeding.
- **Position:** Lay the child flat and lift the feet 12 inches.
- **Stay Alert:** Try to keep the child talking to monitor their consciousness level.
- **No Food:** Do not give any sugar-water or milk.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with belly trauma, sleepy"] --> B["Check pulse and breathing immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay child flat and raise both feet 12 inches"]
  C --> D["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any liquids or home medicine"]
  E --> F["Transport child to CHC/District Hospital for scan"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sleepy child after trauma | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fainting after belly hit | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hits to the left upper belly can damage the spleen and cause rapid, hidden internal bleeding.

query: A person fell into a deep dry well (or pit). Their body looks twisted, and they are groaning, but no blood is visible. People are trying to pull them out with ropes.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Groaning or unconsciousness
- Twisted body/Limbs at strange angles (Possible bone fracture/spine injury)
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT use simple ropes to pull, secure neck/spine first, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Spinal Immobilization:** Before moving, assume a spine injury. Secure the head, neck, and torso together (like a single piece of wood).
- **Rescue Method:** Do not pull with ropes around the armpits; use a door, plank, or stretcher if possible.
- **Handle Gently:** Move with absolute smoothness. Sudden jolts can kill the patient by increasing nerve damage or internal bleeding.
- **Maintain Temperature:** Keep them warm on the way to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fall into well with twisted body"] --> B["Check for consciousness and breathing"]
  B --> C["Prevent ALL movement of head, neck, and torso"]
  C --> D["Do NOT pull the patient vertically with ropes"] 
  D --> E["Rescue vertically using a rigid stretcher or plank only"]
  E --> F["Transfer using flat transport (door/board) to CHC"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fall from height/pit | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Twisted body/Groaning | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Falling with blunt force can crush internal organs. No traditional balm can treat this; hospital is the only option.

query: A woman has severe lower belly pain and fainted. Her last period was 2 months ago. Her family wants to use a traditional clay poultice on her belly.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fainting after belly pain in reproductive age
- History of missed period
- Stomach feels hard, tight, or tender
- Pale skin, cold sweat

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Lay flat, lift legs, no poultice, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Position for Shock:** Lay her flat and lift the feet 12 inches high. Fainting after lower belly pain is a sign of internal bleeding from an ectopic pregnancy.
- **No Poultice:** Absolutely do not use a traditional clay poultice, hot pack, or massage. This will worsen the bleeding.
- **Nothing to Drink:** Do not give any water or medicine by mouth.
- **Immediate Referral:** Rush to a hospital with a maternity specialist and surgery facility.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Severe belly pain, missed period, fainted"] --> B["Lay woman flat and raise both feet immediately"]
  B --> C["Check pulse and breathing closely"]
  C --> D["Do NOT massage belly or apply poultices"]
  D --> E["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or rush to hospital with surgery facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fainting + belly pain in reproductive age | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Stomach hard or tight | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
lower abdominal bleeding from a ruptured ectopic pregnancy can be life-threatening in minutes.

query: A man fainted and then hit his head. Now he has fainted again, has red eyes, and his nose is bleeding clear fluid. Is this internal blood?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- One pupil larger than the other
- Repeated fainting (Faint -> Wake -> Faint)
- Fluid leakage (clear or red) from ears or nose
- Very red eyes

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Do NOT plug the nose, keep head still, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Airway Check:** Since he fainted, check breathing. If vomiting, turn his whole body together onto the side (recovery position).
- **Do NOT Plug:** Do not put cotton, cloth, or any traditional powder inside the nose or ears. The fluid/blood must come out.
- **Keep Head Still:** Immobilize the head and neck using bricks or rolled blankets.
- **Refer:** Clear fluid from the nose after a head hit is a critical sign of internal bleeding. Rush to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Repeated fainting, nasal fluid after head hit"] --> B["Check breathing and airway immediately"]
  B --> C["Ensure the fluid can drain from the nose and ears"]
  C --> D["Prevent ALL movement of the head and neck"] 
  D --> E["Do NOT apply pressure to the nose or head wound"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital with CT scan facility urgently"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fluid from nose/ears after head hit | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Repeated fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is a sign of a critical head injury. No amount of home rest or oil massage can save him.

query: A child has multiple purple bruises on their arms and torso without any trauma. Now they have a large bruise on their left eye and are sleepy.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Multiple purple bruises in different places without trauma
- Bleeding from gums or nose that is hard to stop
- Sleepiness/Confusion after head bruise

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Handle child gently, check consciousness, refer to District Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **gentle handling:** This is likely a blood clotting disorder (like hemophilia) and can cause spontaneous internal bleeding. Handle the child with extreme gentleness.
- **consciousness:** Check if you can wake the child easily. Head bruises with sleepiness are critical signs.
- **No Food:** Do not give any food or liquid.
- **Referral:** Go to a hospital with a hematologist or specialized blood care unit.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Spontaneous bruises, child sleepy after head bruise"] --> B["Check consciousness level carefully"]
  B --> C["Handle child with extreme gentleness to prevent new bleeds"]
  C --> D["Check pulse and breathing"]
  D --> E["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  E --> F["Transport urgently to District Hospital/Hematology unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Spontaneous bruises/gums bleed | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Head bruise + sleepiness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A simple bump on the head in a child with a clotting disorder can cause life-threatening internal bleeding.

query: A woman says she feels a 'bubbling' feeling inside her chest after a bad fit of coughing. She is pale and her heart is pounding.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Breathing difficulty or chest pain
- 'Bubbling' feeling inside the chest
- Skin turning pale or blue
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Keep sitting slightly up if breathing is hard, otherwise lay flat |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Position:** If breathing is difficult, keep her chest raised. If she feels faint, lay her flat with legs raised.
- **Loosen Clothing:** Unbutton tight blouses or waistbands to help breathing.
- **Comfort:** Stay with her and keep her calm. Anxiety increases heart rate.
- **Referral:** Go to the hospital for a chest X-ray. 'Bubbling' inside could be blood or air in the chest cavity.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bubbling chest, pale after severe cough fit"] --> B["Check pulse and breathing carefully"]
  B --> C["If breathing is difficult, propped her head and chest up"]
  C --> D["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any Liquids or home medicine"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital for chest internal check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| 'Bubbling' feeling in chest | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fast heart rate/Pale skin | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Coughing fits can burst blood vessels in the lung cavity. This is hidden internal bleeding.

query: A child swallowed a small, sharp piece of bone or metal. He says his stomach hurts and has a large bruise on his chest.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Chest or belly pain
- Hard, 'board-like' stomach
- Vomiting red or coffee-colored blood
- Coughing up blood

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Do NOT make him vomit, keep flat, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Vomit:** This is the most important rule. Making him vomit will cause the sharp object to tear the esophagus or stomach again.
- **No Food:** Do not give any food or liquid.
- **Stay Still:** Do not let the child run or jump. Keep him completely still.
- **Referral:** Rush to a hospital with a surgery facility and pediatric specialist.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child swallowed sharp object, belly pain"] --> B["Check breathing and conscious state"]
  B --> C["Do NOT attempt to make the child vomit"]
  C --> D["Ensure child is kept calm and completely still"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any food or liquids by mouth"]
  E --> F["Transport child urgently to CHC with surgery facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sharp object swallowed/Belly pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Stomach hard or board-like | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A sharp internal object can puncture blood vessels, causing hidden, life-threatening internal bleeding.

query: A person fell into some bushes. Now they have blood in their urine. They say they have severe belly pain. Can I give traditional herbs for urine problems?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pink, red, or dark brown (coffee-colored) urine
- Pain when you touch the belly or back
- Shock signs (pale, cold sweat)

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Lay flat, lift legs, do not give water, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Treat for Shock:** Since they have pain and a trauma history, lay them flat and raise the legs to prevent collapse.
- **No Food:** Do not give any water, tea, or food.
- **No Home Remedy:** Do not use any traditional 'urine-clearing' powders, oils, or leaves.
- **Save Sample:** If possible, save a small sample of the urine to show the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fall, red urine, severe belly pain"] --> B["Check breathing and consciousness"]
  B --> C["Lay the person flat and raise both legs immediately"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any Liquids or home medicine"]
  D --> E["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  E --> F["Transport urgently to a hospital with surgery facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pink, red, brown urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Belly pain after trauma | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Red urine after a fall is a critical sign of internal bleeding from the kidney or bladder. Home remedy can worsen this.

query: An old man fainted without any trauma. His stool is pink and watery. He is very confused.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pink, red, or dark watery stool (Hematochezia)
- Confusion/Extreme sleepiness
- Weak, fast pulse

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Lay flat, raise legs, keep warm, transport immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Treat for Shock:** Keep the old man lying down on his back with legs elevated. Fainting after belly trauma is a sign of internal bleeding.
- **No Food:** Do not give any sugar-water or food.
- **Sample:** Save a small amount of the pink/watery stool for the doctor.
- **Referral:** Rush to a hospital with a surgery facility and specialized internal care.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly, pink watery stool, confused"] --> B["Lay person flat on their back, lift legs"] 
  B --> C["Check pulse and breathing carefully"]
  C --> D["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any liquids or home medicine"]
  E --> F["Transport immediately to CHC with blood transfusion unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pink, red, or black stools | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion/Weakness in elderly | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pink or red stool indicates hidden bleeding in the colon, rectum, or upper intestine. It can cause critical blood loss.

query: A child has a bruise behind their ear after falling. Now they can't wake up properly. What should I check?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bruise behind the ear (Battle's sign)
- Cannot be woken easily (Unconscious/Stupor)
- Clear fluid or blood leakage from ears or nose
- One pupil larger than the other

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Keep head still, don't shake, rush to District Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Spinal Immobilization:** Before moving, assume a spine injury. Secure the head, neck, and torso together (like a single piece of wood).
- **Check consciousness:** Try to wake the child with a loud voice. If they don't wake properly, this is critical.
- **Handle Gently:** Move with absolute smoothness. Sudden jolts can kill the patient by increasing nerve damage or internal bleeding.
- **Referral:** Rush to a hospital with a pediatric intensive care unit and CT scan facility.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with ear bruise cannot be woken"] --> B["Check for consciousness, airway, and breathing carefully"]
  B --> C["Prevent ALL movement of head, neck, and torso"]
  C --> D["Ensure any fluid can drain from the nose and ears"]
  D --> E["Monitor vital signs (pulse, breathing) closely"]
  E --> F["Transport urgently to District Hospital/Pediatric Intensive care unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fall from height/pit | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Bruise behind ear/Nas fluid after head hit | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A bruise behind the ear is a clear sign of a skull base fracture, which is an immediate emergency. Do not attempt any traditional remedy.

query: A person has many deep bruises on their legs and arms without any trauma. They say they feel warm and have a fever.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Multiple bruises without trauma history
- Spontaneous bleeding from gums or nose
- Red-purple dots on skin (petechiae)
- Shock signs (pale, cold sweat)

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Handle gently, do not give water, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Gently handling:** A patient with spontaneous bruises could have internal bleeding, so handle them with care to avoid new bruises.
- **No Food:** Do not give any water or liquid.
- **Comfort:** Stay with the patient and keep them calm.
- **Referral:** Go to a hospital with a hematologist or specialized blood care unit.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Spontaneous bruises, fever, gum bleed"] --> B["Check consciousness level carefully"]
  B --> C["Handle child with extreme gentleness to prevent new bleeds"]
  C --> D["Check pulse and breathing"]
  D --> E["Maintain body warmth with a blanket"]
  E --> F["Transport child urgently to District Hospital/Pediatric Hematology unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple deep bruises | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Gum/Nose bleed | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A simple bump on the head in a patient with a clotting disorder can cause a life-threatening internal bleed. Do not delay referral.

query: A boy fell from a mango tree. His arm is bent in a strange way and he is screaming in pain. There is a lot of swelling. How can I help him using things from the village?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bone is sticking out through the skin
- Fingers or toes are turning blue, cold, or numb
- No pulse felt below the injury site
- Severe bleeding that doesn't stop
- Weakness or fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Keep the child still, support the arm with a cloth sling, give comfort |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Use a wooden stick as a splint, tie firmly but not tight, call 108 |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk of shock; do not move the person, support the limb with pillows |
| Pregnant | Female | Lay her on her left side if possible while supporting the fracture |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding:** If there is a wound, press a clean cloth on it gently.
- **Immobilize:** Do NOT try to straighten the bone. Keep it in the position you found it.
- **Make a Splint:** Use a flat piece of wood, a thick rolled-up newspaper, or a sturdy cardboard. Place it along the bone.
- **Tie the Splint:** Use strips of cloth or a dupatta to tie the splint. Tie above and below the break. Do not tie over the break.
- **Local Knowledge:** Apply a cold compress using a cloth dipped in **cold water or ice** to reduce swelling. Do NOT apply hot oil or massage.
- **Support:** For an arm, use a triangular cloth to make a 'sling' around the neck.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess for bleeding and bone exposure"] --> B["Cover any open wound with a clean cloth"]
  B --> C["Place a rigid splint like wood or cardboard along the limb"]
  C --> D["Secure splint with cloth strips above and below the injury"]
  D --> E["Check if fingers or toes are warm and have feeling"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray and plaster"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bone sticking out | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Blue/Cold fingers | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Strange angle/deformity | Refer for X-ray | Never |
| Mild pain, can move limb | Refer to PHC | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is only temporary support. A fracture needs a proper X-ray and a plaster cast (P.O.P) by a doctor. Poorly set bones can lead to lifelong disability.

query: An old man tripped and fell on his hip. He cannot stand up and one leg looks shorter than the other. What is the first aid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Unable to move the leg at all
- Severe pain in the groin or hip
- Leg is rotated outward strangely

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Do NOT move him; tie both legs together for support, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Still:** Do not try to lift him or make him walk. 
- **Leg Splinting:** Use the good leg as a splint. Place a blanket or soft cloth between the legs and tie them together gently at the ankles and knees.
- **Pillows:** Place rolled-up clothes or pillows around the hip to prevent any movement.
- **Nothing by Mouth:** Do not give water or food in case surgery is needed.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient cannot stand after hip fall"] --> B["Check pulse and skin temperature of both feet"]
  B --> C["Place soft padding between the legs"]
  C --> D["Tie legs together gently at knees and ankles"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and transport on a flat hard board"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| One leg shorter than other | Emergency - Rush to CHC | Never |
| Pain in groin area | Refer to CHC immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hip fractures in the elderly are very serious. Do not attempt a massage or 'Pahalwan' bone setting.

query: A worker's leg was crushed by a heavy stone. The bone is seen through the skin and there is dirt in the wound. What do I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Visible bone** (Open Fracture)
- Dirt or mud inside the wound
- Heavy spurting of blood

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Control bleeding, cover bone with clean cloth, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cover the Bone:** Do NOT try to push the bone back in. Cover it with a clean, dry cloth immediately.
- **Clean the Edges:** Gently wipe away dirt from *around* the wound, but do not wash inside the wound.
- **Splinting:** Secure a long stick or board from the hip to the ankle to stop the leg from moving.
- **Refer:** This is a high-risk infection case. Rush to the hospital for Tetanus and surgery.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Visible bone and open wound"] --> B["Cover wound and bone with a clean dry cloth"]
  B --> C["Apply pressure to stop bleeding nearby"]
  C --> D["Splint the entire leg to prevent any movement"]
  D --> E["Do NOT wash the bone with water or apply oils"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC/District hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bone seen in wound | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dirty wound | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Open fractures can lead to severe infection (Sepsis) or loss of limb if not treated with hospital antibiotics within hours.

query: A child fell while running. His wrist is swollen and he cannot hold his pencil. Is it a break or a sprain?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling that increases very fast
- Bruising appearing quickly
- Wrist looks 's-shaped' or bent

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Apply a cold pack, make a small splint, visit PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Rest:** Tell the child to keep the hand still.
- **Ice:** Use a cloth dipped in cold water. This is the best local remedy to stop internal swelling.
- **Splint:** Use a flat wooden scale or piece of cardboard under the wrist. Tie it loosely.
- **Refer:** If the child cannot move his fingers or has no feeling, go to the PHC today.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with swollen wrist"] --> B["Apply cold water compress for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply a flat cardboard splint under the wrist"]
  C --> D["Wrap with a clean bandage or cloth dupatta"]
  D --> E["Check finger movement and warmth"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC for evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot move fingers | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Swelling not going down | Refer for X-ray | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Never 'pull' the fingers or wrist to 'reset' them. This can damage nerves and blood vessels.

query: A farmer's leg was hit by a plough. The shin is swollen and he can't walk. I suspect a fracture. How do I transport him to the clinic?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Leg cannot bear any weight
- Grating sound when the leg moves
- Skin over the bone is very tight and shiny

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Splint from ankle to knee, use a stretcher, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Splinting:** Find two long sticks. Place one on the inside and one on the outside of the leg. Tie them in 3-4 places.
- **Padding:** Put clothes or hay between the stick and the skin so it doesn't hurt.
- **Transport:** Do not let him sit in a rickshaw with the leg hanging. Use a flat charpai or a board as a stretcher.
- **Local Knowledge:** You may apply a **Turmeric (Haldi) paste** *around* the swelling to help, but don't move the bone to do it.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Leg hit, cannot walk"] --> B["Apply sticks as splints on both sides of leg"]
  B --> C["Add soft cloth padding between sticks and leg"]
  C --> D["Tie sticks firmly with cloth strips"]
  D --> E["Carry patient on a flat board or charpai"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot walk | Refer for X-ray | Never |
| Grating bone sound | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Moving a fractured leg without a splint can cause the sharp bone to cut an artery inside.

query: A girl fell from a bicycle and her ankle is very big and blue. Her father wants to use a hot oil massage. Should I stop him?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Intense pain that doesn't stop
- Skin color turning very dark or purple
- Foot feels cold to touch

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | Stop the massage, apply cold pack, elevate leg |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NO HEAT:** Tell the father that hot oil or heat will increase the internal bleeding and swelling. 
- **Cold is Best:** Use cold water or a wet cloth. This is the correct natural way to calm the injury.
- **Elevation:** Keep the ankle higher than the level of the heart using pillows.
- **Refer:** If she cannot move her toes, she must see a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Swollen blue ankle"] --> B["Stop all hot oil massages immediately"]
  B --> C["Elevate the ankle using pillows or cushions"]
  C --> D["Apply cold water compress every hour"]
  D --> E["Wrap ankle loosely with a cloth bandage"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue/Cold foot | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Rapid swelling | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Massaging a fracture is dangerous and can cause permanent joint stiffness or nerve damage.

query: An ASHA worker sees a woman with a forearm injury. The bone isn't sticking out but the arm is shaped like a spoon. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- 'Spoon' or 'Dinner fork' shape of the wrist
- Numbness in the fingers
- Inability to rotate the hand

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Make a splint, use a sling, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immobilize:** Keep the hand and elbow from moving.
- **Splint:** Place a flat piece of wood from the elbow to the fingers.
- **Sling:** Use a dupatta or triangular bandage to support the weight of the arm.
- **Check:** Ensure the bandage is not so tight that the fingers turn blue.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Deformed forearm shape"] --> B["Place flat splint under the arm"]
  B --> C["Secure splint with cloth ties"]
  C --> D["Apply a triangular sling for support"]
  D --> E["Check for finger pulse and warmth"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for bone setting"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deformity (strange shape) | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Numbness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Forearm fractures need perfect alignment or the woman will never be able to cook or work properly again.

query: A man has a broken thigh bone (femur) after a bike accident. He is in shock and very pale. What are the rural first-aid steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pale skin, sweating, and fast heart rate (Shock)
- Thigh is much thicker than the other one (Internal bleeding)
- Extreme pain

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Long splint from armpit to ankle, call 108, treat shock |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Shock Treatment:** Lay him flat. Do not give water.
- **Long Splint:** Use a very long board or two poles. Tie from the armpit all the way down to the ankle. 
- **Tie Both Legs:** Tie the injured leg to the healthy leg for extra support.
- **Refer:** This is a life-threatening injury. Rush to a District Hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Thigh injury with signs of shock"] --> B["Lay patient flat on a hard surface"]
  B --> C["Apply a long splint from armpit to ankle"]
  C --> D["Tie both legs together for extra support"]
  D --> E["Cover with a blanket to keep warm"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Thigh fracture | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Pale/Sweating skin | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A broken thigh bone can cause a person to lose 1.5 liters of blood internally. It is a major emergency.

query: A child has a finger caught in a door. The tip is hanging and the bone is broken. How to manage in the village?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Tip of finger is pale or blue
- Heavy bleeding from the nail bed

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Clean gently, tape to next finger, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Clean:** Wash with clean water.
- **Buddy Splint:** Place a small piece of cotton between the broken finger and the healthy finger next to it. Tape or tie them together gently.
- **Elevation:** Keep the child's hand raised above the heart level to stop throbbing pain.
- **Natural Remedy:** A drop of **Honey** can be put on a small cut, but don't use it if the bone is showing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Crushed/Broken finger"] --> B["Gently wash with clean water"]
  B --> C["Place cotton between broken and next finger"]
  C --> D["Tie both fingers together (Buddy Splint)"]
  D --> E["Raise hand high above heart level"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC for cleaning and Tetanus shot"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bone showing | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Blue finger tip | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Finger fractures need careful treatment to ensure the child can use their hand properly later in life.

query: A man fell and says he heard a 'crack' in his shoulder. He is holding his elbow with his other hand. What should an ASHA worker do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Shoulder looks square instead of round
- Numbness in the whole arm
- Pulse at wrist is missing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT move shoulder, use a sling and swathe, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sling:** Use a large dupatta to support the forearm.
- **Swathe:** Use another cloth to tie the arm against the chest so it doesn't swing.
- **Padding:** Place a small soft cloth or towel in the armpit for comfort.
- **Refer:** This could be a collarbone break or a shoulder dislocation. Do NOT try to 'pop' it back in.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Shoulder/Collarbone injury"] --> B["Place a support pad in the armpit"]
  B --> C["Apply a triangular sling for the forearm"]
  C --> D["Tie the arm to the body with a wide cloth"]
  D --> E["Check for pulse at the wrist"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deformed shoulder | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Can't move fingers | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shoulder injuries can damage major nerves. Never let a local 'bone setter' pull on the arm.

query: A person has a suspected leg fracture and we are in a remote field. No sticks or wood around. How do I splint it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling spreading fast
- Foot feels numb

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Tie both legs together, use the 'Good' leg as the splint |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Leg-to-Leg Splint:** Place the injured leg next to the healthy leg.
- **Padding:** Put grass, leaves, or extra clothes between the knees and ankles.
- **Tying:** Use a belt, dupatta, or strips of clothes to tie the legs together at the ankles, knees, and thighs.
- **Transport:** Carry the person out on a blanket or flat surface.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Leg fracture, no splint available"] --> B["Straighten the healthy leg next to the injured one"]
  B --> C["Place soft padding between the legs"]
  C --> D["Tie both legs together firmly at three points"]
  D --> E["Check foot temperature and color"]
  E --> F["Transport to the nearest road/CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Suspected fracture | Always Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even without a wooden splint, you must stop the bone from moving to prevent more damage.

query: A boy has a deep cut and a broken bone in his leg. Blood is oozing out. What is the priority?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Heavy bleeding
- Exposed bone
- Shock (pale/faint)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Stop bleed first, cover wound, splint leg, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pressure:** Apply direct pressure *around* the bone to stop bleeding. Do not press on the bone itself.
- **Cover:** Use a clean, sterile cloth to cover the open wound.
- **Splint:** Secure the leg with sticks without moving the wound area.
- **Refer:** This is an 'Open Fracture'. It is an emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Open fracture with bleeding"] --> B["Apply direct pressure to stop bleeding"]
  B --> C["Cover wound with the cleanest cloth available"]
  C --> D["Splint leg with wood or sticks"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply oils or herbs to open bone"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital for surgery and Tetanus shot"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Open wound with fracture | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Open fractures need hospital cleaning. If not cleaned properly, the bone can get a permanent infection.

query: A woman fell and her elbow is locked in a bent position. She is in extreme pain. Can I try to move it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Numbness in the hand
- No pulse at the wrist
- Elbow looks completely out of place

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Do NOT move the elbow, splint as it is, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Movement:** Do not try to straighten or bend the elbow further. It may crush the main artery.
- **Splint in Place:** Use two sticks to form a 'V' or support it with pillows in the position it is locked.
- **Sling:** Support the forearm with a sling.
- **Check Fingers:** Make sure she can still wiggle her fingers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Locked/Deformed elbow"] --> B["Check for pulse and sensation in the hand"]
  B --> C["Support the elbow with pillows in current position"]
  C --> D["Use a sling to keep the arm weight off the elbow"]
  D --> E["Do NOT try to straighten the arm"]
  E --> F["Transport urgently to CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Locked joint | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Pulse missing at wrist | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Elbow fractures are very dangerous for blood supply. Minutes matter if the pulse is gone.

query: A child has a leg injury. How do I know if the splint I tied is too tight?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Toes are blue or pale
- Toes feel cold
- Child says the foot is 'asleep' or tingling
- Child cannot feel you touching their toes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Loosen the ties immediately, but do not remove the splint |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check:** Look at the color of the toes every 15 minutes.
- **Loosen:** If toes are blue, loosen the strips of cloth used to tie the splint.
- **Elevation:** Keep the leg slightly raised.
- **Refer:** If the color doesn't return to normal after loosening, go to the PHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Splint applied to leg"] --> B["Pinch the toenail and see if color returns fast"]
  B --> C["Check if toes are warm to the touch"]
  C --> D["If toes are cold or blue, loosen the tie strips"]
  D --> E["Wait 5 minutes and re-check color"]
  E --> F["If still blue, rush to CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blue toes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Persistent numbness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A splint that is too tight can cause 'Compartment Syndrome', leading to muscle death.

query: A man has a broken upper arm bone (humerus). How do I splint this since it's close to the shoulder?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Wrist drop (cannot lift the hand up)
- Severe bruising on the chest and arm

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Splint from shoulder to elbow, use a sling, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Splint:** Place a padded stick on the outside of the arm from the shoulder to the elbow.
- **Sling:** Use a dupatta to support the wrist/forearm.
- **Swathe:** Tie the whole arm to the chest with a wide cloth to stop it from moving outward.
- **Check:** Ask the man to move his fingers and wrist.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Upper arm injury"] --> B["Check if patient can lift their wrist"]
  B --> C["Place a padded splint on the outside of the arm"]
  C --> D["Apply a wrist sling"] 
  D --> E["Bind the arm firmly against the torso"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wrist drop | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe pain | Refer to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Upper arm breaks often damage the 'Radial Nerve'. If the wrist is 'dropping', it is a serious sign.

query: An old lady fell and hurt her knee. It's very swollen and she can't straighten it. She wants to apply a 'Lep' (paste).
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Knee joint is visibly out of place
- Foot is numb

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Female | Do NOT move the knee, splint it straight or as it is, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NO Paste:** Explain that the paste won't reach the bone and the movement to apply it might hurt her more.
- **Splint:** Use a long board under the leg from the heel to the thigh. Tie it loosely.
- **Ice:** Apply a cold water cloth to the swelling.
- **Refer:** Knee injuries in the elderly often mean a broken kneecap or joint damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Swollen knee, cannot straighten"] --> B["Apply cold compress to reduce swelling"]
  B --> C["Place a long board under the whole leg"]
  C --> D["Tie board at ankle, above knee, and thigh"]
  D --> E["Do NOT force the leg to straighten"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot walk | Refer for X-ray | Never |
| Numb foot | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Knee fractures can be tricky. Don't let anyone 'crack' or 'pull' the knee joint.

query: A person has a broken arm and the nearest hospital is 5 hours away. What should I monitor during the journey?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fingers becoming cold or blue
- Increased swelling that makes the bandage tight
- Loss of pulse at the wrist

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Monitor 'CMS' (Circulation, Movement, Sensation) every 30 mins |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **C (Circulation):** Pinch the fingernail; it should turn pink again in 2 seconds.
- **M (Movement):** Ask them to wiggle their fingers.
- **S (Sensation):** Ask if they can feel you touching their hand.
- **Adjust:** If they lose any of these, loosen the splint ties immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Long transport with fracture"] --> B["Check finger warmth and color every 30 mins"]
  B --> C["Ask patient to wiggle fingers"]
  C --> D["If fingers are cold/numb, loosen the splint ties"]
  D --> E["Keep the limb elevated during the trip"]
  E --> F["Reach CHC/Hospital as soon as possible"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Long transport | Always Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Monitoring the hand/foot is more important than the splint itself during a long journey.

query: A child has a 'greenstick' fracture. It's not fully broken but bent like a young tree branch. Does it need a splint?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Arm or leg is visibly bowed or curved
- Child refuses to use the limb

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Yes, splint it and go to the PHC for a proper cast |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Splint:** Even if it's not 'snapped', it needs support to grow back straight.
- **Sling:** For an arm, use a sling to keep it still.
- **Gentleness:** Do not try to 'unbend' the bone. 
- **Refer:** Children's bones heal fast, but if they heal bent, it's a permanent problem.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child's limb is bent but not snapped"] --> B["Apply a rigid splint for support"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to the area"]
  C --> D["Use a sling for arm injuries"]
  D --> E["Do NOT try to straighten the bend"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC/CHC for a proper plaster cast"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bent limb | Refer for X-ray | Never |
| Pain with movement | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Greenstick fractures only happen in children. They must be set by a professional or the bone will grow deformed.

query: A man broke his lower leg bone. We have no charpai or stretcher. How to carry him?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bone pieces moving under the skin
- Severe pain on moving

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Splint first, then use a 'two-man carry' or 'chair carry' |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Splint First:** You MUST tie his leg to a stick or his other leg before moving him.
- **Chair Carry:** Two people can lock their arms to form a seat for him.
- **Blanket Stretcher:** If you have a strong blanket or sari, lay him on it and 4 people carry the corners.
- **Do NOT:** Do not let him hop on one leg; the vibration can move the broken bone.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fracture with no stretcher"] --> B["Splint the injured leg to the good leg first"]
  B --> C["Use a strong cloth/blanket as a temporary stretcher"]
  C --> D["Carry with 4 people holding the corners"]
  D --> E["Keep the injured leg horizontal"]
  E --> F["Transport to the nearest road or CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Leg fracture | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Improper carrying can cause a 'closed' fracture to become an 'open' one (bone breaking through skin).

query: A person has a suspected fracture but the skin is not broken. How do I know if I should use a bandage or a splint?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Grating sensation (feeling bones rub together)
- Limb is a different length than the other
- Cannot move the joint above or below the injury

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Always use a splint first, then a bandage to hold the splint |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Rule:** If you suspect a break, treat it like a break. 
- **Splint First:** Use a hard object (wood/bamboo) to stop movement.
- **Bandage:** Use the cloth bandage *over* the splint to hold it in place. 
- **Padding:** Always put soft cloth between the splint and skin.
- **Refer:** A simple bandage (like Crepe) is for sprains, not fractures.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pain and swelling after hit"] --> B["Assume it is a fracture for safety"]
  B --> C["Apply a hard splint with soft padding"]
  C --> D["Secure the splint with a cloth bandage"]
  D --> E["Check pulse and warmth below the injury"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC for X-ray to confirm"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Grating/Crunching feeling | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Joint lock | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Using only a bandage on a fracture allows the bone to move, which causes more pain and internal damage.

query: A man fell while climbing a ladder and his shoulder looks square and out of place. He is crying in pain. Should I pull it back into the socket?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Numbness or tingling in the arm or fingers
- Skin turning blue or feeling very cold
- No pulse at the wrist
- Severe swelling or skin tearing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Support arm with a sling, do NOT pull the bone, go to CHC |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of fracture; immobilize completely and call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Pull:** Never try to force the bone back into the socket. This can tear nerves and blood vessels.
- **Immobilize:** Keep the joint in the exact position you found it.
- **Apply Cold:** Use a cloth dipped in **cold water** to reduce pain and swelling. This is a safe natural way to calm the area.
- **Sling and Swathe:** For a shoulder, place the arm in a cloth sling and tie another cloth around the chest to stop the arm from moving.
- **Refer:** Take the patient to a hospital with an X-ray facility immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient arrives with deformed joint"] --> B["Check for pulse and feeling in fingers or toes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT attempt to pull or reset the joint"]
  C --> D["Apply cold water compress for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Secure the limb in the current position using a sling or soft padding"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray and professional reduction"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Joint looks deformed | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Loss of feeling/Numbness | Immediate Emergency | Never |
| Pulse missing at wrist | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Trying to reset a dislocation without an X-ray can cause permanent paralysis if a nerve is trapped. Always seek professional help.

query: A child was pulled by the hand while playing and now won't move her elbow. She is holding it slightly bent. What can an ASHA worker do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Child is unusually pale or quiet
- Hand looks blue or cold
- Severe swelling around the elbow

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Female | Support the arm in a sling, do not move the elbow, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Comfort:** Keep the child calm. Do not try to straighten the arm.
- **Sling:** Use a triangular cloth (dupatta) to support the weight of the arm.
- **Local Remedy:** Apply a **cold pack** (ice wrapped in cloth) to the elbow to soothe the pain.
- **Check:** Ensure the child can wiggle her fingers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child refuses to move elbow after pull"] --> B["Check for finger warmth and movement"]
  B --> C["Apply a soft sling to support the forearm"]
  C --> D["Do NOT try to 'crack' or pull the arm to fix it"]
  D --> E["Transport gently to the nearest PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Refusal to move limb | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Blue/Cold hand | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is often called 'Pulled Elbow'. While common, it must be fixed by a trained person to avoid joint damage.

query: A woman's kneecap has shifted to the side after a twist. It looks very strange. Should we use hot oil to rub it back?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Knee is locked and cannot move
- Loss of pulse in the foot
- Severe bruising appearing quickly

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Do NOT use hot oil, splint the leg as it is, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Heat/Massage:** Hot oil and rubbing will increase internal bleeding and pain. Avoid this completely.
- **Cold Compress:** Use cold water cloths. Cold is the best natural remedy for joint trauma.
- **Splint:** Place soft pillows or rolled blankets around the knee to stop it from shaking or moving.
- **Refer:** This needs a doctor to slide the bone back safely.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Kneecap shifted to the side"] --> B["Check for pulse behind the ankle"]
  B --> C["Stop all hot oil massages or rubbing"]
  C --> D["Support the knee with soft pillows in the current position"]
  D --> E["Apply cold compress for 15 minutes"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC on a flat stretcher"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Kneecap out of place | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Foot feels cold | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A knee dislocation can damage the main artery behind the knee. It is a limb-threatening emergency.

query: An old man's jaw is stuck open after a large yawn. He cannot speak or swallow. How to help in the village?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Difficulty breathing
- Excessive drooling that causes choking
- Severe pain in the temples

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Do NOT force the mouth shut, support the jaw, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Support:** Use a soft cloth to wrap under the chin and over the head to support the jaw, but keep it loose enough to breathe.
- **Keep Calm:** Ask him not to try to talk or move his jaw.
- **Refer:** A doctor needs to use a specific technique to reset the jaw. Do not try to do it yourself as you could break the bone.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Jaw stuck in open position"] --> B["Check that the person can breathe easily"]
  B --> C["Apply a soft wrap under the chin for support"]
  C --> D["Do NOT try to force the jaw shut or pull it"]
  D --> E["Transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Jaw stuck open | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Inability to swallow | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Jaw dislocations require professional 'reduction' to avoid chronic jaw clicking or permanent misalignment.

query: A man has dislocated his finger during a wrestling match. It's bent at a sharp angle. Can I pull it straight?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Finger tip is pale or blue
- No feeling when you touch the finger tip
- Bone is sticking through the skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT pull, buddy-tape to the next finger, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Pull:** Pulling can trap the joint capsule inside the joint, requiring surgery.
- **Buddy Splint:** Place a small piece of cotton between the injured finger and the healthy finger next to it. Tie them together gently.
- **Cold Water:** Dip the hand in cold water to reduce pain.
- **Refer:** A PHC doctor can reset this easily after an X-ray.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Finger bent at strange angle"] --> B["Check finger tip color and sensation"]
  B --> C["Place cotton between injured and healthy finger"]
  C --> D["Tie both fingers together (Buddy Taping)"]
  D --> E["Apply cold water to reduce swelling"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC for X-ray and treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Finger deformity | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Blue finger tip | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
What looks like a dislocation could be a fracture. Taping them together is the safest way to travel.

query: A person has an ankle dislocation after a motorcycle accident. Foot is facing the wrong way. We are far from the city.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Foot is cold, blue, or white
- No pulse felt on the top of the foot
- Wound over the joint (Open dislocation)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Immobilize with pillows, do not move the foot, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immobilize:** Use the thickest padding available—pillows, rolled-up quilts, or heavy jackets. Wrap them around the ankle and tie securely.
- **Pulse Check:** Check if the toes are warm. This is vital.
- **Elevation:** Keep the leg slightly raised if it doesn't cause more pain.
- **Refer:** This is a major emergency. Go to a hospital with an Orthopedic Surgeon.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ankle facing the wrong way"] --> B["Check for pulse and warmth in the toes"]
  B --> C["Wrap the ankle in thick padding/pillows"]
  C --> D["Secure padding with cloth to stop all movement"]
  D --> E["Do NOT attempt to turn the foot back"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to the District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Ankle facing wrong way | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Open wound with dislocation | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Ankle dislocations often involve multiple fractures. Any attempt to 'fix' it in the field can cause permanent nerve damage.

query: A man's hip was hurt in a tractor accident. He is lying in a strange position and one leg looks longer. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Intense pain in the hip or groin
- Leg is rotated or looks longer/shorter
- Numbness in the whole leg

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT move the person, support the hip, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Flat:** Lay him on a hard board or the ground. Do not try to move the leg.
- **Support:** Use sandbags or heavy bags of grain on both sides of the leg and hip to prevent rolling.
- **Nothing by Mouth:** Do not give food or water in case he needs surgery.
- **Refer:** Hip dislocation is a surgical emergency. Every hour counts for the health of the bone.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Suspected hip dislocation after trauma"] --> B["Lay patient flat on a hard surface"]
  B --> C["Check for pulse and feeling in the foot"]
  C --> D["Place heavy padding around the hip and leg"]
  D --> E["Do NOT attempt to move or pull the leg"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for immediate hospital transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hip deformity | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Leg rotation/Length change | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A dislocated hip can cut off blood supply to the bone (Avascular Necrosis). This must be treated within 6 hours.

query: A woman dislocated her wrist. It is very swollen and has a 'step' shape. Can I apply a Turmeric and salt paste?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Fingers are numb or tingling
- Hand is turning white or blue
- Skin is very tight and shiny over the joint

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Do NOT apply pastes yet, splint the wrist, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Paste:** Avoid applying heavy pastes or 'Leps' now as the doctor needs to see the skin and bone position clearly. 
- **Splint:** Use a flat board (like a wooden scale) under the wrist. Tie it loosely with a dupatta.
- **Ice/Cold:** A cold water compress is the best natural way to control the swelling until you reach the doctor.
- **Refer:** Wrist dislocations often involve small bone fractures. Needs X-ray.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wrist with 'step' deformity"] --> B["Check finger movement and sensation"]
  B --> C["Place a flat board splint under the wrist"]
  C --> D["Secure with cloth, leaving fingers visible"]
  D --> E["Apply a cold compress for 15 minutes"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for X-ray and treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deformed wrist | Refer to CHC | Never |
| Tingling in fingers | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pressure on the median nerve in the wrist from a dislocation can cause permanent hand weakness if not corrected.

query: A child's shoulder popped out and popped back in by itself. He is still in pain. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain that is getting worse
- Weakness in the arm
- Bruising around the shoulder joint

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Support with a sling, apply cold, visit PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Rest:** Even if it 'went back', the tissues are torn. Keep the arm still.
- **Sling:** Use a cloth sling to rest the shoulder for 2-3 days.
- **Cold Compress:** Use a cloth dipped in cold water 3 times a day.
- **Refer:** See a PHC doctor to check for hidden fractures or ligament tears.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Joint popped back in spontaneously"] --> B["Apply a cloth sling for support"]
  B --> C["Apply cold water compress for pain"]
  C --> D["Do NOT allow the child to play or lift weights"]
  D --> E["Check for full finger and hand movement"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC for check-up within 24 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Joint moved back in | PHC visit within 24h | Sling and rest at home |
| Continued severe pain | Refer to PHC | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A joint that dislocates once is likely to do it again. Proper medical evaluation is needed to strengthen it.

query: A person has a dislocated elbow. It's locked and I can't feel a pulse at the wrist. What is the emergency action?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No pulse at the wrist** (Medical Emergency)
- Hand is blue, cold, or pale
- Total loss of sensation in the hand

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT move it, call 108 immediately, rush to surgery |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Urgent Referral:** This is a 'Vascular Emergency'. The bone is pinching the artery.
- **Position:** Keep the arm exactly as it is. Support it with a pillow during travel.
- **Loosen Clothing:** Remove any bangles, rings, or tight sleeves immediately.
- **Do NOT Wait:** Every minute without a pulse increases the risk of losing the hand.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Locked elbow with no wrist pulse"] --> B["Remove all jewelry and tight clothing on that arm"]
  B --> C["Support the arm on a pillow in current position"]
  C --> D["Do NOT attempt any movement or resetting"]
  D --> E["Call 108 or transport to District Hospital immediately"]
  E --> F["Keep arm at heart level during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No wrist pulse | Immediate Emergency | Never |
| Cold/Blue hand | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If the blood supply is not restored within hours, the hand muscles will die (Gangrene). This is a top-priority emergency.

query: Is there a way to tell the difference between a fracture and a dislocation in the village before taking the patient to the hospital?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bone sticking through skin (Fracture)
- Joint looks completely out of its socket (Dislocation)
- Grating sound when moving (Fracture)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Treat both the same: Immobilize and refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Treatment is the Same:** In the village, do not try to distinguish them by moving the limb. 
- **Immobilize:** Use splints or slings for both.
- **Cold Compress:** Safe for both to reduce swelling.
- **Refer:** Both need an X-ray to be sure. 

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unsure if fracture or dislocation"] --> B["Do NOT try to move the limb to test it"]
  B --> C["Apply a splint or sling for immobilization"]
  C --> D["Apply cold water compress for pain"]
  D --> E["Check for pulse and sensation below injury"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for X-ray diagnosis"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deformity or severe pain | Always Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Many dislocations also have small fractures attached. Only an X-ray can confirm the diagnosis.

query: A man has a recurring shoulder dislocation. It comes out often. Can I teach him to put it back himself?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Weakness in the shoulder
- Frequent popping out with small movements

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT teach him to reset it; refer for strengthening |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Activity:** Tell him to avoid lifting his arm above his head or throwing.
- **Sling:** Use a sling during the painful period.
- **Refer:** He needs professional physiotherapy or surgery. If he keeps putting it back himself, he will damage the joint forever.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["History of recurring dislocation"] --> B["Apply sling for 2 weeks during pain"] 
  B --> C["Avoid overhead activities and heavy lifting"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Refer to Orthopedic specialist at District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Repeated dislocations | Refer for surgery/rehab | Temporary sling at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Recurring dislocations cause the bone to wear down. This needs a permanent medical fix, not just first aid.

query: A child's toe looks like it's pointing the wrong way after a heavy object fell on it. Can I pull it straight?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Toe is purple or black
- Skin is split and bone is visible

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Buddy-tape to the next toe, do not pull, visit PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Buddy Taping:** Place a small cotton ball between the injured toe and the healthy one next to it. Tape them together.
- **Cold Dip:** Put the foot in cold water to help with pain.
- **Elevation:** Keep the foot raised.
- **Refer:** Visit a PHC to ensure there is no fracture.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Toe pointing the wrong way"] --> B["Check for skin tears and toe color"]
  B --> C["Place cotton between injured and next toe"]
  C --> D["Tape toes together (Buddy Taping)"]
  D --> E["Apply cold water and keep foot raised"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC for check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deformed toe | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Dark/Black toe | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Toes are small but vital for balance. Do not try to 'crack' them back into place.

query: A woman's jaw dislocated while she was laughing. She is in a lot of distress. Can I use a traditional oil to rub the jaw joints?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Cannot swallow saliva (drooling)
- Pain spreading to the ear

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Do NOT rub with oil, keep jaw supported, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Oil Massage:** Rubbing the jaw joint can cause more muscle spasms and make it harder to reset.
- **Support:** Use a soft dupatta to support the jaw. Tie it from under the chin to the top of the head (loosely).
- **Calmness:** Tell her to breathe slowly through her nose.
- **Refer:** Go to the CHC where a doctor can reset it with their thumbs.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Jaw stuck open while laughing"] --> B["Prevent the patient from trying to speak"]
  B --> C["Gently support the jaw with a soft cloth wrap"]
  C --> D["Do NOT massage the jaw with oils or heat"]
  D --> E["Transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Jaw stuck open | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Forcing a dislocated jaw can lead to a fracture of the mandible (jaw bone).

query: A person has a dislocated shoulder and we have to travel for 3 hours. How should I position them in the vehicle?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Arm becomes numb during the journey
- Hand turns blue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Sit upright, support the arm with a sling and a pillow |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sitting Up:** It is usually more comfortable for a shoulder injury to sit upright than to lie down.
- **Sling:** Use a wide cloth sling.
- **Pillow:** Place a pillow between the arm and the body to fill the gap and provide support.
- **Stability:** Tie the arm to the torso (swathe) so it doesn't bounce during the ride.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Long travel with shoulder dislocation"] --> B["Apply a wide cloth sling for the arm"]
  B --> C["Place a pillow under the arm for extra support"]
  C --> D["Tie the arm to the chest (swathe) to stop bouncing"]
  D --> E["Check finger warmth every 30 minutes"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC in an upright sitting position"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Long transport | Always Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Proper immobilization during travel prevents the bone from hitting nerves while the vehicle moves over bumps.

query: What is the best natural way to stop the swelling of a dislocated joint before we reach the hospital?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling that is so tight the skin feels hard
- Blue or cold skin below the swelling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Use cold water compresses, never use heat |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cold Water:** Soak a clean cloth in cold well water and wrap it around the joint.
- **Change Frequently:** As the cloth gets warm from the body, dip it in cold water again.
- **No Herbs:** Do not apply thick herbal pastes (Leps) as they trap heat and make the doctor's job harder.
- **Elevation:** Keep the swollen joint higher than the heart if possible.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Joint swelling after injury"] --> B["Dip a clean cloth in cold water"]
  B --> C["Wrap around the swollen area for 15-20 minutes"]
  C --> D["Repeat every hour until you reach the hospital"]
  D --> E["Keep the limb elevated above heart level"]
  E --> F["Go to CHC for professional evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling with deformity | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cold is for swelling; Heat is for muscle pain. Using heat on a fresh dislocation will make the swelling much worse.

query: A man's ankle is dislocated but the skin is not broken. Can we use a 'Pahalwan' or bone setter to fix it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Bone rubbing sounds (crepitus)
- Numbness in the foot
- Foot is turning a dark color

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT go to a bone setter; go to a hospital with X-ray |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Caution:** Bone setters often use force without seeing the bone position. This can break the bone or tear arteries.
- **Splint:** Secure the ankle with sticks or padding.
- **Refer:** Take the patient to a CHC. A doctor needs an X-ray to see if there are hidden fractures.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ankle out of place"] --> B["Check pulse and skin color of the foot"]
  B --> C["Apply a rigid splint or heavy padding"]
  C --> D["Do NOT allow any forceful manipulation or pulling"]
  D --> E["Keep the leg still and elevated"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC/Hospital with X-ray facility"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Ankle deformity | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Many lifelong disabilities in villages are caused by incorrect 'bone setting' of dislocations. Always get an X-ray first.

query: A person fainted after a shoulder dislocation from the pain. What should I do first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Patient does not wake up after 1 minute
- Difficulty breathing
- Weak or fast pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Lay flat, raise legs, support the shoulder, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay Flat:** Make the person lie down on their back. 
- **Raise Legs:** Lift their feet 1 foot high to help blood reach the brain.
- **Shoulder Support:** Place a pillow under the injured shoulder to stop it from moving.
- **No Water:** Do NOT try to pour water into their mouth while they are unconscious.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient faints from joint pain"] --> B["Lay patient flat and raise feet 12 inches"]
  B --> C["Check for breathing and pulse"]
  C --> D["Place a support pillow under the dislocated joint"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any water or food to drink"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fainting after injury | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting can be a sign of 'Neurogenic Shock' from extreme pain. This needs hospital stabilization.

query: A woman has a dislocated shoulder. She is wearing many glass bangles and rings. Should I remove them?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Hand starting to swell
- Bangles or rings becoming very tight
- Fingers turning blue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | REMOVE all jewelry from that arm immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Urgent Removal:** Remove all rings, bangles, and watches. As the arm swells, these will cut off the blood supply like a wire.
- **How to Remove:** Use soap or oil to slide off tight rings. If glass bangles are too tight, they may need to be broken carefully.
- **Sling:** Put the arm in a sling after removing jewelry.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dislocation with jewelry on limb"] --> B["Check for swelling and skin color"]
  B --> C["Gently remove all rings, bangles, and watches"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress to reduce swelling"]
  D --> E["Apply a sling to support the arm"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Jewelry stuck/tight | Emergency - Remove and Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Jewelry becomes a 'tourniquet' during injury swelling. It can cause the loss of fingers if not removed early.

query: A man's knee was dislocated, but he says it popped back in when we moved him. He now says he feels okay and wants to walk. Should I let him?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Knee feels 'unstable' or like it will give way
- Foot feels numb
- Pulse behind the ankle is weak

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT let him walk, splint the leg, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Walking:** The ligaments are definitely torn. Walking can cause permanent damage to the joint surface.
- **Splint:** Splint the leg straight using a long board.
- **Refer:** He must get an X-ray and a professional check of the blood vessels.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Joint popped back in but knee is weak"] --> B["Do NOT allow the patient to stand or bear weight"]
  B --> C["Apply a rigid splint from thigh to ankle"]
  C --> D["Check for pulse behind the ankle bone"]
  D --> E["Apply a cold compress for pain"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for Orthopedic check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Joint moved back in | Refer to CHC | Rest and splint at home |
| Instability/Giving way | Refer for surgery | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Spontaneous reduction (popping back in) of a knee is dangerous because it can hide a major artery tear. He needs a doctor.

query: A woman's saree caught fire while cooking. Her back and arms are burnt. The skin is red and some parts have blisters. What should I do first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Burned area is larger than the person's palm
- Face, hands, feet, or private parts are burned
- Skin looks charred, black, or bone-white
- Difficulty breathing or soot in the nose/mouth
- Person is drowsy or unconscious

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool with water for 20 mins, cover with clean cloth, rush to PHC |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Stop the fire, cool with water, remove jewelry, refer if large |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk of shock; cool and refer to CHC immediately |
| Pregnant | Female | Cooling is priority; monitor for fetal distress and refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Burning:** Make the person roll on the ground if still on fire.
- **Cool with Water:** Pour clean, room-temperature water over the burn for at least **20 minutes**. Do NOT use ice.
- **Remove Jewelry:** Gently take off rings, bangles, or watches before swelling starts.
- **Do NOT:** Do not apply toothpaste, ink, butter, or dung. These cause infections.
