- **Protect Blisters:** Do NOT pop blisters. Cover the area loosely with a clean, dry cotton cloth or plastic wrap (if available and clean).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ensure scene is safe and fire is out"] --> B["Pour cool running water on burn for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Remove jewelry and tight clothing gently"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any pastes or pop blisters"]
  D --> E["Cover loosely with a clean dry cloth"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC if burn is large or on face/hands"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Large area (larger than palm) | Emergency - Go to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Charred/White skin | Emergency - Go to CHC | Never |
| Small redness (like sunburn) | Monitor at home | Use cool water and keep clean |
| Blisters on face/hands | Always refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is for immediate first aid only. Severe burns can lead to dehydration and life-threatening infections. Always seek medical help for deep or large burns.

query: A small child touched a hot iron/tava. There is a small red patch on the finger. The mother wants to put cold cow dung on it. Help!
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- The finger turns white or black
- The child cannot move the finger
- Pus starts coming out after a day

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Hold under tap water for 20 mins, leave open or light bandage |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Dung/Paste:** Tell the mother cow dung contains bacteria that will cause a dangerous infection (Sepsis).
- **Water is Medicine:** Use clean, cool water for 20 minutes. This stops the heat from going deeper into the skin.
- **Natural Remedy:** Only if the skin is NOT broken, you may apply fresh **Aloe Vera gel** after cooling with water.
- **Keep Clean:** Wash with mild soap and water daily.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child touches hot object"] --> B["Immediately hold under cool running water for 20 mins"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply dung, toothpaste, or ink"]
  C --> D["Check if skin is broken or blisters formed"]
  D --> E["Apply fresh Aloe Vera gel ONLY if skin is intact"]
  E --> F["Monitor for signs of infection like pus or fever"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Small red patch (no blister) | Not needed unless pain persists | Home care with water/cleaning |
| Fever or pus | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even small burns in children need close monitoring. If the child doesn't stop crying, refer to the PHC.

query: A man was caught in a house fire. He is coughing and his eyebrows are burnt. What should the ASHA worker check first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Burnt nose hairs or eyebrows** (Sign of airway burn)
- Hoarse voice or difficulty speaking
- Coughing up black soot
- Wheezing or gasping for breath

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Keep upright, give oxygen if available, call 108 immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority - Airway:** Breathing is more important than skin burns. If eyebrows are burnt, his throat may swell shut.
- **Position:** Sit the person upright. Do NOT let them lie down.
- **Fresh Air:** Move them to an open area away from smoke.
- **Refer:** This is a top-level emergency. Go to a hospital with an ICU/Ventilator.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient rescued from smoke/fire"] --> B["Check for burnt nose hair and hoarse voice"]
  B --> C["Sit the patient upright to help breathing"]
  C --> D["Loosen any tight clothing around the neck"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and monitor breathing every minute"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC/District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Soot in mouth/nose | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Hoarse voice | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Airway burns can cause the throat to close within minutes. Speed is the only way to save a life.

query: The skin is sticking to the clothes of a burn victim. Should I pull the clothes off before pouring water?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin peeling off with clothes
- Large areas of stuck fabric

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Cut around the cloth, do NOT pull stuck parts |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pour Water First:** Pour water over the clothes and the burn together. This cools the area faster.
- **Do NOT Pull:** If cloth is stuck to the burn, leave it there. Pulling it will tear the skin off.
- **Cut Clothing:** Use scissors to cut away the loose parts of the clothing.
- **Cover:** Wrap the stuck cloth and the burn in a clean, wet sheet for transport.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cloth stuck to burn site"] --> B["Pour cool water over both cloth and skin"]
  B --> C["Cut away loose cloth with clean scissors"]
  C --> D["Leave stuck fabric in place to avoid skin tearing"]
  D --> E["Cover with a clean damp cloth/sheet"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stuck clothing | Refer to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Removing stuck clothing is a surgical task that should only be done in a hospital under sterile conditions.

query: A person has deep burns and is very thirsty. Can I give them plenty of water to drink?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Person is vomiting
- Burn covers more than half the body
- Person is faint or confused

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Give small sips only if conscious; rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Small Sips:** If the person is fully awake, you can give small sips of clean water or ORS.
- **Avoid Large Amounts:** Do NOT let them gulp down a lot of water as it can cause vomiting.
- **No Food:** Do not give any food.
- **Refer:** Severe burns cause the body to lose fluid quickly. They need an IV drip at the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Severe burn with intense thirst"] --> B["Check if patient is fully conscious and alert"]
  B --> C["Give small sips of water or ORS if alert"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give food or large amounts of liquid"]
  D --> E["Cover patient with a clean dry sheet"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Intense thirst/Deep burn | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Thirst is a sign of shock. The real treatment is IV fluids, so don't delay transport while giving water.

query: A kerosene lamp exploded near an old woman. Her entire left leg is burnt. The skin is white and dry. Should I apply Haldi paste?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin looks dry, waxy, white, or charred black (Deep Burn)
- The burn surrounds the entire leg like a ring
- Patient feels no pain in the whitest areas

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk! Cool with water briefly (10 mins), cover, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Haldi/Pastes:** Do NOT apply haldi (turmeric) paste, ink, or oils. These cause infection and make hospital cleaning harder.
- **Cooling:** Pour cool clean water over the area for about 10 minutes to cool the skin, but do not make her too cold.
- **Remove Constraints:** Gently take off any anklets or bangles before swelling occurs.
- **Refer:** This is a serious deep burn. Go to CHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Deep kerosene lamp burn on elderly"] --> B["Pour cool water on leg for 10 minutes (not longer)"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply haldi or any home pastes"]
  C --> D["Cover the entire leg loosely with a clean dry sheet"]
  D --> E["Call 108 and monitor pulse constantly"]
  E --> F["Transport to District Hospital urgently"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Waxy/White/Charred skin | Emergency - Go to Hospital | Never |
| Burn goes around the leg | Emergency - Go to Hospital | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Deep burns destroy pain nerves, so they might not hurt, but they are far more dangerous than painful burns.

query: A child has burnt her hand with a burning firecracker (diya). There are small blisters. The grandmother says we must pop them to let the heat out. Help!
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Blisters cover the whole hand
- The burn involves the wrist
- Severe pain that makes child faint

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool for 20 mins, do NOT pop blisters, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Pop Blisters:** Explain that blisters are the body's natural bandage. Popping them causes infection.
- **Cool with Water:** Immediately hold the hand under cool running water for 20 minutes.
- **Natural Remedy:** Only if blisters are very small and child is awake, after cooling, you can apply fresh **Aloe Vera gel** or **Honey** loosely.
- **Refer:** Any blister on a child's hand needs to be checked at the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Firecracker burn with blisters"] --> B["Hold hand under cool tap water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT pop blisters or apply traditional remedies initially"]
  C --> D["Apply fresh Aloe Vera gel/Honey loosely if only small areas"]
  D --> E["Cover hand loosely with a clean dry cloth"]
  E --> F["Visit nearest PHC for check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blisters formed | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Small redness only | Home care with water/honey | Safe after cooling |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hands are critical for future use. Any child's hand burn requires professional assessment.

query: There was a big farm fire and my neighbour is badly burnt over his stomach and thighs. He is breathing but feels very cold. What should I do while waiting for the ambulance?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Burned area is very large (over half the body)
- Charred black skin
- Breathing difficulty
- Faint pulse

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Stop fire, cool with water briefly (10 mins), cover to keep warm, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Fire:** Use a blanket or water to put out any lingering fire. Make him roll.
- **Brief Cooling:** Cool the largest burnt parts with water for 10 minutes, but do NOT over-cool. Over-cooling leads to hypothermia.
- **Keep Warm:** Since he feels cold, cover his body (including the burns) loosely with a clean, dry sheet and a blanket.
- **Do NOT:** Do NOT apply any pastes or try to remove stuck clothing.
- **Wait:** Sit with him and keep talking while waiting for the ambulance.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ensure neighbours saree/clothes are not still burning"] --> B["Pour cool water for 10 minutes over burnt areas"]
  B --> C["Gently cut away unburnt loose clothing"]
  C --> D["Cover entire body loosely with clean dry sheets"]
  D --> E["Cover with blanket to prevent body from getting cold"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and monitor breathing constantly"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Large burn area | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Unconscious/Pale/Faint | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe burns lead to 'shock' where the body gets cold and cannot keep blood flowing. This is a critical situation.

query: A person has burnt their face in a garbage fire. The skin is swollen and red. What home items can I use before taking them to the health centre?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Difficulty breathing (Emergency)
- Charred or white patches on face
- Swelling that restricts breathing
- Soot in the mouth or nose

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Priority is breathing! Keep upright, cool briefly, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Breathing:** This is priority #1. If breathing is okay, proceed.
- **Cool with Water:** Pour clean water or apply a clean cloth dipped in cool water over the face for 10 minutes.
- **Do NOT apply:** Do not apply ghee, mud, or toothpaste. These can cause infection and worsen face swelling.
- **Keep Clean:** Use only cool water. Gently dab dry with a clean cloth.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check neighbours face and mouth for soot"] --> B["Assess breathing - if rapid or hard, call 108"]
  B --> C["Apply cloth dipped in cool water for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply traditional face pastes or oils"]
  D --> E["Keep neighbour sitting upright while travelling"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for professional facial burn care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Charred/White skin | Emergency - Go to CHC | Never |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Small redness, no breathing issue | Refer to PHC | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Face burns are serious because swelling can easily close the airway. All face burns must be seen by a doctor.

query: A child swallowed a small firecracker (diya). It wasn't burning, but it made her throat hot. Is this a burn?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drooling or unable to swallow saliva
- Trouble breathing or wheezing sound
- Severe sore throat or pain
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Do NOT make her vomit, keep upright, give nothing to eat, rush to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Emergency:** This can be an 'Internal Burn' or poisoning from the firework chemical.
- **Do NOT induce vomiting:** Do NOT make the child vomit as the chemical could burn the throat again on the way up.
- **Nothing by Mouth:** Do not give milk, water, or 'Ghutti'.
- **Position:** Keep the child sitting upright.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Suspicion of swallowing firework chemical/ diya"] --> B["Check breathing and drooling immediately"]
  B --> C["Do NOT attempt to make child vomit"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything by mouth to drink"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any change in childs condition"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for gastric lavage"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swallowed chemical/Diya | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Breathing problem | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal chemicals cause burns or systemic poisoning that cannot be seen externally. This is always a major emergency.

query: A person has an infected burn wound on their arm. It smells bad and has pus. Should I use Neem paste on it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Large amount of pus and foul smell
- Redness spreading up the arm
- High fever with chills
- Burned person feels very weak

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT apply home pastes, keep clean, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Pastes:** Do NOT use neem paste, cow dung, or mud on an infected burn. These cause worse infection.
- **Keep Clean:** Gently wash with boiled and cooled water. Apply a thin layer of **Neem Oil** only around the healthy skin edges.
- **Refer:** This needs an antibiotic cream (like Silver Sulphadiazine) and possibly antibiotic tablets. Go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["ASHA worker arrives at infected burn"] --> B["Check for smell, pus, and spreading redness"]
  B --> C["Do NOT use home traditional pastes or ink"]
  C --> D["Cover loosely with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  D --> E["Ask about fever and check pulse rate"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for antibiotic treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Smelly burn wound | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Fever and chills | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Spreading redness | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infected burns can lead to 'Sepsis' (blood infection), which can be fatal. Prompt treatment at PHC is essential.

query: A neighbour has a burn on his hand from a small fire. The skin is slightly red and a small blister formed. It hurts. Is there any natural village cure?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain that keeps getting worse
- Blister gets bigger and covers whole hand

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Cool with water, do NOT pop blister, elevate hand |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool with Water:** First step must be clean water for 20 minutes.
- **No Dung/Paste:** Explain cow dung/ink/dung cause severe infection.
- **Do NOT pop blister:** Leave it alone.
- **Natural Remedy:** After water, you can gently apply **Honey** or fresh **Aloe Vera gel** loosely. 
- **elevation:** Keep the hand raised above the level of the heart.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Neighbour arrives with small hand burn"] --> B["Immediately hold under cool well water for 20 mins"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply traditional face pastes or oils"]
  C --> D["If blister present do NOT pop, keep clean"]
  D --> E["Apply a thin layer of Honey if only mild red"]
  E --> F["Cover hand loosely with a clean cotton dupatta"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blister formed | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Only small redness | Home care with cooling/honey | Safe after cooling |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hands are essential for livelihood. Any burn on hands should be assessed to ensure no long-term movement issues arise.

query: What is the best type of water to use on a burn in the village, tap water or well water?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Water causes severe pain
- Water is freezing cold or dirty

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Use any clean, room-temperature water available immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cleanliness:** Use the cleanest water available. Clean tap water or well water is good.
- **Temperature:** Use room temperature water. Do NOT use freezing cold water as it can shock the body.
- **Immediate action:** The most important thing is to get water onto the burn quickly. Don't wait to heat or cool it.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Burn happens on a limb"] --> B["Immediately pour any clean room-temp water onto burn"] 
  B --> C["Continue pouring for at least 20 minutes"] 
  C --> D["Do NOT look for pastes while pouring water"] 
  D --> E["Gently cover with a clean damp cloth"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC/CHC for proper assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Burn of any size | Refer to CHC/PHC | Only minor redness |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Water is the only proven initial treatment for burns. Cooling the skin prevents the heat from traveling deeper.

query: A woman has extensive deep burns after a cooking gas cylinder leak. Her breathing is okay. Her sari is stuck in several places. How should ASHA workers help during transport?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Charred black or dry waxy skin patches
- Large area of body burned
- Weak or fast pulse

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Brief cooling, cover body, monitor pulse, rush to District Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Sticking clothes removal:** Absolutely do not try to peel stuck clothing. Cut around it.
- **Brief Cooling:** Cool largest burned parts (like stomach) with water for 10 minutes, then cover.
- **Maintain Temperature:** Keep her warm with a blanket over the coverings. Shock makes the body cold.
- **Elevate Leg:** Elevate both legs 1 foot to help blood flow to the head.
- **Refer:** This is a major medical emergency. Rush to a hospital with a dedicated burn unit.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Woman with major deep burns after gas leak"] --> B["Ensure cylinder is off and scene is safe"]
  B --> C["Pour cool water for 10 minutes over major burns"]
  C --> D["Cover body loosely with clean dry sheets and blankets"]
  D --> E["Elevate both legs 1 foot from the floor"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for intensive care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Major Deep Burn | Emergency - Go to Hospital | Never |
| Pale skin/Weak pulse | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe burns lead to rapid fluid loss and 'Sepsis' which is fatal. Immediate hospital care is the only chance of survival.

query: A person was burnt by a burning cloth in a temple. The skin is just red, no blisters. It hurts. What village remedies can I use?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Pain getting worse day by day
- Fever starting

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Cool with water, apply Honey loosely, leave open |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool with Water:** First step must be clean water for 20 minutes.
- **No Dung/Paste:** Explain cow dung/ink/dung cause severe infection.
- **Natural Remedy:** After water, gently apply **Honey** or fresh **Aloe Vera gel** loosely. You can also use cooled, boiled **Neem water** to clean daily.
- **Refer:** If the pain is very bad, visit the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Minor redness on skin after temple burn"] --> B["Hold under cool tap water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Check again - is it just red, no blisters formed?"]
  C --> D["If yes, apply fresh Aloe Vera gel loosely"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any signs of infection like pus or fever"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC if pain does not subside within one hour"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Only small redness | Not needed unless pain is bad | Home care with water/honey |
| Fever or pus | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
For minor burns, water and natural ointments are excellent first aid, but never underestimate a burn in a vulnerable person.

query: A man's hand has charred black skin after a farm fire. He says it doesn't hurt. Does he need a doctor?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Charred black or dry waxy white skin (Deep Burn)
- No pain felt (Nerves are destroyed)
- Hand is cold or blue beyond the burn

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT apply anything, cover loosely, rush to Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Referral:** This is a deep burn (Third Degree). The fact it doesn't hurt is a critical danger sign.
- **No Pastes:** Do not apply haldi, ink, or dung.
- **Protect:** Cover the hand and fingers loosely with a clean dry cotton cloth.
- **Monitor Pulse:** Check the pulse at the wrist constantly.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Man arrives with charred black hand"] --> B["Pinch finger nail - if color doesn't return, call 108"]
  B --> C["Do NOT allow any forceful manipulation or pulling"]
  C --> D["Apply a cool water compress for brief cooling only"]
  D --> E["Cover loosely with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for specialized surgery"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Charred/Black skin | Emergency - Go to Hospital | Never |
| No pain in deep burn area | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Deep burns destroy muscle, nerve, and skin. Delay in treatment can mean permanent hand deformity or loss.

query: A child has burnt her leg on a burning cloth. The whole skin peeled off with the cloth. There is bleeding. What village steps to take?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Whole skin layer peeled off (Deep Burn)
- Active bleeding
- Burn covers a large area of leg

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Apply pressure around wound, briefly cool, cover, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding First:** Use the cleanest cloth available to press firmly *around* the edges of the burn. Do NOT press in the center.
- **No Pastes:** Do not apply ink, dung, butter, or dung.
- **Protect:** Cover the entire area loosely with a clean, dry sheet.
- **Brief Cooling:** Cool with water only after bleeding slows down, and for only 10 minutes.
- **Refer:** This is a serious deep burn. Go to hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["EnsureScene is safe and cloth fire is out"] --> B["Apply direct pressure around wound edge to stop bleed"]
  B --> C["If no active bleed, cool for 10 minutes (not long) with water"]
  C --> D["Cover entire leg loosely with clean dry sheets"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply traditional face pastes or oils"]
  E --> F["Transport urgently to District Hospital for surgery"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin peeled off completely | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Active bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A burn that bleeds is often deep and carries a massive risk of severe infection and scar contractures.

query: A person has fainted after a serious fire burn. They have no visible neck pulse. What should ASHA worker do first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Unconscious for more than 1 minute
- Not breathing after trauma
- No carotid pulse felt in neck

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Start CPR if trained, call 108, rush for life support |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **CPR First:** This is a critical life-saving situation. If you are trained in CPR, start chest compressions immediately.
- **Check Breathing:** If not breathing, call 108 immediately.
- **Do NOT:** Do NOT try to give water or move him unless there is lingering fire.
- **Refer:** This is a major medical emergency. Rush to a hospital with an ICU and ventilator.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Neighbour faints after serious burn injury"] --> B["ASHA worker checks for consciousness and breathing"]
  B --> C["If neighbour unconscious, lay them flat on their back"]
  C --> D["Check pulse and breathing continuously"]
  D --> E["If not breathing, call 108 and transport urgently"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC/District Hospital with ventilation unit"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious/Pale/Faint | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting after a burn indicates 'shock' and needs intensive hospital care to maintain blood pressure.

query: A child has a burn on his thumb after touching a burning diya. Blister is present. He is crying but moving his hand. Do I need to take him to PHC?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- The blister keeps getting bigger and covers whole hand
- Child cannot move the thumb at all
- Severe swelling spreading to wrist

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Cool for 20 mins, buddy-tape loosely to index, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool with Water:** First step must be clean water for 20 minutes.
- **No Dung/Paste:** Explain cow dung/ink/dung cause severe infection.
- **Do NOT pop blister:** Leave it alone.
- **Buddy Splint:** Tape the injured thumb gently to the index finger with a piece of cotton in between.
- **Refer:** Yes, any blister on a child's hand must be assessed by the PHC.

query: A farmer spilled concentrated pesticide/acid on his arm. It is burning badly and the skin is turning yellow. Can I apply cow dung or lime to neutralize it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin looks charred, white, or is peeling off
- Burn is larger than the palm of the hand
- Severe pain or total loss of feeling in the area
- Dizziness or difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (12-60) | Any | Flush with water for 30 mins, remove contaminated clothes, refer |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk of shock; flush and call 108 immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Flush with Water:** Immediately wash the area with large amounts of clean, running water for at least **30 minutes**. 
- **Do NOT Neutralize:** Never apply lime, vinegar, or cow dung. Chemical reactions can create more heat and burn deeper.
- **Remove Clothing:** While washing, carefully remove any clothes or jewelry that touched the chemical. Wear gloves if possible.
- **Dry Powder:** If the chemical is a dry powder, brush it off with a dry cloth *before* washing with water.
- **Refer:** All chemical burns need to be seen at the CHC after washing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical spill on skin reported"] --> B["Brush off dry powder or immediately start water flush"]
  B --> C["Continue flushing with running water for 30 minutes"]
  C --> D["Remove contaminated clothing and jewelry during flush"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any home pastes, lime, or oil"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC with the chemical container if safe"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any chemical burn | Always refer to CHC | Never |
| Eye involvement | Emergency - Flush and go | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chemicals continue to burn until they are completely washed away. Water is the only safe way to stop the damage.

query: Acid splashed into a woman's eye while cleaning. She is screaming. Should I put milk or honey in the eye?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Eye looks cloudy or white
- Unable to open the eyelid
- Vision loss or severe blurring
- Extreme redness and swelling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Flush eye with water for 20 mins, do NOT rub, rush to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Flush Immediately:** Hold the eye open and pour clean water gently from the inner corner to the outer corner for **20 minutes**.
- **No Milk/Honey:** Do NOT put anything except clean water in the eye. Other substances can cause infection or trap the chemical.
- **Do NOT Rub:** Rubbing can scratch the eye surface and drive the chemical deeper.
- **Cover:** After flushing, cover the eye loosely with a clean, dry pad and go to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical splash in eye"] --> B["Hold eyelid open and flush with clean water gently"]
  B --> C["Continue washing for 20 minutes without stopping"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put milk, honey, or medicine in the eye"]
  D --> E["Cover eye loosely with a clean cotton pad"]
  E --> F["Rush to Eye Specialist or District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chemical in eye | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Eye burns are a leading cause of blindness in rural areas. Constant washing for the first 20 minutes is the only way to save sight.

query: A child swallowed a small amount of liquid floor cleaner (bleach/acid). He is crying and his lips are red. Should I make him vomit?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Drooling or unable to swallow
- Hoarse voice or difficulty breathing
- Severe pain in the chest or stomach
- Gray or white patches inside the mouth

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Do NOT induce vomiting, give small sips of water, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Induce Vomiting:** Forcing the child to vomit will burn the throat a second time. This is very dangerous.
- **Rinse Mouth:** Gently wash the child's mouth with water. Do not let them swallow the rinse water.
- **Small Sips:** If the child can swallow, give a few sips of water or milk to dilute the chemical.
- **Refer:** Take the child and the chemical bottle to the hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child swallowed chemical cleaner"] --> B["Check breathing and ability to swallow"]
  B --> C["Do NOT make the child vomit"]
  C --> D["Rinse mouth with water - do not swallow rinse"]
  D --> E["Give small sips of milk or water if swallowing is okay"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital with the bottle"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swallowed chemical | Immediate Emergency | Never |
| Drooling/Choking | Call 108 immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal chemical burns can cause the throat to swell shut or the stomach to tear. Professional medical monitoring is mandatory.

query: A laborer got wet lime (chuna) on his legs. It is starting to sting. We don't have much water. Can we use oil?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin feels slippery or 'soapy'
- Deep holes or ulcers forming in the skin
- Swelling that makes skin feel very tight

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Brush off dry bits, wash with any clean water for 30 mins |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Brush Off:** If there is dry powder, brush it off the skin and clothes first. 
- **Wash with Water:** You MUST find water. Use at least 2-3 buckets to wash the area for 30 minutes. Oil will not remove the chemical.
- **No Pastes:** Do not apply any herbs or oils while the chemical is still on the skin.
- **Refer:** Lime burns are 'alkali' burns and go very deep. Go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wet lime (chuna) on skin"] --> B["Brush off any dry powder from skin and clothes"]
  B --> C["Wash with large amounts of water for 30 minutes"]
  C --> D["Remove shoes/socks if lime is inside them"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply oil or grease to the area"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for wound dressing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lime burn | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alkali burns (like lime) are 'sneaky'—they feel mild at first but continue to destroy tissue for hours if not washed.

query: A worker has acid on his clothes and chest. He is in a lot of pain. Should we wait to get him to the hospital before removing clothes?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Chest pain or trouble breathing
- Skin is peeling off with the clothes
- Rapid, shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Remove clothes immediately while flushing with water |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove Clothes NOW:** The clothes are soaked in chemical and will keep burning him. Cut them off immediately.
- **Flush with Water:** Start pouring water over the body even before the clothes are off. Continue for 30 minutes.
- **Protect Yourself:** Do not touch the chemical with your bare hands. Use a cloth or gloves.
- **Refer:** Call 108. Major chemical burns lead to shock.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical soaked clothing on patient"] --> B["Immediately start pouring water over the person"]
  B --> C["Cut away all contaminated clothes and jewelry"]
  C --> D["Continue water flush for 30 minutes"]
  D --> E["Cover with a clean dry sheet to prevent cold"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for emergency transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Extensive chemical burn | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Removing the source (the clothes) is the most important step in stopping the burn. Do not wait.

query: A woman has red marks on her hand after using a strong toilet cleaner. It stings but the skin is not broken. Can she use cold water and curd?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Redness is spreading
- Blisters start to form
- Finger tips become numb

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash with water for 20 mins, no curd, monitor at home |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash:** Use running tap water for 20 minutes. Curd or yogurt is not as effective as water at removing the chemical.
- **No Curd:** Curd can hide the injury and cause infection if there are micro-scratches.
- **Natural Remedy:** After washing for 20 minutes, if there are no blisters, you can apply fresh **Aloe Vera gel**.
- **Refer:** If blisters form or pain increases, visit the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stinging from toilet cleaner splash"] --> B["Wash hand under running water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT apply curd, ink, or toothpaste"]
  C --> D["Apply fresh Aloe Vera only if skin is not broken"]
  D --> E["Check for blisters or spreading redness over 2 hours"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC if pain continues or blisters appear"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Mild stinging, no blisters | Not needed | Wash and monitor |
| Blisters or deep red skin | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Household cleaners can be very strong. Always wash for longer than you think you need to.

query: A person was cleaning a metal tank with chemicals and collapsed. He has no burns on skin but he is dizzy. What do I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Unconscious or confused
- Difficulty breathing or blue lips
- Vomiting
- Seizures

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Move to fresh air, do NOT enter the tank, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety First:** Do NOT enter the tank to save them without a mask. You might collapse too.
- **Fresh Air:** Move the person to an open, windy area immediately.
- **Position:** Lay them on their side (Recovery Position) if they are unconscious.
- **Refer:** This is 'Chemical Inhalation'. They need oxygen at the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person collapses in chemical tank"] --> B["Ensure scene is safe before approaching"]
  B --> C["Move patient to fresh air immediately"]
  C --> D["Loosen clothing around the neck and waist"]
  D --> E["Lay patient on their side if unconscious"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Inhaled chemical/Fainting | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fumes can burn the lungs from the inside. This is a life-threatening emergency even if no skin burns are seen.

query: A child got some car battery liquid on his shirt. His chest is red. What is the ASHA worker's advice?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin looks white or has a 'hole' in it
- Child is crying uncontrollably
- Child becomes very sleepy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Remove shirt, wash chest for 30 mins, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove Clothing:** Take off the shirt immediately. Battery acid is very strong.
- **Wash:** Use a lot of water. Pour it over the chest for 30 minutes. 
- **No Soap:** Just use plain water first. 
- **Refer:** Take the child to the PHC to ensure the acid hasn't burned deep into the skin.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Battery acid on child's chest"] --> B["Immediately remove the shirt and undergarments"]
  B --> C["Wash the chest with water for 30 minutes"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any home remedies or pastes"]
  D --> E["Cover loosely with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC for wound assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Battery acid burn | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Battery acid (sulfuric acid) causes deep tissue damage very quickly. Long washing is critical.

query: A young man got hair dye in his eyes. He is washing it but says it still stings. Should he use rose water (Gulab Jal)?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Eye is very red and swollen
- Pain does not go away after 15 mins of washing
- Sensitivity to light

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash with water for 20 mins, do NOT use rose water, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Rose Water:** Do NOT use rose water or any eye drops. Some rose waters have preservatives that react with chemicals.
- **Water Only:** Continue to wash the eye with clean, cool water for another 20 minutes.
- **Refer:** If it still stings after 20 minutes of washing, see a doctor at the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Hair dye splash in eye"] --> B["Wash eye with clean water for 20 minutes"] 
  B --> C["Do NOT use rose water or home eye drops"]
  C --> D["Remove contact lenses if the person is wearing them"]
  D --> E["Check vision by asking to read something"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if stinging or redness persists"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Persistent stinging/redness | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chemicals in hair dye can cause severe allergic reactions and eye surface damage.

query: A person has an acid burn and I have some baking soda. Can I put it on the burn to stop the acid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Sizzling sound or heat when powder is added
- Skin turning black

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT use baking soda, use water for 30 minutes |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use Baking Soda:** When baking soda meets acid, it creates a lot of heat (exothermic reaction). This will cause a 'Heat Burn' on top of the 'Chemical Burn'.
- **Water is Best:** Use only water. It dilutes and washes the acid away safely.
- **Flush:** Keep pouring water for 30 minutes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Acid burn with baking soda available"] --> B["Do NOT use baking soda or any base"]
  B --> C["Start flushing with water for 30 minutes immediately"]
  C --> D["Remove any jewelry in the splash area"]
  D --> E["Cover loosely with a clean dry cloth"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for professional care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Acid burn | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Trying to 'neutralize' a chemical burn with another chemical usually results in more tissue damage. Stick to water.

query: A woman has white patches on her skin after handling chemical fertilizers with bare hands. It doesn't hurt much. Is it okay to leave it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin feels hard or leathery
- Numbness in the fingers
- Swelling around the white patches

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash with water for 20 mins, go to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash:** Even if it doesn't hurt, wash with water for 20 minutes to remove all chemical residue.
- **Don't Ignore:** White patches can mean a deep chemical burn that has killed the nerves (that's why it doesn't hurt).
- **Refer:** Visit the PHC to check for deep tissue damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["White patches after fertilizer contact"] --> B["Wash skin with soap and water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Check for feeling/numbness in the white area"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any oil, ghee, or herbs"]
  D --> E["Mark the area with a pen to see if it spreads"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| White/Painless patches | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chemical fertilizers can be caustic. Painless burns are often the deepest and most dangerous.

query: A villager got paint thinner in a large cut on his leg. What is the first-aid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Patient feels dizzy or 'drunk'
- Severe pain in the wound
- Redness spreading away from the cut

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Flush wound with water for 20 mins, do NOT use soap inside cut, refer |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Flush:** Pour clean water *into* and over the cut for at least 20 minutes to wash out the thinner.
- **No Soap:** Do not put soap directly into the deep cut. Use only clean water.
- **Refer:** Chemicals entering the bloodstream through a cut is an emergency. Go to the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical/Thinner in an open cut"] --> B["Flush the cut with clean water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put any creams or ointments in the cut"]
  C --> D["Apply a clean dry bandage over the cut"]
  D --> E["Monitor for dizziness or fast heartbeat"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for wound cleaning"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chemical in open wound | Refer to CHC | Never |
| Dizziness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Solvents like thinner can be absorbed into the body and damage organs. This needs medical monitoring.

query: A person has a chemical burn on their back. Should I have them lie down in a tub of water?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Shivering or feeling very cold
- Fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT soak in a tub, use running water (shower/bucket) |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Running Water:** Use a bucket or hose to pour water *over* the back. Do NOT soak in a tub because the chemical will just sit in the water and touch other parts of the body.
- **Direction:** Pour water so it runs *off* the body, not onto the unburned skin.
- **Time:** Keep pouring for 30 minutes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Large chemical burn on back"] --> B["Do NOT soak the patient in a tub or pool"]
  B --> C["Pour water over the back so it drains away from healthy skin"]
  C --> D["Continue pouring water for 30 minutes"]
  D --> E["Remove all clothing and jewelry during the process"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and transport to CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Large chemical burn | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Running water is required to physically remove the chemical from the skin surface. Still water is not effective.

query: A farmer got pesticide in his mouth while spraying. He spat it out but his tongue is burning. Can he drink lemon juice?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Tongue or throat swelling
- Difficulty speaking
- Excessive drooling
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Rinse mouth with water, do NOT drink lemon juice, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Rinse:** Rinse the mouth with clean water many times. Spit it out every time.
- **No Lemon Juice:** Pesticides can be acidic or alkaline. Adding lemon juice (acid) can cause a chemical reaction and more pain.
- **Do NOT swallow:** Make sure he doesn't swallow the rinse water.
- **Refer:** Take the pesticide container to the CHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pesticide contact with mouth/tongue"] --> B["Rinse mouth repeatedly with water and spit out"]
  B --> C["Do NOT swallow any water or drink lemon juice"]
  C --> D["Check for swelling of the tongue or lips"]
  D --> E["Keep patient sitting upright to help breathing"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC with the pesticide label/bottle"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pesticide in mouth | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pesticides are highly toxic. Even a small amount absorbed through the tongue can be dangerous.

query: A person has a chemical burn from cement. It's on his knees from kneeling in it all day. Should I use warm water?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Skin looks like it's rotting or has deep pits
- Severe pain that keeps him awake

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Use cool/room-temp water for 20 mins, do NOT use warm water |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool Water:** Use only cool or room-temperature water. Warm water opens the pores and makes the chemical go deeper.
- **Wash:** Scrub very gently with a soft cloth and a lot of water to remove all cement dust.
- **Refer:** Cement burns are chemical burns. Go to the PHC for a dressing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cement burn from kneeling in wet cement"] --> B["Wash knees with cool water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Remove any cement particles with a soft cloth gently"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use warm water, oil, or ghee"]
  D --> E["Apply a clean dry bandage to the area"]
  E --> F["Visit PHC for check-up within 24 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Deep cement burn | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cement contains 'Chromium' and is very alkaline. It can cause 'cement holes' in the skin if not washed thoroughly.

query: A person has an acid burn and I have some wood ash. The elders say wood ash stops acid. Should I use it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Heat being felt when ash is applied
- Skin turning gray

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT use ash, use clean water for 30 minutes |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Ash:** Wood ash is alkaline. Like baking soda, it will react with the acid and create intense heat, burning the person even more.
- **Water Only:** Plain water is the only safe first aid. 
- **Time:** Flush for 30 minutes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Acid burn with wood ash available"] --> B["Do NOT apply wood ash, mud, or lime"]
  B --> C["Immediately wash with clean water for 30 minutes"]
  C --> D["Remove clothing and jewelry in the area"]
  D --> E["Cover with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for professional treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Acid burn | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Neutralizing' a burn is a laboratory task. In the field, dilution with water is the only way to save the skin.

query: A child has chemical on his hands and is rubbing his eyes. What should I do first?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Child cannot open eyes
- Severe redness in eyes and hands

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash eyes first for 20 mins, then wash hands, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Priority:** Wash the eyes first. They are more delicate than the hands.
- **Wash Eyes:** Flush for 20 minutes. Keep the child's hands away from the face.
- **Wash Hands:** After the eyes are safe, wash the hands with soap and a lot of water for 10 minutes.
- **Refer:** See a doctor immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical on hands and child rubbing eyes"] --> B["Immediately wash eyes with water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Hold hands away from face to prevent more contact"]
  C --> D["Wash hands thoroughly for 10 minutes after eyes"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any ointments to eyes or hands"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for eye check-up"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chemical in eye | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Damage to the eye can happen in seconds. Every moment spent washing saves the child's vision.

query: What should the ASHA worker do with the chemical container/bottle after a burn happens?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Leaking bottle causing more burns

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Put in a plastic bag and take to the doctor |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Secure:** Close the bottle tightly. Put it in a plastic bag so it doesn't leak during transport.
- **Label:** If there is a name or label, make sure it is visible.
- **Refer:** Give the bottle to the doctor. Knowing the chemical helps them give the right treatment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical container at the scene"] --> B["Secure the bottle so it does not leak further"]
  B --> C["Place the bottle in a sealed plastic bag"]
  C --> D["Do NOT taste or smell the chemical to identify it"]
  D --> E["Wash your own hands after handling the bottle"]
  E --> F["Hand the bottle to the doctor at the hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Container present | Bring to hospital | Never leave it behind |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Identifying the chemical is key to medical treatment. Do not try to clean or throw away the bottle until the doctor sees it.

query: A woman has a chemical burn and her bangles are stuck because of swelling. Should we leave them?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Hand/Fingers turning blue or cold
- Loss of pulse at the wrist

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | REMOVE bangles immediately, use soap/oil if needed, go to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove NOW:** Bangles will act like a wire, cutting off blood as the arm swells. This can lead to losing the hand.
- **Method:** Use soap, oil, or even a thread to slide them off. If they are glass, break them carefully away from the burn.
- **Wash:** Continue to flush the area with water after removing them.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Chemical burn with jewelry/bangles in place"] --> B["Start water flush immediately"]
  B --> C["Gently remove all bangles, rings, and watches"]
  C --> D["Use soap or oil only to slide off jewelry"]
  D --> E["Continue water flush for 30 minutes"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC to check blood circulation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling with jewelry | Emergency - Remove and Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Jewelry is the biggest danger during burn swelling. It must be removed within the first few minutes.

query: A person has a chemical burn and is shivering and pale. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Shivering/Feeling very cold
- Faint or fast pulse
- Confusion or drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Stop washing, cover with dry sheet, call 108 |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Shock:** The person is going into shock. 
- **Stop Cooling:** If they are shivering, stop the water. They are losing too much body heat.
- **Cover:** Dry the unburned skin and cover the whole person with a clean, dry sheet and a blanket.
- **Position:** Lay them flat and raise their feet 1 foot high.
- **Call 108:** This is a life-threatening emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient shivering/pale after chemical burn"] --> B["Stop water flush if person is shivering severely"]
  B --> C["Lay patient flat and raise feet 1 foot high"]
  C --> D["Cover loosely with a clean dry sheet and blanket"]
  D --> E["Check pulse and breathing every minute"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shivering/Pale skin | Immediate Emergency | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In major burns, 'Shock' and 'Hypothermia' (getting too cold) can kill the person before the burn does.

query: A farmer was working in the rain and touched a motor pump. He is now lying unconscious. What is the first thing I should do as an ASHA worker?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconsciousness or no response**
- Blue tint on lips or fingernails
- No visible breathing or chest movement
- Burn marks at the entry (hand) and exit (feet) points

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------| 
| Adult | Any | **Do NOT touch** until power is off. Use dry wood to move him. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety**: Ensure the motor is unplugged or the main switch is off.
- Use a **dry bamboo or wooden stick** to push the person away from the pump.
- Check for breathing. If not breathing, start **chest compressions** (CPR) immediately.
- Keep the person flat on their back. 
- Cover any burn wounds with a clean, dry cloth.
- Call **108** and transport to CHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confirm power is OFF or use dry wood to move victim"] --> B["Check for response and breathing"]
  B --> C["If no breath, start chest compressions immediately"]
  C --> D["Cover burn wounds with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  D --> E["Keep the person lying flat and keep them warm"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and rush to the nearest Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Visible entry/exit burns | Immediate Referral | Never |
| No breathing | Emergency - CPR and Refer | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Electrical shocks can stop the heart. Even if the person wakes up, they must see a doctor for a heart check (ECG).

query: A village woman got a shock from a wet switch. Her hand is red and painful, but she is talking. Can I use turmeric or oil on it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Dizziness or blurred vision
- Numbness in the arm
- A burn that looks like a deep hole
- Heart racing or skipping beats

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Cool the burn, remove jewelry, check for chest pain. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop**: Do NOT apply turmeric, oil, ghee, or toothpaste. These trap heat and cause infection.
- Hold the hand under **clean, running tap water** for 15-20 minutes.
- Remove bangles and rings immediately before the hand starts to swell.
- Cover the area loosely with a clean **saree/dupatta**.
- If she feels any chest tightness, she must go to the hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Disconnect victim from power source safely"] --> B["Pour clean running water over the burn for 20 mins"]
  B --> C["Remove rings and bangles from the affected hand"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply oil, turmeric, or toothpaste"]
  D --> E["Cover loosely with a clean cotton cloth"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if pain persists or if chest feels tight"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blisters forming | Refer to PHC for dressing | Never |
| Mild redness only | Visit PHC to be safe | Not recommended |
| Numbness in fingers | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Domestic shocks are often underestimated. Always monitor the person for 4 hours for any heart issues.

query: A teenager was trying to fix a wire at home and his clothes caught fire. He has burns on his chest. How to manage this?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Difficulty breathing or coughing**
- Soot (black dust) around the nose or mouth
- Deep white or charred black skin
- Very fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Youth | Any | Stop, Drop, Roll. Cool the chest area. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Tell the boy to **Stop, Drop, and Roll** if he is still on fire. 
- Use a thick cotton blanket to smother flames, then remove it immediately so it doesn't hold heat.
- **Do NOT** pull off clothes that are stuck to the skin.
- Pour **clean, cool water** over the chest area for a long time.
- Cover the area with a clean, wet bedsheet.
- Give sips of water if he is fully conscious.
- Rush to the District Hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the fire using 'Stop, Drop, and Roll'"] --> B["Pour cool water over the burns for 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Do NOT peel off clothing stuck to the skin"]
  C --> D["Cover the chest with a clean, damp cotton sheet"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any difficulty in breathing"]
  E --> F["Rush to the Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Burn on the chest | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Clothing stuck to skin | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Hoarse voice or cough | Emergency - Airway risk | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chest burns can affect breathing. Keep the person in a semi-sitting position during transport.

query: An electric wire fell on a bullock cart. The driver is thrown off and has a burn on his foot. He is complaining of a 'pins and needles' feeling.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Inability to move the foot or toes
- Severe muscle cramps or spasms
- Dark-colored urine (later sign)
- Confusion or headache

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Check for exit wound on feet, immobilize, refer. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Check the feet for **'exit wounds'** where the electricity left the body.
- Keep the leg still. Do not let him walk on the injured foot.
- Clean the foot burn with cool water and cover with a clean bandage.
- **Hydrate**: Give the person plenty of water to drink (helps protect the kidneys from muscle damage).
- Refer to the hospital to check for internal muscle or nerve damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Keep the patient lying down and still"] --> B["Check for exit burns on the soles of the feet"]
  B --> C["Clean and cover the foot burn with a clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Give plenty of water to drink if conscious"]
  D --> E["Do NOT allow the patient to walk"] 
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for nerve and kidney evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pins and needles feeling | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Foot looks charred | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Muscle stiffness | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Electrical burns can 'cook' muscles from the inside. This can lead to kidney failure if not treated with IV fluids.

query: A toddler touched an open socket and his fingers are white and hard. He is not crying, which is worrying me. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No pain** (this means the nerves are destroyed)
- White, leathery, or charred skin
- Child is unusually quiet or sleepy
- Swelling of the hand

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | High emergency. Do not wait for him to cry. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Gently rinse the fingers with cool water. 
- Do NOT try to pop any blisters if they appear later.
- Do NOT apply any household creams or pastes.
- Wrap the hand loosely in a clean, soft cotton cloth.
- **Refer immediately**: A child not crying after a severe burn often means a deep, full-thickness burn.
- Monitor the child's heartbeat and breathing on the way to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ensure socket is safe and child is away from it"] --> B["Rinse the hand with cool water gently"] 
  B --> C["Do NOT apply any ointments or home remedies"]
  C --> D["Wrap hand loosely in a clean cotton cloth"]
  D --> E["Keep the child warm and monitor breathing"]
  E --> F["Rush to the Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| White/Hard skin | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |
| No pain in a deep burn | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |
| Child is lethargic | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Deep electrical burns may not hurt initially but can cause permanent loss of finger movement. Professional care is vital.

query: A man got a shock from an overhead line while carrying a ladder. He has a big wound on his shoulder and he is very confused. Help me.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Confusion or memory loss**
- Large open wound on the shoulder
- Severe headache or vomiting
- Weakness in the legs

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | High voltage risk; check for head injury from fall. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If he fell, do **not** move his neck or back in case of a spine injury.
- Cover the shoulder wound with a large, clean, dry cloth.
- Do NOT give anything to eat or drink if he is confused.
- Keep him lying flat and talk to him to keep him awake.
- This is a high-voltage injury; he needs a surgical team.
- Rush to the nearest District Hospital/Trauma Center.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Ensure victim is safe from wires and ladder"] --> B["Keep neck and spine still if he fell"] --> C["Cover the shoulder wound with a clean dry cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give food or water due to confusion"]
  D --> E["Monitor for seizures or vomiting"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital/Trauma Center"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Vomiting | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| High voltage shock | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |
| Large open wound | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
High-voltage shocks from overhead lines are extremely lethal and often cause internal organ blowouts. Every second counts.

query: An ASHA worker sees a person with a small burn from a heater. The person says they feel okay but their urine is very dark/red. What does this mean?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dark red or tea-colored urine**
- Muscle pain or weakness
- Decreased amount of urine
- Swelling of the whole body

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | This is a medical emergency; kidney risk. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Emergency**: Dark urine after a shock means muscles are breaking down and blocking the kidneys.
- Encourage the person to drink as much water as possible immediately.
- Do not let them walk; use a stretcher or vehicle.
- Clean the local burn but focus on getting them to a hospital.
- They need IV fluids (drips) to save their kidneys.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify dark urine as a sign of muscle damage"] --> B["Have the person drink plenty of water immediately"]
  B --> C["Keep the person resting; no walking"] 
  C --> D["Clean the small skin burn with water"]
  D --> E["Inform the doctor about the urine color"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC for IV fluids and kidney check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dark/Red urine | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |
| Muscle pain after shock | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Small burn with body swelling | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Myoglobinuria' (muscle protein in urine) can cause permanent kidney failure within hours of an electric shock.

query: A child bit a mobile charging cable. There is a gray mark on the tongue and the child is drooling. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling of the tongue or throat
- High-pitched sound when breathing (stridor)
- Bleeding from the mouth
- Difficulty swallowing saliva

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Risk of airway blockage. Stay calm. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Calm**: Don't let the child cry excessively as it increases swelling.
- Sit the child upright on your lap.
- Gently offer small sips of cool water to soothe the area.
- **No Food**: Do not give solid food.
- Do not apply any 'honey' or 'herbs' inside the mouth.
- Watch for any sudden bleeding from the lip or tongue.
- Transport to a hospital with a pediatrician.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Unplug the charger immediately"] --> B["Keep the child sitting upright and calm"] 
  B --> C["Check for swelling or difficulty breathing"]
  C --> D["Give small sips of cool water if child can swallow"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply anything to the mouth burn"]
  E --> F["Rush to the Hospital and watch for bleeding"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Tongue/Mouth burn | Immediate Hospital Referral | Never |
| Constant drooling | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Trouble breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Electric burns to the mouth can cause the 'labial artery' to burst days later. Hospitalization is necessary for observation.

query: A person was fixing a light and fell from a stool after the shock. They have a burn on the hand and their back hurts. How to move them?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Numbness or inability to move legs
- Severe back or neck pain
- Weakness in grip
- Loss of bladder control

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Suspect spine injury. Do NOT bend the back. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Log Roll**: If you must move them, keep the head, neck, and back in one straight line.
- Do NOT let them sit up or stand.
- Use a stiff board (like a wooden door or plank) as a stretcher.
- Tie the person to the board gently so they don't slide.
- Treat the hand burn with cool water and cover with a clean cloth.
- Rush to a hospital with X-ray/CT scan facilities.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Keep the person lying flat and still"] --> B["Check for movement in hands and feet"]
  B --> C["Use a hard flat board as a stretcher"] --> D["Do NOT bend or twist the person's back"]
  D --> E["Clean the hand burn with water and cover"]
  E --> F["Transport to Hospital with spine precautions"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Back pain after a fall | Emergency Hospital Referral | Never |
| Leg numbness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Hand burn only | PHC for dressing | Not recommended |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
An electric shock often causes violent muscle contractions that can break bones or throw a person, causing hidden spine injuries.

query: A woman’s saree caught in a motor and she got a shock and a friction burn. The skin is torn and there is grease on it. How to clean it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Heavy bleeding from the torn skin
- Bone visible in the wound
- Fainting or extreme paleness
- Heart palpitations

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Focus on cleaning and infection prevention. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stop the motor and disconnect power first.
- Use **mild soap and lots of clean water** to gently wash away grease/dirt.
- Do NOT scrub the burn area harshly.
- Apply a clean, non-stick dressing or a clean washed cloth (dhoti).
- Check if she has had a Tetanus injection in the last 6 months.
- Refer to PHC for wound cleaning and antibiotic advice.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Turn off motor and ensure safety"] --> B["Wash the wound gently with soap and clean water"]
  B --> C["Remove grease carefully without scrubbing hard"]
  C --> D["Cover with a clean dry cotton cloth"]
  D --> E["Check for pulse and signs of shock"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Tetanus and dressing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Grease/Dirt in wound | Refer to PHC for cleaning | Never |
| Deep skin tear | Refer to PHC for stitches | Never |
| Shock signs (cold skin) | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Grease and dirt in an electric/friction burn carry a very high risk of infection and Tetanus. Professional cleaning is required.

query: What to do if someone's heart stops after an electric shock? I am the only ASHA worker nearby.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- No pulse at the neck
- No chest rise/breathing
- Pupils are large and do not react to light
- Cold, blue skin

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **CPR is the only chance.** Start now. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the person on a **hard floor** (not a bed).
- Place both hands in the center of the chest.
- Push **hard and fast** (100-120 times per minute).
- Allow the chest to come back up after every push.
- Shout for help and ask someone to call **108**.
- Do not stop until the ambulance arrives or the person wakes up.
- Ignore any skin burns until the heart starts.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confirm no breathing and no pulse"] --> B["Lay person on a hard flat surface"] 
  B --> C["Start hard and fast chest compressions"] --> D["Ask someone to call 108 immediately"]
  D --> E["Continue CPR without stopping"]
  E --> F["Transport to Hospital in a vehicle while doing CPR"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No heartbeat | Extreme Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Unconscious | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) is a life-saving skill. Electric shock victims often have 'shockable' hearts that can be restarted if CPR is done early.

query: A person has many small 'spark' burns all over their body from a short circuit. They are itchy. Can I give them a bath?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Burns covering a large part of the chest or back
- Blisters that are popping and oozing
- Fever or chills starting
- Swelling of the eyes or face

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Cool water only; no scrubbing. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- You can use a **cool water sponge bath** but do not use ice-cold water.
- Do NOT use soap on the burned areas.
- Pat the skin dry very gently with a soft towel; do not rub.
- Do NOT apply any local 'neem' pastes or oils.
- If it is very itchy, they may need an antihistamine (refer to PHC).
- Have them wear loose, clean cotton clothes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess the total area of the body with spark burns"] --> B["Sponge with cool clean water gently"]
  B --> C["Pat dry with a soft cloth - do NOT rub"]
  C --> D["Apply no oils, pastes, or powders"]
  D --> E["Provide loose cotton clothes to wear"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if itching or pain is severe"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever after burns | Refer to PHC for infection | Never |
| Burns on face/eyes | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Itching only | Visit PHC | Monitor at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Multiple spark burns can indicate that the person was very close to a blast. Monitor for any hearing loss or eye pain.

query: An elderly man with a heart condition got a minor shock from a radio. He is sweating and looks pale. What is the risk?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sweating and paleness**
- Shortness of breath
- Pain in the left arm or jaw
- Pulse is very slow or very fast

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Male | High risk of heart failure after shock. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Have him lie down with his **legs raised** slightly.
- Loosen his dhoti/shirt and ensure good air flow.
- Do not let him walk or exert himself.
- Even a tiny shock can trigger a heart attack in elderly patients.
- Note if he is already taking any heart medicines.
- Transport immediately to a hospital with an ICU/Emergency ward.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Have the patient lie down with legs raised"] --> B["Loosen all tight clothing for better breathing"]
  B --> C["Check pulse rate and rhythm at the wrist"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything to eat or drink"]
  D --> E["Reassure the patient to keep him calm"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sweating + Minor shock | Emergency Referral | Never |
| History of heart disease | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dizziness | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In the elderly, electrical interference can cause the heart to stop or go into an abnormal rhythm easily. Do not treat lightly.

query: A person has a burn on their hand from a heater. The skin is peeling off and it is very red. Should I put a bandage on it?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Burn is larger than the person's palm
- Pus or yellow fluid leaking out
- Foul smell from the wound
- Fever

## 🏥 Actions by Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What To Do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Use non-stick dressing or clean cotton cloth. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the hand with clean water for 15 minutes first.
- If the skin is peeling, it is a second-degree burn.
- **Do NOT** put a tight bandage. It will stick to the raw skin.
- Use a **clean, dry piece of plastic wrap** (if available) or a very loose, clean cotton cloth.
- Keep the hand raised above the level of the heart to reduce swelling.
