  D --> E["Check if bleeding stops after 10-20 minutes"]
  E --> F["If bleeding continues, rush to the nearest PHC/hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding stops within 15 mins | No need to refer | Rest in a cool place |
| Bleeding continues > 20 mins | Refer to PHC immediately | While traveling, keep pinching |
| Dizziness or fainting | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Bleeding after head injury | Call 108 / Emergency | Do not wait |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is first-aid guidance. If the person has frequent nosebleeds or high blood pressure, they must consult a doctor.

Query: An old man's nose started bleeding suddenly while he was resting. He has high BP. How should I handle this in the village?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extremely fast heartbeat or chest pain**
- Blood coming from the back of the throat even when pinching the nose
- Very pale skin or cold hands/feet
- Confusion or slurred speech

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Male | Sit upright, pinch nose, check BP if possible, refer if it persists. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Strict Upright Position:** Ensure he sits in a chair. Do not let him lie flat.
- **Continuous Pinch:** Pinch the nostrils shut for 15 minutes. Use a timer; do not peek to check if it stopped.
- **Cold Water:** Splash cold water on the head and face.
- **Spit out blood:** Tell him to spit out any blood in his mouth. Swallowing blood causes vomiting.
- **Avoid Strain:** Tell him not to blow his nose, cough, or strain for the next few hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person with nosebleed"] --> B["Sit patient upright in a chair leaning forward"]
  B --> C["Pinch soft part of nose firmly for 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Tell patient to spit out blood, do not swallow"]
  D --> E["Check if patient has high BP or takes blood thinners"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC if bleeding persists or BP is very high"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| High BP + Bleeding | Refer to PHC for BP check | Pinch nose for 15 mins |
| Bleeding stops quickly | Monitor at home | Avoid heat and exertion |
| Blood in vomit | Refer immediately | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In elderly patients, nosebleeds can be caused by high blood pressure. Stopping the bleed is first aid; treating the BP is the cure.

Query: A pregnant woman has a nosebleed. She is scared it will affect the baby. What can I do to stop it naturally?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blurred vision or severe headache**
- Bleeding accompanied by a sudden rise in BP (Pre-eclampsia sign)
- Bleeding that starts after a fall or trauma to the belly

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Calm her down, sit upright, pinch nose, refer for BP check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Calm Reassurance:** Tell her nosebleeds are common in pregnancy due to increased blood flow.
- **The Pinch:** Pinch the soft part of the nose for 10 minutes while she breathes through her mouth.
- **Cold Compress:** Apply a cold wet cloth to her forehead and nose.
- **Hydration:** Give her sips of water after the bleeding stops.
- **Natural Humidifier:** If the air is dry, keep a bowl of water near her or use a wet towel in the room to keep the air moist.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with nosebleed"] --> B["Sit her down and lean her forward gently"]
  B --> C["Pinch the nostrils shut for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply cold cloth to forehead and bridge of nose"]
  D --> E["Check for headache or vision changes"]
  E --> F["Refer to ANM/PHC for blood pressure check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Simple nosebleed | Monitor at home | Pinch and cold compress |
| Severe headache | Refer immediately | Emergency checkup |
| High BP reading | Refer to PHC/CHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
While often harmless, nosebleeds in pregnancy should be reported to the ANM during the next checkup to monitor blood pressure.

Query: A man's nose is bleeding because he put a sharp stick inside to itch it. It is bleeding a lot. What is the ASHA worker's job here?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Visible deep cut inside the nostril**
- **Object or stick piece stuck inside the nose**
- Heavy pulsatile (spurting) bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Pinch nose, do not look for the cut, apply pressure. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Inspect:** Do not try to open the nose to see the cut. You will break the clot.
- **Firm Pressure:** Pinch the nose hard for 15 minutes. This is the only way to stop a cut from bleeding.
- **Ice:** If available, put an ice cube in a cloth and hold it against the side that is bleeding.
- **No Stuffing:** Do not put cotton, cloth, or leaves inside the nose. It will cause infection in the wound.
- **Rest:** The person must sit still. Any movement or talking can restart the bleeding.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed from internal injury/scratch"] --> B["Pinch nostrils firmly for 15 minutes without release"]
  B --> C["Apply ice or cold cloth to the side of the nose"]
  C --> D["Do NOT insert cotton or leaves into the nostril"]
  D --> E["Tell patient to avoid talking or moving"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if bleeding is spurting or won't stop"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shallow scratch | If stops with pressure | Keep clean, avoid picking |
| Deep cut/Spurting | Refer immediately | Keep pinching on the way |
| Piece of stick inside | Refer for removal | Never try to pull at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Traumatic nosebleeds can involve deeper vessels. If pressure doesn't work within 15 minutes, hospital care is required.

Query: A child gets nosebleeds every time he plays in the heat. The village elders say to put onion juice. What is the right way?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding occurs daily or multiple times a week**
- Child looks pale and tired all the time (Anemia risk)
- Easy bruising on other parts of the body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Immediate pinch, then prevent heat exposure. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The Pinch:** Use the 10-minute pinch method immediately.
- **Cooling:** Move the child to the shade or a cool room. Apply a wet cloth to the head.
- **Onion Juice Reality:** While a common home remedy, do **NOT** drop onion juice inside the nose. You can hold a freshly cut onion *near* the nose for the person to sniff; the strong smell can sometimes help close vessels, but it's not a substitute for pinching.
- **Lubrication:** Once bleeding stops, apply a tiny bit of Vaseline or Ghee inside the nostril rim to prevent dryness.
- **Avoid Pick:** Tell the child not to pick their nose.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Heat-related nosebleed in child"] --> B["Move child to shade and sit them upright"]
  B --> C["Pinch nostrils for 10 minutes and apply cold water to head"]
  C --> D["Sniffing cut onion is okay, but NO drops in nose"]
  D --> E["Apply Ghee/Vaseline to nostril rim after stopping"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if child is also very pale or weak"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Occasional (heat) | Treat at home | Pinching and shade |
| Frequent (daily) | Refer to PHC for blood test | Not for home care only |
| Bruises on legs/arms | Refer to CHC immediately | Possible blood disorder |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Frequent nosebleeds in children can be a sign of anemia or other underlying issues. Get a one-time checkup at the PHC.

Query: ASHA worker report: A man in the field has had a bleeding nose for an hour. Pinching isn't working. His wife wants to use cow dung. Stop her and tell me what to do.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Continuous heavy bleeding despite 20 minutes of firm pinching**
- Vomiting large amounts of blood
- Signs of shock (cold clammy skin, fast weak pulse)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Emergency. Keep pinching on one side or both, transfer immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NO Cow Dung:** Stop her immediately. Placing cow dung or any substance in or on the nose will cause severe, fatal infection (like Tetanus).
- **Pinch and Hold:** Even if he says it isn't working, he likely stopped pinching too soon. Pinch the nose firmly again, non-stop for another 15 minutes. Use force.
- **Cold water on neck:** Pour cold water on the back of his neck and his head.
- **Transfer Immediately:** If still bleeding after 20 minutes of continuous pressure, this is an emergency. Get him to the nearest CHC/Hospital.
- **Keep Pinching during travel:** Tell him to continue pinching his nose while being transported.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Continuous heavy nose bleeding > 30 mins"] --> B["Pinch nose again firmly for 15 minutes without release"]
  B --> C["Apply cold water to neck and forehead"]
  C --> D["Tell patient to spit out blood, do not swallow"]
  D --> E["Do NOT use cow dung or herbs inside nose"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transport immediately to hospital"]
  F --> G["Keep pinching nose during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Heavy bleed > 20 mins pressure | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Vomiting blood | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |
| Cold clammy skin | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Heavy nosebleeds that don't respond to pressure can be life-threatening. Professional medical packing is required.

Query: A teenage girl has a dry cough and now her nose is bleeding. How do I help her at home? Can I use lemon juice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding starts every time she coughs**
- Blood mixed with coughed-up phlegm
- Trouble breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager | Female | Sit up, lean forward, pinch nose, treat cough. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding First:** Use the pinch method for 10 minutes.
- **NO Lemon Juice:** Do not put lemon juice in the nose. The acid will burn and damage the already raw, dry lining, making it bleed more.
- **Breathe Through Mouth:** Explain this is necessary while pinching.
- **Treat the Cough:** After bleeding stops, give her warm water or a home remedy like Ginger-Tulsi-Honey for the cough to prevent more episodes.
- **Moisturize:** Apply Ghee or pure coconut oil to the inside of her nostrils after the bleeding has stopped.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed during dry cough"] --> B["Pinch nostrils firmly for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  C --> D["Tell patient to breathe through mouth"]
  D --> E["Give Ginger-Tulsi-Honey for cough AFTER bleeding stops"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if cough or bleeding worsens"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Nosebleed from cough | If persists despite cough relief | Pinch, moisturize with ghee |
| Blood in phlegm | Refer to PHC immediately | Not safe at home |
| Shortness of breath | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Coughing increases pressure in the head and can easily rupture dry vessels. The cough needs separate treatment.

Query: My neighbor has a severe cold with a blocked nose. Today when he tried to blow his nose, it started bleeding from one side. What should I tell him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Large amounts of blood being swallowed**
- Severe pain in the face or eyes
- Feeling very faint or dizzy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Pinch the nose, use steam, advise not to blow nose. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pinch and Lean:** Sit up, lean forward, and pinch the nostrils for 10 minutes.
- **Do NOT blow nose:** Advise him strongly not to blow his nose, sneeze, or sniff hard for at least 6 hours, as it will break the clot and restart the bleeding.
- **Steam Inhalation:** Encourage breathing steam (just plain water) to loosen mucus without forceful blowing.
- **Moisture:** Apply Ghee to the nose opening.
- **If Sneezing:** If he must sneeze, tell him to do it with his mouth open to reduce pressure in the nose.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed after blowing nose during cold"] --> B["Pinch nostrils for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  C --> D["Advise NOT to blow nose for 6 hours"]
  D --> E["Tell patient to spit out swallowed blood"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if bleeding returns heavily"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cold + Nosebleed | Treat at home | Pinch, steam, ghee |
| Blocked nose only | Use steam and rest | Use steam at home |
| Severe headache | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
 Forcefully blowing your nose when it is congested can rupture small blood vessels.

Query: A man fell off his bike in the field and hit his nose. It is now bleeding. Should I make him lie down? What about some local herbs?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Clear fluid leaking with blood from nose** (sign of skull fracture)
- Head pain that gets worse, or cannot stay awake
- Nose looks bent or crooked
- Bruising around the eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Do not move neck, keep upright, pinch nose. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Lie Down:** Keep him sitting upright, leaning forward, supporting his neck if possible.
- **NO herbs inside:** Placing herbs in the nose after trauma can cause severe infection and worsen internal bleeding.
- **Pinch the Nose:** Pinch the nose firmly for 15 minutes, if the bone is not broken or very crooked.
- **Cold Water:** Pour cold water on the head.
- **Transfer:** If the hit was hard, he needs an X-ray. If clear fluid is mixed with blood, transfer immediately without moving his neck much.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed after fall/trauma"] --> B["Sit patient upright leaning forward"]
  B --> C["Pinch nostrils for 15 minutes if not crooked"]
  C --> D["Apply ice to nose bridge and cold water to head"]
  D --> E["Check for clear fluid or crookedness"] 
  E --> F["Refer immediately to hospital for head injury check"]
  F --> G["Keep neck immobilized during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Nose bleeding after fall | Always refer for evaluation | Not safe at home |
| Clear fluid leak | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Drowsiness after fall | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Nose is crooked | Refer for fixing | Never treat at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Any head injury with a nosebleed must be taken seriously. The first concern is brain injury, not just the bleeding nose.

Query: My 6-year-old child picks her nose a lot and it bleeds. How do I stop it and how do I help it heal naturally without doctors?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding occurs multiple times a day**
- Large bruises appearing on other body parts without injury
- Frequent fevers or feeling very tired

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Pinch nose for 10 minutes, prevent nose picking. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Stop:** Use the pinch method (pinch soft part, lean forward) for 10 minutes.
- **Stop the Picking:** Keep her fingernails very short. Cover her finger with a cloth during sleep if she picks it then.
- **Moisturize Naturally:** Every night, apply Ghee (clarified butter) or pure coconut oil to the inside rim of her nostrils with a clean finger to prevent dryness and scabbing.
- **Breathe Steam:** Let her breathe steam from plain hot water before bed.
- **No Force:** Tell her not to pick at any scabs, as they will bleed again.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed from nose picking"] --> B["Pinch nostrils firmly for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  C --> D["Trim fingernails and discourage nose picking"]
  D --> E["Apply Ghee/Coconut Oil inside nostrils daily before bed"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if frequent or easy bruising noticed"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Nose picking related | Treat at home | Pinch, ghee, trim nails |
| Scab formation | Treat at home | Apply Ghee, avoid picking |
| Large body bruises | Refer to PHC immediately | Not safe at home |
| Bleeding is daily | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Nose-picking is the most common cause of nosebleeds in children. Healing the dry lining with natural oils is key.

Query: A man in my village is taking a medicine for 'blood thinning' and his nose started bleeding lightly this morning. It keeps stopping and starting. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding becomes heavy and won't stop with pressure**
- Passing very dark, black stool or blood in urine
- Large red/purple patches on the skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Pinch nose, do not stop medication on your own, refer to doctor. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pinch and Lean:** As soon as it starts bleeding, have him sit up, lean forward, and pinch the nose firmly for 15 minutes.
- **Cold Water:** Pour cold water on his head and face.
- **Refer to doctor:** He must contact the doctor who prescribed the medicine TODAY. The dose may need adjustment.
- **NO change in dose:** Tell him NOT to stop taking his medicine or change the dose unless his doctor tells him to.
- **Moisturize:** Use Ghee to prevent dryness.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Light nosebleed in patient on blood thinners"] --> B["Sit patient upright leaning forward"]
  B --> C["Pinch nostrils shut for 15 minutes firmly"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  D --> E["Do NOT change or stop medicine on your own"] 
  E --> F["Refer to prescribing doctor TODAY for review"]
  F --> G["Rush to CHC if bleeding becomes heavy and continuous"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Light bleeding (thinners) | Refer to his doctor TODAY | Pinch nose, cold water |
| Heavy bleeding (thinners) | Emergency referral to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Black stools/Blood in urine | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bleeding while on blood thinners means the medication dose might be too high. Immediate medical review is necessary.

Query: A 1-year-old baby's nose is bleeding after it fell on its face. It's just a small scratch. Should I put something on it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pus leaking from the nose scratch (infection)**
- Deep cut inside the baby's mouth or on the lips
- Baby is very sleepy and won't play or eat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Clean gently, no internal application. | 
| Child (1-2) | Any | Apply gentle pressure to the scratch, no internal medicine. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Gentle Pressure:** If the scratch is outside, apply gentle pressure with a clean cloth for 5 minutes.
- **Pinch slightly:** If it is inside, pinch the very top part of the soft nose gently for a few minutes.
- **NO herbs or cream inside:** Do not put anything inside the baby's nose. Clean with boiled cooled water.
- **Clean the Outside:** If the scratch is on the outside, wash with boiled cooled water and mild soap. No strong medicines.
- **Suck out blood (if inside):** If blood is blocking the nose, use an infant nose suction bulb, or just help the baby to sneeze.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Baby with small nose scratch bleeding"] --> B["Clean with boiled cooled water and mild soap"]
  B --> C["Apply gentle pressure to the scratch with clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put any medicines or herbs inside baby's nose"] 
  D --> E["Monitor for signs of infection (pus, redness)"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if baby is drowsy, not eating, or cut is deep"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Small scratch, no pus | Treat at home | Clean and monitor |
| Baby drowsy or not eating | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |
| Pus from scratch | Refer to PHC immediately | Never treat at home |
| Baby won't stop crying | Refer to PHC | Monitor at home first |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Baby skin is very sensitive. Stick to simple cleaning and gentle pressure. Any change in the baby’s activity requires medical attention.

Query: A teenager has a nosebleed and his eye is red on the same side. What should I advise? Can I put rose water in the eye?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding continues even after 20 minutes of pinching**
- Eye is painful and vision is blurry
- Swelling around the eye or nose
- Bleeding after a serious head injury or fight

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager | Male | Pinch nose, don't put anything in the eye, refer if vision changes. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pinch and Lean:** Sit upright, lean forward, pinch the nostrils firmly for 10-15 minutes.
- **NO Rose Water:** Do not put rose water or any drops in the eye. The red eye could be due to pressure or injury, and unknown substances can cause infection.
- **Cold Compress:** Place a cold cloth on the bridge of the nose and the forehead.
- **Check vision:** Ask him if his vision is blurry, double, or changed. If yes, this is an emergency.
- **Rest:** Tell him to sit in a quiet, cool place.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed with red eye on same side"] --> B["Sit patient upright and lean forward"]
  B --> C["Pinch nostrils firmly for 10-15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put rose water or drops in the eye"] 
  D --> E["Ask patient about vision changes"] 
  E --> F["Refer immediately to hospital for head/eye injury check"]
  F --> G["Keep patient calm and avoid sudden movements"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eye pain or blurriness | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Double vision | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Red eye, no vision change | Monitor at home | Pinch nose, rest |
| Swelling around eye | Refer to hospital | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A nosebleed with an eye injury can be serious, pointing to a bone fracture. Vision checks are priority.

Query: A man says his nose has been bleeding for days and is also having very dark, black stools. What is your advice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Very dark, black stools** (indicates bleeding in the stomach/intestines)
- Nosebleed that won't stop or is recurring frequently
- Feeling extremely weak, dizzy, or short of breath

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Treat nosebleed, refer immediately to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding First:** If the nose is bleeding now, use the pinch method for 15 minutes.
- **Cold Water:** Pour cold water on his head.
- **REFER IMMEDIATELY:** The black stools are a serious sign of internal bleeding (gastrointestinal bleeding) and the nosebleed could be related. This is an emergency.
- **Keep him calm:** During transport, keep him comfortable and encourage deep breathing.
- **Check pulse:** If possible, check his pulse. If it’s very fast and weak, rush.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Recurrent nosebleeds with black stool"] --> B["Sit patient upright, lean forward, pinch nose (if bleeding)"]
  B --> C["Ensure patient is calm and does not strain"]
  C --> D["Check for signs of weakness or shortness of breath"]
  D --> E["Ask about medications or history of ulcer/liver problems"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transfer immediately to hospital"]
  F --> G["Keep patient hydrated but do not give food/drink on the way"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Nosebleeds + Black Stool| EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Never treat at home |
| Vomiting blood | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Extreme weakness/Dizziness| EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Nosebleed only, stops with pressure| Refer to PHC within 24 hours | Pinch, rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Black stools are a sign of significant upper gastrointestinal bleeding. A bleeding nose could be another manifestation of a single bleeding disorder. Emergency treatment is essential.

Query: A child in the village gets nosebleeds every time there's a strong wind. I want to use 'Ghee' to stop it. What's the right way?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding occurs multiple times a day**
- Large bruises on other body parts without injury
- Child is unusually pale and tired

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Pinch nose for 10 minutes, use Ghee daily to prevent dryness. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Bleed:** If it's bleeding now, pinch the soft part of the nose, leaning forward, for 10 minutes.
- **Pinch properly:** Tell the child to breathe through their mouth while pinching.
- **Ghee (for prevention):** Every day, use a clean finger to apply a tiny bit of Ghee (clarified butter) or pure coconut oil to the inside *rim* of the nostrils, especially before sleep. This prevents dryness from the wind.
- **Steam Inhalation:** Encourage the child to breathe in steam (plain water) to moisturize the nose.
- **NO Ghee during bleeding:** Do not apply ghee or any substance into the nose *while* it is bleeding, as it will break the clot and cause infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed from wind/dryness"] --> B["Sit child upright and lean forward gently"]
  B --> C["Pinch the nostrils shut for 10 minutes non-stop"]
  C --> D["Apply Ghee/Coconut Oil daily to nostril rims (prevention only)"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply anything during active bleeding"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if frequent, or easy bruising noted"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Wind related, occasional | Treat at home | Pinch, Ghee daily |
| Scab formation | Treat at home | Apply Ghee, avoid picking |
| Large body bruises | Refer to PHC immediately | Not safe at home |
| Frequent (multiple times/week)| Refer to PHC for blood test | Not for home care only |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dry wind can crack the delicate lining of the nose. Using natural oils daily is effective prevention, but not during bleeding. Frequent cases need a medical review.

Query: A teenage girl has a dry cough and now her nose is bleeding. How do I help her at home? Can I use lemon juice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding continues even after 20 minutes of pinching**
- Vision becomes blurry or double
- Swelling around the eyes or face
- Bleeding after a serious head injury or fight

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Teenager | Female | Pinch nose, don't put anything in the eye, refer if vision changes. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding First:** Use the pinch method for 10 minutes.
- **Pinch correctly:** Sit up, lean forward, pinch the nostrils firmly, and breathe through your mouth.
- **NO Lemon Juice:** Do not put lemon juice or any drops in the eye. The red eye could be due to pressure or injury, and unknown substances can cause infection.
- **Cold Compress:** Place a cold cloth on the bridge of the nose and the forehead.
- **Rest:** Tell her to sit in a quiet, cool place.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed during dry cough"] --> B["Pinch nostrils firmly for 10 minutes"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put lemon juice or eye drops in the eye"]
  D --> E["Apply Ghee/Coconut Oil to nostril rim after bleeding stops"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if cough or bleeding worsens"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eye pain or blurriness | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Double vision | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Red eye, no vision change | Monitor at home | Pinch nose, rest |
| Swelling around eye | Refer to hospital | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A dry, raw nose lining is very sensitive. Lemon juice will burn it. Always prioritize pinching and cold compress to stop the bleeding. Consult a doctor if cough is severe.

Query: A man tells me his nose has been bleeding recurrently over the past few weeks and he is also losing weight. What should I advise?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Recurrent nosebleeds without obvious cause**
- Significant unintended weight loss
- New lumps or swelling in the neck or around the eyes
- Pain in the face or ear

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Pinch nose, refer to hospital for evaluation. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Bleeding First:** If the nose is bleeding now, have him sit up, lean forward, and pinch the nostrils for 15 minutes.
- **Cold Water:** Pour cold water on his head.
- **REFER IMMEDIATELY:** Recurrent nosebleeds with weight loss can be a sign of a serious underlying condition, including liver disease or cancer. A full medical checkup at a hospital is crucial.
- **Keep him calm:** During transport, keep him comfortable and encourage deep breathing.
- **Ask about habits:** Politely ask about smoking or alcohol consumption, as these increase risk.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Recurrent nosebleeds with weight loss"] --> B["Sit patient upright, lean forward, pinch nose (if bleeding)"]
  B --> C["Check for new lumps in the neck or face"]
  C --> D["Ask about smoking, alcohol, and history of liver problems"]
  D --> E["Monitor for signs of facial pain or ear blockage"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or transfer immediately to hospital"]
  F --> G["Keep patient calm and provide reassurance during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Recurrent nosebleeds + weight loss| EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Never treat at home |
| Facial pain or ear block | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Lumps in the neck | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Nosebleed only, stops with pressure| Refer to PHC within 24 hours | Pinch, rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Recurrent nosebleeds with weight loss can be caused by various serious illnesses. Emergency medical evaluation is essential.

Query: A child with nose bleeding has been given 'cold water' with 'jaggery' by his family. Is this okay? What should I do next?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding continues even after 20 minutes of pinching**
- Child looks pale, tired, or dizzy
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Pinch nose, allow jaggery water, refer if bleeding won't stop. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Allow Jaggery Water:** Yes, cold water with jaggery is fine to drink and can help with energy after a nosebleed, but it will not stop the bleeding directly.
- **Stop Bleeding First:** The most important step is to pinch the nostrils shut firmly for 10 minutes, leaning forward, breathe through your mouth.
- **Cold Compress:** Apply a cold wet cloth to the forehead and bridge of the nose.
- **Lubricate:** After the bleeding stops, apply a tiny bit of Ghee or Coconut Oil to the inside of the nostrils to prevent dryness.
- **Refer if necessary:** If bleeding continues after 20 minutes of pinching, go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with nose bleed has jaggery water"] --> B["Allow child to drink jaggery water for energy"]
  B --> C["Sit child upright and lean forward, pinch nostrils for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply cold compress to bridge of nose"]
  D --> E["Apply Ghee/Coconut Oil inside nostrils after bleeding stops"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if bleeding persists over 20 minutes despite pressure"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Simple nosebleed | Treat at home | Pinch, cold compress |
| Bleeding > 20 mins | Refer to PHC immediately | While traveling, keep pinching |
| Dizziness or fainting | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Jaggery is good for energy, but firm pinching is the primary way to stop a nosebleed. Always prioritize the pinch method.

Query: A woman has nose bleeding and severe headache. Her husband thinks it is due to 'heat stroke'. Should I believe him and what is the treatment?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extremely fast heartbeat or confusion**
- Dizziness, fainting, or blurry vision
- High fever or muscle stiffness
- Trouble breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Move to shade, cool body, pinch nose, refer immediately for headache. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool the Person:** Move her to a cool, shady area. Loosen any tight clothing. Apply cold water or ice to her head, neck, and face.
- **Stop Bleeding:** Sit her upright, leaning forward. Pinch the nostrils firmly for 10-15 minutes, breathing through the mouth.
- **Hydrate:** If she is conscious and can drink, give her sips of cool water or ORS. No tea or coffee.
- **REFER IMMEDIATELY:** Severe headache with a nosebleed is serious. It could be due to a spike in blood pressure or a stroke. Go to the nearest CHC/Hospital.
- **Keep calm:** Encourage slow, deep breaths during transport.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Nosebleed and severe headache, suspected heat stroke"] --> B["Move patient to shade, loosen clothing, cool body"]
  B --> C["Sit patient upright leaning forward, pinch nose for 10-15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Check for confusion, high fever, or blurry vision"]
  D --> E["Give ORS or cool water sips if conscious"] 
  E --> F["Call 108 or transport immediately to hospital"]
  F --> G["Monitor pulse and breathing during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe headache | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Confusion or fainting | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| High fever | EMERGENCY REFERRAL | Not safe at home |
| Simple nosebleed (after cooling)| Monitor at home | Pinch, rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe headache with a nosebleed can be a sign of dangerously high blood pressure. Emergency medical care is essential.

Query: A small child was eating a guava and now he is not able to breathe or cry. His face is turning blue. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Inability to cry, cough, or speak**
- **Face or lips turning blue/grey**
- High-pitched noises while breathing
- Clutching the throat (the universal choking sign)
- Loss of consciousness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1 year) | Any | 5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts |
| Child (1-12) | Any | Abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) standing or kneeling |
| Adult | Any | Strong abdominal thrusts until object is out |
| Pregnant | Female | Chest thrusts instead of abdominal |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If coughing strongly, do nothing but encourage coughing.
- If silent, stand behind them and wrap arms around the waist.
- Make a fist just **above the navel**.
- Pull **inward and upward** quickly.
- Repeat until the object is out or the person faints.
- For infants, lay them face down on your arm; give **5 firm back blows**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confirm person cannot breathe or speak"] --> B["Stand behind the person and wrap arms around waist"]
  B --> C["Place fist above navel and pull inward and upward sharply"]
  C --> D["Repeat thrusts until the object is forced out"]
  D --> E["If person faints, lay them flat and call 108"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Object stuck, turning blue | Emergency - Rush to Hospital | Perform thrusts while moving |
| Person becomes unconscious | Emergency - Call 108 | Do NOT wait |
| Object out, breathing normal | Visit PHC for throat check | Monitor at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
These actions are for emergency first-aid only. If the object is not removed within a minute, transport to the nearest facility immediately.

Query: An old man in the village got a large piece of dry roti stuck in his throat and is gasping. He is very weak. How can I help him?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Gasping for air without sound**
- **Weak or silent cough**
- Eyes bulging and face turning red
- Grabbing the neck with both hands

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Support their back; perform abdominal thrusts gently but firmly |
| Adult | Any | Standard abdominal thrusts (Heimlich Maneuver) |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Ask: "Are you choking?" If they nod, act immediately.
- Stand behind them and provide support so they do not fall.
- Perform **abdominal thrusts**: pull upward and inward above the belly button.
- If they are too heavy or weak to stand, perform thrusts while they sit in a chair.
- Do NOT reach into the throat; you may push the roti deeper.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify gasping and silent choking in elderly patient"] --> B["Stand behind patient and support their weight"]
  B --> C["Apply quick upward abdominal thrusts above the navel"]
  C --> D["Repeat until the food piece is expelled"]
  D --> E["If object remains stuck, call 108 immediately"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital while keeping patient upright"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Breathing remains difficult | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Fainting/Unconsciousness | Call 108 immediately | Not safe at home |
| Object out but throat hurts | Visit PHC for evaluation | Soft liquids at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal injuries can occur during thrusts. Always have the patient examined by a doctor after the incident.

Query: A toddler has swallowed a small plastic bead. He is making a whistling sound when he breathes but he is still conscious. What are the steps?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Whistling or wheezing sound**
- **Sudden change in voice or cry**
- Drooling because they cannot swallow saliva

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Toddler (1-3) | Any | Kneel behind them; perform light abdominal thrusts |
| Infant (<1) | Any | 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If wheezing but breathing, **do not slap their back**; the bead might shift and block the airway completely.
- Keep the child calm and still; crying can draw the bead deeper.
- If the child stops breathing or coughs weakly, perform **abdominal thrusts**.
- Rush to the nearest CHC where they have specialized tools.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with whistling breath from swallowed bead"] --> B["Keep the child calm and upright"]
  B --> C["Check if child can still speak or cry"]
  C --> D["Do NOT slap back if child is still breathing"]
  D --> E["Rush to CHC or Hospital immediately for removal"]
  E --> F["If breathing stops, start abdominal thrusts during transport"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Whistling sound | Immediate referral to CHC | Not safe at home |
| Bead coughed out | Visit PHC to ensure no damage | Monitor at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Small objects can move and cause sudden total blockage. Always seek professional help.

Query: A man was eating fish and a bone got stuck. He is pointing to his throat and looks in a lot of pain, but he can talk. Should I do the abdominal pull?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Inability to swallow saliva**
- **Blood in spit**
- Pain deep in the chest or throat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Do NOT perform thrusts if they can talk; refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If the person can speak or cough, **do NOT** do abdominal thrusts.
- Do not try to force it down with large balls of rice or banana; this may push the bone deeper into the throat wall.
- Ask the person to open their mouth; if the bone is visible and easy to reach, use tweezers. Otherwise, stop.
- Give small sips of water. If pain persists, go to the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person choking on fish bone but can speak"] --> B["Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts or back blows"]
  B --> C["Do NOT force patient to eat rice or banana balls"]
  C --> D["Check mouth for visible bone near the surface"]
  D --> E["If bone not visible, refer to PHC for removal"]
  E --> F["Monitor for any difficulty breathing or blood"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe pain or bleeding | Refer to CHC/Hospital | Not safe at home |
| Bone visible and shallow | PHC for safe removal | If it falls out on its own |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fish bones can puncture the throat. If pain is severe, it is a medical emergency.

Query: A 6-month-old baby stopped breathing while playing with a tamarind seed. I tried to pull it out but it went deeper. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Silent struggling in an infant**
- **Skin turning blue or pale**
- Limp body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | 5 back blows followed by 5 chest thrusts |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay the baby face down on your forearm, supporting the jaw.
- Give **5 firm blows** between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
- If the seed doesn't come out, turn the baby face up.
- Give **5 chest thrusts** using two fingers in the center of the chest.
- Repeat until the seed is out or the baby faints.
- Never perform a blind finger sweep; it pushes objects deeper.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant choking on tamarind seed"] --> B["Perform 5 back blows with heel of hand"]
  B --> C["If object stays, perform 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers"]
  C --> D["Repeat back blows and chest thrusts until seed is out"]
  D --> E["If baby faints, start rescue breaths and rush to hospital"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or local transport immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any infant choking episode | Always refer to PHC after | Emergency only |
| Unconscious infant | Emergency - Hospital | Never treat at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infants have very fragile airways. Even if the seed comes out, they must be checked for lung injury.

Query: A pregnant woman is choking on a betel nut (supari). I am afraid to press her belly. What is the right way?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Clutching throat**
- **Inability to speak**
- Red/Blue face

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | **Chest thrusts** instead of abdominal thrusts |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stand behind the woman.
- Wrap your arms under her armpits and across her **chest**.
- Place your fist in the middle of her breastbone (chest).
- Pull backward sharply.
- Repeat until the supari is coughed up.
- Do NOT press on the stomach/uterus area.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman choking"] --> B["Stand behind her and wrap arms under armpits"]
  B --> C["Place fist on the middle of the breastbone"]
  C --> D["Perform sharp chest thrusts backward"]
  D --> E["Repeat until object is expelled"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC to check mother and fetal health"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Choking during pregnancy | Always refer to CHC after | Emergency thrusts only |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Ensure the pressure is on the bone of the chest, not the lower ribs or abdomen.

Query: A very fat man is choking on meat. My arms won't go around his waist. What do I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Silent panic**
- **Unable to draw air**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Obese Adult | Any | Chest thrusts or perform against a wall |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If your arms don't reach around the waist, move them up to the **chest**.
- Perform thrusts on the breastbone (like for pregnant women).
- If he falls, perform chest compressions while he is on his back.
- Use the weight of your body to push the object out.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Obese person choking"] --> B["Try chest thrusts if waist is too wide"]
  B --> C["If unsuccessful, place them against a wall"]
  C --> D["Apply pressure to the chest bone"]
  D --> E["If they collapse, call 108 and start chest compressions"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Persistent blockage | Call 108 immediately | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Speed is more important than perfect technique. Use force on the chest if the waist is unreachable.

Query: I am alone and choking on a hard candy. No one is here to help me. How can I save myself?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Panic and inability to breathe**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Self | Any | Self-Heimlich using a chair or table |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Try to cough as hard as you can.
- Find a fixed firm object like the **back of a wooden chair** or a table edge.
- Lean over the edge and press your abdomen (above the navel) against it.
- Use the chair to push inward and upward into your body.
- Repeat until the candy is out.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Alone and choking"] --> B["Find a chair or table edge"] --> C["Lean abdomen against the hard edge"] --> D["Thrust body against the edge repeatedly"] --> E["Keep going until object is coughed out"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sore throat or chest | Visit PHC to check for injury | Home care after check |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
If you cannot get the object out, try to get to a neighbor or the street immediately so you are seen.

Query: A child swallowed a coin. He is not choking now but says it hurts to swallow. Is it dangerous?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Drooling**
- **Refusing to eat**
- Chest pain

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- If the child is breathing fine, do NOT do any back blows or thrusts.
- Do not give any laxatives or oils to 'slide' the coin out.
- Keep the child calm and take them to a hospital with an **X-ray**.
- Coins can get stuck in the esophagus (food pipe) and cause damage over time.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child swallowed coin; breathing fine"] --> B["Do NOT perform emergency thrusts"] --> C["Check for drooling or chest pain"] --> D["Refer to Hospital for X-ray immediately"] --> E["Monitor for any sudden breathing trouble"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swallowed coin | Refer to Hospital for X-ray | Never treat at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Never wait for the coin to 'pass' without an initial X-ray to see where it is stuck.

Query: A woman has fainted because she was choking on food. What do I do now that she is on the floor?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unconsciousness**
- **No chest movement (not breathing)**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Lay her flat on her back on the ground.
- Call **108** immediately.
- Start **chest compressions** (pushing hard and fast in the center of the chest).
- Every time you stop to check, look inside the mouth. If you see the food, take it out.
- Do NOT do abdominal thrusts on an unconscious person.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Choking victim faints"] --> B["Lay them on their back on firm ground"] --> C["Call 108 immediately"] --> D["Start chest compressions (30 times)"] --> E["Check mouth for object and remove if seen"] --> F["Repeat until medical help arrives"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | Emergency - Hospital | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
CPR (chest compressions) is the only way to help a fainted choking victim.

Query: A toddler was playing with a marble and now he is making a 'crowing' sound. He is conscious but scared.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Crowing or high-pitched sound (Stridor)**
- **Nostrils flaring**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- The crowing sound means the airway is partially blocked.
- Do NOT shake the child or turn them upside down.
- If they are coughing, let them cough.
- If the sound gets weaker or they can't breathe, perform **abdominal thrusts**.
- Take the child to the CHC immediately; do not wait for the sound to go away.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child making high-pitched crowing sound"] --> B["Encourage child to cough if possible"] --> C["Do NOT shake child"] --> D["Refer to CHC immediately"] --> E["If sound stops and child turns blue, start thrusts"]
```

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A marble is heavy and smooth; it can easily slip deeper. Professional removal is required.

Query: An ASHA worker is at a wedding and someone is choking. There is a lot of noise. How do I lead the crowd?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Shout for people to move back to give the person air.
- Identify yourself as a trained worker.
- Ask the victim: "Are you choking?" If they nod, begin thrusts.
- Tell someone specifically to "Call 108".
- Continue thrusts until the object comes out.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Public choking incident"] --> B["Clear the crowd and identify yourself"] --> C["Confirm choking and start Heimlich Maneuver"] --> D["Assign one person to call for help"] --> E["Perform thrusts until object is expelled"]
```

Query: Can I give water to a person who just stopped choking? They are asking for it.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wait at least 5-10 minutes after the object is out.
- First, ask them to swallow their own spit. If they can do that without pain, give a tiny sip of water.
- Do NOT give large amounts of water immediately as the throat may be swollen or injured, leading to more choking.

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain while drinking | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Water in the lungs (aspiration) is a risk if they haven't fully recovered.

Query: What if the person is much taller than me? How do I reach to do the pull?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Have the person sit in a sturdy chair while you stand behind them.
- Or, have them lean forward so their chest is lower.
- Perform the thrusts at the same level (above navel) using your full strength.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Victim is much taller than rescuer"] --> B["Have victim sit in a chair"] --> C["Stand behind the chair and reach around waist"] --> D["Perform upward abdominal thrusts"] --> E["Repeat until object is out"]
```

Query: A child swallowed a small battery from a toy. He is not choking. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **This is an extreme emergency even without choking.**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT** induce vomiting.
- **Do NOT** give food or water.
- Button batteries can burn through the throat or stomach in **2 hours**.
- Rush to the highest level hospital nearby (District Hospital) immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child swallowed button battery"] --> B["Do NOT perform thrusts if breathing"] --> C["Do NOT give any food or water"] --> D["Rush to District Hospital for immediate removal"] --> E["Time is critical - act within minutes"]
```

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Battery acid causes permanent damage. This is more dangerous than a coin.

Query: Can I use a spoon to get food out of a child's throat?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NEVER** put a spoon, finger, or any tool into the throat blindly.
- You will likely push the object further down or cause the throat to swell shut from injury.
- Use only back blows and chest/abdominal thrusts.

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Mechanical injury from spoons can cause bleeding that makes it impossible for the doctor to see the object later.

Query: A man is choking and also having a fit (seizure). How do I handle this?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT put anything in his mouth.
- Roll him onto his side (recovery position) to let spit or food drain out.
- Wait for the shaking to stop before trying to clear the airway.
- If he is not breathing after the fit, check the mouth and call 108.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Choking and Seizure together"] --> B["Roll patient to their side"] --> C["Do NOT put hands in mouth"] --> D["After shaking stops, check breathing"] --> E["Call 108 immediately"]
```

Query: The object came out, but the person is still coughing and sounds 'wet'. What now?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wet, gurgling breath**
- Fever starting later

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- A 'wet' sound means some liquid or small pieces might have gone into the lungs.
- This can cause **Pneumonia**.
- Refer them to the PHC for a lung check (listening with a stethoscope).

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Aspiration pneumonia is a common killer after a choking incident. Don't ignore a wet cough.

Query: A baby is choking on milk. He is coughing and turning red.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stop feeding immediately.
- Sit the baby up and lean them forward slightly.
- Pat the back gently.
- If the baby stops coughing and turns blue, then start 5 back blows.
- Most milk choking is solved by just changing position and burping.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Baby choking on milk"] --> B["Stop feeding and sit baby upright"] --> C["Gently pat back to help clear milk"] --> D["If baby turns blue or stops breathing, start 5 back blows"] --> E["Clear mouth of excess milk"]
```

Query: Is there any herbal paste or oil to help a stuck betel nut slide down?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No.** Oil or pastes can get into the lungs and cause suffocation.
- Choking is a **mechanical block**. It needs a **mechanical push** (thrusts) to come out.
- Do not waste time on home remedies; every second without air kills brain cells.

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Brutal Honesty: Using oils or home remedies for a total airway block is a waste of life-saving time. Use the Heimlich maneuver only.

Query: Didi, a small boy in the colony has red itchy spots all over his body and a high fever. He is very cranky. What should I tell the mother?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rash that does not fade when pressed with a glass**
- **High fever with a stiff neck**
- Difficulty breathing or swelling of the face
- Purple or blood-colored spots
- Rapidly spreading blisters

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Keep hydrated, use cold compress, monitor fever |
| Adult | Any | Avoid scratching, use loose cotton clothes |
| Infant (<1) | Any | Immediate referral if fever is present with rash |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Clean the area gently with plain cool water.
- Apply a paste of **fresh Neem leaves** or use Neem water to soothe itching.
- Wear loose, clean cotton clothes to prevent sweating.
- Keep the person away from others until fever subsides.
- Do NOT apply strong chemical soaps or perfumes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check if rash fades under pressure"] --> B["Check for high fever and neck stiffness"]
  B --> C["Apply cool Neem water compress for itching"]
  C --> D["Ensure the patient drinks plenty of fluids"]
  D --> E["If breathing is difficult, rush to CHC immediately"]
  E --> F["If rash spreads to eyes or mouth, refer to doctor"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Purple/non-fading spots | Emergency - Rush to Hospital | Never treat at home |
| Mild itching, no fever | Monitor at home | Use Neem/Aloe Vera |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency referral | Not safe at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is first-aid guidance. Rashes with fever can be serious infections. Always consult a medical officer at the PHC.

Query: A farmer has developed a very itchy, red, weeping rash on his legs after working in the monsoon fields. What home remedy can I suggest?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pus oozing from the rash**
- **Red streaks spreading away from the area**
- Swelling and extreme heat in the legs
- Fever and chills

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (Farmer) | Any | Wash with soap, dry thoroughly, apply Turmeric paste |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the legs thoroughly with clean water and mild soap after field work.
- Apply a paste of **Turmeric (Haldi) and Coconut Oil**; it acts as a natural antiseptic.
- Keep the area dry; moisture makes fungal and bacterial rashes worse.
- Avoid wearing tight synthetic clothes or wet socks.
- Do NOT use mud or cow dung on the rash.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash affected area with clean water and soap"] --> B["Dry the skin completely with a clean cloth"]
  B --> C["Apply Turmeric and Coconut oil paste gently"]
  C --> D["Keep the area open to air as much as possible"]
  D --> E["If pus or fever develops, refer to PHC for antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Oozing pus or foul smell | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |
| Itching without fever | Use Haldi/Coconut oil | Home care |
| Red streaks on skin | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Monsoon rashes often need antifungal treatment. If local remedies don't show improvement in 2 days, visit the PHC.

Query: A woman has a painful rash that looks like a belt of blisters on one side of her waist. She says it burns like fire.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rash near the eyes or forehead**
- **Severe, unbearable pain**
- Blisters that seem to be getting infected with pus

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult/Elderly | Any | Keep blisters dry, do not burst them, refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply a cool, wet compress to the area to reduce the burning sensation.
- Do NOT burst the blisters; this leads to infection and scarring.
- Use **Sandalwood (Chandan) paste** for a cooling effect if the skin is not broken.
- Keep the area covered with a light, loose bandage to prevent rubbing against clothes.
- Refer to the doctor immediately for medicine to stop the virus.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify painful one-sided blister belt"] --> B["Apply cool wet cloth for pain relief"]
  B --> C["Do NOT burst or pick at the blisters"]
  C --> D["Keep the area clean and loosely covered"]
  D --> E["Refer to CHC/PHC for antiviral treatment within 48 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blisters near eyes | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Burning pain | Refer for medication | Use cooling pastes for comfort |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Painful one-sided rashes need specific medical treatment. Home remedies only help with comfort, not the cure.

Query: A baby has small red bumps in the diaper area and is crying whenever he passes urine. What can the mother do at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin is peeling or bleeding**
- **Pimples with yellow heads (pus)**
- Fever in the baby

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Change diaper/nappy immediately, keep skin dry |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Leave the baby without a nappy for a few hours every day to let the skin breathe.
- Clean only with plain water; avoid scented wipes.
- Apply a thin layer of **Coconut oil** to act as a barrier against urine.
- Use soft, old cotton clothes instead of plastic or synthetic nappies.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove wet nappy and clean with plain water"] --> B["Pat the skin dry gently; do not rub"]
  B --> C["Apply pure Coconut oil as a protective layer"]
  C --> D["Give the baby 'nappy-free' time to air out skin"]
  D --> E["If rash stays for 3 days or has pus, visit PHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pus or bleeding skin | Refer to PHC | Not safe at home |
| Redness only | Use oil and air-drying | Home care |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Diaper rashes can quickly turn into fungal infections. Seek help if simple oil and drying don't work.

Query: Several children in one house have very itchy rashes between their fingers and on their wrists, especially at night. What should the family do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Secondary infection with pus and swelling**
- **Sores from constant scratching**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Wash all clothes in boiling water, refer all family members |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This spreads easily. Treat the **whole family** at once even if they don't itch yet.
- Boil all bedsheets, towels, and clothes in hot water and dry them in direct sun.
- Apply a paste of **Neem and Turmeric** over the body before bathing.
- Keep nails short to prevent skin damage from scratching.
- Go to the PHC for the specific 'scabies' lotion.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify itching between fingers, worse at night"] --> B["Boil all household clothes and bedding in water"] --> C["Dry all clothes in bright sunlight"] --> D["Refer entire family to PHC for treatment together"] --> E["Apply soothing Neem paste for itching"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itching between fingers | Refer to PHC (needs lotion) | Boiling clothes at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Home remedies will not kill the mites causing this rash. You must get the lotion from the clinic.

Query: A child has a 'slapped cheek' looking red rash on the face and a lacy rash on the arms. No fever. Can he go to school?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Joint pain**
- **Pale skin or tiredness**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the child hydrated with water and coconut water.
- Use **Aloe Vera gel** (fresh from the plant) to soothe the skin if it feels hot.
- Avoid hot baths as heat makes the rash look brighter and itch more.
- If the child is active and has no fever, they are usually not contagious once the rash appears, but check with the local school.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Red cheeks and lacy body rash"] --> B["Check for fever or joint pain"] --> C["Apply fresh Aloe Vera gel for cooling"] --> D["Avoid hot water baths"] --> E["Monitor for return of fever or paleness"]
```

Query: Someone touched a wild plant in the jungle and now has red, itchy streaks on their arms with small fluid-filled bumps.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Immediately wash the area with plenty of soap and water to remove plant oils.
- Apply **cold milk** or a paste of **Sandalwood** to stop the itching.
- Do NOT scratch or rub the area as the oil can spread to other parts of the body.
- If the itching is severe, use a cold cloth compress.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash area with soap and water immediately"] --> B["Apply cold compress or Sandalwood paste"] --> C["Do NOT touch eyes or face with affected hands"] --> D["If swelling occurs in face/throat, call 108"]
```

Query: An old lady has a dry, scaly rash in the folds of her skin (under breasts and armpits). It smells a bit sour.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Keep the skin folds dry by using a clean cotton cloth to pat them frequently.
- Dust the area with a little bit of **dry roasted Gram flour (Besan)** to absorb moisture.
- Avoid using oils or heavy creams in the folds as they trap moisture.
- Wear loose cotton blouses.

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Foul smell or raw skin | Refer to PHC | Keep dry and clean |
```

Query: A man took a new tablet for headache and now has itchy hives (raised red welts) all over his back. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of the lips or tongue**
- **Hoarseness of voice or difficulty breathing**
- Feeling faint or dizzy

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stop the medication immediately.
- Apply a **cold water compress** to the hives to reduce swelling.
- Drink plenty of plain water.
- If there is any trouble breathing, rush to the nearest emergency center immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the suspected medicine immediately"] --> B["Check for lip swelling or breathing trouble"] --> C["Apply cold compress to raised welts"] --> D["Refer to doctor to record the drug allergy"] --> E["If breathing is hard, call 108 immediately"]
```

Query: The children have small red spots that turn into 'dew drop' blisters all over the body. It started with a mild fever.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever that doesn't go down**
- **Vomiting or confusion**
- **Severe cough or chest pain**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Isolate the child to prevent spreading to others (especially pregnant women).
- Apply **Baking soda mixed with water** or Neem water to help with the itching.
- Do NOT give Aspirin for the fever; use only what the PHC gives.
- Keep nails short to prevent scratching the blisters.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify 'dew drop' blisters with mild fever"] --> B["Isolate the patient from others"] --> C["Apply cooling Neem water to skin"] --> D["Keep child hydrated and resting"] --> E["Refer if cough or high fever develops"]
```

Query: What to do for 'Ghamori' (Heat Rash) in kids during the peak summer?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Bathe the child twice a day in cool water.
- Use **Multani Mitti (Fuller's Earth)** paste on the affected areas for 10 minutes then wash off.
- Ensure they stay in the shade and wear thin, light-colored cotton.
- Do not use thick oils or prickly heat powders that block pores further.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bathe child in cool water"] --> B["Apply Multani Mitti paste for cooling"] --> C["Switch to thin cotton clothing"] --> D["Keep child in a well-ventilated, cool area"]
```

Query: A teenager has a circular, red, scaly patch with a clear center on his arm. It itches a lot.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This is a fungal infection. Keep it dry.
- Apply **Garlic juice or crushed Garlic** mixed with a bit of coconut oil (test on a small spot first).
- Do NOT share towels, soap, or clothes with anyone else.
- Wash all bedding in hot water.
- Refer to the PHC for an antifungal cream.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify ring-shaped scaly patch"] --> B["Avoid sharing personal items"] --> C["Keep the skin patch dry"] --> D["Refer to PHC for antifungal cream"] --> E["Wash towels and bedding in hot water"]
```

Query: An infant has thick, yellow, crusty scales on the scalp. It doesn't seem to itch.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply a little **Coconut oil** to the scalp and leave it for 20 minutes.
- Gently brush the scales with a soft baby brush or cloth.
- Wash the scalp with a mild baby soap.
- Do NOT try to scrape the scales off with your fingernails.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Apply Coconut oil to crusty scalp"] --> B["Wait 20 minutes for scales to soften"] --> C["Gently brush with a soft cloth"] --> D["Wash with mild soap and water"]
```

Query: A woman has red, itchy bumps only where she wears her artificial jewelry or metal watch.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stop wearing the jewelry/metal immediately.
- Apply a **cold compress** to the area.
- Use **fresh Aloe Vera gel** to soothe the irritation.
- If the area is weeping or has blisters, keep it dry and don't apply anything else.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove all jewelry and metal from the area"] --> B["Apply cold compress for itching"] --> C["Apply fresh Aloe Vera gel"] --> D["Avoid further contact with that metal"]
```

Query: Is it okay to use cow dung or mud on a very itchy rash to cool it down?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Infection risk is very high**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NO.** Never use cow dung or untreated mud on a rash.
- These contain bacteria that can cause a serious skin infection or even Tetanus.
- Use **Multani Mitti** or **Chandan** (Sandalwood) instead if you want a cooling effect.
- If you have already used dung/mud, wash it off immediately with soap and clean water.

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Using animal waste on broken skin is a major cause of infections in rural areas. Always use clean, natural cooling agents.

Query: A child has small, firm, skin-colored bumps with a tiny dimple in the middle. They are spreading.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Do NOT squeeze or pick at the bumps; they contain a virus that will spread more if popped.
- Keep the child's towel and clothes separate from siblings.
- Cover the bumps with clothing to prevent scratching.
- Refer to the PHC as these can take a long time to go away without help.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify bumps with a central dimple"] --> B["Do NOT squeeze or pick bumps"] --> C["Keep child's clothes and towels separate"] --> D["Refer to PHC for treatment"]
```

Query: After walking in tall grass, a man has many tiny red dots around his ankles that itch like crazy.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Take a hot soapy bath immediately to wash off any remaining mites/insects.
- Apply a paste of **Baking soda and water**.
- Use a **cold cloth** to stop the itching sensation.
- Do NOT scratch, as it will lead to sores.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Take a hot soapy bath immediately"] --> B["Apply Baking soda paste to bites"] --> C["Apply cold compress for itching"] --> D["Avoid scratching the area"]
```

Query: A girl has a bright red rash around her mouth and her skin is very dry and cracked there.
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This is often from lip-licking. Stop the child from licking the area.
- Apply **Ghee** or **Coconut oil** multiple times a day to provide a moisture barrier.
- Avoid spicy or sour foods that might sting the cracked skin.
- Wash the face with plain water only.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the child from licking lips"] --> B["Apply Ghee or Coconut oil frequently"] --> C["Avoid spicy or acidic foods"] --> D["Wash face with plain water only"]
```

Query: Can I use 'Dettol' or 'Savlon' directly on an itchy rash?
Answer:
## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No.** Do not apply undiluted antiseptic liquids directly to a rash.
- They are too strong and can burn the skin or cause an allergic reaction, making the rash worse.
- If you must use them, dilute them heavily in a bucket of water for bathing.
- Clean water and mild soap are much better for rashes.

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chemical burns from concentrated antiseptics are common. Always dilute or stick to plain water.

Query: The whole village is getting a rash with joint pain and a high fever. What should the ASHA do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding from gums or nose**
- **Extreme weakness or fainting**
- **Severe abdominal pain**

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- This sounds like a viral outbreak (like Dengue or Chikungunya).
- Tell everyone to use mosquito nets and clear stagnant water.
- Encourage high fluid intake: ORS, coconut water, and rice kanji.
- Give Paracetamol for fever (NOT Aspirin).
- Report the cluster of cases to the Medical Officer immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Report case cluster to Medical Officer"] --> B["Encourage high fluid intake (ORS/Kanji)"] --> C["Ensure use of mosquito nets"] --> D["Check for any bleeding signs (emergency)"] --> E["Monitor for fever using Paracetamol only"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding/Gums/Nose | Emergency - Hospital | Never |
| Mild Joint pain + Fever | Home care + Fluids | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Outbreaks require immediate government notification. Do not try to manage multiple similar cases alone.

query: A child ate groundnuts and now his face is swelling and he is scratching his whole body. He says his throat feels tight. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling of lips, tongue, or throat
- **Difficulty breathing or wheezing**
- Blue or pale skin
- Fainting or sudden weakness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Emergency. Sit them upright, do not give food/water, rush to CHC. |
| Adult | Any | Identify trigger, monitor breathing, keep airway clear. |
| Elderly | Any | High risk. Call 108 immediately if breathing is noisy. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove the trigger** (stop the food/item).
- Sit the person **upright** to help them breathe.
- If they feel faint, lay them down with **legs raised**.
- Loosen tight clothing around the neck.
- Apply a **cool, wet cloth** to hives/rashes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify reaction and remove trigger"] --> B["Check for throat swelling or wheezing"]
  B --> C["Sit the person upright to open airway"]
  C --> D["Loosen tight clothing around neck"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give anything to drink or eat"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Throat swelling | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Mild skin rash | Visit PHC | Cold compress and monitor |
| Dizziness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe allergies (Anaphylaxis) kill quickly. Do not wait for symptoms to improve at home.

query: A woman was working in mustard fields; her eyes are red, watery, and she is sneezing non-stop with itchy skin.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Sudden swelling of the face
- **Feeling of a lump in the throat**
- Shortness of breath

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Move away from field, wash skin with clean water. |
| Pregnant | Female | Monitor closely; refer to PHC for safe medication. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Move away** from the field or pollen source.
- **Wash eyes/face** with cool, clean water for 5 minutes.
- Change clothes to remove dust/pollen.
- Apply **Neem water** (boiled then cooled) to itchy skin.
- Do not rub eyes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Move person to fresh air away from field"] --> B["Wash eyes and face with cool water"]
  B --> C["Change dusty clothes immediately"]
  C --> D["Apply cool cloth or Neem water to skin"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any breathing difficulty"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if sneezing persists"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red eyes only | If no relief in 1 hour | Wash and rest |
| Facial swelling | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Hard breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pollen allergies can lead to asthma attacks. Refer if the person is gasping for air.

query: A man took a new fever tablet and now has red itchy spots all over his chest. What is the help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin peeling or blisters**
- Swelling of lips/tongue
- Fever with the rash

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | STOP the medicine. Save the strip/packet. |
| Elderly | Any | Immediate PHC referral; drug reactions are risky. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the medicine** immediately.
- Keep the medicine strip to show the doctor.
- Apply **Chandan (Sandalwood) paste** or cool water for itching.
- Give plenty of water to drink.
- Do NOT apply hot water to the rash.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the medicine and save the strip"] --> B["Check for mouth sores or lip swelling"]
  B --> C["Apply cool water to itchy areas"] 
  C --> D["Give sips of water if swallowing is easy"]
  D --> E["Transport to PHC for drug reaction check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rash after medicine | PHC immediately | Never - Stop dose |
| Blisters | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Never give the same medicine again. A second reaction is often much more dangerous.

query: A boy was stung by a wasp. His hand is swelling fast and he is crying. What are the steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling spreading to the neck/face
- **Tightness in the chest**
- Nausea or stomach pain

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Scrape out stinger, apply cold, monitor for 1 hour. |
