| Infant | Any | Emergency referral; rapid swelling risk. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Scrape stinger out** with a plastic card. Do NOT squeeze.
- Wash with **soap and water**.
- Apply **Ice or a cold metal spoon** for 15 minutes.
- Apply **Tulsi leaf juice** for pain.
- Keep the hand raised high.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Scrape out stinger with flat card"] --> B["Wash area with soap and water"]
  B --> C["Apply ice or cold cloth for 15 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply Tulsi juice to sting site"]
  D --> E["Monitor for body-wide itching"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if swelling is very large"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pain at sting site | If worsening after 2 hours | Cold compress/Tulsi |
| Multiple stings | Refer to CHC | Never |
| Sting inside mouth | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stings on the face or neck are high risk because they can block the airway.

query: A person used hair dye; scalp is burning and forehead is swelling. Eye is also closing.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling moving to the neck**
- Oozing or burning skin
- Vision changes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Wash hair for 20 mins with mild soap. |
| Elderly | Any | Refer to PHC; skin is thin and more sensitive. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash scalp** with running water for 20 minutes.
- Use mild soap to remove all chemicals.
- Apply **Aloe Vera gel** to the forehead.
- Do NOT apply oil to the burning skin.
- Keep the person in a cool room.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash hair and scalp with running water"] --> B["Remove all dye residue with mild soap"]
  B --> C["Apply Aloe Vera gel to swollen skin"]
  C --> D["Monitor if swelling moves to the neck"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply heat or hair oil"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for skin care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swollen eyes | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Scalp itching | If no relief after wash | Wash and Aloe |
| Puss or oozing | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Chemical burns from dye can get worse after 2 days. Keep checking the person.

query: A man ate fish and now his stomach hurts very much and he has itchy 'thappad' (hives) on his back.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe vomiting or stomach cramps**
- Swelling of lips
- Rapid heartbeat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Stop eating, drink water, monitor for 2 hours. |
| Child | Any | Refer to PHC; food allergies can turn severe fast. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Stop the food immediately.
- Apply **cool coconut oil** or water to hives.
- If vomiting, give small sips of water.
- Encourage the person to sit quietly; do not let them run or work.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the food trigger"] --> B["Check for stomach pain or vomiting"]
  B --> C["Apply cool coconut oil to skin hives"]
  C --> D["Keep the person sitting and calm"]
  D --> E["Monitor for lip swelling or dizziness"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if symptoms increase"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hives on skin | If no relief in 2 hours | Cool compress |
| Stomach cramps | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Lip swelling | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stomach pain after food can be a sign of a severe whole-body allergic reaction.

query: A girl brushed against a 'Bichu Booti' (Stinging Nettle) plant. Her arm is red and she is screaming from the sting.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wide body rash**
- Breathing trouble (rare with plants but possible)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash area, do not rub, apply local remedies. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area** with soap and water WITHOUT rubbing (rubbing spreads the plant needles).
- Use **sticky tape** to pull out tiny plant hairs if visible.
- Apply a paste of **baking soda** or **Mustard oil**.
- Use **cold mud** or a cool cloth for burning sensation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Gently wash area without rubbing skin"] --> B["Use tape to remove tiny plant hairs"] 
  B --> C["Apply baking soda paste or cold mud"]
  C --> D["Do NOT let the child scratch the area"]
  D --> E["Monitor for 1 hour for any breathing issues"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Local burning | No need unless persistent | Home remedies |
| Rash on face | Visit PHC | Never |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Most plant stings stay on the skin, but if the person feels dizzy, it is an emergency.

query: A newborn has small red bumps after a new soap was used. Mother is worried.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever**
- **Pus-filled bumps**
- Crying that doesn't stop

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant | Any | Stop the soap, use only plain water. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the new soap** immediately.
- Bathe the baby in **lukewarm plain water** (no soap).
- Pat dry gently with a soft cotton cloth (do not rub).
- Apply a thin layer of **Coconut oil** if the skin is dry.
- Keep the baby in loose cotton clothes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop using the new soap immediately"] --> B["Wash baby with plain lukewarm water"]
  B --> C["Pat skin dry gently with soft cloth"]
  C --> D["Apply pure coconut oil to red areas"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or pus in the bumps"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for pediatric checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Small red bumps | PHC for confirmation | Plain water wash |
| Fever/Pus | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Spreading rash | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Newborn skin is very thin. Never use adult creams or strong herbal pastes on them.

query: An old man got bitten by many ants. He is feeling very itchy and his blood pressure seems to be falling.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dizziness or cold/clammy skin**
- **Confusion**
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Lay flat, raise legs, call 108 immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay the person flat** on their back.
- **Raise the legs** above the heart level using pillows/bags.
- Remove ants and wash the skin.
- **Keep the person warm** with a blanket.
- Do NOT give them anything to drink if they are dizzy.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove ants and lay person flat"] --> B["Raise legs above heart level using pillows"]
  B --> C["Check pulse and breathing carefully"]
  C --> D["Keep the person warm with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Call 108 for ambulance immediately"]
  E --> F["Do NOT give water if person is dizzy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness/Weakness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Only itchy bites | Treat at home | Cold water wash |
| Fainting | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Falling blood pressure (Shock) from an allergy is an extreme emergency.

query: A woman used a local 'Ubtan' (face pack). Her face is burning and turning bright red with swelling.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Swelling around the eyes
- **Difficulty opening mouth**
- Blisters on the face

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash face immediately, avoid sunlight. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the face** with cool water for 15 minutes.
- Apply **cold milk** or **curd (dahi)** to soothe the burn.
- Apply **Aloe Vera** gel.
- Stay indoors and away from the kitchen fire/smoke.
- Do not use any more creams or soaps.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash face with cool water for 15 minutes"] --> B["Apply cold milk or curd to soothe skin"]
  B --> C["Keep the person away from heat and fire"]
  C --> D["Check for eye swelling or blisters"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply any other skin creams"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if redness increases"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Redness and burning | If no relief in 2 hours | Cold milk/Curd |
| Blisters/Oozing | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Swelling of eyes | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Local pastes can have strong chemicals or irritants. Always wash thoroughly first.

query: A man's throat is itchy after drinking local forest honey. He is coughing and his voice is changing.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Change in voice (Hoarseness)**
- **Constant coughing**
- Swelling of the tongue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Refer to CHC immediately; voice change means throat is closing. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the honey** immediately.
- Keep the person **sitting upright**.
- Ask them to breathe slowly and deeply.
- Do NOT give them water if they are coughing a lot.
- Keep the honey sample for the doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the food source immediately"] --> B["Check for voice change or hoarseness"]
  B --> C["Keep the person sitting upright"] 
  C --> D["Observe breathing and tongue swelling"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give water if coughing persists"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Voice change | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Itchy throat only | Visit PHC | Monitor for 1 hour |
| Coughing/Wheezing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Honey allergy can cause rapid throat swelling. Voice change is a very serious warning.

query: A child is wearing a new cheap plastic sandal and now there are red itchy lines on his feet. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blisters that are popping**
- **Yellow fluid coming out** (infection)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Remove sandals, wash feet, apply oil. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove the sandals** and do not wear them again.
- Wash feet with **mild soap and water**.
- Apply **Coconut oil** or a cool cloth.
- Let the feet stay open (no socks or shoes).
- Do not let the child scratch the area.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove the new plastic sandals"] --> B["Wash feet with soap and water"] 
  B --> C["Apply coconut oil to the red lines"]
  C --> D["Keep feet open to air"] 
  D --> E["Monitor for blisters or yellow puss"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if itching is severe"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itchy red lines | No need | Oil and air |
| Blisters/Puss | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Fever | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Contact dermatitis from plastic is common. Removing the item is the best treatment.

query: A man got 'Ghamori' (heat rash) but it has turned into big red itchy patches after he applied some strong powder. He is in pain.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Intense burning pain**
- Skin turning dark or purple
- Fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash off the powder, keep skin dry and cool. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash off the powder** with plenty of cool water.
- Apply **Multani Mitti (Fuller's Earth)** mixed with water for cooling.
- Use a hand fan to keep the area dry.
- Wear loose cotton clothes.
- Do NOT use any more powders or oils.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash off all powder with cool water"] --> B["Apply Multani Mitti paste to the area"]
  B --> C["Use a fan to keep the skin cool"]
  C --> D["Wear loose, clean cotton clothing"]
  D --> E["Do NOT apply oil or heat to the area"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if pain is very high"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Large red patches | If no relief in 4 hours | Multani Mitti |
| Severe pain | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Fever | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Strong powders can burn skin that is already irritated by heat. Cool water is the first step.

query: A person ate mushrooms from the forest and is now feeling very dizzy, nauseous, and has hives.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting with blood**
- **Drowsiness or fainting**
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Emergency. Save a sample of the mushroom. Rush to CHC. |
| Child | Any | Immediate referral; high risk of liver damage. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop eating** immediately.
- Keep a sample of the mushroom or the cooked dish for the doctor.
- Keep the person lying on their side (to prevent choking if they vomit).
- Do NOT give them home remedies to make them vomit unless told by a doctor.
- Call 108 immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the food and save a sample"] --> B["Lay person on their side (Recovery Position)"] 
  B --> C["Check for breathing and consciousness"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any home remedies"] 
  D --> E["Call 108 or rush to CHC immediately"]
  E --> F["Inform doctor about the mushroom source"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dizziness/Nausea | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Hives after forest food | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Wild mushroom reactions can be both an allergy and a poisoning. Always treat as a life-threatening emergency.

query: A pregnant woman's hands are very itchy and red after using a new detergent for laundry.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Facial swelling**
- Rash spreading to the stomach
- Breathing trouble

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Wash hands, use coconut oil, consult PHC for safety. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash hands** with plain water for 10 minutes.
- Stop using the detergent immediately.
- Apply **Coconut oil** or **Aloe Vera**.
- Do not scratch as it can cause skin infections.
- Check if there is any rash elsewhere on the body.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash hands with plain water for 10 minutes"] --> B["Apply pure coconut oil to the red areas"] 
  B --> C["Check for rash on other body parts"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use the detergent again"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC to check baby's health status"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itchy hands only | Visit PHC | Coconut oil |
| Any facial swelling | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Rash on belly | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
During pregnancy, skin is more sensitive. Always consult a doctor before taking any allergy pills.

query: A man got bitten by a spider in the store room. The area is red, and he feels like he is going to throw up.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe stomach or back cramps**
- **Difficulty breathing**
- Hard, painful swelling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash bite, keep limb still, rush to PHC. |
| Child | Any | Emergency referral; small bodies react faster to toxins. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the bite** with soap and water.
- Apply a **cold cloth** to the area.
- Keep the bitten part **below the heart level**.
- Do NOT cut or suck the bite.
- Keep the person calm and lying down.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash bite area with soap and water"] --> B["Apply cold cloth for pain and swelling"]
  B --> C["Keep the bitten limb below heart level"]
  C --> D["Observe for vomiting or stomach pain"]
  D --> E["Do NOT cut or squeeze the bite"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC/CHC for monitoring"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting/Nausea | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Only local redness | Visit PHC | Cold compress |
| Breathing trouble | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Some spider bites in India can cause severe reactions. If the pain is spreading, refer immediately.

query: A boy ate a bright colored candy from the fair. Now his lips are very big and he is drooling.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Drooling or inability to swallow**
- **Noisy breathing (Stridor)**
- Tongue sticking out due to swelling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Emergency. Do NOT give water. Rush to CHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the child leaning forward** to let saliva drain out.
- Do NOT give anything to eat or drink.
- Try to see if the child can speak or make sounds.
- Loosen his shirt collar.
- **Call 108** or use the fastest transport to CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify lip swelling and drooling"] --> B["Keep the child leaning forward"] 
  B --> C["Do NOT give any water or food"]
  C --> D["Check if the child can still speak"]
  D --> E["Loosen tight clothing around neck"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC immediately via 108"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swollen lips | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Drooling | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Noisy breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Drooling means the throat is so swollen the person cannot even swallow spit. This is a critical emergency.

query: An ASHA worker asks: A woman got an injection for pain and now she is sweating and her eyes are puffy. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Puffy eyes and face**
- **Cold, sweaty skin**
- Rapid, weak pulse
- Feeling of 'doom' (scared/anxious)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Lay flat, legs up, call the doctor who gave the injection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay the person flat** on the ground.
- **Raise the legs** to keep blood flowing to the brain.
- Loosen all tight clothing.
- Do NOT give them any water.
- Tell the doctor immediately that they are having a reaction to the injection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Lay the person flat on their back"] --> B["Raise legs above heart level"] 
  B --> C["Check breathing and heart rate"]
  C --> D["Loosen tight clothes and give air"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give water if they are dizzy"]
  E --> F["Call 108 or doctor who gave the shot"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sweating/Dizziness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Puffy eyes | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Fainting | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Injection reactions are the fastest and most dangerous. Stay with the person until help arrives.

query: A person used a new type of incense stick (Agarbatti) and now is coughing and feels chest tightness.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Wheezing (whistling sound while breathing)**
- **Lips turning blue**
- Chest sucking in with every breath

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Remove incense, open windows, sit upright. |
| Elderly | Any | Higher risk of asthma attack. Refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Extinguish the incense stick** immediately.
- Move the person to **fresh air** (outside or by a window).
- Have the person sit in a **'Tripod' position** (sitting, leaning forward with hands on knees).
- Ask them to take slow, calm breaths.
- If they have a prescribed inhaler, help them use it.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Stop the incense and open windows"] --> B["Move person to fresh outdoor air"]
  B --> C["Sit the person leaning forward on knees"]
  C --> D["Encourage slow, deep breathing"]
  D --> E["Check for whistling sound in chest"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if coughing continues"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Chest tightness | Refer to PHC | Fresh air/Rest |
| Wheezing sound | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Blue lips | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Smoke allergies can trigger a severe asthma attack. If breathing does not improve in 10 minutes, refer.

query: A girl put on some imitation jewelry earrings and her earlobes are red, swollen, and leaking clear fluid.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever**
- **Swelling spreading to the neck**
- Yellow pus (infection)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Adult | Female | Remove earrings, clean with water, apply coconut oil. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Remove the jewelry** immediately.
- Wash the earlobe with **clean water and mild soap**.
- Apply **Coconut oil** or a cool cloth.
- Do NOT put the jewelry back in, even if the girl wants to.
- Avoid using 'Haldi' (turmeric) if it is an open wound as it might sting.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove the earrings immediately"] --> B["Wash earlobes with clean water and soap"]
  B --> C["Apply coconut oil to the swollen area"]
  C --> D["Keep the area clean and dry"]
  D --> E["Monitor for fever or yellow puss"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if infection develops"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red, swollen earlobes | No need | Oil and removal |
| Leaking yellow puss | Visit PHC | Never |
| Swelling moves to neck | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Metal allergies are common. Once a reaction happens, that person should avoid that type of metal forever.

Query: A child was playing in the grass and something bit his leg. Now there is a big red lump, it is very hot to touch, and he is crying. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat**
- **Whistling sound while breathing (wheezing)**
- Dizziness or fainting
- **Rapid heartbeat or cold, sweaty skin**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Clean with soap, apply cold pack, monitor breathing for 30 mins. |
| Adult | Any | Wash area, apply local soothing paste, keep limb raised. |
| Elderly | Any | Monitor for heart distress; refer to PHC if swelling is very large. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area** thoroughly with soap and clean water.
- Apply **Ice or a cold metal spoon** to the swelling for 10 minutes.
- Apply a paste of **Baking Soda and water** or **Tulsi leaf juice** to reduce itching.
- **Do NOT** scratch or squeeze the bite site.
- Keep the affected part **raised** (above the level of the heart).
- If the sting is visible, scrape it off with a flat card; do not use tweezers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the bite site with soap and clean water"] --> B["Check for breathing trouble or throat swelling"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress or ice for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply Tulsi juice or Baking Soda paste to the lump"]
  D --> E["Keep the swollen limb raised high"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if swelling spreads beyond the joint"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling on face/neck | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Breathing difficulty | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Local redness/itching | Refer if no relief in 24 hrs | Use cold pack and Tulsi |
| Fever or pus | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Most insect bites are minor, but any swelling of the face or trouble breathing is a life-threatening emergency. Seek help immediately.

Query: Didi, a farmer got bitten by many red ants while working. His hands are covered in small white bumps and they are burning like fire. Suggest some home remedy.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Difficulty swallowing or hoarse voice**
- **Widespread body rash (hives)**
- Vomiting or stomach cramps

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Wash with cold water, apply Coconut oil or Neem paste. |
| Pregnant | Female | Monitor closely for any fever or systemic reaction; refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cool the skin** by pouring cold water over the hands for 5-10 minutes.
- Apply **Coconut oil** mixed with a pinch of Camphor (Kapoor) for cooling.
- Alternatively, apply **Aloe Vera gel** or fresh **Cucumber juice**.
- Do NOT break the small white blisters; this causes infection.
- Encourage the person to sit in a cool, shaded area.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash hands with cold water for 10 minutes"] --> B["Apply Coconut oil or Aloe Vera gel to burning skin"]
  B --> C["Check for any rash appearing on the stomach or chest"]
  C --> D["Do NOT pop or squeeze the white blisters"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing and pulse for 1 hour"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if burning sensation does not stop"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple ant bites | If feeling dizzy/weak | Cold wash and oil |
| Small white blisters | Only if they turn into pus | Keep clean and dry |
| Body-wide itching | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Multiple bites can sometimes cause a severe reaction even in healthy adults. Watch for dizziness.

Query: Something bit a woman's eye at night while she was sleeping. The eye is swollen shut and very red. She cannot see through that eye. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling moving toward the neck**
- **Severe headache or confusion**
- Pus or blood leaking from the eye

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Refer to PHC immediately; eye swelling is high risk. |
| Elderly | Any | Emergency referral; check for secondary infection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT rub the eye** or press on the eyelid.
- Gently splash the closed eye with **cool, clean drinking water**.
- Apply a **cold, damp clean cloth** over the eye (do not apply pressure).
- Do NOT put any 'Surma', 'Kajal', or local eye drops.
- Keep the head elevated using two pillows while lying down.
- Rush to the nearest PHC to check if the eye globe is safe.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Gently splash closed eye with clean water"] --> B["Check for vision loss or severe pain"]
  B --> C["Place a cool damp cloth over the eye without pressure"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any local pastes or eye drops"]
  D --> E["Keep head raised with pillows while resting"]
  E --> F["Transfer to PHC for professional eye exam"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Eye swollen shut | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Vision changes | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Mild redness on lid | Refer if not better in 6 hrs | Cold compress |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites near the eye can lead to serious infections that can spread to the brain. Always get a medical checkup for eye bites.

Query: A child was bitten by a 'Tatiri' (Wasp) and the arm is swelling up to the elbow. The skin is very tight. Should I tie a cloth around it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blue or cold fingers**
- **Loss of feeling in the hand**
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Do NOT tie; keep arm raised, apply cold, rush to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT tie any cloth (tourniquet)**; this can stop blood flow and damage the arm.
- **Remove rings or bangles** immediately before the swelling gets worse.
- Apply **cold mud (Multani Mitti)** or ice to the sting site.
- Keep the arm **raised above the heart level** to drain the fluid.
- Use a paste of **vinegar and water** if available for wasp stings.
- Monitor the child for any signs of vomiting or sleepiness.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove rings, bangles, or tight sleeves"] --> B["Apply cold mud or ice pack to the arm"]
  B --> C["Keep the arm raised above heart level"]
  C --> D["Do NOT tie any tight bands around the arm"]
  D --> E["Check for warmth and movement in fingers"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if swelling crosses the elbow"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swelling past joint | Refer to PHC/CHC | Never |
| Cold/Numb fingers | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Local sting pain | Treat at home | Cold mud/Tulsi |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Large local reactions can be painful and scary. Elevating the limb is the most important step to stop the swelling from spreading.

Query: An old man got bitten by a 'Kankhajura' (Centipede). He says the pain is unbearable and his foot is turning black. What is the remedy?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin turning black or dark purple**
- Fever and chills
- **Foul smell from the bite site**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk of infection/gangrene. Refer to CHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the site** with hot water (as hot as the person can comfortably bear) — this helps break down centipede venom.
- Apply a **warm compress**, not a cold one, for centipede bites.
- Apply **Onion juice** or a slice of raw onion to the bite for pain relief.
- Keep the foot clean and do not apply cow dung or unsterilized pastes.
- Monitor for fever; if the person starts shivering, it is a serious infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash bite with warm soapy water"] --> B["Apply warm compress to the site for pain"] 
  B --> C["Apply raw onion slice to the bite area"]
  C --> D["Check for skin color changes or foul smell"]
  D --> E["Monitor for fever or shivering"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for antibiotics and wound care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unbearable pain | Refer to PHC for painkiller | Warm soak |
| Black skin/Blisters | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Fever/Chills | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Centipede bites are very painful but rarely fatal; however, in the elderly, they can lead to deep skin infections.

Query: A toddler was bitten by a spider in the store room. There is a small blister and a red ring around it. What should I do at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Red lines spreading away from the bite**
- Stomach pain or muscle cramps
- **Drowsiness or refusing to eat**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<2) | Any | Emergency referral; do not treat at home. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area gently** with soap and water.
- Apply a **cool cloth** (not ice directly) to the site.
- Keep the child **still and calm**.
- Do NOT pop the blister.
- **Draw a circle** around the redness with a pen to see if it grows.
- Refer to the PHC immediately; spider bites in small children can cause blood issues.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the bite area gently with soap"] --> B["Mark the edges of redness with a pen"]
  B --> C["Apply a cool damp cloth to the blister"]
  C --> D["Keep the child calm and monitor energy levels"]
  D --> E["Do NOT pop the blister or apply heat"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for observation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red ring/Blister | Refer to PHC | Keep clean/Cool |
| Muscle cramps | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Spreading redness | Emergency - PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Spider bites can take hours to show full effect. Marking the skin with a pen is vital to track the spread.

Query: Didi, my hand is swelling because of a honeybee sting. I already took out the 'kaanta' (stinger). Can I apply lime (Chuna)?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of the lips or tongue**
- **Difficulty breathing or wheezing**
- Body-wide itching

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Apply soothing paste, keep hand raised, monitor for 1 hour. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use Chuna (Lime)**; it is too strong and can burn the skin further.
- Apply **Honey** or a paste of **Tulsi leaves** to the site.
- Alternatively, apply **Mustard Oil** mixed with a bit of salt.
- Keep the hand **elevated** above the level of your chest.
- Drink plenty of water.
- If you feel itchy all over your body, go to the PHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check if the stinger is completely removed"] --> B["Apply Honey or Tulsi paste to the sting"]
  B --> C["Keep the hand raised above heart level"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply Chuna or strong chemicals"]
  D --> E["Monitor for body-wide hives or itching"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if breathing feels heavy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Localized swelling | Treat at home | Honey/Cold pack |
| Whole hand swelling | Visit PHC | Raise hand/Cold |
| Throat tightness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Honeybee stings are acidic; while mild alkaline bases help, strong 'Chuna' causes chemical burns. Use mild remedies.

Query: A girl brushed against a hairy caterpillar (Kambal Keeda). Her skin is red, very itchy, and swelling. How to remove the hairs?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling on the face or eyes**
- Fever
- Rash spreading to the whole body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | Use tape to remove hairs, wash with soap, apply oil. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT rub the area**; this pushes the tiny hairs deeper.
- Use **sticky tape** (like cello-tape) or a **Wheat flour dough (Atta)** ball to roll over the skin and pick up the hairs.
- Wash the area with **soap and running water** after removing hairs.
- Apply **Neem oil** or **Coconut oil** to soothe the itch.
- Apply **Lassi or cold Curd** to stop the burning sensation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Do NOT rub the skin with hands"] --> B["Use tape or Atta dough to pull out tiny hairs"]
  B --> C["Wash area with soap and running water"]
  C --> D["Apply Coconut oil or cold Curd to soothe"] 
  D --> E["Monitor for spreading rash or fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if itching is unbearable"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itching and redness | Home care | Atta/Tape/Oil |
| Hairs in the eye | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Fever/Large swelling | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Caterpillar hairs act like tiny poison needles. Rubbing is the worst thing you can do.

Query: An old woman has a bite that has become a big 'Phoda' (abscess). It is leaking yellow water and her whole leg is swollen. She has a fever.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever with shivering (chills)**
- **Red streaks moving up the leg**
- Person is very sleepy or confused

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Female | Emergency referral; risk of blood infection (Sepsis). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the leg raised** and still.
- Gently wash the area with **boiled and cooled water**.
- Do NOT squeeze the pus out; this pushes the infection into the blood.
- Cover with a **clean, dry cotton cloth**.
- Give plain water or ORS to drink.
- Transfer to a CHC immediately for antibiotic treatment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the abscess gently with boiled cooled water"] --> B["Check for fever or red streaks on skin"]
  B --> C["Cover with a clean dry cotton cloth"] 
  C --> D["Do NOT squeeze or press the swelling"]
  D --> E["Keep the leg raised on pillows"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Swelling | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Yellow pus/Oozing | Refer to PHC/CHC | Never |
| Small red bump | Refer if it grows | Clean with water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infections in the elderly can spread to the whole body very quickly. Fever is a sign of internal danger.

Query: Something bit a man on his neck. Now he is coughing and says his voice feels 'bhari' (heavy). He is sweating a lot.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Change in voice or hoarseness**
- **Difficulty breathing or gasping**
- Swelling of the neck or face
- **Excessive sweating and panic**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Emergency. Call 108. Sit upright, do not give water. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit the person upright** to keep the airway as open as possible.
- **Loosen the shirt collar** and any jewelry around the neck.
- Encourage the person to stay calm and take slow breaths.
- **Do NOT give anything by mouth** (no water, food, or medicine).
- If the person faints, lay them on their side (recovery position).
- Rush to the nearest hospital with emergency facilities.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify bite on neck and voice change"] --> B["Sit the person upright and loosen collar"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food or water by mouth"]
  C --> D["Check for gasping or blue lips"]
  D --> E["Lay on side if the person loses consciousness"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Voice change | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Neck swelling | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Mild itch on neck | Visit PHC | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites on the neck can cause internal swelling that blocks the windpipe. Every minute counts.

Query: A boy has many mosquito bites and he is scratching them until they bleed. Now the bites are red and swollen. How to stop the itch?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever**
- **Yellow crusting on the bites**
- Swelling of the nearby glands (neck or armpit)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Trim nails, apply Neem paste, keep skin clean. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Trim the child's nails** to prevent further skin damage from scratching.
- Apply a paste of **Neem leaves** (ground with water) to the bites.
- Apply **Coconut oil** to keep the skin from becoming dry and itchy.
- Use a **cool water soak** to reduce the heat in the skin.
- Dress the child in long-sleeved cotton clothes to protect the skin.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Trim nails to stop damage from scratching"] --> B["Wash bites with mild soap and water"]
  B --> C["Apply Neem paste or Coconut oil to skin"]
  C --> D["Use cool water soak for 10 minutes"]
  D --> E["Check for yellow crusts or fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if infection develops"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itching only | Home care | Neem/Coconut oil |
| Bleeding bites | Keep clean/Refer if pussy | Clean water wash |
| Fever/Gland swelling | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Excessive scratching leads to skin infections (impetigo). Keep the child's hands clean.

Query: A woman was bitten by a 'Bhid' (Hornet). The sting site is very hard and the size of a lemon. She feels nauseous. What home care can I give?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting or severe stomach pain**
- **Feeling of a lump in the throat**
- Dizziness or blurred vision

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Apply cold, give sips of Ginger water, monitor for 2 hrs. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply **Ice or cold river water** to the hard swelling immediately.
- Apply a paste of **Sandalwood (Chandan)** or **Multani Mitti** to cool the area.
- If she feels nauseous, give a small piece of **Ginger** to chew or Ginger tea.
- Keep her lying down in a cool, well-ventilated room.
- If she vomits even once, take her to the PHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Apply ice or cold pack to the hard swelling"] --> B["Give a piece of Ginger for nausea"]
  B --> C["Apply Chandan paste to the sting site"]
  C --> D["Check for vomiting or throat tightness"]
  D --> E["Keep the person lying down and cool"]
  F --> F["Refer to PHC if nausea worsens or vomiting starts"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hard swelling | Home care | Cold compress |
| Nausea/Vomiting | Refer to PHC | Ginger (initial only) |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Hornet stings are more powerful than bee stings. Nausea can be an early sign of a serious whole-body reaction.

Query: An ASHA worker asks: A newborn has a red bite mark on the arm. The area is slightly swollen. Can I use medicine?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever in the newborn**
- **Poor feeding or lethargy (too sleepy)**
- Fast breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Do NOT use adult creams. Wash with plain water; refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area gently** with plain lukewarm water (no soap).
- Do NOT apply any local herbs, 'Surma', or adult tubes/ointments.
- You may apply a single drop of **pure Coconut oil** if the skin is dry.
- Monitor the baby's feeding; if the baby refuses milk, it is an emergency.
- Keep the baby in soft, clean cotton clothes.
- Take the baby to the PHC for a pediatric checkup.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the bite area with plain lukewarm water"] --> B["Check baby's temperature and feeding"] 
  B --> C["Apply one drop of Coconut oil if skin is dry"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use any herbal pastes or adult tubes"]
  D --> E["Observe for any fast breathing or rash"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for pediatric evaluation"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any bite on newborn | Refer to PHC | Clean with water |
| Fever/Poor feeding | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Fast breathing | Emergency - PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Newborns are very sensitive. What is safe for an adult can be poisonous for a baby's skin. Always refer.

Query: A man's leg has a bite that is now hot, shiny red, and he cannot walk. It looks like it is filled with fluid. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Redness spreading very fast (within hours)**
- **High fever and headache**
- Skin feels like it is 'crackling' when pressed

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Keep leg raised, apply cold, refer to PHC/CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the leg completely still** and raised high on pillows.
- Apply a **cool, damp cloth** over the red area (do not use ice directly on shiny skin).
- Draw a line with a pen at the edge of the redness and note the time.
- Do NOT massage or apply any oil or grease.
- Take the person to a doctor; they likely need antibiotics for 'Cellulitis'.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Keep the leg raised high on pillows"] --> B["Mark the edge of redness with a pen and note time"]
  B --> C["Apply a cool damp cloth over the shiny skin"]
  C --> D["Check for high fever or spreading redness"]
  D --> E["Do NOT massage or apply oil/grease"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC/CHC for antibiotic treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shiny/Hot/Red skin | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Spreading redness | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| High fever | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shiny, hot skin after a bite is a sign of a deep skin infection (Cellulitis). It cannot be treated with home remedies alone.

Query: A girl was bitten by a beetle and now has a long red mark like a 'trail' on her arm. It burns a lot. What is the remedy?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Blisters on the face or eyes**
- Swelling of the nearby joints

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | Wash with soap/water, apply Aloe Vera or cold Lassi. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the 'trail'** immediately with plenty of soap and water to remove the beetle's juice (acid).
- Do NOT rub the area, as it spreads the acid further.
- Apply **Aloe Vera gel** or fresh **Curd (Dahi)** for cooling.
- A paste of **Baking Soda and water** helps neutralize the acid.
- If the mark is near the eye, refer to the PHC immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the 'trail' with soap and water immediately"] --> B["Do NOT rub or scratch the burning area"]
  B --> C["Apply Aloe Vera or fresh Curd for cooling"]
  C --> D["Apply Baking Soda paste to neutralize acid"]
  D --> E["Check for blisters or spreading redness"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if the mark is on the face"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Red trail on limb | Home care | Soap/Aloe/Curd |
| Blisters on face | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Eye involvement | Emergency - PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
This is often caused by 'Blister Beetles'. The 'trail' is a chemical burn. Washing the acid off is the first priority.

Query: Something bit a man while he was cleaning the grain store. Now his whole face is puffy and he is feeling very sleepy. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme sleepiness or confusion**
- **Swelling of the lips or eyes**
- Difficulty breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Emergency. Call 108. Keep airway clear, rush to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the person awake** by talking to them constantly.
- Sit them in an **upright position**.
- Loosen any tight clothing around the neck and chest.
- Do NOT give them water if they are feeling very sleepy (they might choke).
- Check for any bite marks on the body and show them to the doctor.
- Call 108 immediately; sleepiness after a bite can be a sign of a severe reaction or toxin.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check consciousness and keep person awake"] --> B["Sit the person in an upright position"]
  B --> C["Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give any water or food"]
  D --> E["Check for bite marks on the body"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Puffy face | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Sleepiness/Confusion | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Breathing difficulty | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Confusion or extreme sleepiness after a bite is a brain-level danger sign. Do not wait for symptoms to 'go away' at home.

Query: A boy's ear is swollen and red because an insect bit it. The ear is sticking out and he has a headache. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dizziness or loss of balance**
- **Fever**
- Discharge (fluid) coming from inside the ear

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Male | Apply cold pack, do not put anything IN the ear, visit PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply a **cold compress** to the outer part of the ear for 10 minutes.
- **Do NOT put any oil, water, or medicine INSIDE the ear canal.**
- Keep the child sitting up; lying down may increase the pain.
- Give a small piece of **jaggery (Gur)** to distract the child if they are in pain.
- Take the child to the PHC to ensure the insect or stinger isn't stuck inside.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Apply cold pack to the outer swollen ear"] --> B["Do NOT put any liquid inside the ear canal"]
  B --> C["Keep the child in a sitting position"]
  C --> D["Check for fever or balance issues"]
  D --> E["Do NOT let the child poke their ear"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for professional checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swollen outer ear | Visit PHC | Cold pack |
| Pain inside the ear | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Dizziness/Balance loss | Emergency - PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites on the ear can affect hearing or balance. Never drop anything into the ear canal without a doctor's advice.

Query: A woman has many bites on her legs from 'Pissu' (Fleas/Ticks). They are very itchy and purple. She is worried it is poison. What care?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever or joint pain**
- **Rash that looks like a 'target' or 'bullseye'**
- Extreme weakness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash with Neem water, apply Coconut oil, monitor for fever. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Wash the legs with **warm Neem water** (boiled Neem leaves in water).
- If you see a tick still attached, pull it straight out with a thread or tweezers; do not twist it.
- Apply **Coconut oil** or a paste of **Tulsi and Neem**.
- Wash all bedding and clothes in hot water to kill any 'Pissu'.
- If she develops a fever in the next few days, she must see a doctor immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash legs with warm Neem water"] --> B["Remove any visible ticks by pulling straight out"] 
  B --> C["Apply Coconut oil or Tulsi-Neem paste to bites"]
  C --> D["Wash bedding and clothes in hot water"]
  D --> E["Monitor for fever or joint pain over 1 week"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if fever develops"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Itchy purple bites | Home care | Neem wash/Oil |
| Fever/Joint pain | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Bullseye-shaped rash | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Tick and flea bites can carry diseases like 'Scrub Typhus'. Fever following these bites is a serious warning sign.

Query: A man's hand is swollen like a 'Dhol' (Drum) after a bite. The skin is very itchy and he has a cold. Is it related?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sneezing, runny nose, and itchy eyes** (signs of allergy)
- **Tightness in the chest**
- Swelling of the lips

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Identify allergy, apply cold pack, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Apply an Ice pack** or cold water to the hand to reduce the drum-like swelling.
- Keep the hand **raised high** on pillows.
- Remove any rings or watches immediately.
- If he is sneezing or has a runny nose, it means the whole body is reacting to the bite.
- Take him to the PHC for an anti-allergy injection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Remove rings or watches from the swollen hand"] --> B["Apply ice pack or cold water to the swelling"]
  B --> C["Keep the hand raised above heart level"]
  C --> D["Check for sneezing or runny nose (allergy signs)"]
  D --> E["Monitor for chest tightness or lip swelling"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for anti-allergy treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drum-like swelling | Refer to PHC | Raise hand/Cold |
| Sneezing/Runny nose | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Chest tightness | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
When an insect bite causes 'cold' symptoms (sneezing/runny nose), it is a sign of a systemic allergy that needs medical treatment.

Query: Something bit a toddler on the tongue while he was eating a fruit. The tongue is getting big and he is crying. Help!
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Drooling (spit falling out of mouth)**
- **Inability to speak or make sounds**
- **Blue color of the face or lips**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Toddler (<3) | Any | Emergency. Give an ice cube to suck, rush to CHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Give the child an ice cube** or very cold water to suck on (if they are old enough to not choke on the ice).
- **Keep the child leaning forward** to let saliva drain out.
- Do NOT give any food or solid items.
- Try to keep the child as calm as possible (crying increases swelling).
- **Rush to the nearest CHC immediately.** Bites inside the mouth are life-threatening as they can block the windpipe within minutes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify bite on tongue and give ice cube to suck"] --> B["Keep the child leaning forward to drain saliva"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food or medicine by mouth"]
  C --> D["Check for blue lips or gasping for air"]
  D --> E["Keep the child calm and still while moving"]
  F --> F["Rush to CHC or call 108 immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any bite inside mouth | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Drooling/Noisy breathing | Emergency - Call 108 | Never |
| Tongue swelling | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A bite on the tongue can lead to total airway blockage in minutes. This is a top-tier emergency.

Query: A young boy has a very painful, red, and hard 'Baal-tod' (boil) on his thigh. It is swelling and he cannot walk properly. What can I do at home?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever or shivering**
- **Red streaks** spreading away from the boil
- Multiple boils appearing in a cluster (Carbuncle)
- Boil appearing on the **face, spine, or near the eye**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Apply warm compress, keep the area clean, do not allow scratching. |
| Adult | Any | Use warm soak, apply Haldi paste, monitor for spreading redness. |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of infection spread; check for sugar (diabetes). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Apply a warm compress** (clean cloth soaked in warm water) for 10-15 minutes, 3 times a day.
- Apply a paste of **Haldi (Turmeric)** and a drop of mustard oil to the boil.
- **Do NOT squeeze**, poke with a needle, or try to pop the boil.
- Keep the area covered with a clean, loose bandage or cloth.
- Wash hands thoroughly with soap before and after touching the boil.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the boil and surrounding skin with soap"] --> B["Apply warm wet cloth for 15 minutes to bring pus to head"]
  B --> C["Apply Haldi paste and cover with clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT squeeze or pop the boil manually"]
  D --> E["Monitor for fever or red lines on skin"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if pain increases or fever starts"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Single small boil | If not draining in 7 days | Warm compress + Haldi |
| Fever or Chills | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Boil on the face | Refer to PHC immediately | Never |
| Multiple boils joined | Refer to CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Popping a boil can push the infection deeper into the blood. If the person has diabetes, even a small boil needs medical attention.

Query: A woman has a boil under her armpit. It is very soft now and leaking yellow pus. How should I clean it safely?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Foul smell** coming from the pus
- Large swelling in the neck or armpit glands
- Extreme weakness or dizziness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Clean with boiled water, cover with bandage, avoid sharing towels. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wash the area** gently using boiled and cooled water with a little salt.
- Let the pus drain naturally; do not press hard.
- Apply **Neem water** (water boiled with Neem leaves) to disinfect the skin.
- Use a **sterile gauze** or a fresh ironed cotton cloth to cover the opening.
- Burn or deeply bury the used cotton/cloth to prevent spreading infection to others.
- Advise the patient **not to share** towels, soap, or clothes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Clean the draining boil with boiled cooled salt water"] --> B["Use clean cotton to wipe away pus gently"]
  B --> C["Apply Neem water to the surrounding skin"]
  C --> D["Cover with a clean dry bandage"] 
  D --> E["Dispose of used cleaning materials safely"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if drainage does not stop in 2 days"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pus draining | If fever starts | Clean and cover |
| Deep hole (ulcer) | Refer to PHC for dressing | Never |
| Severe pain | Refer to PHC for painkillers | Warm compress |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Pus from a boil is highly contagious. Strict handwashing is required to protect the rest of the family.

Query: An old man with sugar (diabetes) has a big blackish boil on his back. He says it doesn't hurt much but it looks bad. What is the first aid?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Black or purple skin** around the boil
- Numbness in the area
- **High sugar levels** or confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Refer to CHC immediately; diabetic boils are dangerous. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT touch** or apply any home pastes to the boil.
- Cover it gently with a **dry, sterile cloth**.
- Check his temperature for fever.
- Keep the person hydrated with plain water.
- **Refer to the nearest CHC** immediately, as diabetic patients can get gangrene from boils.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify boil in a patient with diabetes"] --> B["Check for fever and skin color changes"]
  B --> C["Cover the boil with a dry clean cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any herbal pastes or oil"]
  D --> E["Do NOT attempt to pop or squeeze"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC immediately for professional care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any boil in Diabetic | Emergency referral | Never |
| Black/Dark skin | Emergency referral | Never |
| No pain but large size | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In diabetic patients, a boil can be a sign of a very deep infection that can lead to limb loss or death if not treated with hospital antibiotics.

Query: A child has multiple small boils on the head after the rains. It is very itchy. What should the mother do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow crusty fluid** leaking (Impetigo)
- Swollen glands behind the ears or neck
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Wash head with Neem water, trim nails, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Trim the child's fingernails** very short to prevent skin damage from scratching.
- Bathe the head with **Neem water** (leaves boiled and cooled) twice a day.
- Apply **Coconut oil** mixed with a little bit of crushed Neem leaves.
- Keep the child's hair short or tied back and away from the boils.
- Change the pillowcase and bedsheet daily.
- Refer to the PHC for antibiotic ointment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the head with boiled and cooled Neem water"] --> B["Trim nails short to stop scratching damage"]
  B --> C["Apply Coconut oil to reduce itching"]
  C --> D["Check for swollen nodes in the neck"]
  D --> E["Wash pillowcases in hot water daily"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for skin medication"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple small boils | Refer to PHC | Neem wash/Trim nails |
| Yellow crusting | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Swollen neck nodes | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Boils on the head can lead to deeper scalp infections. Do not use strong soaps or chemicals on a child's scalp.

Query: A man has a boil that is very hard and won't open. He wants to use a hot 'atta' (flour) poultice. Is it safe?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Boil feels like it is 'throbbing' with the heartbeat**
- Skin around the boil is very hot and tight
- Pain is so bad he cannot sleep

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Warm compress is safer; do not use very hot flour. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Use a **warm compress** (not scalding hot) to soften the boil.
- You can use a **warm betel leaf (Paandher)** with a little oil to help the boil 'ripen'.
- Do NOT use very hot flour as it can cause a second-degree burn on top of the boil.
- If the boil remains hard and purple for more than 3 days, it needs to be opened by a doctor.
- Give plain paracetamol if the pain is high (as per PHC guidelines).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check if the boil is hard or soft"] --> B["Apply warm compress or warm betel leaf"] 
  B --> C["Monitor for the appearance of a 'yellow head'"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use extremely hot pastes to avoid burns"]
  D --> E["Watch for signs of fever or throbbing pain"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if it doesn't open in 3 days"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hard boil >3 days | Refer to PHC | Warm compress |
| Throbbing pain | Refer to PHC | Never |
| Burn from poultice | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Never use a needle to prick a hard boil. This can lead to a serious condition called cellulitis.

Query: A baby has a small boil on the buttock. It is red and the baby is crying during diaper/cloth changes. What is the natural cure?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Boil is spreading to the private parts**
- Baby stops feeding or has a weak cry
- Persistent fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant | Any | Keep area dry, use only plain water, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the area as dry as possible**; leave the baby without a nappy/diaper for some time.
- Clean only with **lukewarm plain water** and a soft cotton ball.
- Apply a very thin layer of **Coconut oil**.
- Do NOT apply any strong herbal pastes or 'Surma' to a baby's skin.
- Ensure the baby is wearing loose, clean cotton clothes.
- Take the baby to the PHC to ensure it is not a deeper infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the area gently with lukewarm water"] --> B["Keep the area open to air and dry"]
  B --> C["Apply a thin layer of Coconut oil"] 
  C --> D["Do NOT use thick pastes or tight diapers"]
  D --> E["Check for fever or poor feeding"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for pediatric check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any boil on an infant | Refer to PHC | Keep dry and clean |
| Fever/Poor feeding | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Spreading redness | Emergency - PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infant skin is very thin. Infections can reach the blood very quickly. Always refer infants with boils.

Query: A man has a cluster of boils on the back of his neck. It looks like one big honeycomb. He is very sick. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cluster of boils (Carbuncle)**
- **Stiff neck** or difficulty moving the head
- High fever and body ache

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT touch. Refer to CHC/District Hospital immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT try to pop** any part of the cluster.
- Apply a **clean, dry bandage** to prevent further contamination.
- Give the person plenty of fluids to drink.
- Keep the person in a resting position.
- This is a 'Carbuncle' and can cause a blood infection. It **must be treated by a doctor** with strong antibiotics and possibly surgery.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify honeycomb-like cluster of boils"] --> B["Check for high fever and neck stiffness"]
  B --> C["Cover with a large clean dry cloth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any home remedies or pastes"]
  D --> E["Do NOT attempt to squeeze any part"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC or District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cluster of boils | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Neck stiffness | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Carbuncles are deep-seated infections. Trying home remedies on them can lead to life-threatening sepsis.

Query: A girl has a small boil on her upper eyelid. Her eye is red. What should I tell the mother?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vision becomes blurry**
- The whole eye area is swollen and purple
- **Inability to move the eye**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Adult | Female | Warm compress (closed eye), refer to PHC/Eye doctor. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Use a **warm, clean damp cloth** to press gently on the *closed* eyelid for 5 minutes.
- Do NOT put anything *inside* the eye (no honey, no kajal, no drops).
- Do NOT try to pop the boil; the veins from the eye lead directly to the brain.
- Wash hands frequently to avoid spreading it to the other eye.
- Refer to the PHC for antibiotic eye ointment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Gently apply warm compress to the closed eye"] --> B["Do NOT put any drops or pastes inside the eye"]
  B --> C["Check for vision changes or bulging eye"]
  C --> D["Wash hands after touching the face"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC or Eye specialist immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Boil on eyelid | Refer to PHC | Warm compress only |
| Blurry vision | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Whole eye swollen | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infections near the eye are dangerous because they can spread to the brain (Meningitis). Never squeeze an eyelid boil.

Query: A farmer has a boil on his leg that keeps coming back every month. He is otherwise healthy. How to stop this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Boil not healing after 2 weeks
- Weight loss or constant thirst

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Improve hygiene, use Neem soap, refer for sugar test. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Advise the person to **bathe daily** using Neem soap or water boiled with Neem.
- Clean all towels, bedding, and clothes in **boiling water** and dry them in direct sunlight.
- Apply **Coconut oil with a pinch of Camphor (Kapoor)** to the area to prevent new infections.
- Tell him to stop using shared razors or towels.
- **Refer to the PHC** for a blood sugar test, as recurring boils are a common sign of diabetes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Advise daily bath with Neem soap or water"] --> B["Wash all clothes and towels in boiling water"] 
  B --> C["Disinfect common items like razors/soaps"]
  C --> D["Check for thirst or high frequency of urine"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for blood sugar (Diabetes) test"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Recurring boils | Refer for Blood Test | Hygiene + Neem bath |
| Not healing >2 weeks| Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cleanliness is the first step, but recurring boils usually mean an internal issue like low immunity or high blood sugar.

Query: A girl has a boil on her chin. It is very painful and her jaw is also swelling. What home remedy is best?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Difficulty opening the mouth**
- Difficulty swallowing
- Swelling moving toward the neck or throat

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Refer to PHC; face boils can be serious. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Apply a **warm compress** to the chin area for 10 minutes.
- Apply a paste of **Sandalwood (Chandan)** to soothe the heat.
- Do NOT press or squeeze the boil, as it can push infection into the jawbone.
- Keep the head elevated while sleeping.
- Refer to the PHC/Dental clinic to ensure the boil is not related to a tooth infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Apply warm compress to the chin boil"] --> B["Check if the person can open their mouth fully"]
  B --> C["Apply Chandan paste to the swollen area"] 
  C --> D["Do NOT squeeze or press the chin/jaw"]
  D --> E["Refer to PHC for dental and skin check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Face/Chin boil | Refer to PHC | Warm compress/Chandan |
| Jaw swelling | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Mouth opening difficulty | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Face boils are in the 'Danger Triangle.' Infection here can reach the brain easily. Always prefer a doctor's visit for face boils.

Query: Didi, a man has a boil on his palm. He cannot close his hand and it is very red. Should we use an onion poultice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unable to move fingers**
- Numbness in the fingers
- Swelling at the wrist

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Refer to PHC; palm infections spread quickly to tendons. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use a raw onion poultice** if the skin is broken (it can irritate).
- Apply a **warm salt-water soak** (Sendha Namak) for 15 minutes.
- Keep the hand **elevated** (raised above the heart) using a sling or pillows.
- Apply a clean bandage to keep the palm protected.
- Refer to a PHC/CHC immediately; hand boils can cause permanent damage to finger movement if not treated by a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Soak hand in warm salt water for 15 minutes"] --> B["Check for finger movement and wrist swelling"]
  B --> C["Keep hand raised above the heart level"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply irritating raw pastes if open"]
  D --> E["Refer to CHC for tendon safety check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Palm boil | Refer to PHC | Warm salt soak |
| Stiff fingers | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Wrist swelling | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The palm has many important nerves and tendons. A boil here is always a high-risk situation.

Query: A child has a boil on the knee. Every time he plays, it bleeds. Should I put 'mitti' (mud) on it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow pus with blood**
- **Stiffness in the knee joint**
- Child is limping or cannot stand

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Clean with water, cover with bandage, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NEVER put mud or cow dung** on a boil; this causes Tetanus and severe blood poisoning.
- Wash the knee with **soap and clean water**.
- Apply a paste of **Haldi (Turmeric)** which is a natural antiseptic.
- Cover with a **clean cotton bandage** to stop dirt from entering while the child plays.
- Tell the child to avoid running until the boil heals.
- Ensure the child has had their Tetanus injection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Wash the knee boil with soap and clean water"] --> B["Apply Haldi paste to the wound area"]
  B --> C["Cover with a clean dry cotton bandage"]
  C --> D["Strictly forbid applying mud or dung"]
  D --> E["Check for limping or joint stiffness"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Tetanus check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding boil | PHC for dressing | Clean + Haldi |
| Limping/Joint pain | Refer to PHC/CHC | Never |
| Mud applied previously | Emergency - PHC (Tetanus) | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Mud is full of bacteria. A bleeding boil is an open door for infection to enter the bone.

Query: A woman has a boil on her breast. It is very painful and she is breastfeeding. Is it safe to feed the baby?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Whole breast is hard and hot**
- **High fever and body aches**
- Pus coming out from the nipple

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Refer to PHC/CHC immediately; check for Mastitis. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop breastfeeding** from the affected side if there is pus near the nipple (consult PHC first).
- Apply a **warm, clean compress** to the area to relieve pain.
- Wear a loose cotton bra or cloth; avoid tight clothing.
- Keep the area very clean using boiled cooled water.
- **Refer to a doctor** immediately; this could be 'Mastitis' or a breast abscess which needs specific antibiotics safe for nursing mothers.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Apply warm compress to the painful area"] --> B["Check for fever and pus near the nipple"] 
  B --> C["Wear loose cotton clothes and keep clean"]
  C --> D["Do NOT squeeze the breast area manually"]
  D --> E["Refer to CHC for nursing-safe antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Breast boil | Refer to CHC/PHC | Warm compress |
| High Fever | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Pus from nipple | Emergency - CHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Breast infections can become very serious very fast. Early medical help is vital to protect both mother and baby.

Query: Something bit a man and now it has become a boil with a black center. It is not very painful but the area is swollen. What is it?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Black, painless center (Eschar)**
- **Swelling of glands in the neck or groin**
- Headache and fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Male | Do NOT touch. Refer to CHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT try to remove the black part.**
- Cover the area with a **clean, dry cloth**.
- Keep the person rested and separate their clothes/bedding from others.
- **Refer to a CHC immediately**; a black, painless boil after an insect bite/animal contact can be 'Anthrax' or 'Scrub Typhus,' which are serious and need specific hospital medicine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify boil with a black painless center"] --> B["Check for fever and swollen glands"]
  B --> C["Cover with a clean dry bandage"] 
  C --> D["Do NOT apply any home pastes or oils"]
  D --> E["Do NOT attempt to scrape the black center"]
  F --> F["Refer to CHC immediately for blood tests"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Black painless boil | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| History of animal contact | Emergency - CHC | Never |
| Swollen glands | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bites that turn into black boils can be signs of zoonotic diseases (from animals). Professional diagnosis is mandatory.

Query: A child has a boil on the bottom of his foot. He says it feels like a thorn is inside. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Unable to put weight on the foot**
- Redness moving up to the ankle
- **Numbness in the toes**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Soak in warm salt water, check for thorn, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Soak the foot in warm water with salt** for 20 minutes to soften the skin.
- Look closely for a **thorn or splinter**; if visible and loose, remove with sterile tweezers.
- If the 'thorn' is deep, do NOT dig into the skin.
- Apply **Haldi paste** after the soak.
- Have the child wear slippers; do not let them walk barefoot.
- Refer to the PHC to ensure no foreign object is left inside.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Soak the foot in warm salt water for 20 minutes"] --> B["Check for visible thorns or wood splinters"]
  B --> C["Apply Haldi paste to the site after drying"]
  C --> D["Do NOT dig into the skin for deep objects"]
  D --> E["Advise wearing slippers at all times"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for safe extraction if needed"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Boil with 'thorn' feel | Refer to PHC | Warm salt soak |
| Redness at ankle | Emergency - PHC | Never |
| Cannot walk | Refer to PHC | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Objects left inside the foot can cause deep bone infections. Always get it checked if the pain continues.

Query: A woman used a local 'Ubtan' and now her face is covered in small boils. It is burning. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling of the lips or eyes**
- **Difficulty breathing**
- Skin peeling off

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Female | Wash face, apply cold milk, refer to PHC. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
