- **Note the color and amount** of the last urine passed.
- Give **small sips of water** if the patient is fully awake.
- Do NOT give any more medicine or herbs.
- **Transport immediately** to a facility with a dialysis or specialized kidney unit.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dengue patient with cola-colored urine"] --> B["Check amount of urine passed in last 8 hours"]
  B --> C["Give small sips of water only if fully awake"]
  C --> D["Check for yellow eyes or severe back pain"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any more herbs or painkillers"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital for kidney check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cola-colored urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No urine > 6 hours | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mildly dark urine | Refer today | Increase fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dark urine in Dengue is a sign of internal bleeding or severe organ damage. It is a major emergency.

query: A person with Dengue is very restless, tossing and turning, and seems very agitated. Is this normal?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme restlessness or agitation**
- Confusion or talking nonsense
- Fast heart rate
- Bleeding

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Emergency - This is a sign of falling blood pressure (Shock). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Speak calmly** to the patient but act quickly.
- **Lay them flat** and elevate their legs.
- **Check the pulse**: If it is very fast and hard to feel, they are in shock.
- **Do NOT give any sedative** or sleeping medicine.
- **Rush to the hospital** immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dengue patient is very restless and agitated"] --> B["Check pulse and skin temperature immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay the patient flat and elevate their legs"]
  C --> D["Speak calmly but prepare for urgent transport"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any sleeping pills or sedatives"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to hospital for shock management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Restlessness/Agitation | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fast pulse | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Anxiety only | Support and Refer | Calm environment |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Restlessness in a sick Dengue patient is NOT just 'stress'. It is often the first sign that the heart is struggling to pump blood.

query: ASHA worker reporting: A man with fever has developed a very itchy rash on his palms and soles. He is also very weak. Is this Dengue?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Itchy rash on palms and soles** (often seen in recovery)
- Fever with extreme weakness
- Red spots on the rest of the body
- Fast heart rate

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Refer for Platelet check. This 'recovery rash' still needs monitoring. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Avoid scratching**: Scratching can cause bleeding if platelets are low.
- Apply **Coconut oil** or a cool wet cloth to the itchy areas.
- Give **fluids with salt and sugar** (ORS).
- Check for any signs of bleeding from the mouth or nose.
- **Visit the PHC** for a final blood test before assuming he is cured.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Itchy rash on palms/soles after fever"] --> B["Apply coconut oil to reduce itching safely"]
  B --> C["Monitor for any new bleeding or red spots"]
  C --> D["Continue providing plenty of ORS and water"]
  D --> E["Advise against heavy physical work or scratching"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for a follow-up platelet checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rash + Weakness | Refer today | Coconut oil for itch |
| Rash + Bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Intense itching | Refer to PHC | Cool cloth |
| No other symptoms | Refer for final CBC | Monitor at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The itchy rash often appears when the patient is getting better, but internal complications can still happen. A final blood test is essential.

query: A person has Dengue and keeps fainting every time they sit up. What should I tell the family?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fainting or dizziness when sitting up** (Postural Hypotension)
- Very pale skin
- Cold sweat
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Emergency - Severe dehydration or fluid leakage. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Absolute Bed Rest**: The patient must stay lying down at all times.
- **Do NOT allow them to go to the toilet**; use a bedpan or diapers.
- Give **ORS and salt-sugar water** frequently in small amounts.
- **Elevate the legs** with pillows.
- **Transfer to the hospital** in a lying-down position.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dengue patient faints when sitting up"] --> B["Enforce absolute bed rest in a flat position"]
  B --> C["Elevate the legs 12 inches with pillows"]
  C --> D["Provide frequent sips of ORS if conscious"]
  D --> E["Do NOT allow patient to walk to the toilet"]
  E --> F["Transport to hospital in a lying-down position"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Keep flat in bed |
| Dizziness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold sweat | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Stable while lying | Still Refer today | Monitor closely |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fainting in Dengue means the blood volume is too low to reach the brain. This requires urgent IV fluid replacement.

query: Can Dengue be treated at home if we have plenty of coconut water and goat milk?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting the fluids**
- Platelets dropping below 1 Lakh
- Any 'Warning Sign' (Pain, Bleeding, Lethargy)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Home care is only for mild cases with NO warning signs. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Daily Blood Test**: Even if treating at home, you MUST get a platelet count every day.
- **Drink 2-3 liters** of total fluid (Coconut water, ORS, Soup).
- **Use Mosquito Nets**: This is the 'Home Medicine' to protect the rest of the village.
- **Monitor 'Warning Signs'**: If any appear, home care MUST stop immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Family wants to treat Dengue at home"] --> B["Check for any warning signs like pain or bleeding"]
  B --> C["Ensure patient gets a daily platelet count test"]
  C --> D["Maintain intake of 2-3 liters of fluid daily"]
  D --> E["Sleep under a mosquito net at all times"]
  E --> F["Refer immediately if any danger signs appear"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No warning signs | Home Care | CBC daily |
| Platelet < 1 Lakh | Refer for observation | Fluids and rest |
| Any warning sign | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Poor fluid intake | Immediate Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Home treatment is dangerous if the patient is not being monitored by a doctor daily. Dengue can turn deadly in just 4-6 hours.

query: A child has fever and the 'tourniquet test' was positive (many red dots after tying a band). What does this mean?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Positive Tourniquet Test** (sign of weak blood vessels)
- Fever with headache and body ache
- Nausea or loss of appetite

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Immediate Referral. A positive test means higher risk of bleeding. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start ORS immediately**: Don't wait to reach the hospital.
- **Avoid all injury**: Do not let the child play or bump into things (internal bruising risk).
- **Only Paracetamol**: Stop all other pain medicines.
- **Rush to the CHC** for a platelet count and evaluation.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with positive tourniquet test dots"] --> B["Begin immediate hydration with ORS and water"]
  B --> C["Prohibit any active play or rough movement"]
  C --> D["Stop all medicines except plain Paracetamol"]
  D --> E["Check for any current bleeding from gums"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC for blood checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Positive test dots | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Fever + Rash | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Mild fever only | Refer for CBC | Fluids and monitor |
| Loss of appetite | Refer to PHC | Light fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A positive tourniquet test means the blood vessels are fragile and likely to leak. It is a very important warning sign.

query: A person has Dengue and their pulse is very fast (120) and they are breathing very quickly. Should I wait for the blood report?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast pulse (> 100)**
- **Rapid breathing (> 24/min)**
- Restlessness or confusion
- Cold skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Do NOT wait for reports. This is Shock. Rush to emergency. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Emergency Referral**: Clinical signs (pulse/breathing) are more important than a piece of paper.
- **Lay flat with legs up** to help the heart.
- **Provide fresh air** and stay with the patient.
- **Call 108** or arrange the fastest vehicle available.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fast pulse and breathing in Dengue patient"] --> B["Immediately lay patient flat and elevate legs"]
  B --> C["Do NOT wait for lab reports or blood tests"]
  C --> D["Ensure the patient has plenty of fresh air"]
  D --> E["Speak calmly and monitor for consciousness"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to the nearest hospital ICU"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast pulse/Breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold clammy skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Restlessness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Normal pulse | Refer for testing | Fluids and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In Dengue, the clinical state of the patient matters most. A 'good' lab report doesn't matter if the pulse is 120 and the skin is cold.

query: A mother is worried because her baby (6 months) has a fever and won't stop crying. The baby's diaper is dry for 5 hours. Is this Dengue?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dry diaper (> 4-6 hours)**
- **Inconsolable crying**
- Fever in an infant
- Sunken soft spot (fontanelle) on the head

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Emergency Referral. Babies become dehydrated and go into shock very fast. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Breastfeed frequently**: Offer the breast every 15-20 minutes.
- **Give ORS**: Use a clean spoon or dropper to give tiny amounts of ORS.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Use lukewarm water to gently lower the baby's fever.
- **Rush to a Pediatrician** or the nearest Hospital immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant with fever and dry diaper"] --> B["Offer frequent breastfeeding every 15 minutes"]
  B --> C["Give tiny amounts of ORS with a clean spoon"]
  C --> D["Perform gentle tepid sponging for fever"]
  D --> E["Check for sunken soft spot on the head"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to Pediatric hospital/CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dry diaper > 4h | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever in infant | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Sunken soft spot | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Constant crying | Immediate Referral | Breastfeed often |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infants are at the highest risk for severe Dengue. Do not wait for a rash or bleeding; a dry diaper is an emergency.

query: A person with Dengue has a high fever and is now having a fit/seizure. What are the first-aid steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Seizure or Fitting**
- Unconsciousness
- High fever
- Confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any | Any | Critical Emergency - Possible brain involvement (Encephalitis). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety First**: Move furniture away; do NOT restrain the patient.
- **Turn on the Side**: Once the shaking stops, place the patient in the recovery position (on their side).
- **Do NOT put anything in the mouth** (no spoons, keys, or fingers).
- **Tepid Sponging**: Wipe the body with wet cloths to lower the fever quickly.
- **Call 108** and rush to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Dengue patient having a seizure"] --> B["Clear the area and do NOT restrain the patient"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put anything into the mouth"]
  C --> D["Turn patient on their side after shaking stops"]
  D --> E["Perform whole-body tepid sponging for fever"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizure/Fit | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Unconsciousness | Emergency Referral | Recovery position |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponge |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Seizures in Dengue can mean the brain is affected or the blood sugar/salts are very low. This is a life-threatening emergency.

query: A man in the village has very high fever every other day with shivering. Today he is very sleepy and not waking up properly. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme sleepiness or unconsciousness** (Cerebral Malaria)
- **Seizures or fitting**
- **Yellow eyes (Jaundice)**
- Very dark or 'cola-colored' urine
- Inability to drink or breastfeed

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | Emergency - Do not wait. High risk of death. |
| Adult | Any | Immediate referral to CHC for blood slide/RDT and IV treatment. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Perform Tepid Sponging**: Wipe the body with a wet cloth to reduce fever; do not use ice-cold water.
- **Recovery Position**: If the person is sleepy, lay them on their side so they don't choke if they vomit.
- **No Food/Water**: Do not force liquids if the person is semi-conscious.
- **Check for Pallor**: Look at the tongue and palms for severe whiteness.
- **Rush to CHC**: Mention the shivering and sleepiness to the doctor immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["High fever with shivering and drowsiness"] --> B["Lay person on their side in recovery position"]
  B --> C["Perform tepid sponging to lower brain temperature"]
  C --> D["Do NOT force-feed water or food if drowsy"]
  D --> E["Check for yellow eyes or dark urine"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for immediate transport to CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Drowsiness/Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering + Fever | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging during travel |
| Mild fever only | Visit PHC for test | Paracetamol and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria can affect the brain (Cerebral Malaria) very quickly. Delays in hospital treatment can lead to permanent damage or death.

query: A child has a fever and is breathing very fast. He is very pale and won't stop crying. We are in a high malaria area. What are the steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast breathing or gasping**
- **Pale palms and tongue (Severe Anemia)**
- **Vomiting everything**
- Sunken eyes and no urine (Dehydration)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Immediate referral. Children lose blood cells very fast with Malaria. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the child cool**: Remove excess clothes and sponge with room-temperature water.
- **Sips of ORS**: If the child is not vomiting, give small spoonfuls of ORS or coconut water.
- **Do NOT give aspirin**: Only use paracetamol if available and child is conscious.
- **Check for 'Blackwater'**: Ask the parents if the child's urine is dark red or black.
- **Immediate Referral**: Transport to a facility that can give blood and IV antimalarials.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with fast breathing, fever, and paleness"] --> B["Check for paleness of palms and inner eyelids"]
  B --> C["Start tepid sponging and remove tight clothes"]
  C --> D["Give small sips of ORS if child can swallow"]
  D --> E["Check urine color for darkness/blood"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for blood test and IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe paleness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Vomiting everything | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Simple fever | Visit PHC for RDT | Keep hydrated |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria in children often causes severe anemia. They may need a blood transfusion along with malaria medicine.

query: A pregnant woman has a fever and headache. She is shivering and says her joints hurt. Is it safe to wait for the mobile clinic tomorrow?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever in pregnancy** (any temperature)
- Shivering and chills
- Decreased baby movement
- Dizziness or fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate Referral. Malaria is twice as dangerous for pregnant women. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT wait**: Malaria causes miscarriage or low birth weight very quickly.
- **Lower the fever**: Use wet cloth wipes on her forehead and body.
- **Nutrition**: Give her **Jaggery water** and light food like Khichdi if she can eat.
- **Iron/Folic Acid**: Do not stop her pregnancy vitamins unless the doctor says so.
- **Blood Test**: Ensure she gets a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) today at the PHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with fever and shivering"] --> B["Explain that Malaria is an emergency in pregnancy"]
  B --> C["Apply cool damp cloths to reduce temperature"]
  C --> D["Check for baby movements and vaginal bleeding"]
  D --> E["Provide jaggery water for energy"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC/CHC immediately for RDT test"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever in pregnancy | Immediate Referral | Never wait at home |
| Shivering | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Decreased baby movement | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Headache | Refer for checkup | Rest and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria in pregnancy can lead to severe maternal anemia and fetal death. It must be treated with specific pregnancy-safe antimalarials immediately.

query: A young boy was playing and suddenly got a very high fever. Now he is shaking his arms and legs and his eyes are rolling back. What first aid can I give?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Seizures (Fitting)**
- Frothing at the mouth
- Eyes rolling upwards
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Youth | Any | Emergency - Protect from injury and rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Clear the area**: Move away any hard or sharp objects so he doesn't hit them.
- **Side position**: Turn him on his side to keep the tongue from blocking the throat.
- **Do NOT put anything in the mouth**: No spoons, no fingers, no water.
- **Lower fever**: Once the shaking stops, use a wet cloth to cool his head and neck.
- **Rush to CHC**: This is likely Cerebral Malaria and needs IV medicine immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child having a seizure with high fever"] --> B["Move away sharp objects and do NOT restrain"]
  B --> C["Turn the child on their side immediately"]
  C --> D["Do NOT put anything in the child's mouth"]
  D --> E["Once shaking stops, perform tepid sponging"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC for IV medication"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizures/Fitting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever (>103F) | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Eyes rolling back | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Post-seizure sleep | Emergency Referral | Keep on side during travel |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Seizures with fever in a malaria area are a sign of brain involvement. This is a critical emergency.

query: A man has been taking local 'bitter' herbs for his fever, but now he is not passing any urine and his stomach is swollen. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No urine for 12 hours** (Kidney failure)
- **Swollen belly or yellow skin**
- Breathing difficulty
- Bleeding from gums

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Critical Emergency - Rush to District Hospital for dialysis/specialist care. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop all herbs**: Some local herbs can damage the kidneys further during malaria.
- **Do NOT force water**: If he isn't urinating, drinking too much water can fill his lungs.
- **Record urine**: Note exactly when he last went to the toilet.
- **Keep him sitting up**: If he has trouble breathing, use pillows to prop him up.
- **Immediate Referral**: He needs a hospital with a lab to check his kidneys.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Malaria patient not passing urine"] --> B["Stop all local herbs and home medicines"]
  B --> C["Check for yellow eyes and swollen belly"]
  C --> D["Note the time of the last urine passage"]
  D --> E["Sit him upright if breathing is difficult"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Swollen belly | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Yellow skin/eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Bitter taste in mouth | Visit PHC for test | Increase fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria can cause acute kidney failure (Blackwater Fever). This cannot be treated at home or with herbs.

query: An old woman has a fever and is very weak. She is vomiting everything she drinks. She says her body is 'breaking' with pain. How to help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Persistent vomiting** (cannot keep any medicine down)
- **Extreme weakness/cannot stand**
- Very fast pulse
- Sunken eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High Risk - Referral for IV fluids and injectable malaria medicine. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT force-feed**: If she vomits everything, she needs medicine through a needle (injection).
- **Ice chips**: Give small bits of ice or tiny sips of cold water to suck on.
- **Keep her warm**: If she is shivering, use blankets; if fever is high, use tepid sponging.
- **Check for bleeding**: Look for any small red spots on the skin.
- **Transfer to CHC**: She needs IV fluids to prevent her heart from failing.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly person with fever and persistent vomiting"] --> B["Check pulse and look for signs of dehydration"]
  B --> C["Provide tiny sips of water or ice chips"]
  C --> D["Maintain warmth during shivering phases"]
  D --> E["Perform tepid sponging if body feels very hot"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for IV fluids and injections"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot keep medicine down | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Persistent vomiting | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Extreme weakness | Immediate Referral | Not safe at home |
| Body aches | Refer for blood test | Paracetamol and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Elderly patients dehydrate very quickly. If they cannot take oral tablets, malaria becomes fatal within 24-48 hours.

query: How can I tell if a fever is Malaria or just a normal cold in the village?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cyclical fever** (comes and goes every 24 or 48 hours)
- **Severe shivering (Chills) followed by heavy sweating**
- Extreme paleness
- Intense headache and muscle pain

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Get a Rapid Diagnostic Test (RDT) at the PHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Shivering**: Real malaria chills make the whole body shake, even in hot weather.
- **Check the Eyes**: Look for yellowing or severe paleness under the eyelids.
- **Tepid Sponging**: Reduce the fever with wet cloth wipes.
- **Increase Fluids**: Give Lemon water, Coconut water, or ORS.
- **Visit PHC**: Do not guess. A 5-minute RDT test can confirm if it is Malaria.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person has fever with shivering and body ache"] --> B["Check for cycles of fever, chills, and sweating"]
  B --> C["Look for paleness or yellowing of eyes"]
  C --> D["Provide plenty of fluids like coconut water"]
  D --> E["Go to the nearest PHC for an RDT blood test"]
  E --> F["Start government-supplied ACT course if positive"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shivering + Chills | Refer for blood test | Tepid sponging |
| Severe headache | Refer for blood test | Rest in dark room |
| Mild fever + Runny nose | Home care | Monitor for 24 hours |
| Any fever in child/pregnant | Immediate Referral | Never wait |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Waiting to 'see if it goes away' is dangerous. In malaria zones, every fever should be tested for Malaria within 24 hours.

query: A person is sweating a lot and their fever just went down. Does this mean they are cured?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sweating after a high fever** (this is the 'Sweating Stage' of Malaria)
- Sudden drop in body temperature (feeling cold and clammy)
- Weakness after the fever breaks

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | This is part of the malaria cycle; the fever *will* return. Get tested. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Change clothes**: Dry the person and put on clean, dry clothes.
- **Hydrate**: The person has lost a lot of salt and water in the sweat. Give ORS.
- **Monitor**: The fever usually comes back in 24 or 48 hours. Record the time.
- **Do NOT stop medicine**: If they have started tablets, they *must* finish the full course even if they feel better.
- **Visit PHC**: Get a blood test even if the fever is gone right now.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fever breaks and patient is sweating heavily"] --> B["Dry the patient and change into dry clothes"]
  B --> C["Give ORS or salted lemon water for hydration"]
  C --> D["Record the time and date the fever broke"]
  D --> E["Explain that the fever will likely return soon"]
  E --> F["Go to PHC for a blood test to confirm Malaria"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sweating stage | Refer for blood test | Change clothes, give fluids |
| Clammy skin/Weakness | Refer to PHC | Monitor pulse |
| No fever right now | Still refer for RDT | Do not wait for next fever |
| Feeling 100% better | Finish meds course | Check for recurrence |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria parasites hide in the liver and blood. Feeling 'better' for a few hours is a trick of the disease. Only a full course of medicine kills the parasite.

query: A man has malaria and his urine is very dark, like coffee or burnt oil. He is very pale. What does this mean?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dark red or black urine** (Blackwater Fever)
- **Severe paleness** (skin and eyes)
- Rapid breathing
- Pain in the back (kidneys)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Critical Emergency - Blood cells are bursting. Immediate hospital referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate Referral**: He needs hospital care, IV fluids, and potentially a blood transfusion.
- **Absolute Rest**: Do not let him walk or work.
- **Save Urine Sample**: If possible, take a sample of the dark urine to show the doctor.
- **Hydration**: If he can drink, give small sips of water, but do not overfill.
- **Do NOT give local herbs**: They may worsen kidney damage.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Malaria patient with coffee-colored urine"] --> B["Enforce absolute bed rest; no movement"]
  B --> C["Collect a sample of dark urine for the doctor"]
  C --> D["Check for severe paleness and rapid pulse"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any local herbal 'cleansers'"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Black/Dark urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe paleness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Back pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Normal urine | Visit PHC for test | Increase fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Blackwater Fever' is a very dangerous complication of Falciparum Malaria where blood cells break. It causes kidney failure and can be fatal without hospital treatment.

query: Can we use papaya leaf juice or neem tea to cure Malaria if the hospital is far?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting everything**
- **Drowsiness or confusion**
- High fever not coming down with herbs

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Local herbs do NOT kill the malaria parasite. Use them only as support, never as a cure. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Use Herbs for Comfort ONLY**: Neem or Tulsi tea can help with fever, but they will not stop the malaria parasite.
- **Prioritize Government Medicine**: Get **ACT (Artemisinin) tablets** from the ASHA or PHC. These are the only real cure.
- **Lower Fever**: Use tepid sponging with room-temperature water.
- **Watch for Danger**: If the patient becomes sleepy or eyes turn yellow, stop everything and rush to the hospital.
- **Keep Patient Hydrated**: Give ORS or coconut water.
- **Visit PHC**: Get a proper blood test today.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Family wants to use herbs for malaria"] --> B["Explain that herbs do not kill the malaria parasite"]
  B --> C["Advise using herbs only for mild fever relief"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging to manage high temperature"]
  D --> E["Obtain ACT tablets from ASHA/PHC immediately"]
  E --> F["Refer to hospital if drowsiness or yellow eyes appear"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever > 24 hours | Refer for RDT test | Tepid sponging |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Vomiting | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Mild aches | Visit PHC for test | Support with ginger/neem tea |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria is a parasite, not a simple cold. Relying only on herbs is a leading cause of malaria deaths in rural areas. Use government-approved medicine.

query: ASHA Reporting: A woman with malaria is 4 months pregnant. She is dizzy and her palms are very white. Should I give her iron tablets?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **White palms/tongue (Severe Anemia)**
- **Faintness or dizziness**
- Fast heart rate
- Fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral. Do not start high-dose iron until malaria is treated. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Refer immediately**: She likely needs an IV for malaria and possibly a blood transfusion.
- **Keep her lying down**: Do not let her walk; she might faint and fall.
- **Hydration**: Give her **Jaggery water** (Gur) for a small energy and iron boost safely.
- **Check for Malaria**: Ensure she gets a pregnancy-safe antimalarial (like Quinine or ACT, as per govt policy).
- **Wait on Iron**: Doctors usually treat the malaria first, then give iron tablets later.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with malaria and white palms"] --> B["Lay her flat on her side and prevent walking"]
  B --> C["Provide jaggery water for energy support"]
  C --> D["Do NOT start new iron tablets during high fever"]
  D --> E["Check for baby movements and vaginal bleeding"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC immediately for blood and malaria treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| White palms + Pregnancy | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dizziness | Emergency Referral | Keep lying down |
| Fever in pregnancy | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Poor appetite | Refer for counseling | Jaggery and light food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Iron supplements can sometimes feed certain infections if given during a high fever. Always treat the malaria infection first under medical supervision.

query: A baby (6 months old) has a fever. He won't take the breast and is very floppy. The mother says he has been like this since morning. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Floppy body (Lethargy)**
- **Refusing to breastfeed**
- High fever
- Unusual high-pitched cry

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Critical Emergency - Rush to hospital immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Tepid Sponge**: Remove the baby's clothes and wipe with a damp cloth to lower the heat.
- **Do NOT force milk**: If he is floppy, forcing milk can cause it to go into the lungs.
- **Keep him warm**: During transport, keep the baby wrapped but ensure the head is cool if he has a fever.
- **Check for Pallor**: Look at the baby's tongue and soles of feet for paleness.
- **Immediate Referral**: Babies can die from malaria within hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Floppy baby with fever; not nursing"] --> B["Remove clothes and perform tepid sponging"]
  B --> C["Check for paleness of tongue and soles of feet"]
  C --> D["Do NOT force-feed or force-breastfeed if floppy"]
  D --> E["Wrap baby comfortably for transport to CHC"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital for emergency IV treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Floppy/Lethargic | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Not breastfeeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High-pitched cry | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Warm skin only | Visit PHC for checkup | Monitor feeding |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In infants, malaria progresses very fast. 'Floppiness' is a sign that the baby's body is failing. Immediate medical help is the only way.

query: A person has started the 3-day malaria course but feels better after 1 day and wants to stop. Is this okay?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stopping medicine early** (leads to Malaria return and Resistance)
- Relapse of fever within a few days
- Vomiting the tablets

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | They MUST finish the full course, even if they feel 100% better. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Explain Resistance**: Tell them that if they stop now, the 'sleeping' parasites will wake up and be harder to kill next time.
- **Check for Vomiting**: If they vomit the tablet within 30 minutes, they must take another dose.
- **Take with Food**: Malaria medicine works better and hurts the stomach less if taken after a meal (except if told otherwise).
- **Monitor**: Watch for any new yellowing of eyes or dark urine.
- **ASHA Check**: Ensure the ASHA worker witnesses the doses (DOTS-like approach).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient wants to stop malaria medicine early"] --> B["Explain that unfinished medicine causes the disease to return"]
  B --> C["Verify that the patient is not vomiting the tablets"]
  C --> D["Encourage taking medicine after a small meal"]
  D --> E["Check for any new danger signs like yellow eyes"]
  E --> F["Ensure the full 3-day course is completed at home"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting tablets | Refer for injection | Try taking with food |
| Fever returns | Refer to PHC | Check if doses were missed |
| Feeling better | Home care | Finish the full course |
| Allergic rash | Immediate Referral | Stop medicine and refer |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stopping malaria medicine early allows the strongest parasites to survive and spread to others in the village. This is how 'Drug-Resistant Malaria' starts.

query: The person has malaria and is breathing very fast like they just ran a race. They are sitting up and gasping. What is the plan?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fast, gasping breath (Respiratory Distress)**
- **Blue lips or fingertips**
- Cold sweat
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Critical Emergency - Likely severe anemia or lung fluid. Rush to District Hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sit the person upright**: Use many pillows or a chair. This makes breathing easier than lying down.
- **Provide Fresh Air**: Open all windows and clear people away from the patient.
- **Do NOT give food or water**: They may choke while gasping for air.
- **Keep calm**: Panic makes breathing harder. Speak softly to the patient.
- **Immediate Referral**: This patient needs oxygen and urgent medical care.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Malaria patient gasping for air"] --> B["Sit the person upright with firm back support"]
  B --> C["Open windows and ensure maximum fresh air"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give anything to eat or drink"]
  D --> E["Check for blue lips or cold skin"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital for oxygen"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Gasping for air | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Blue lips/nails | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold, clammy skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fast pulse only | Immediate Referral | Keep sitting up |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fast breathing in malaria is a sign of 'Metabolic Acidosis' or lung edema. It is a sign that the body is shutting down. Hospital care is mandatory.

query: A person has a fever and their eyes are very yellow. They also have pain on the right side under the ribs. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow eyes and skin (Jaundice)**
- **Pain in the liver area** (under right ribs)
- Dark urine
- Vomiting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Emergency - Malaria is affecting the liver. Immediate referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Hydration**: Give ORS or glucose water if they can drink.
- **Absolute Rest**: Do not let them walk; moving can worsen liver pain.
- **No Herbs**: Stop any local 'yellow fever' or 'jaundice' herbs; they can be toxic to the liver during malaria.
- **Lower Fever**: Use tepid sponging with room-temperature water.
- **Rush to Hospital**: This requires IV antimalarials and liver monitoring.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Fever with yellow eyes and liver pain"] --> B["Prohibit any local jaundice herbs or oils"]
  B --> C["Give small sips of ORS or glucose water"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging for high fever"]
  D --> E["Note the color of urine and any vomiting"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Liver area pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dark urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Shivering only | Refer for RDT test | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria-induced jaundice is a sign of severe disease. It is not the same as normal hepatitis and needs specific malaria injections.

query: My patient with malaria has a very cold body and a very low pulse, but they were burning with fever two hours ago. Is this good?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden drop in temperature (Hypothermia)**
- **Weak and thready pulse**
- Cold, clammy sweat
- Drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Critical Emergency - This is 'Algid Malaria' (Shock). Rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Warm**: Cover with blankets and use hot water bottles (wrapped in cloth) at the feet.
- **Raise Legs**: Lay the person flat and lift their legs 12 inches to help the heart.
- **Sugar Water**: If conscious, give a sweet drink for an energy boost.
- **Do NOT use ice**: Even if they had a fever before, they are now in shock and need warmth.
- **Emergency Referral**: They need IV fluids immediately to save their life.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient turns very cold and weak after fever"] --> B["Lay flat and raise legs 12 inches high"]
  B --> C["Cover with blankets to restore body heat"]
  C --> D["Provide a sweet drink if the person is awake"]
  D --> E["Monitor for very weak or fast pulse"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Hospital for shock care"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cold/Clammy skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Weak pulse | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Drowsiness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Heavy sweating only | Refer to PHC | Dry clothes and ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Algid Malaria' is a rare but deadly form where the patient goes into circulatory shock. It looks like the fever is 'gone', but the patient is actually dying.

query: Can malaria spread from person to person by touching or eating together?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever in multiple family members**
- Mosquitoes seen in the house
- No use of bed nets

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | No, it only spreads via mosquito bites. Protect the family. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Bed Nets**: Put the sick person under a Long-Lasting Insecticidal Net (LLIN) immediately so mosquitoes don't bite them and spread it to others.
- **No Isolation Needed**: You can touch, hug, and eat with a malaria patient safely.
- **Test the Family**: If others have fever, they must get an RDT test today.
- **Clean the Area**: Remove standing water (pots, tires) near the house where mosquitoes breed.
- **Neem Smoke**: Use neem leaves smoke outside at sunset to keep mosquitoes away.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["One family member confirmed with malaria"] --> B["Place the sick person under a bed net immediately"]
  B --> C["Check all other family members for fever symptoms"]
  C --> D["Remove any stagnant water around the home"]
  D --> E["Ensure the sick person finishes their medication"]
  E --> F["Refer any other feverish family members for RDT tests"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever in family | Refer for RDT test | Clean surroundings |
| Chills/Shivering | Refer for RDT test | Use bed nets |
| No fever | Home care | Use mosquito repellent |
| Recovered patient | Home care | Continue using nets |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Malaria is not contagious like a cold. It needs a mosquito to carry the blood from a sick person to a healthy one. Using bed nets stops this cycle.

query: A man with malaria is bleeding from his gums and has small red bruises on his skin. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding from gums or nose**
- **Small red/purple spots on skin (Petechiae)**
- Blood in stool or vomit
- Severe paleness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Emergency - Low platelet count (Bleeding Malaria). Rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Absolute Rest**: Any fall or bump can cause major internal bleeding.
- **Soft Food**: Give only soft Khichdi; hard food can make gums bleed more.
- **Do NOT give Ibuprofen/Aspirin**: These medicines make bleeding much worse. Only use Paracetamol.
- **Pressure**: If a nosebleed starts, pinch the soft part of the nose for 10 minutes.
- **Immediate Referral**: He needs a hospital to check his blood platelets.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Malaria patient with bleeding gums or skin spots"] --> B["Enforce strict bed rest to avoid any injury"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any aspirin or brufen medicine"]
  C --> D["Provide only soft, easy-to-chew foods"]
  D --> E["Check for blood in urine or stools"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding gums | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Skin bruises/spots | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Nosebleed | Emergency Referral | Pinch nose for 10 mins |
| High fever only | Refer for RDT test | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Bleeding in malaria is a sign of 'Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation' or very low platelets. This is a life-threatening complication.

query: The person has malaria and is complaining of a very loud ringing in their ears and can't hear me well. Is it the disease?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Ringing in the ears (Tinnitus)**
- Hearing loss
- Dizziness or vomiting
- Confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | This is often a side effect of certain malaria medicines (like Quinine). Refer to the doctor. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT stop medicine** without a doctor's order, but **refer immediately** to see if the dose needs to be changed.
- **Keep the person calm**: Explain that the ringing usually goes away when the medicine stops.
- **Safe Environment**: Because they are dizzy/deaf, they may fall. Help them move.
- **Check for Sugar**: Sometimes this happens along with low blood sugar. Give **Jaggery water**.
- **Visit PHC**: The doctor needs to check if the medicine is too strong for the patient.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient on malaria meds has ringing ears/deafness"] --> B["Provide jaggery water to rule out low blood sugar"]
  B --> C["Ensure the patient does not walk alone due to dizziness"]
  C --> D["Record the time and the name of the medicine taken"]
  D --> E["Do NOT stop the course but seek medical advice immediately"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC/Doctor to adjust the medication dose"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sudden deafness | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Severe ringing ears | Immediate Referral | Give sugar water |
| Dizziness/Falls | Immediate Referral | Help with walking |
| Mild dizziness | Refer for checkup | Rest and monitor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Quinine-based medicines can cause 'Cinchonism' (ringing ears/hearing loss). It must be managed by a doctor to prevent permanent ear damage.

query: A person has a fever and is acting very strangely—laughing for no reason and then getting very angry. They don't recognize their family. What's wrong?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Strange behavior/Personality changes**
- **Confusion or not recognizing people**
- Violent outbursts or extreme fear
- High fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Emergency - This is a sign of brain malaria (Cerebral Malaria). Rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety first**: If they are violent, keep them in a safe, soft area so they don't hurt themselves.
- **Keep it quiet**: Darken the room and remove loud noises; this helps calm the brain.
- **Lower the fever**: Perform tepid sponging even if they resist.
- **Do NOT argue**: Speak in a calm, soothing voice.
- **Call 108**: They need IV antimalarials immediately before they go into a coma.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Malaria patient acting strangely or confused"] --> B["Keep the room dark and quiet to calm the patient"]
  B --> C["Remove any objects the patient could use to hurt themselves"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging to reduce brain heat"]
  D --> E["Speak calmly and do not restrain the person forcefully"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Personality change | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Not recognizing family | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion/Delirium | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever only | Refer for RDT test | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Behavioral changes with fever in a malaria area are almost always due to brain involvement. This is an extreme emergency and can lead to a coma within hours.

query: A child in the village has had a high fever for a week. The fever stays low in the morning and goes very high like steps in the evening. He has a stomach ache and a few red spots on his chest. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Step-ladder fever** (fever that gets higher each day)
- **Severe stomach pain and swelling**
- Passing black or bloody stools
- Confusion, delirium, or extreme sleepiness
- Constant vomiting and inability to drink

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | High risk of dehydration. Give ORS and refer to PHC for blood test immediately. |
| Adult | Any | Rest, high fluid intake, and visit CHC for antibiotic course. |
| Elderly | Any | High risk of heart/gut complications. Immediate referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Tepid Sponging**: Use room-temperature water to wipe the body and keep the fever below 102°F.
- **Fluid Therapy**: Give ORS, boiled and cooled water, or coconut water frequently in small sips.
- **Diet**: Give soft, easy-to-digest foods like thin Khichdi, porridge (Daliya), or boiled potatoes. Avoid spicy or fried food.
- **Hygiene**: Ensure the patient washes hands with soap after using the toilet to stop spreading it to the family.
- **Refer to PHC**; Typhoid needs a full course of antibiotics to prevent the gut from bursting.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient has prolonged fever and stomach pain"] --> B["Perform tepid sponging to lower body temperature"]
  B --> C["Start frequent sips of ORS and boiled water"]
  C --> D["Provide soft, bland food like thin khichdi"]
  D --> E["Strict hand-washing with soap for all family members"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Widal/TyphiDot test and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Blood in stool | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever > 5 days | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging at home |
| Severe belly swelling | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild weakness | Refer to PHC | ORS and soft diet |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid is a serious bacterial infection. If antibiotics are stopped early, the fever will return and can cause a hole in the intestines.

query: One of my village mothers says her son has 'Moti-Jhara' (Typhoid). He is very weak, has a dry tongue, and hasn't passed stool in 3 days. What is the first aid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme weakness (cannot sit up)**
- Sunken eyes and very dry tongue (Severe dehydration)
- Hard, painful stomach
- High fever that does not come down with medicine

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Adult | Any | Focus on rehydration and soft diet. Refer for stool/blood testing. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Increase Fluids**: Since he is constipated and weak, give **warm water with honey** or **thin Bael fruit juice** (if available) to help the gut.
- **Oral Rehydration**: Give **ORS** every 15 minutes to moisten the dry tongue.
- **Do NOT give strong laxatives**; the typhoid-affected gut is very thin and can tear easily.
- **Home Care**: Keep the patient in a separate bed and clean the toilet with bleach/lime after use.
- **Refer to CHC** for specific Typhoid treatment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Prolonged fever with constipation and dry tongue"] --> B["Give frequent sips of ORS to combat dehydration"]
  B --> C["Provide thin, warm fluids like dal water or bael juice"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use strong laxatives or 'churan'"]
  D --> E["Monitor for sudden stomach pain or blood in stool"]
  E --> F["Refer to medical facility for antibiotic therapy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Constipation > 3 days | Refer to PHC | Soft food and warm water |
| Dry tongue/Sunken eyes | Immediate Referral | ORS sips |
| Constant high fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid germs are passed through stool. Strict hygiene is the only way to protect the rest of the village.

query: A man finished 3 days of Typhoid medicine and feels better, so he wants to stop. He is now eating spicy mutton. Should I allow this?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Relapse** (fever coming back worse)
- Sudden sharp pain in the belly after a heavy meal
- Vomiting blood

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Educate on completing antibiotic course and strict bland diet. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Spicy Food**: Tell him the gut is like 'thin paper' during Typhoid. Heavy, spicy food can cause a hole in the intestine (perforation).
- **Enforce the Medicine**: He MUST complete the full 7-14 day course even if the fever is gone. If not, the bacteria will hide and come back stronger.
- **Diet**: Continue soft food like curd-rice, bananas, and boiled veg for at least 1 week after fever ends.
- **Advise Rest**: No heavy farm work for 2 weeks.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient wants to stop medicine and eat heavy food"] --> B["Explain that the gut is weak and can tear (perforation)"]
  B --> C["Enforce completion of full antibiotic course"]
  C --> D["Restrict diet to soft, bland foods (khichdi, curd)"]
  D --> E["Prohibit spicy, fried, or hard-to-digest foods"]
  E --> F["Monitor for sudden abdominal pain for 7 days"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sudden sharp belly pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever returns | Immediate Referral | Complete the course |
| Loss of appetite | Refer to PHC | Soft fluids and curd |
| Normal energy | Routine checkup | Finish all tablets |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stopping Typhoid medicine early creates 'carriers' who spread the disease to others without knowing it.

query: A village girl has Typhoid and she is now acting very strange, talking to people who aren't there and she can't recognize her mother. Fever is 104°F. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Typhoid State** (Confusion, muttering, and picking at bedsheets)
- **Very high fever (104°F+)**
- Unresponsiveness
- Rapid, weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Critical Emergency. Lower the fever and rush to a hospital with IV facilities. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Cold Sponging**: Use cool water to sponge her entire body immediately to protect the brain from the high heat.
- **Do NOT give food**: If she is confused, she may choke.
- **Position**: Lay her on her side if she is drowsy.
- **Call 108**: She needs IV antibiotics and fluids immediately; the infection has reached her nervous system.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["High fever with confusion and delirium"] --> B["Perform whole-body cold sponging immediately"]
  B --> C["Do NOT attempt to give food or water by mouth"]
  C --> D["Place in recovery position if unconscious"]
  D --> E["Call 108 for emergency ambulance transport"]
  E --> F["Transfer to District Hospital for IV therapy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion/Delirium | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever > 103°F | Emergency Referral | Cold sponging |
| Picking at clothes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild headache | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Delirium in Typhoid is a sign of severe toxicity (Encephalopathy). This cannot be treated with home remedies.

query: Multiple people in one street have fever and stomach ache. They all use the same well water. What should I tell the village?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Outbreak** (many people sick at once)
- High fever and diarrhea/constipation in the community

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Community | All | Water sanitation and hygiene education. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Boil All Water**: Tell everyone to boil drinking water for at least 1 minute (until big bubbles appear).
- **Chlorination**: Use chlorine tablets (Halazone) in the well if provided by the PHC.
- **Wash Hands**: Use soap after toilet and before cooking. This is a 'hand-to-mouth' disease.
- **Cook Food Well**: Eat only hot, freshly cooked food. Avoid raw vegetables washed in well water.
- **Report**: Inform the Sanitary Inspector or PHC about the cluster of cases.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Multiple cases of fever in one area"] --> B["Order all families to boil drinking water for 1 minute"]
  B --> C["Chlorinate common water sources (wells/tanks)"]
  C --> D["Educate on strict hand-washing with soap"]
  D --> E["Prohibit eating raw or uncovered food"]
  E --> F["Report the outbreak to the local health authority"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever > 3 days | Refer to PHC | Boil all water |
| Vomiting/Diarrhea | Refer for ORS/Testing | Clean toilet hygiene |
| Severe pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No symptoms | Community Prevention | Hand-washing education |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid is caused by water contaminated with stool. If one person has it, the whole water source might be dangerous.

query: A child has Typhoid and his stomach is very bloated and hard. He is crying and won't let me touch his belly. He is very pale. What are the steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Hard, 'board-like' stomach**
- Patient won't let you touch the belly (Guardness)
- **Sudden paleness and cold sweat**
- No bowel sounds or passing of gas

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Surgical Emergency. Do not give anything by mouth and rush to a surgeon. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NPO (Nothing by Mouth)**: Do NOT give water, food, or medicine. If the gut has a hole, food will leak into the belly and cause death.
- **Keep him lying still**: Move him very gently on a stretcher or cot.
- **Do NOT use hot water bags** on the stomach.
- **Rush to the District Hospital** immediately; he likely needs emergency surgery for an intestinal tear.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient with hard, painful, bloated belly"] --> B["Stop all food, water, and oral medicine immediately"]
  B --> C["Check for cold skin and rapid pulse (shock)"]
  C --> D["Do NOT apply heat or massage the stomach"]
  D --> E["Call 108 for immediate surgical referral"]
  E --> F["Transfer to hospital with surgical facilities"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hard/Board-like belly | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold sweat/Shock | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Bloating + Fever | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild stomach ache | Refer to PHC | Soft diet only |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Intestinal perforation is the most common cause of death in Typhoid. It happens usually in the 3rd week of fever. Surgery is the only cure.

query: A mother asks if she can give her Typhoid-sick daughter 'Tulsi and Neem' juice to cure the fever. The girl is 10 years old.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever lasting more than 4 days**
- Weakness getting worse despite herbs
- Coated tongue (white/grey fur on tongue)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Female | Herbs are supportive only; antibiotics are mandatory to kill the bacteria. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Explain the Bacteria**: Tell her that Typhoid is a 'germ' (bacteria) that needs specific English medicine (antibiotics) to die. Herbs alone cannot kill it.
- **Herbs are okay for comfort**: Tulsi tea is fine for the throat, but it is NOT a cure.
- **Avoid bitter Neem juice**: It can cause stomach irritation in a gut already weakened by Typhoid.
- **Refer for Testing**: Take the child for a blood test (Widal) today.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Parent wants to use only herbal cures for typhoid"] --> B["Explain that germs in the blood need antibiotics to die"]
  B --> C["Allow mild tea (Tulsi) for comfort only"]
  C --> D["Discourage harsh juices that irritate the stomach"]
  D --> E["Monitor for danger signs like belly pain"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for proper diagnosis and medicine"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| High fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Coated tongue | Refer for testing | Clean mouth with salt water |
| Vomiting | Refer to PHC | ORS sips |
| Cough | Refer to PHC | Tulsi tea |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Treating Typhoid only with home remedies often leads to the infection spreading to the brain or causing internal bleeding.

query: What food should I tell the family to give to a Typhoid patient? They are giving only dry roti and chili pickle.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stomach pain after eating**
- Extreme weight loss
- Diarrhea getting worse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Change to soft, liquid, or semi-solid bland diet immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **STOP Roti and Chilies**: Hard bread (Roti) and spices (Chili) irritate the infected gut and can cause bleeding.
- **Give Soft Foods**: Thin Khichdi, mashed bananas, boiled rice with curd, and boiled potatoes.
- **Focus on Liquids**: Coconut water, pomegranate juice (strained), and dal water.
- **Small Meals**: Give food every 2-3 hours in small amounts instead of 2 big meals.
- **Boil everything**: Ensure all food is cooked thoroughly and water is boiled.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient eating hard/spicy food"] --> B["Stop roti, chilies, and fried foods immediately"]
  B --> C["Switch to soft foods like curd-rice and mashed banana"]
  C --> D["Provide frequent liquids like coconut water and ORS"]
  D --> E["Cook all food thoroughly and serve it warm"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if appetite does not improve in 2 days"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Refusing all food | Refer for IV fluids | Try thin liquids first |
| Diarrhea with blood | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild nausea | Refer to PHC | Ginger water |
| Improving appetite | Routine monitoring | Continue soft diet |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Diet is 50% of the treatment in Typhoid. A wrong meal can cause an intestinal emergency.

query: A patient has Typhoid and is passing stools that look like 'Pea Soup' (watery and green-yellow). He is very dizzy. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pea-soup diarrhea** (typical of Typhoid)
- **Signs of dehydration**: Sunken eyes, skin stays up when pinched
- Fainting when standing up
- Pulse is very fast and weak

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | High-volume rehydration and immediate medical referral for electrolytes. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start ORS immediately**: Give one cup of ORS after every watery stool.
- **Check for bleeding**: Look closely at the stool. If it looks black like coal, there is internal bleeding.
- **Keep him lying down**: Do not let him walk to the toilet; use a bedpan to prevent fainting.
- **Zinc Supplements**: If available (especially for children), give Zinc to help the gut lining.
- **Rush to PHC**; he may need IV fluids to stay alive.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient with pea-soup diarrhea and dizziness"] --> B["Provide ORS immediately after every loose stool"]
  B --> C["Enforce bed rest to prevent fainting and injury"]
  C --> D["Inspect stool for any signs of black color (blood)"]
  D --> E["Give zinc supplements if available for gut health"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for IV fluid stabilization"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pea-soup diarrhea | Refer to PHC | Constant ORS |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Black stools | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild diarrhea | Refer to PHC | Soft diet and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe diarrhea in Typhoid can lead to sudden heart failure due to loss of salts (electrolytes). Hospital care is urgent.

query: ASHA reporting: A mother is worried because her 5-year-old has a fever and is constantly coughing, but has no runny nose. His stomach also hurts. Could it be Typhoid?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dry cough with high fever** (common in early Typhoid)
- **Stomach pain and bloating**
- No relief with normal cold medicine
- Coated tongue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (5) | Any | Refer for Widal test. Do not treat as a simple cold. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check for Fever Pattern**: Ask if the fever is higher in the evening than the morning.
- **Look at the Tongue**: If it has a thick white coating in the center, it is a sign of Typhoid.
- **Palpate the Belly**: Gently press the right side of the belly. If the child cries, the liver/spleen may be swollen.
- **Do NOT give strong cough syrups**; they can hide the signs of the disease.
- **Refer to a doctor**; Typhoid in children often starts like a lung infection.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with dry cough, fever, and stomach ache"] --> B["Check for coated tongue and evening fever spikes"]
  B --> C["Gently feel the belly for tenderness or swelling"]
  C --> D["Provide sips of warm water for the dry cough"]
  D --> E["Do NOT use heavy cough suppressants"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Typhoid testing (Blood culture/Widal)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cough + Belly pain | Refer to PHC | Warm fluids |
| Coated tongue | Refer for testing | Oral hygiene |
| High fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Rapid breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In children, Typhoid can look like many other things (Pneumonia or Malaria). Only a blood test can tell the truth.

query: A man has Typhoid. He says he feels fine and wants to go back to work as a 'Dhaba' (roadside eatery) cook. Can he go?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Carrier Status** (carrying germs without being sick)
- **Spread to others** via food handling

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult (Worker) | Any | Strictly prohibit food handling until cleared by a doctor with 3 negative stool tests. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Explain the Risk**: Tell him he can give Typhoid to everyone who eats his food. The bacteria stay in the stool long after the fever is gone.
- **Enforce Quarantine**: He must stay away from cooking for others for at least 2-4 weeks.
- **Hand Hygiene**: He must wash hands with soap and hot water every single time he uses the toilet.
- **Refer to PHC**: He needs stool tests to prove he is no longer 'shedding' the bacteria.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient wants to return to food handling work"] --> B["Explain that he can spread the disease via his hands"]
  B --> C["Prohibit any work involving cooking or serving food"]
  C --> D["Instruct on strict hand-washing with soap after toilet"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of fever or diarrhea"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for stool clearance tests before return"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Asymptomatic carrier | Refer for stool tests | Strict personal hygiene |
| Fever returns | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Family members get sick | Refer entire family | Boil all water |
| Normal health | Routine follow-up | Complete rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A single Typhoid carrier working in a kitchen can cause a massive village outbreak. Hygiene is a community duty.

query: One girl with Typhoid has started having very dark, black, sticky stools. She is very weak and her heart is beating fast. What is happening?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Black, tarry stools (Melaena)**
- **Internal bleeding** in the intestines
- Rapid heart rate (above 100)
- Sudden drop in body temperature (feeling cold)
- Fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Female | Life-threatening Emergency. Stop all food and rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay her flat** and elevate her legs to keep blood in the brain.
- **NPO (Nothing by Mouth)**: Do not give water or food; it can make the bleeding worse.
- **Check for Shock**: If her hands are cold and pulse is weak, she is losing a lot of blood.
- **Call 108**: She needs a blood transfusion and IV medicine to stop the bleeding.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient passing black stools with fast heart rate"] --> B["Lay patient flat and elevate legs 12 inches"]
  B --> C["Stop all oral intake (no food or water)"]
  C --> D["Monitor pulse and check for cold, clammy skin"]
  D --> E["Identify potential blood donors in the family"]
  E --> F["Call 108 for emergency transfer to District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Black/Tarry stools | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fast heart rate | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold skin | Emergency Referral | Cover with blanket |
| Dizziness | Emergency Referral | Keep lying down |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Internal bleeding usually happens in the 3rd week of Typhoid. It can be painless but deadly. Hospital care is mandatory.

query: A patient is taking antibiotics for Typhoid but he is vomiting every time he takes the pill. He has not taken medicine for 2 days. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Inability to keep medicine down**
- Constant vomiting
- Fever rising again
- Signs of dehydration

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Referral for IV/Injectable antibiotics. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT force the pills**: If he vomits them, the dose is lost and the bacteria will become resistant.
- **Sips of ORS**: Give small sips of cold ORS or ginger water to settle the stomach.
- **Try small doses**: Sometimes taking the pill with a small amount of curd (dahi) helps, but do not wait more than a few hours.
- **Refer to CHC**: He needs 'drips' (IV fluids) and injections to get the medicine into his blood directly.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient vomiting all oral medication"] --> B["Stop trying to give pills if vomiting persists"]
  B --> C["Provide cold ORS in tiny, frequent sips"]
  C --> D["Check for dry mouth and sunken eyes"]
  D --> E["Monitor for return of high fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for IV antibiotics and fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Vomiting medicine | Immediate Referral | Tiny sips of ORS |
| Dehydration | Immediate Referral | ORS/Coconut water |
| High fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Nausea only | Refer to PHC | Ginger/Mint water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid medicine must be in the blood constantly. If pills aren't working, injections are the only way to save the patient.

query: I see some small, rose-colored spots on the chest and belly of a fever patient. He has a headache and constipation. Is this dangerous?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rose Spots** (faint pink spots that disappear when pressed)
- Severe, constant headache
- Constipation followed by diarrhea
- High, steady fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult/Child | Any | Classical sign of Typhoid. Start isolation and refer for testing. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Mark the spots**: Use a pen to circle them and see if they spread or change.
- **Manage Headache**: Apply a cold cloth to the forehead. Do NOT give aspirin (it can increase bleeding risk in Typhoid).
- **Check Temperature**: Note if the fever is 'Step-ladder' (slowly rising every day).
- **Refer for Widal Test**: These spots are a classic warning of Typhoid infection in the blood.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient has pink spots on chest and steady fever"] --> B["Apply cold cloth to forehead for headache"]
  B --> C["Monitor for abdominal pain or constipation"]
  C --> D["Do NOT give aspirin for pain relief"]
  D --> E["Enforce strict water boiling and hand hygiene"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Typhoid blood test (Widal/Culture)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rose spots | Refer to PHC | Monitor and mark them |
| Severe headache | Refer to PHC | Cold compress |
| Constipation | Refer to PHC | Increase fiber-rich liquids |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rose spots are only seen in about 10-20% of cases but are a very strong sign of Typhoid. Early treatment prevents the 'danger' stage.

query: A child has Typhoid and his nose has started bleeding. He is otherwise okay but weak. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Nosebleed (Epistaxis)** in a fever patient
- Bruising on the body
- Bleeding gums
- Extreme paleness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Manage bleeding and refer to check platelet count/complications. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Pinch the Nose**: Have the child sit up, lean forward slightly, and pinch the soft part of the nose for 10 minutes.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply ice or a cold cloth to the bridge of the nose.
- **Do NOT let him blow his nose** for at least 4 hours after it stops.
- **Refer to CHC**: Bleeding in Typhoid can mean the infection is affecting the blood's ability to clot.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient with active nosebleed"] --> B["Sit patient up and lean forward slightly"]
  B --> C["Pinch the soft part of the nose for 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Apply cold cloth to the bridge of the nose"]
  D --> E["Prohibit nose blowing or picking for 4 hours"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for blood clotting and platelet check"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bleeding won't stop | Emergency Referral | Pinching and cold cloth |
| Recurrent nosebleeds | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Bleeding gums | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Mild weakness | Refer to PHC | Soft food and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Nosebleeds in Typhoid can be a sign of the 'toxic' phase. It must be investigated by a doctor immediately.

query: ASHA worker reporting: A Typhoid patient's heart rate is very slow (55) even though he has a high fever (103°F). Is this a problem?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Relative Bradycardia** (Slow pulse with high fever)
- Feeling very cold or clammy
- Dizziness or fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | This is a typical but serious sign of Typhoid. Refer for monitoring. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep the patient lying down**: A slow heart rate means he may faint if he stands up suddenly.
- **Monitor Fever**: Use tepid sponging to bring the temperature down safely.
- **Check Pulse every hour**: If it drops below 50, it is an emergency.
- **Refer to CHC**: The heart is under stress from the bacterial toxins.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Slow pulse with high fever in typhoid patient"] --> B["Enforce absolute bed rest to prevent fainting"]
  B --> C["Monitor pulse and temperature every 60 minutes"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging for the high fever"]
  D --> E["Check for signs of cold skin or confusion"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for cardiac and blood monitoring"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pulse < 55 | Immediate Referral | Absolute bed rest |
| Fainting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| High fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Normal pulse | Routine checkup | Continue meds |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Usually, fever makes the heart go faster. If it goes slower in Typhoid, it means the body is struggling with high levels of infection.

query: A patient with Typhoid has a very swollen and painful right side of the neck. He can't swallow. What is this?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Painful swelling in the neck/jaw** (Typhoid Parotitis)
- Inability to swallow saliva (Drooling)
- Difficulty breathing
- Very high fever

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
