|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Refer for treatment of secondary infection and pain management. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Warm Compress**: Apply a warm, clean cloth to the swollen area for pain relief.
- **Small Sips**: If he can swallow, give tiny sips of water or warm milk. Do not force large amounts.
- **Mouth Hygiene**: Have him rinse his mouth with warm salt water if possible.
- **Rush to CHC**: Swelling in the neck can block the airway and needs special antibiotics.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient with painful neck/jaw swelling"] --> B["Apply warm compress to the swollen area for pain"]
  B --> C["Check if the patient can swallow or breathe easily"]
  C --> D["Provide tiny sips of fluids if swallowing is possible"]
  D --> E["Rinse mouth with warm salt water if tolerated"]
  E --> F["Refer immediately to CHC for airway management"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cannot swallow | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Painful swelling | Immediate Referral | Warm compress |
| Fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Sometimes Typhoid affects the glands in the neck. This can be very painful and dangerous if it swells too much.

query: What should the family do with the clothes and bedsheets of a Typhoid patient in a small village house?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Spread of infection** to other family members

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Family | All | Strict disinfection of all linens and patient items. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Soak in Disinfectant**: Soak all soiled clothes and sheets in water with bleach (Dettol/Phenyle) or boil them for 20 minutes.
- **Wash Separately**: Do NOT wash patient clothes with the rest of the family's clothes.
- **Sun Dry**: Hang clothes in direct sunlight for at least 6 hours; sunlight kills many bacteria.
- **Boil Utensils**: Boil the patient's plate, spoon, and glass after every use.
- **Handwashing**: The person washing the clothes MUST wash their hands with soap and hot water afterward.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Query on handling typhoid patient's linens"] --> B["Soak all soiled items in disinfectant or boil them"]
  B --> C["Wash patient's clothes separately from the family"]
  C --> D["Dry all linens in direct sunlight for 6 hours"]
  D --> E["Boil all patient utensils after every single use"]
  E --> F["Wash hands with soap after handling any soiled items"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Diarrhea in family | Refer all to PHC | Boil all water |
| Skin rash in washer | Refer to PHC | Use gloves/soap |
| Patient soiled bed | Home Care | Immediate disinfection |
| No bleach available | Home Care | Boil clothes in water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid is a disease of 'dirt and water'. If you don't clean the clothes properly, the whole family will get sick.

query: A pregnant woman has a high fever for 4 days and a headache. Her test shows Typhoid. What are the special steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever in pregnancy**
- Decreased movement of the baby
- Sudden belly pain or bleeding
- Severe dehydration

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | Immediate referral to a hospital. Typhoid can cause miscarriage or harm the baby. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lower Fever Immediately**: High fever can harm the baby's brain. Use tepid sponging and safe medicine (Paracetamol) as per the doctor.
- **Hydrate Constantly**: Give ORS and coconut water; pregnancy needs extra fluids.
- **Monitor Baby**: Ask her to note the baby's kicks.
- **Refer to Obstetrician**: She needs special antibiotics that are safe for the baby and closer monitoring.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with confirmed Typhoid fever"] --> B["Perform tepid sponging to lower fever immediately"]
  B --> C["Provide frequent sips of ORS and coconut water"]
  C --> D["Monitor fetal movements and check for bleeding"]
  D --> E["Do NOT use antibiotics without doctor's advice"]
  E --> F["Refer immediately to a facility with an Obstetrician"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever + Pregnancy | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Reduced baby kicks | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Bleeding | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe headache | Immediate Referral | Cold compress |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Typhoid in pregnancy is high-risk. It can lead to early labor or infection of the baby. Always treat in a hospital.

query: A person is getting better from Typhoid but now has a very painful and swollen gall bladder area (right side under ribs). What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pain in the upper right belly** (Cholecystitis)
- Yellowing of eyes (Jaundice)
- Fever returning with shivering
- Vomiting after eating anything fatty

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | This is where Typhoid bacteria 'hide'. Refer for urgent evaluation. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop all fatty/oily food**: Give only plain water, coconut water, or thin khichdi.
- **Apply a warm compress** to the painful area for 10 minutes.
- **Monitor for Jaundice**: Check if her eyes or urine are turning yellow.
- **Refer to CHC**: She may need an ultrasound and specific treatment to prevent the gall bladder from bursting.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Typhoid patient with right-side rib pain and fever"] --> B["Stop all oily, fatty, and heavy foods immediately"]
  B --> C["Apply warm compress to the painful area for 10 mins"]
  C --> D["Check eyes and urine for signs of yellowing"]
  D --> E["Provide only plain water and thin dal water"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for ultrasound and medical review"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe right-side pain | Immediate Referral | Warm compress |
| Yellow eyes/Jaundice | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever with chills | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Mild nausea | Refer to PHC | Zero-oil diet |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The gall bladder is the 'home' of Typhoid bacteria. If it gets infected, the patient can become a lifelong carrier or suffer a rupture.

query: A man in the village has been coughing for 3 weeks, has a fever every evening, and is losing a lot of weight. His spit has streaks of blood. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Coughing up large amounts of bright red blood**
- Severe chest pain
- **Extreme difficulty breathing or gasping**
- High fever with confusion
- Blue tint to lips or fingernails

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<12) | Any | Urgent referral; children get very sick quickly with TB. |
| Adult (12–60) | Any | Provide a mask, encourage fluids, and refer to PHC for sputum test. |
| Elderly (>60) | Any | High risk of weakness; arrange transport for testing immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Masking**: Give the person a clean cloth or mask to cover their mouth when coughing or sneezing.
- **Ventilation**: Keep the person in a room with open windows; fresh air moves the germs away.
- **Sputum Collection**: Advise them to collect their early morning 'balgam' (spit) in a clean container for the PHC.
- **Nutrition**: Provide high-protein local food like roasted Chana, eggs, or milk to help the body fight.
- **Isolation**: Advise the family to keep children and elderly away from the person until treatment starts.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Person coughing for >2 weeks with weight loss"] --> B["Provide a mask or clean cloth for coughing"]
  B --> C["Isolate from children and elderly family members"]
  C --> D["Encourage early morning sputum collection"]
  D --> E["Provide high-protein food and fresh air"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Sputum and X-ray testing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Coughing blood | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cough > 2 weeks | Refer to PHC for testing | Support with diet |
| Difficulty breathing | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Night sweats/Fever | Refer to PHC | Keep room airy |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB is highly contagious through the air. Only the full course of DOTS (Government treatment) can cure it. Local syrups will not work.

query: A patient started TB medicine (DOTS) but stopped after 1 month because he felt better. Now his cough is back and he is very weak. What are the steps?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Total inability to walk due to weakness**
- High fever that does not go down
- Severe weight loss (looking like 'skin and bones')
- Vomiting every time medicine is taken

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Critical Referral. Stopping TB medicine halfway makes the germs much stronger (MDR-TB). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Counseling**: Explain to the patient and family that stopping medicine halfway is more dangerous than never starting.
- **Nutrition**: Give **Haldi (Turmeric) milk** and **Jaggery** to provide strength for the harsh medicines.
- **Infection Control**: Ensure the patient is using a separate plate and glass for now.
- **Restart Protocol**: Do NOT let him take the old leftover tablets. He needs a fresh checkup and new medicines.
- **Immediate Referral**: Take him back to the TB supervisor or DOTS center today.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient stopped TB medicine early"] --> B["Explain danger of drug resistance to the family"]
  B --> C["Check for severe weight loss and fever"]
  C --> D["Provide energy-rich food like jaggery and milk"]
  D --> E["Collect all old medicine strips to show the doctor"]
  E --> F["Refer to DOTS center for fresh testing and restart"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stopped medicine | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Severe weakness | Immediate Referral | High protein diet |
| Returning cough | Refer for testing | Masking and fresh air |
| Yellow eyes (Jaundice) | Emergency Referral | Stop medicine and refer |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stopping TB treatment early leads to Multi-Drug Resistant TB, which is very hard and expensive to treat. The full course is mandatory.

query: A young girl has a large, hard, painful swelling on the side of her neck (Gaanth). She has a low fever. Is this TB?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Swelling (Lump) that is leaking pus**
- Difficulty swallowing or breathing
- Sudden weight loss
- Very high fever with the lump

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child/Young Adult | Any | Refer to PHC for 'Gaanth' (Lymph Node) TB checkup. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT squeeze or massage the lump.** This can cause the infection to spread.
- **Warm Compress**: Apply a clean, warm cloth to the swelling to help with the pain.
- **Check for other lumps**: Look under the armpits and in the groin area.
- Provide **Amla or Lemon water** to boost her immunity.
- **Refer for FNAC/Biopsy**: Only a needle test at the hospital can confirm if this is TB.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Hard, painful lump on neck with fever"] --> B["Prohibit squeezing or massaging the lump"]
  B --> C["Apply a clean warm compress for pain relief"]
  C --> D["Check for other lumps in armpits or groin"]
  D --> E["Identify any weight loss or evening fever"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for needle testing (FNAC)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lump in neck | Refer to PHC | Warm compress only |
| Pus leaking | Immediate Referral | Keep area clean |
| Fever | Refer with the lump | Tepid sponging |
| Difficulty swallowing | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB does not always affect the lungs; it can affect the neck, bones, and stomach. Lumps in the neck must always be tested.

query: A TB patient is complaining that the government medicine makes his urine look bright orange/red. He is scared and wants to stop. What should I tell him?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow eyes or yellow skin (Jaundice)**
- Dark brown 'cola-colored' urine
- Persistent vomiting or stomach pain
- Itchy skin or rashes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Patient on Rifampicin | Any | Counsel that orange urine is normal, but yellow eyes are an emergency. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Brutal Honesty**: Tell him clearly: "The orange color is just the medicine leaving your body. It is NOT blood. Do NOT stop the medicine."
- **Check for Jaundice**: Look at the white part of his eyes. If they are yellow, he must stop and see a doctor immediately.
- Give him **extra water** to drink to keep his kidneys clean.
- Advise him to take the medicine after a small meal if it causes mild nausea.
- **Refer only if** he has vomiting, rashes, or yellow eyes.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient scared of orange/red urine"] --> B["Explain that orange urine is a normal side effect"]
  B --> C["Check the whites of the eyes for yellowing (Jaundice)"]
  C --> D["Encourage drinking 8-10 glasses of water daily"]
  D --> E["Ensure he does not skip today's dose"]
  E --> F["Refer ONLY if yellow eyes or vomiting occur"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Orange urine | Never | Normal side effect |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Stop medicine and refer |
| Itchy skin | Refer to PHC | Keep skin clean |
| Mild nausea | Home care | Take medicine with food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Stopping TB medicine over harmless side effects like orange urine can lead to drug-resistant TB. Counseling is key.

query: A child in a house with a TB patient is looking very thin, has a small cough, and won't play. What should the ASHA worker do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever with a very stiff neck**
- Constant crying and vomiting
- Seizures or fitting
- Swelling on the back (Spine TB)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5 years) | Any | Highest Priority. All children under 5 in a TB house need preventive medicine. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Screen the child**: Ask the mother if the child has gained any weight in the last 3 months.
- Check for **swollen lumps** in the neck or armpits.
- Provide **protein-rich snacks** like peanuts and jaggery.
- **Refer to Pediatrician**: The child needs a Mantoux test or chest X-ray immediately.
- **Isoniazid (IPT)**: Ask the doctor about 'Preventive Therapy' for the child to stop TB from starting.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child living with TB patient is thin/weak"] --> B["Check for weight loss and swollen neck lumps"]
  B --> C["Look for signs of stiff neck or constant crying"]
  C --> D["Provide peanuts, eggs, and milk for strength"]
  D --> E["Isolate the child from the active TB patient's cough"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Mantoux test and IPT therapy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Contact with TB patient | Refer for screening | Mandatory for children |
| Weight loss | Refer to PHC | High calorie diet |
| Stiff neck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild cough | Refer for testing | Fresh air and nutrition |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB is the leading cause of death in children living with infected adults. Every child in the house MUST be tested, even if they seem okay.

query: A TB patient says his joints hurt so much he can't walk. His big toe is swollen and red. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden inability to move a limb**
- Severe swelling in multiple joints
- High fever with joint pain

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult on TB Meds | Any | Refer for Uric Acid check (side effect of Pyrazinamide). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Increase Water**: Tell him to drink 10-12 glasses of water to flush out the acids.
- **Cold Compress**: Apply a cold cloth to the painful joint to reduce swelling.
- Avoid 'red' foods like lentils (Dal) or meat for a few days.
- Do NOT stop the TB medicine without a doctor's order.
- **Refer to PHC**: He may need a temporary change in medicine or a painkiller.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient with severe joint pain/swelling"] --> B["Increase water intake to 3 liters per day"]
  B --> C["Apply cold compress to the swollen joint"]
  C --> D["Reduce intake of pulses and meat temporarily"]
  D --> E["Do NOT stop the anti-TB tablets"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for Uric Acid test and relief"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Swollen big toe/joints | Refer to PHC | Cold compress and water |
| Fever with pain | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Mild muscle ache | Home Care | Warm bath and hydration |
| Difficulty walking | Refer to PHC | Rest and fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Certain TB drugs can cause high Uric Acid (Gout). This is painful but treatable without stopping TB cure.

query: An old lady has a hunchback that appeared quickly and she has constant back pain and fever. She is very pale. What could this be?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Weakness or numbness in the legs**
- Loss of control over urine or stool
- Inability to stand up
- Sharp, localized pain in the spine

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly/Adult | Any | Immediate referral for Spine TB (Pott's Disease). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Prohibit Massage**: Tell the family NEVER to massage the back; it can break the spine and cause paralysis.
- **Hard Bed**: Have her lie on a firm, flat surface (like a wooden 'charpai' with a thin mat).
- Provide **Calcium-rich foods**: Milk, Ragi (finger millet), and small fish.
- Keep her as still as possible during transport.
- **Refer for X-ray/MRI**: Spine TB can lead to permanent paralysis if not treated early.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Back pain, hunchback, and fever"] --> B["Stop all local back massages immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay the patient on a firm, flat surface"]
  C --> D["Check for leg weakness or numbness"]
  D --> E["Provide milk and Ragi for bone strength"]
  E --> F["Refer to Orthopedic/TB specialist immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| New hunchback | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Leg weakness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Constant back pain | Refer for X-ray | Flat bed rest |
| Fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB of the spine is very common in India. If the bone collapses, the person will be paralyzed for life. Early medicine prevents this.

query: A TB patient is complaining of a 'pins and needles' feeling and burning in his feet and hands. What can I do at home?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Loss of balance while walking**
- Severe burning that prevents sleep
- Muscle wasting in hands/feet

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult on TB Meds | Any | Refer for Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine) deficiency check. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Dietary Boost**: Give foods rich in B-vitamins: Peanuts, whole grains, and green vegetables.
- **Foot Care**: Advise him to wear soft footwear and check for any injuries he might not feel.
- Gently **soak feet in cool water** for burning relief.
- Ensure he is taking the **small white tablet (B6)** usually given with TB meds.
- **Refer to PHC** to increase the dose of Vitamin B6.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient with burning/tingling in hands and feet"] --> B["Verify if he is taking Vitamin B6 daily"]
  B --> C["Add peanuts and green leafy vegetables to diet"]
  C --> D["Cool water soaks for temporary pain relief"]
  D --> E["Advise protective footwear for numb feet"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for B6 dose adjustment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Burning/Tingling | Refer to PHC | B-vitamin rich foods |
| Loss of balance | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Mild numbness | Refer for checkup | Foot hygiene |
| Hand weakness | Refer to PHC | Hand exercises |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Isoniazid (TB drug) causes Vitamin B6 deficiency. If not corrected, the nerve damage can become permanent.

query: A man has been coughing blood for 2 days. He is too scared to go to the hospital because he thinks he will be locked up. What should I say?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Large gushes of blood (Hemoptysis)**
- Cold, clammy skin
- Rapid, weak pulse
- Fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Critical Emergency - Internal bleeding risk. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop the Panic**: Tell him: "Hospital is for cure, not jail. If you stay home, you will bleed to death."
- **Positioning**: Have him lean forward or lie on the side that is hurting; this prevents blood from choking the other lung.
- **Do NOT** let him talk or walk; he must stay completely still.
- Provide **Ice water** to sip; cold helps shrink bleeding vessels.
- **Call 108** or arrange a vehicle immediately.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Coughing blood and refusing hospital"] --> B["Explain that bleeding at home is fatal"]
  B --> C["Position him leaning forward or on his side"]
  C --> D["Prohibit talking, walking, or physical effort"]
  D --> E["Give small sips of ice-cold water"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital with an ER"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Any blood in spit | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cold skin/Shock | Emergency Referral | Keep warm and still |
| Fear/Stigma | Counsel and Refer | Stay with patient |
| Chest pain | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Coughing up blood is a sign of a hole in the lung or a broken blood vessel. It can be fatal within minutes if not treated in a hospital.

query: A TB patient's wife is pregnant. Can she catch it? Can she take her husband's medicines if she starts coughing?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Pregnant woman coughing for >2 weeks**
- Evening fever and weight loss
- Heavy breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant Woman | Female | Immediate screening at PHC. TB is very dangerous for both mother and baby. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Separation**: The husband must sleep in a separate, well-ventilated area for the first 2 weeks of his treatment.
- **Do NOT share medicine**: TB medicine for pregnant women is specific; she must never take his tablets.
- **Masking**: Both should wear masks when in the same room.
- Boost her diet with **Iron and Protein**: Spinach, eggs, and milk.
- **Refer for Testing**: She needs a safe sputum test and a shielded X-ray (if required) at the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman living with a TB patient"] --> B["Ensure husband is isolated in a ventilated room"]
  B --> C["Provide masks for both husband and wife"]
  C --> D["Prohibit sharing of any anti-TB medications"]
  D --> E["Increase intake of iron and protein rich foods"]
  E --> F["Refer wife to PHC for urgent TB screening"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Contact with TB | Mandatory Screening | Isolate and mask |
| Coughing | Immediate Referral | Fresh air and rest |
| Weight loss | Immediate Referral | High nutrition |
| Normal health | Regular ANC checkups | Hygiene and masking |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Untreated TB in pregnancy leads to low birth weight, premature birth, and can even infect the baby. Treatment is safe during pregnancy but must be supervised.

query: A patient on TB treatment says he cannot see clearly and colors look 'faded'. He thinks he needs glasses. Is this normal?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sudden loss of vision**
- Difficulty distinguishing Red and Green colors
- Blurred vision that doesn't go away

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult on Ethambutol | Any | Emergency Referral - Drug-induced eye damage (Optic Neuritis). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Test at Home**: Show him a bright red cloth and a green leaf. Ask him to name the colors. If he can't, his eyes are affected.
- **Do NOT stop medicine** without a doctor's confirmation, but **Refer TODAY**.
- Tell him to stay in a **dimly lit room** if lights hurt his eyes.
- **Refer to Eye Specialist**: If the drug (Ethambutol) is not stopped/changed, he could become permanently blind.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient with blurred vision or color loss"] --> B["Perform simple red/green color test at home"]
  B --> C["Identify if vision loss is in one or both eyes"]
  C --> D["Do NOT change dose without medical order"]
  D --> E["Stay in low light if eyes are sensitive"]
  E --> F["Emergency referral to Eye Specialist and TB Doctor"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Faded colors | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Blurred vision | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Eye pain | Immediate Referral | Dim light rest |
| Normal vision | Routine checkup | Monthly eye check |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Ethambutol can cause damage to the eye nerve. This is reversible if caught early but leads to blindness if ignored.

query: A person has a cough and fever but is also an alcoholic. He says he will only take TB medicine if he can keep drinking. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Yellow eyes (Jaundice)**
- Vomiting blood
- Severe pain in the right side of the stomach
- Confusion or shaking hands

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult/Alcoholic | Male | Critical Counseling - Alcohol + TB medicine = Liver Failure. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Brutal Honesty**: Tell him: "If you drink and take these pills, your liver will burn and you will die within weeks. Choose the bottle or your life."
- Give **Sugarcane juice or Lemon water** to help the liver detoxify.
- **Supervision**: He must take the medicine in front of the ASHA worker (DOTS).
- Provide **Jaggery** to reduce the craving for alcohol.
- **Refer to CHC**: He may need medicine to help him quit alcohol safely while starting TB treatment.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Alcoholic patient refusing meds without drink"] --> B["Explain that alcohol + TB meds equals liver death"]
  B --> C["Provide sugarcane juice and jaggery for liver support"]
  C --> D["Enroll in strict DOTS for daily supervision"]
  D --> E["Check for yellowing of eyes and dark urine"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for alcohol de-addiction and TB start"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Alcoholism + TB | Immediate Referral | Strict supervision |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Stop and refer |
| Shaking hands | Refer for detox | Hydration |
| Liver pain | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Alcoholism is the biggest reason for TB treatment failure in India. The liver cannot handle both toxins at once.

query: The TB medicine is too big for the patient to swallow. Can I crush the tablets and give them in water?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting the medicine back up**
- Choking while swallowing
- Refusing to take meds due to size

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Consult TB Supervisor. Many DOTS tablets are 'fixed dose' and should not be crushed. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The Banana Trick**: Put the tablet inside a piece of soft banana; it slips down the throat easily.
- **Yogurt/Curd**: Have the patient take it with a spoonful of thick curd or 'malai'.
- **Check the strip**: If it is a 'Film Coated' or 'Sustained Release' tablet, **DO NOT CRUSH**. It will not work properly.
- If he vomits within 30 minutes, he must repeat the dose.
- **Refer to DOTS center**: They may have smaller sized or liquid versions for difficult cases.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient unable to swallow large TB tablets"] --> B["Try the banana or curd method to ease swallowing"]
  B --> C["Check if tablets are 'coated' - do NOT crush these"]
  C --> D["Ensure a full glass of water is taken with meds"]
  D --> E["If vomited within 30 mins, repeat the dose"]
  E --> F["Refer to DOTS center for alternate formulations"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Choking on meds | Refer for liquid meds | Banana/Curd method |
| Frequent vomiting | Refer to PHC | Take after light meal |
| Refusal to swallow | Refer for counseling | Supervision |
| Normal intake | Routine DOTS | Standard swallowing |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Crushing certain TB medications can make them ineffective or cause severe stomach irritation. Always check with the supervisor first.

query: A TB patient has been coughing for 1 month but also has a lot of loose motions and sores in his mouth. What should I check for?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme weight loss**
- Long-term diarrhea (>1 month)
- White patches in the mouth
- Skin rashes or boils

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Urgent Referral - TB is often a sign of underlying HIV infection. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Confidentiality**: Do not talk about your suspicion with others in the village.
- **ORS**: Give Salt-Sugar water to stop weakness from diarrhea.
- **Mouth Care**: Apply **Honey or Ghee** to mouth sores for temporary relief.
- **Encourage Testing**: Tell him: "Sometimes one illness hides another. We need a full blood check to cure you completely."
- **Refer to ICTC**: Take him to the 'Integrated Counseling and Testing Center' for an HIV test.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB signs + mouth sores + diarrhea"] --> B["Maintain strict privacy and confidentiality"]
  B --> C["Provide ORS for diarrhea and honey for mouth sores"]
  C --> D["Encourage a full blood checkup including HIV"]
  D --> E["Check for fever and weight loss history"]
  E --> F["Refer to ICTC center for confidential testing"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Mouth sores/Diarrhea | Refer to ICTC/PHC | ORS and Honey |
| Cough > 2 weeks | Refer for TB test | Masking |
| Severe weight loss | Refer to PHC | High calorie diet |
| Skin rashes | Refer to PHC | Keep skin clean |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB and HIV often go together (Co-infection). TB is the leading cause of death for people with HIV, but it is curable with medicine.

query: A village woman says her husband has TB but they are sleeping in the same room because of the winter. How can I keep the family safe?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Small children in the house coughing**
- Patient not using a mask
- Room smells 'stuffy' with no air

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Household Members | Any | Prevention via ventilation and sun. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sunlight**: Germs die in the sun. Put the patient's bedding and clothes in **direct sunlight** every day for 4 hours.
- **Cross-Ventilation**: Keep at least two windows or doors open on opposite sides, even in winter. Use blankets for warmth, not closed doors.
- **Cough Etiquette**: He must cough into a **thick cloth** that is washed in boiling water daily.
- **Masking**: He must wear a mask when talking to children or the wife.
- **Sputum Disposal**: He must spit into a bottle with a lid containing **Phenyle or Bleach**, not on the floor or outside.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Family living in one room with TB patient"] --> B["Place all bedding in direct sunlight for 4 hours daily"]
  B --> C["Ensure cross-ventilation by opening windows slightly"]
  C --> D["Instruct patient to cough only into a thick cloth"]
  D --> E["Provide a lidded spit bottle with disinfectant"]
  E --> F["Screen all family members at PHC for TB"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stuffy room | Advice on ventilation | Open windows |
| Sharing bed | Advice on separation | Use extra blankets |
| Spit on floor | Advise on spit bottle | Bleach cleaning |
| Family members cough | Mandatory Referral | Isolation |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB is not spread by touch or sharing food, only by air. Ventilation and sunlight are the best home-made 'vaccines' for the family.

query: A TB patient has severe stomach pain after taking his tablets. He says he feels like his stomach is on fire. Can he take 'Antacid' or milk?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting blood**
- Black, tarry stools
- Severe pain that makes him double over

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult on TB Meds | Any | Manage acidity without interfering with meds. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Timing**: Take the TB medicine 30 minutes **after** a light, non-spicy meal (like Poha or Khichdi).
- **Cold Milk**: Sipping cold milk can soothe the burning feeling.
- **Avoid Spices**: Stop all chilies, oil, and tea/coffee for now.
- **Antacids**: Do NOT take antacids at the same time as TB pills; they stop the TB medicine from working. Wait 2 hours.
- **Refer to PHC** if the pain continues; he may have a stomach ulcer.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient with severe stomach burning"] --> B["Advise taking meds after a non-spicy meal"]
  B --> C["Provide sips of cold milk for cooling effect"]
  C --> D["Prohibit chili, oil, and strong tea/coffee"]
  D --> E["Ensure 2-hour gap between antacids and TB pills"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if pain is constant or worsening"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Stomach fire | Home care trial | Cold milk/Bland food |
| Constant pain | Refer to PHC | Avoid spicy food |
| Black stools | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Vomiting blood | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB medicine is very strong and can irritate the stomach. Taking it on a totally empty stomach is not recommended if it causes severe pain.

query: An old man with TB is very confused and has started sleeping all day. He doesn't recognize his family. What is the emergency?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme drowsiness (cannot be woken up)**
- **Stiff neck**
- High fever
- Slow or irregular breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly | Any | Critical Emergency - Possible Brain TB (Meningitis). |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Safety**: Do NOT try to force-feed him or give water; he might choke.
- **Positioning**: Lay him on his side (recovery position).
- **Check for Fever**: Use cool water sponging if he is very hot.
- **Identify meds**: Collect all his TB and other medicine strips for the doctor.
- **Rush to the District Hospital**; Brain TB can kill or cause permanent brain damage in hours.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient is confused and drowsy"] --> B["Place in recovery position on his side"]
  B --> C["Do NOT give any food or water by mouth"]
  C --> D["Perform tepid sponging if fever is high"]
  D --> E["Gather all current medicine strips"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to District Hospital for Brain TB"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Drowsiness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Stiff neck | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Fever | Emergency Referral | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB germs can travel from the lungs to the brain. This is the most fatal form of TB. Immediate hospital care is the only chance.

query: A pregnant woman on TB medicine is worried about the baby. She heard the baby will be born with TB. Is it true?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bleeding during pregnancy**
- No fetal movement
- Mother has severe jaundice (yellow eyes)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant Woman | Female | Counseling - TB treatment is the best way to protect the baby. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Reassurance**: Tell her: "If you take the medicine, the baby will be born healthy. If you STOP, the baby will get infected."
- **Vitamin B6**: Ensure she takes Pyridoxine (B6) daily to protect her and the baby's nerves.
- **Breastfeeding**: Tell her that she *must* breastfeed the baby after birth, even while on TB meds.
- **Extra Nutrition**: Give her **Iron, Calcium, and extra Protein** (Paneer, Pulses, Eggs).
- **Refer for regular ANC**: She needs both a TB doctor and a Gynecologist.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant TB patient worried about baby"] --> B["Explain that medicine prevents baby from getting TB"]
  B --> C["Ensure she takes Vitamin B6 daily without fail"]
  C --> D["Provide diet rich in iron, calcium, and protein"]
  D --> E["Explain safety of breastfeeding while on treatment"]
  E --> F["Refer to both TB clinic and ANC clinic monthly"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pregnancy + TB | Routine Referral | High nutrition |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Stop and refer |
| Heavy cough | Refer to TB clinic | Masking/Fresh air |
| Fever | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
TB medicines used in India are generally safe for the baby. The risk of untreated TB is much higher than the side effects of the pills.

query: A person has a cough and night sweats. He has been taking a lot of 'Aayurvedic' leaf pastes and smoking 'Bidi' to cure the cough. What's the plan?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Weight loss despite eating**
- Difficulty breathing
- Coughing for more than 2 weeks

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Brutal Honesty - Herbs and smoking make TB worse, not better. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Smoking**: Tell him: "Smoking 'Bidi' is like pouring oil on a fire. It destroys your lungs and stops the medicine from working."
- **Herbs**: Tell him leaf pastes can be used *with* government medicine for comfort, but they will NEVER kill the TB germs.
- **Hydration**: Give him **warm water with Honey** for the cough instead of smoking.
- **Refer for Testing**: He must go for a Sputum test immediately at the PHC.
- **Counseling**: Explain that TB is a germ, not a 'bad wind' or curse; it needs real medicine.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient using smoking/herbs to treat cough"] --> B["Instruct patient to stop smoking 'Bidi' immediately"]
  B --> C["Explain that herbs do not kill TB germs"]
  C --> D["Provide honey and warm water for cough comfort"]
  D --> E["Encourage immediate sputum test at PHC"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for formal TB screening"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Smoking history | Refer for TB test | Stop smoking help |
| Using only herbs | Refer for medical care | Honey for throat |
| Cough > 2 weeks | Mandatory Referral | Masking |
| Night sweats | Refer to PHC | High protein diet |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Delayed diagnosis due to 'self-treatment' is the main reason TB becomes fatal. TB germs are only killed by specific antibiotics (DOTS).

query: A TB patient is getting very dark patches on his face and arms. He is embarrassed to go out. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Large peeling of skin (Blisters)**
- Yellow eyes
- Severe itching all over the body

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult on TB Meds | Any | Counsel on skin darkening as a common side effect of some TB drugs. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Sun Protection**: Tell him the medicine makes the skin sensitive to the sun. He must cover his face and arms with a **clean white cloth** when going out.
- **Moisturize**: Apply **Coconut oil** to the dark areas to keep the skin soft.
- **Reassurance**: Tell him: "The dark marks will slowly go away once the 6-month treatment is over. Your life is more important than the marks."
- **Refer to PHC** only if there is itching or if the skin starts to peel off.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["TB patient with dark skin patches"] --> B["Advise covering face/arms when in the sun"]
  B --> C["Apply coconut oil to keep skin soft"]
  C --> D["Explain it is a temporary medicine side effect"]
  D --> E["Check for severe itching or skin peeling"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if skin blisters or rashes occur"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Dark patches | Never (unless severe) | Sun protection/Oil |
| Severe itching | Refer to PHC | Clean clothes |
| Skin peeling | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Yellow eyes | Emergency Referral | Stop and refer |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Skin darkening is common with drugs like Clofazimine or sometimes Isoniazid. It is not dangerous, but stopping treatment because of it is a big mistake.

query: A man in the village has started passing loose motions that look like rice-water. He is vomiting and his eyes are sunken. What should I do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rice-water stools** (watery, white, and frequent)
- **Sunken eyes** and very dry mouth
- Skin stays in a pinch (loss of elasticity)
- **No urine passed in 6 hours**
- Extreme thirst or inability to drink

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (<5) | Any | High risk. Start ORS immediately and rush to PHC. |
| Adult | Any | Start continuous ORS and refer to isolation ward/CHC. |
| Elderly | Any | Rapid dehydration risk. Call 108 for IV fluids. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start ORS immediately**: Give one cup of ORS after every loose motion. Drink in small sips.
- **Homemade Solution**: If ORS is not there, mix 6 level teaspoons of sugar and 1/2 teaspoon of salt in 1 liter of boiled-then-cooled water.
- **Do NOT stop fluids** even if the person is vomiting; wait 10 minutes and try again slowly.
- **Isolate**: Keep the person away from the common kitchen and water source to prevent spread.
- **Refer immediately**; cholera can kill within hours due to water loss.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient has watery rice-water stools"] --> B["Start ORS or salt-sugar solution immediately"]
  B --> C["Check for sunken eyes and skin pinch test"]
  C --> D["Isolate the patient and wash hands with soap"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give plain water only; must have salt/sugar"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC/Hospital for IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rice-water stool | Immediate Referral | Start ORS while moving |
| Sunken eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No urine | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild diarrhea | PHC within 4 hours | Continuous ORS and Zinc |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cholera is a fast killer. First aid is ORS, but the cure is medical rehydration and antibiotics at a clinic.

query: Many people in the basti have watery diarrhea after the heavy rains. One child is very sleepy and won't wake up. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Drowsiness or unconsciousness**
- **Cold and clammy skin**
- Very weak or no pulse felt at the wrist
- **Bluish nails or lips**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Critical Emergency. Do not try to force-feed. Rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Lay the child on their side** (recovery position) to prevent choking if they vomit.
- Do **NOT** force water or ORS into the mouth of a sleepy/unconscious person.
- Keep the child **warm** with a blanket.
- **Clean the area**: Use bleach (diluted) to clean any stool/vomit to stop the spread to others.
- **Call 108/Ambulance** immediately for IV fluids; this is severe shock.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child is sleepy/unconscious with diarrhea"] --> B["Lay child on side in recovery position"]
  B --> C["Do NOT put any fluids in the mouth"]
  C --> D["Keep the child warm with a blanket"]
  D --> E["Notify health officials of multiple cases in area"]
  E --> F["Emergency transport for life-saving IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious | Critical Emergency | Never |
| Cold skin | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Multiple cases | Report to health worker | Isolate and clean |
| Liquid stool | Immediate Referral | ORS during transport |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
When multiple people have diarrhea, the water source is contaminated. Boil all drinking water for the entire village immediately.

query: The patient is vomiting everything I give. How do I give ORS if they can't keep it down?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Continuous vomiting**
- Inability to drink even sips
- Cramps in legs and stomach (loss of salt)
- Voice becoming hoarse or 'whispering'

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Small sips every 1-2 minutes. If vomiting persists, hospital IV is the only way. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The 10-Minute Rule**: If the person vomits, wait for 10 minutes to let the stomach settle.
- **Small Sips**: Use a small spoon. Give one spoonful of ORS every 2 minutes. Slow intake is better absorbed.
- **Leg Cramps**: Gently stretch the muscles and give sips of salty water/ORS.
- **Avoid 'Gripe Water' or Soda**: These do not help and can make the diarrhea worse.
- **Transfer to Hospital** if they cannot keep any fluid down for more than 1 hour.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient vomiting ORS and fluids"] --> B["Wait 10 minutes after the last vomit"]
  B --> C["Give one spoonful of ORS every 2 minutes"]
  C --> D["Check for leg cramps and sunken eyes"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give sugary sodas or plain water"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC if vomiting continues for 1 hour"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Constant vomit | Emergency Referral | Sips via spoon |
| Muscle cramps | Immediate Referral | Salt-rich fluids |
| Hoarse voice | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Thirst | Refer if stools continue | Continuous ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe vomiting in cholera leads to rapid kidney failure. Do not struggle at home if fluids are not staying down.

query: I am an ASHA worker. A mother is asking if she should stop breastfeeding her baby who has watery diarrhea. What to tell her?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Weak cry or no tears** when crying
- Sunken 'soft spot' (Fontanelle) on top of the head
- Baby is too weak to suckle
- Green or bloody stools (indicates other infection)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1) | Any | Continue breastfeeding frequently. Refer to pediatrician/PHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NEVER stop breastfeeding**: Breast milk is the safest fluid and helps fight the infection.
- **Give ORS** in between feeds using a clean spoon/cup.
- **Keep the baby warm** (Skin-to-skin contact).
- Wash the mother's hands with soap after every diaper/nappy change.
- **Rush to the hospital** if the baby becomes drowsy or stops breastfeeding.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Infant has watery diarrhea"] --> B["Instruct mother to continue breastfeeding frequently"]
  B --> C["Give small spoons of ORS between feeds"]
  C --> D["Check top of head for sunken soft spot"]
  D --> E["Wash hands with soap after every cleaning"]
  E --> F["Immediate referral to PHC/Pediatrician"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sunken soft spot | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Refusing breast | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No tears | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Watery stool | Immediate Referral | Breastfeed + ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infants with cholera dehydrate much faster than adults. They must be seen by a doctor even if they are still nursing.

query: A man has cholera and the family is using the same bucket for drinking water. What first aid for the family?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Multiple family members getting sick**
- Vomit or stool near the water source
- No soap used in the house

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Household | All | Disinfect water and isolate the sick person immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Boil Water**: Boil all drinking and cooking water for 20 minutes.
- **Chlorine**: Use chlorine tablets (Halazone) if available (1 tablet for 20 liters or as per pack).
- **Handwashing**: Everyone must wash hands with soap and *pukka* water before eating and after using the toilet.
- **Isolate the sick**: Use a separate pot for the patient's stool; pour bleach/lime powder over it before burying or flushing.
- Give **Zinc tablets** to children in the house (as per health worker guidance).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["One cholera case in household"] --> B["Boil all drinking water for 20 minutes"]
  B --> C["Separate patient's utensils and bedding"]
  C --> D["Ensure everyone washes hands with soap"]
  D --> E["Use bleach/lime on patient's waste"]
  E --> F["Refer new cases to isolation center immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| New diarrhea case | Immediate Referral | Isolation |
| Fever starts | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponging |
| Household prevention | Home Care | Boiling water/Hygiene |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cholera is highly contagious through water. One case is a warning for the whole village. Cleanliness is the only protection.

query: A villager is having muscle cramps and cold skin from diarrhea. Is there a natural remedy to stop the motions?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cold, clammy skin** (Shock)
- Severe muscle cramps (Legs/Arms)
- Rapid, weak pulse
- Watery diarrhea that won't stop

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Salt-sugar-lemon water for electrolytes. Immediate hospital transfer. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT try to 'stop' the motions** with local barks or chemicals; the body needs to flush the bacteria out. Focus only on **replacing the water**.
- Give **Coconut water** or **salty Lassi/Buttermilk** (if they are not vomiting).
- **Keep the patient warm**; cover them with a blanket during transport.
- Mix **Lemon, Sugar, and a pinch of Salt** in water—this is a natural ORS.
- **Rush to the CHC**; muscle cramps mean the body has lost critical salts (potassium/sodium).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Diarrhea with muscle cramps and cold skin"] --> B["Give salty fluids like buttermilk or lemon-salt water"]
  B --> C["Keep the person covered and warm"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use medicines to stop diarrhea"]
  D --> E["Monitor pulse and level of thirst"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC for IV minerals"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Muscle cramps | Emergency Referral | Salty drinks |
| Cold skin | Emergency Referral | Keep warm |
| Rapid motions | Immediate Referral | ORS/Coconut water |
| Mild thirst | PHC visit | ORS at home |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Stopping' the diarrhea with local medicine can trap the cholera toxin inside the body. Replacing fluids is the only safe way.

query: The patient's stomach is bloated and they have stopped passing motions but look even more sick. What is happening?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bloated, hard stomach**
- Sudden stop in diarrhea but patient is getting weaker
- Severe abdominal pain
- Inability to pass gas

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Critical Emergency - Suspected bowel complication. Stop all food/water and rush to hospital. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop giving anything by mouth** (even ORS) if the stomach is very bloated and they are vomiting green fluid.
- **Keep them in a side-lying position** with knees bent.
- Check for **fever**.
- **Rush to a District Hospital** with a surgeon; this is not normal cholera and could be a blocked or burst bowel.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Bloated stomach and sudden stop of diarrhea"] --> B["Stop all oral fluids and food immediately"]
  B --> C["Lay patient on side with knees bent"]
  C --> D["Check for fever or green vomiting"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give laxatives or 'churan'"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to a surgical hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bloated belly | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Green vomiting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No gas/stool | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Liquid stool | Immediate Referral | ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A sudden stop in cholera diarrhea followed by bloating is a sign of 'Paralytic Ileus' or bowel failure. This needs surgery or intensive care.

query: Can I give the person solid food like rice and dal while they have cholera?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Constant vomiting**
- Bloody stool (not cholera, could be dysentery)
- Severe weakness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Focus on soft, liquid-based foods once vomiting stops. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Wait for vomiting to stop** before giving any solid food.
- Give **soft, easy-to-digest food**: Khichdi (watery), mashed bananas, or curd rice.
- **Continue ORS** even when eating; food does not replace the need for salts.
- Avoid **oily, spicy, or very sugary foods** as they make diarrhea worse.
- If they cannot eat and are only drinking, they still must be **referred to a doctor**.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient asks for food during diarrhea"] --> B["Ensure vomiting has stopped for at least 2 hours"]
  B --> C["Provide watery khichdi or mashed bananas"]
  C --> D["Continue giving ORS after every motion"]
  D --> E["Avoid all spices, oils, and milk (except curd)"]
  E --> F["Refer if they cannot eat for more than 24 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Refusing all food | Refer to PHC | Offer small portions |
| Bloody stool | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Rice-water stool | Immediate Referral | Soft diet + ORS |
| Improving hunger | Home Care | Khichdi and Curd |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Food provides energy, but in cholera, it is the **water and salt** loss that kills. Fluids are always the first priority.

query: A woman has watery diarrhea and is very thirsty, but she is also 7 months pregnant. Any special care?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Reduced movement of the baby**
- **Cramping or contractions** in the belly
- Sunken eyes and severe thirst
- Dizziness or fainting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Pregnant | Female | High Risk Emergency. Start ORS and rush to a hospital with labor room. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Aggressive Rehydration**: Give ORS continuously. Dehydration can cause premature labor or kill the baby.
- **Lay her on her left side** while traveling to the hospital.
- Check for any **leaking fluid** or blood from the vagina.
- Give **Coconut water** for extra potassium.
- **Rush to CHC**; she likely needs IV fluids to protect the pregnancy.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Pregnant woman with watery diarrhea"] --> B["Give ORS immediately and continuously"]
  B --> C["Monitor for fetal movement and contractions"]
  C --> D["Position her on her left side during transport"]
  D --> E["Check for signs of labor or bleeding"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Watery stool + Pregnant | Emergency Referral | ORS during travel |
| Less baby movement | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Contractions | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild thirst | Immediate Referral | ORS and Coconut water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cholera in pregnancy is an emergency. Dehydration reduces the blood flow to the placenta, putting the baby at immediate risk.

query: How do I make the 'rice-water' motions stop quickly? The patient is passing it every 10 minutes.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Motions more than 10 times a day**
- Large volumes of watery stool
- Severe exhaustion (cannot sit up)
- Rapid breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | You cannot stop the motion; you must keep up with the loss. Emergency Referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT use 'Loperamide' or 'Rok' tablets** to stop motions; in cholera, this can cause the gut to burst or cause toxic shock.
- **The Replacement Rule**: For every bowl of motion passed, the person must drink at least 1.5 bowls of ORS.
- Use a **clean bucket/pot** to monitor the amount of fluid lost.
- Give **Zinc tablets** (20mg for children over 6 months) for 14 days to reduce future episodes.
- **Rush to the hospital** for an IV; if motions are every 10 minutes, drinking may not be enough.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Watery motions every 10-20 minutes"] --> B["Measure the amount of stool being passed"]
  B --> C["Replace loss with 1.5x the amount of ORS"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use 'stop-motion' medications"]
  D --> E["Provide Zinc tablets as per local guidance"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer for IV rehydration"] 
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Frequent motions (>10/day) | Emergency Referral | ORS replacement |
| Inability to keep up fluids | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Extreme weakness | Emergency Referral | Never |
| 3-4 motions a day | PHC visit | ORS and Zinc |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In cholera, the 'diarrhea' is the body's way of removing the toxin. Trying to stop the flow with medicine without treating the cause is dangerous.

query: A man with cholera has a very dry tongue and is not peeing for the last 10 hours. What to do?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No urine for 8–10 hours** (Kidney failure risk)
- Dry, coated tongue (like leather)
- Sunken eyes with no moisture
- Drowsiness or confusion

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Critical Medical Emergency. Total dehydration. Immediate IV fluids needed. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Start ORS immediately** while the vehicle is being arranged.
- Give **small sips of salty water** if ORS is finished.
- **Do NOT wait** even one more hour; the kidneys are shutting down.
- If he starts to breathe very fast or deep, he is in grave danger.
- **Rush to the nearest District Hospital** or CHC with 24-hour IV facilities.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["No urine for 10 hours + dry tongue"] --> B["Check for drowsiness or rapid breathing"]
  B --> C["Start giving ORS sips immediately"]
  C --> D["Arrange for emergency vehicle transport"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give plain water; only salt-sugar mix"]
  E --> F["Emergency transport to District Hospital"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No urine > 6 hours | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Dry/Leathery tongue | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Confusion | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Thirst with urine | Refer to PHC | Continuous ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Failure to pass urine is a sign that the body has no fluid left to spare. Without IV rehydration, this is fatal.

query: The patient is having severe leg pain and says they are 'burning' inside. He has watery diarrhea. How to help the pain?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Severe muscle cramps (Legs/Abdomen)**
- **Cold skin with 'burning' feeling inside**
- Sunken eyes
- Watery diarrhea

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Mineral replacement (Electrolytes) and immediate referral. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Salt and Potassium**: Give **Coconut water, salty Buttermilk, or Banana mash**. These help stop the 'burning' and cramps.
- **Massage**: Gently massage the cramped muscles with a dry cloth.
- **ORS**: Give ORS with a squeeze of fresh lemon.
- **Do NOT** use painkiller tablets (Ibuprofen/Diclofenac) as they can damage dehydrated kidneys.
- **Refer to hospital** for IV electrolytes (Ringer's Lactate).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Watery diarrhea with severe leg cramps"] --> B["Give coconut water or banana for potassium"]
  B --> C["Gently massage cramped muscles with dry cloth"]
  C --> D["Provide ORS with lemon juice"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give painkiller tablets like Ibuprofen"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for IV Ringer's Lactate"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Severe cramps | Immediate Referral | Coconut water |
| Burning sensation | Immediate Referral | Salty drinks |
| Skin pinch > 2 sec | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild ache | PHC visit | ORS and rest |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Muscle pain in cholera is caused by the loss of salts (electrolytes) through stool. Only replacing those salts will stop the pain.

query: An old lady has rice-water motions. Her skin stays up like a tent when I pinch it. What does this mean?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin 'tenting'** (Skin pinch takes > 2 seconds to go back)
- Sunken eyes
- Very weak pulse
- Drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Elderly (>60) | Any | Emergency Referral. High risk of heart/kidney failure from fluid loss. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate ORS**: Every minute counts. Give sips every 30 seconds.
- **Keep her warm**: Use blankets as dehydration makes the body cold.
- **Check pulse**: If you can't feel the pulse at the wrist, she is in severe shock.
- **Transport immediately** in a lying-down position with legs slightly raised.
- **Refer to District Hospital** for immediate IV support.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Elderly patient with skin tenting and diarrhea"] --> B["Start continuous sips of ORS immediately"]
  B --> C["Cover with blankets to maintain body heat"]
  C --> D["Monitor pulse at the wrist for weakness"]
  D --> E["Lay flat with legs elevated during transport"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Hospital for IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin pinch > 2 sec | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Weak pulse | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Rice-water stool | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild diarrhea | Immediate Referral | ORS + monitor |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
In the elderly, cholera can lead to sudden heart failure because they cannot handle the loss of minerals. Do not delay referral.

query: A person has cholera and there is vomit and stool all over the floor. How do I clean it so others don't get sick?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Stool/Vomit near water or food**
- No protective gear (gloves/shoes) used during cleaning
- Foul smell and flies in the area

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Caregiver | Any | Use bleach, wash hands, and isolate the area. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Use Bleach**: Mix 3 tablespoons of bleach in 1 liter of water. Pour it over the stool/vomit and leave for 10 minutes before cleaning.
- **Protect Yourself**: Use rubber gloves or plastic bags on hands. Use plastic/rubber slippers.
- **Wash Cloths**: Soak the patient's soiled clothes in boiling water or bleach solution for 30 minutes.
- **Wash Hands**: Use soap and scrub for 20 seconds after every cleaning task.
- **Refer the patient** to an isolation ward so the home environment stays clean.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cholera stool/vomit on the floor"] --> B["Mix 3 tbsp bleach in 1 liter water"]
  B --> C["Pour solution over waste and wait 10 minutes"]
  C --> D["Clean using gloves/plastic bags on hands"]
  D --> E["Soak soiled clothes in boiling water/bleach"]
  E --> F["Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Multiple stools | Refer for isolation | Deep cleaning with bleach |
| Soiled bedding | Wash with bleach | Never mix with family wash |
| Flies in room | Use mosquito nets | Keep room clean |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
One gram of cholera stool contains enough bacteria to infect millions. Hygiene is a medical necessity in an outbreak.

query: The patient's voice has become a thin whisper and they look like they are 'dying' from thirst. Watery diarrhea is still going.
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **'Cholera voice'** (Weak, hoarse whisper)
- Sunken eyes and hollow cheeks
- **Skin pinch takes many seconds** to return
- Inability to drink despite extreme thirst

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Critical Emergency - Severe Dehydration. Immediate IV fluids needed. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Give ORS with a spoon**; do not let them gulp large amounts as they might vomit.
- **Keep the airway clear** if they are very weak.
- Check for **weak pulse**.
- **Emergency Referral**: This person has lost about 10% of their body weight in water. They need Ringer's Lactate IV immediately.
- **Rush to District Hospital**; this stage is often followed by coma.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Patient has hoarse voice and sunken eyes"] --> B["Give ORS by spoon carefully to avoid vomit"]
  B --> C["Lay patient flat with legs elevated"]
  C --> D["Check pulse and breathing rate"]
  D --> E["Arrange for vehicle with space to lie down"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Hospital for IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Hoarse whisper | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Hollow cheeks | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Skin pinch > 2 sec | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Constant thirst | Emergency Referral | ORS by spoon |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The 'whispering voice' in cholera is a sign of extreme fluid loss affecting the vocal cords. It is a sign of impending collapse.

query: A child has cholera. I have no ORS packets. What can I make at home with village things?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sunken 'soft spot' on head**
- No urine for 6 hours
- **Child won't play or is very irritable**
- Skin pinch stays up

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child | Any | Start Homemade ORS and Zinc. Refer to PHC immediately. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Homemade ORS recipe**: 1 Liter boiled/cooled water + 6 level teaspoons of **Sugar** + 1/2 level teaspoon of **Salt**. Mix until dissolved.
- **Rice Water (Kanji)**: Give the water from boiled rice with a pinch of salt. This is very effective for cholera.
- **Zinc**: Give one 20mg Zinc tablet (crushed in milk/water) daily for 14 days.
- **Do NOT give only plain water**; it lacks the salt needed to keep the child alive.
- **Refer to PHC** to get professional ORS and check for complications.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child with watery diarrhea and no ORS"] --> B["Mix 1L water + 6 tsp sugar + 1/2 tsp salt"]
  B --> C["Give salty rice water (Kanji) as a fluid"]
  C --> D["Give daily 20mg Zinc tablet for 14 days"]
  D --> E["Check for sunken soft spot and skin pinch"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC/CHC for professional checkup"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No ORS packets | Refer to PHC | Make homemade solution |
| Sunken eyes | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Irritable/Weak | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Watery stool | Immediate Referral | Kanji and ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Homemade ORS must be measured correctly. Too much salt can be dangerous for children. Always follow the '6 sugar, 1/2 salt' rule.

query: ASHA worker reporting: A man with watery motions has a pulse that I can barely feel. He is very cold. What should I do during the 1-hour drive to the hospital?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Weak or thready pulse**
- **Cold, clammy skin**
- Unconscious or confused
- Rapid, shallow breathing

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | Critical Shock. Elevation of legs, warmth, and spoon-feeding ORS. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Elevate Legs**: Keep the patient lying flat with legs raised 1 foot high (Trendelenburg position) to keep blood in the heart.
- **Keep Warm**: Cover with blankets. Use a heater if available in the vehicle.
- **Sips only**: If conscious, give tiny sips of ORS every minute. If unconscious, do not give anything.
- **Airway**: Ensure the tongue is not blocking the throat.
- **Drive fast**: He needs IV fluids within minutes to survive.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Weak pulse and cold skin in cholera patient"] --> B["Lay patient flat and raise legs 1 foot high"]
  B --> C["Cover with blankets to maintain warmth"]
  C --> D["Give tiny sips of ORS only if fully conscious"]
  D --> E["Monitor breathing and pulse constantly"]
  E --> F["Emergency transfer to CHC/Hospital with IV capacity"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Weak pulse | Emergency Referral | Elevate legs |
| Cold skin | Emergency Referral | Keep warm |
| Unconscious | Critical Emergency | Side-lying position |
| Watery stool | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
A weak pulse in cholera is 'Decompensated Shock'. This is the stage just before death. Immediate IV rehydration is the only cure.

query: Can we use a piece of ginger or local herbs to stop the vomiting in cholera?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting more than 3 times an hour**
- Green or yellow bile in vomit
- Severe stomach pain
- Inability to drink fluids

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Home remedies for comfort are okay, but medical anti-emetics are needed if it persists. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Ginger**: A small piece of ginger or **Ginger juice with honey** can help settle mild nausea.
- **Wait and Spoon**: If vomiting is frequent, wait 10 minutes then give ORS by spoon (5ml at a time).
- **Lemon water**: Smelling a cut lemon can sometimes reduce the urge to vomit.
- **Do NOT** give any heavy herbal pastes that the patient might choke on.
- **Refer to hospital** if they vomit more than 10 times in 2 hours; they need an injection (Ondansetron) to stop it.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Vomiting during watery diarrhea episode"] --> B["Wait 10 minutes after vomiting stops"]
  B --> C["Try ginger juice or smelling a cut lemon"]
  C --> D["Give ORS by spoon very slowly (5ml sips)"]
  D --> E["Check for sunken eyes or skin pinch test"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC if vomiting prevents fluid intake"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Projectile vomiting | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Cannot keep ORS down | Emergency Referral | Spoon-feeding sips |
| Mild nausea | PHC visit | Ginger/Lemon water |
| Vomiting stopped | Home Care | ORS and Kanji |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Vomiting is dangerous in cholera because it prevents oral rehydration. If it doesn't stop, the patient will dehydrate even if they are trying to drink.

query: A person has cholera and is also having a very high fever (103 F). Is this normal?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Fever > 102°F with diarrhea**
- Shivering or chills
- Bloody or green stool
- Confusion or drowsiness

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Age | Any | Referral for suspected mixed infection (Typhoid/Malaria) or Sepsis. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Tepid Sponging**: Use room-temperature water to wipe the forehead, neck, and armpits to lower the fever.
- **ORS**: Fever makes dehydration worse; give extra fluids.
- **Keep separate**: Fever with diarrhea can spread quickly if it's typhoid or cholera.
- **Do NOT give Aspirin**; use Paracetamol only if the patient can swallow safely.
- **Refer to CHC** for blood tests and correct antibiotics (like Azithromycin).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Diarrhea with high fever (103 F)"] --> B["Perform tepid sponging to lower temperature"]
  B --> C["Increase ORS intake for extra fluid loss"]
  C --> D["Check for blood or pus in the stool"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give Aspirin; use Paracetamol if needed"]
  E --> F["Refer to CHC for blood tests and antibiotics"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever > 102 F | Immediate Referral | Tepid sponging |
| Shivering | Immediate Referral | Warm fluids |
| Bloody stool | Immediate Referral | Never |
| Mild fever | PHC within 4 hours | ORS and Paracetamol |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Classical cholera usually does **not** cause high fever. High fever with watery diarrhea suggests another serious infection like Typhoid or Malaria.

query: I am in a remote village with a cholera outbreak. I have no vehicle. How can I save the patients?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Multiple people with rice-water stool**
- Sunken eyes in children
- Weak adults

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Community | All | Establish a local 'ORS Center' and disinfect water. |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **ORS Center**: Set up a clean station where people can get free homemade salt-sugar solution and Kanji (rice water).
- **Aggressive Drinking**: Force patients to drink 1 glass of ORS after *every* motion.
- **Village Lockdown**: Stop people from using the common well/pond; boil all water from it for 20 minutes.
- **Zinc Distribution**: Give Zinc to all children in the area immediately.
- **Send a runner**: Send someone to the nearest CHC to bring back a medical team with IV kits.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Cholera outbreak in remote village"] --> B["Set up station for Salt-Sugar-Water and Rice Water"]
  B --> C["Boil all village drinking water for 20 minutes"]
  C --> D["Measure stool loss and replace with 1.5x fluids"]
  D --> E["Isolate the sick and use bleach on waste"]
  E --> F["Send a runner to CHC for medical assistance"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sunken eyes | Emergency Referral | Continuous ORS |
| Multiple cases | Report to health dept | Boil water/Isolate |
| Vomiting | Emergency Referral | Sips by spoon |
| Liquid stool | Immediate Referral | Salt-Sugar-Water |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Even without a vehicle, most cholera patients (80%) can be saved by oral fluids alone. Only the most severe cases need an IV. Keep them drinking!

Query: Didi, a 2-year-old child in the basti has passed water-like stools 6 times since morning. His eyes look sunken. What should I do immediately?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Sunken eyes or no tears when crying
- Very dry mouth and tongue
- Child is very sleepy or unconscious
- Blood in the stool
- Not able to drink or breastfeed
- Persistent vomiting

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infants (<6 mo) | Any | Continue exclusive breastfeeding more frequently; seek immediate referral if drinking poorly |
| Children (6 mo - 5 yrs) | Any | Give 50-100ml ORS after every loose stool; start Zinc syrup (20mg) for 14 days |
| Adults | Any | Give 200-400ml ORS or home fluids (Lassi, Lemon water) after every stool; stay hydrated |
| Pregnant | Female | High risk of dehydration; start ORS and refer to PHC for monitoring |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- Start **ORS (Oral Rehydration Salts)** immediately; if not available, use Salt-Sugar solution (6 level spoons sugar + 1/2 spoon salt in 1L water)
- Give small sips frequently, even if the person is vomiting
- Give **Zinc supplementation** (20mg for kids >6 months) to reduce duration
- Use **Rice water (Kanji)**, Dal water, or Buttermilk with a pinch of salt
- Continue feeding soft, mashed foods like Khichdi or Bananas
- Do **NOT** give plain water only; it lacks essential salts

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
A["Patient has 3 or more loose stools"] --> B["Check for sunken eyes and skin pinch test"]
B --> C["Start ORS and home fluids immediately"]
C --> D["Give Zinc tablet/syrup for 14 days"]
D --> E["Monitor for danger signs like blood or vomiting"]
E --> F["If condition worsens or child is lethargic, call 108"]
```
