| Vision loss | Emergency referral | Lead poisoning or infection |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Shared Kajal sticks are the #1 way eye infections spread in Indian villages. Throw them away if used during infection.

query: A farmer was hit by a sugarcane leaf in his eye. Now it is red and has a white spot. What is the remedy?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **White spot** in the center of the black part
- Pain that is deep and throbbing
- Vision is very low
- Discharge that looks like jelly

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult | Any | **Emergency Referral**; do NOT use home remedies |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **NO HOME REMEDIES:** Leaf injuries often carry **Fungus**. Putting honey or milk will make the fungus eat the eye.
- **Cover:** Tape a clean paper cup or eye shield over the eye so nothing touches it.
- **Fast Transport:** This is a Corneal Ulcer. It can destroy the eye in 48 hours. Go to an eye hospital now.
- **No Rubbing:** Rubbing will make the leaf scratch deeper.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Eye injury by leaf/branch with white spot"] --> B["DO NOT put any home drops or remedies"]
  B --> C["Cover eye with a clean paper cup shield"]
  C --> D["Do NOT rub or touch the eye"]
  D --> E["Transport to Eye Hospital immediately"]
  E --> F["Tell the doctor it was a leaf injury (Fungal risk)"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Leaf injury + White spot | Emergency referral | NEVER treat at home |
| Sharp pain after injury | Emergency referral | Risk of ulcer |
| Scratch with no white spot | Visit PHC | Monitor every 2 hours |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Vegetation (leaves/sticks) injuries are high-risk for fungal ulcers. Standard antibiotic drops from a local shop won't work. See a specialist.

query: A person has very red eyes and says everything looks like it has a 'rainbow' around it. Help?
answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- Rainbow halos around lights
- Severe headache and nausea/vomiting
- Eye feels hard as a stone
- Vision is foggy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Adult/Elderly | Any | **Emergency Referral**; stop all home care |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Wash:** Washing will not help. This is a sign of dangerously high pressure (Glaucoma).
- **Identify:** Rainbow halos + Hard eye + Headache = Glaucoma attack.
- **No Darkness:** Do not stay in a dark room; keep the lights on (this can sometimes help lower pressure slightly).
- **Refer:** This patient will be permanently blind within 24-48 hours if pressure is not lowered by a doctor.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Red eye with 'Rainbow Halos' around lights"] --> B["Check for severe headache and vomiting"]
  B --> C["Gently feel if eyeball is hard as a stone"]
  C --> D["Keep lights on and do NOT use home drops"]
  D --> E["Transport to Eye Hospital immediately"]
  E --> F["Monitor for sudden total vision loss"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Rainbow halos | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Stone-hard eyeball | Emergency referral | Never treat at home |
| Simple redness | Monitor at home | Hygiene only |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rainbow halos are a major warning sign of an Acute Glaucoma Attack. This is not an infection; it is a pressure emergency.

Query: A 2-year-old child in my village has been having very watery diarrhea. Now he is very sleepy and won't wake up properly. His eyes look deep and sunken. What should I do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Child is abnormally sleepy or unconscious**
- **Sunken eyes**
- **Cannot drink** or drinks very poorly
- Skin pinch goes back **very slowly** (more than 2 seconds)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (1-5 years) | Any | Give sips of ORS if conscious, call 108, and rush to CHC/Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Consciousness**: If the child is floppy or won't wake, do not force liquids into the mouth.
- **Sips of Fluids**: If the child can swallow, give Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) or 'Namak-Chini Ghol' immediately.
- **Keep Warm**: Cover the child with a dry cloth during transport.
- **Referral**: This is a medical emergency. The child needs IV fluids at a hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess if child is lethargic or unconscious"] --> B["Check for sunken eyes and dry mouth"]
  B --> C["Perform skin pinch test on abdomen"]
  C --> D["If skin stays peaked for >2 seconds, call 108"]
  D --> E["Provide sips of ORS only if child is awake enough to swallow"]
  E --> F["Rush to the nearest hospital for IV rehydration"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Lethargic/Unconscious | Refer Immediately | Never |
| Sunken eyes | Refer Immediately | Never |
| Skin pinch >2 seconds | Refer Immediately | Never |
| Active, drinking well | Monitor at PHC | Treat with ORS and Home Fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Severe dehydration is life-threatening. Home remedies are only for support during transport; hospital IV fluids are mandatory.

Query: A 10-month-old baby has stopped nursing and the 'soft spot' on the head is dipping in. The mother says the baby hasn't passed urine since morning. Help.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sunken fontanelle** (soft spot on head is dipping)
- **No urine** for more than 6-8 hours
- **Refusing to breastfeed**
- Very dry mouth and tongue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1 year) | Any | Continue to offer breastmilk, give sips of ORS, rush to SNCU/Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Frequent Nursing**: Encourage the mother to keep trying to breastfeed every 10 minutes.
- **ORS**: Give small spoons of ORS or Coconut water if the baby takes it.
- **Check Warmth**: Feel the hands and feet. If cold, wrap the baby skin-to-skin (Kangaroo Care).
- **Immediate Move**: Take the baby to a facility with a Pediatrician or Neonatal unit.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Observe sunken soft spot on head"] --> B["Check if baby has passed urine in 6 hours"]
  B --> C["Try frequent breastfeeding and small spoons of ORS"]
  C --> D["Check for cold hands and feet"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give plain water to an infant under 6 months"]
  E --> F["Transport to District Hospital or SNCU immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sunken soft spot | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No urine for 6+ hours | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Refusing breastmilk | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild diarrhea, nursing well | PHC Visit | Home care with ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Infants dehydrate much faster than adults. A sunken soft spot is a critical warning sign that requires professional medical intervention.

Query: A school-going girl is vomiting everything and has watery stools like 'rice water'. She is very thirsty but throws up the moment she drinks. What is the action?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Vomiting everything** (cannot keep any liquid down)
- **Rice-water stools** (very frequent and white/cloudy)
- **Extreme thirst**
- Weak or fast pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (5-12 years) | Female | Give tiny sips of ORS every minute, rush to CHC for IV fluids |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Slow Rehydration**: Give 1 teaspoon of ORS every 2 minutes. Do not let her gulp a full glass.
- **Natural Fluids**: Use salted Rice Kanji (water from boiled rice) or thin Buttermilk (Chaas).
- **Position**: Keep her sitting up or lying on her side to prevent choking if she vomits.
- **Referral**: If she cannot keep fluids down for 2 hours, she needs an injection for vomiting and IV fluids.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify persistent vomiting and rice-water stools"] --> B["Give one teaspoon of ORS every 2 minutes"]
  B --> C["Monitor for extreme thirst or dry tongue"]
  C --> D["Use salted rice water or buttermilk as natural alternatives"]
  D --> E["If vomiting continues, stop all solid food"]
  E --> F["Transfer to CHC/Hospital for IV rehydration"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cannot keep any fluids down | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Rice-water stools | Refer to CHC | Only if drinking well |
| Cramps in legs | Refer to PHC | Give salted liquids |
| Occasional vomiting | Monitor at PHC | Home care with ORS/Kanji |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
'Rice-water' stools can lead to shock very quickly. If vomiting prevents drinking, home care will fail. Rush to the hospital.

Query: An ASHA worker asks: I checked a child's skin on the stomach. It stayed up like a tent for 3 seconds. The child is drinking greedily. What level is this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin pinch stays up (Tenting)** for >2 seconds
- **Drinking greedily/eagerly**
- Irritability or restlessness
- Sunken eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Child | Any | Give Zinc tablet (20mg), start intensive ORS, and refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate ORS**: Allow the child to drink as much ORS as they want.
- **Zinc Therapy**: If you have Zinc tablets, dissolve one in clean water/milk and give it now.
- **Home Fluids**: Give Coconut water, Lemon water with salt, or Dal-ka-Paani.
- **Monitor**: Check the skin pinch every 30 minutes. If it doesn't improve, rush to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Perform skin pinch test on abdomen"] --> B["Observe if skin takes >2 seconds to flatten"]
  B --> C["Give as much ORS as the child wants to drink"]
  C --> D["Administer 20mg Zinc tablet immediately"]
  D --> E["Check for sunken eyes and restlessness"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC/CHC for professional assessment and monitoring"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Skin pinch >2 seconds | Refer to CHC | Start ORS during transport |
| Drinking greedily | Refer to PHC | Provide unlimited ORS |
| Restless/Irritable | Refer to PHC | Home fluids + Zinc |
| Skin pinch <1 second | Home Care | ORS + Food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Skin tenting is a sign of 'Some' to 'Severe' dehydration. Always refer to a medical officer to confirm the child is not slipping into shock.

Query: A child has had 10 watery motions since morning. There is no ORS packet in the village. What can we make at home that is safe?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Very dry mouth and tongue**
- **No tears** when crying
- Fast, shallow breathing
- Very weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Make Home-ORS, continue breastfeeding, refer if no improvement |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Home-ORS Recipe**: Mix **6 level teaspoons of sugar** and **half a level teaspoon of salt** in 1 liter of clean water.
- **Natural Alternatives**: Give Rice Water (Kanji), Coconut Water, or salted Buttermilk (Chaas).
- **Zinc**: Give one Zinc tablet daily for 14 days (if available in the kit).
- **Referral**: If the child's condition does not improve in 4 hours or stools become bloody.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Confirm 6+ watery motions in a day"] --> B["Mix 1 liter water with 6 tsp sugar and 1/2 tsp salt"] --> C["Give small frequent sips of the solution"]
  C --> D["Continue offering soft foods like banana or khichdi"]
  D --> E["Monitor for urine output and activity levels"]
  E --> F["If child becomes drowsy or stops peeing, rush to CHC"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| 10+ motions in 24 hours | Refer to PHC | Start Home-ORS |
| No tears when crying | Refer to PHC | Increase fluids |
| Blood in stool | Refer to CHC | Never treat at home |
| Passing urine normally | Monitor at home | Home-ORS + Zinc |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Homemade solutions must be measured accurately. Too much salt is dangerous. Refer to the hospital if diarrhea is very frequent.

Query: A child is very weak from diarrhea and is now having a fit (seizure). What is the first-aid for this?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Seizures (Fits)**
- **Unconsciousness**
- Blue lips or nails
- Cold, clammy skin

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Place in recovery position, do not put anything in mouth, rush to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Recovery Position**: Lay the child on their left side with the head tilted back slightly.
- **No Spoons/Water**: Do **NOT** put a spoon, cloth, or water in the mouth during a fit.
- **Clear the Area**: Move away any sharp objects.
- **Immediate Referral**: Fits in a dehydrated child mean the brain is affected. This is a critical emergency (Shock/Electrolyte crisis).

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Child starts having a seizure/fit"] --> B["Lay child on their side in recovery position"]
  B --> C["Do NOT restrain movement or put objects in mouth"]
  C --> D["Check if child is breathing properly"]
  D --> E["Wipe skin with lukewarm water if high fever is present"]
  E --> F["Call 108 and transport to District Hospital immediately"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Seizures/Fits | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Unconscious | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Cold skin/Blue lips | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Drowsy but awake | Refer to CHC | Give ORS sips during transport |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Seizures during dehydration indicate a life-threatening complication. Do not wait; oxygen and IV medications are needed.

Query: A toddler has diarrhea and a very high fever (103°F). He is crying constantly and looks very dry. What should be done first?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **High fever with diarrhea**
- **Extreme irritability**
- No urine for 6 hours
- Dry tongue

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Toddler (1-3 years) | Any | Give Paracetamol (ASHA dose), offer sips of ORS, refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Tepid Sponging**: Wipe the body with a cloth dipped in lukewarm water to bring down the fever.
- **ORS**: Give small amounts of ORS frequently. Fever makes dehydration worse.
- **Zinc**: Give Zinc (20mg) tablet.
- **Referral**: Fever with dehydration often means a serious infection. Needs a doctor's check-up.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check child for high fever and dry tongue"] --> B["Perform tepid sponging with lukewarm water"]
  B --> C["Give age-appropriate dose of Paracetamol"]
  C --> D["Offer ORS or salted Shikanji frequently"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any signs of seizures or lethargy"]
  E --> F["Transport to PHC for infection assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fever >102°F with diarrhea | Refer to PHC | Tepid sponge at home |
| Constant crying/pain | Refer to PHC | Give ORS |
| Child becomes floppy | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Mild fever, active | Home Care | Paracetamol + ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
High fever increases fluid loss through sweat and breathing. Always treat the fever and the dehydration together.

Query: The village medicine man gave 'Kada' for a child's diarrhea, but now the child's stomach is bloated and he isn't passing stool or urine. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Bloated/Swollen stomach**
- **No stool and no urine**
- Severe abdominal pain
- Lethargy

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Stop all local kadas/herbs, do not give solid food, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Herbal Remedies**: Some local 'kadas' can cause the intestines to stop working (Ileus).
- **Check Breathing**: If the child is breathing fast with a bloated stomach, it is a danger sign.
- **No Laxatives**: Do not give anything to 'force' a stool.
- **Referral**: A bloated stomach in a dehydrated child is a major surgical or medical emergency.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify bloated stomach and lack of urine"] --> B["Stop all local herbal drinks and solid food"]
  B --> C["Assess breathing rate and pulse quality"]
  C --> D["Place child in a comfortable position"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give any further local medicine"]
  E --> F["Rush to a facility with an X-ray or Surgeon"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Bloated stomach | Emergency Referral | Never |
| No urine/stool for 8 hours | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Severe tummy pain | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Simple loose motions | PHC Visit | ORS + Zinc |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Abdominal distension (bloating) during diarrhea can be a sign of potassium loss or intestinal blockage. This cannot be treated at home.

Query: A mother is asking: My child has diarrhea. Should I stop giving him milk and food until it stops? He is very weak.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Extreme weakness** (cannot sit up)
- **Sunken eyes**
- Refusing to eat or drink

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Any Child | Any | Continue feeding, give ORS, and refer if weakness increases |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Do NOT Starve**: Starving makes the child weaker and the diarrhea last longer.
- **Breastfeeding**: Continue breastfeeding more frequently than usual.
- **Soft Foods**: Give mashed banana, soft rice (Khichdi) with a little ghee, or boiled potato.
- **ORS**: Give ORS after every watery stool.
- **Referral**: If the child is too weak to eat or swallow, they need a hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Counsel mother NOT to stop feeding the child"] --> B["Continue breastfeeding or give soft, mashed foods"]
  B --> C["Give small sips of ORS after every motion"]
  C --> D["Monitor for signs of sunken eyes or lethargy"]
  D --> E["Avoid sugary juices or spicy oily foods"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if child refuses to eat or drink for 6 hours"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Refusing all food/drink | Refer to PHC | Start ORS |
| Too weak to sit | Refer to CHC | Continue breastfeeding |
| Losing weight rapidly | Refer to PHC | Give energy-dense food |
| Eating but loose stools | Monitor at Home | ORS + Normal Food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Continued feeding is essential for recovery. A child needs nutrients to repair the gut and regain strength.

Query: A child has diarrhea and his hands and feet feel very cold and his pulse is very fast. His skin is pale. What is the emergency step?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Cold hands and feet**
- **Fast and weak pulse**
- Pale or bluish skin
- **Unconsciousness**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Keep warm, raise the legs, call 108, and rush to hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Keep Warm**: Wrap the child in blankets or warm clothes.
- **Raise Legs**: While lying down, lift the child's legs higher than the heart to help blood reach the brain.
- **ORS**: Only give sips if the child is fully awake. If drowsy, do not give anything by mouth.
- **Emergency Move**: This child is in 'Shock'. He needs IV fluids immediately to survive.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify cold extremities and fast pulse"] --> B["Lay child flat and raise legs 1 foot high"] --> C["Wrap child in blankets to maintain warmth"]
  C --> D["Do NOT force water if child is drowsy"]
  D --> E["Call 108 or local transport immediately"]
  E --> F["Rush to hospital for emergency IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Cold hands/feet | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Fast/Weak pulse | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Pale/Mottled skin | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Warm skin, thirsty | Refer to PHC | Give ORS sips |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Cold extremities with a fast pulse mean the body's circulation is failing (Shock). This is a critical life-threat.

Query: ASHA worker reporting: A child has sunken eyes and hasn't peed, but the mother says he is 'only drinking a little bit of water'. No diarrhea right now, but had it all night.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Sunken eyes**
- **No urine** for 6+ hours
- **Unable to drink** enough to match losses
- Dry mouth

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Start intensive ORS (spoon by spoon), rush to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Intensive ORS**: Even if diarrhea stopped, the child is still empty. Give 5-10 spoons of ORS every 5 minutes.
- **Check Weight**: If you have a scale, check the weight and compare it to previous records.
- **Home Care**: Give coconut water or salted rice water if the child refuses ORS.
- **Referral**: If the child doesn't pee within 2 hours of starting intensive ORS, take him to the CHC.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify sunken eyes and no urine output"] --> B["Start intensive rehydration with ORS spoons"] --> C["Monitor for urine output over the next 2 hours"]
  C --> D["Continue offering fluids even if diarrhea has stopped"]
  D --> E["If no urine is passed after 2 hours of fluids"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for kidney function check and IVs"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Sunken eyes | Refer to PHC | Start ORS |
| No urine for 6 hours | Refer to CHC | Start intensive ORS |
| Refusing all fluids | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Peeing, active | Monitor at Home | Give extra fluids |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Dehydration can persist after diarrhea stops. No urine output is a sign that the kidneys are struggling.

Query: A child has had very thin motions and is now breathing very fast, like he has run a race, but he is just lying there. No cough. What's wrong?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Rapid, deep breathing** (Acidotic breathing)
- **No cough or chest indrawing** (distinguishes from pneumonia)
- **Extreme weakness**
- Very dry mouth

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Give ORS sips, rush to hospital immediately |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Breathing**: If the child is 'panting' but the chest isn't sucked in (like in a cold), it is likely because the blood has become acidic from dehydration.
- **ORS**: Give small sips of ORS if he can swallow.
- **No Steam/Vicks**: This is not a breathing problem; do not waste time with chest remedies.
- **Immediate Referral**: This child needs IV fluids and Soda-Bicarbonate treatment at a hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify rapid breathing in a child with diarrhea"] --> B["Check for cough or fever - if absent, suspect acid buildup"]
  B --> C["Give small sips of ORS during transport"]
  C --> D["Do NOT use asthma pumps or chest rubs"]
  D --> E["Keep child's head elevated slightly"]
  E --> F["Rush to District Hospital for blood acid correction"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Fast 'panting' breathing | Emergency (108) | Never |
| No cough, but fast breath | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Sunken eyes | Emergency Referral | Start ORS |
| Normal breathing | Refer to PHC | Home care with ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Rapid breathing in a child with diarrhea is a sign of 'Metabolic Acidosis'. It is a sign of near-death from dehydration.

Query: A child has diarrhea and the mother says the child's hands keep twitching or shaking. He is awake but seems 'lost'. Is this serious?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Twitching or shaking hands**
- **Confusion** (seems 'lost' or doesn't recognize mother)
- **Irritability**
- Sunken eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Give ORS sips, avoid loud noises, rush to CHC/Hospital |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Salt-Sugar Solution**: Twitching is often due to low salts (Calcium or Sodium). Give ORS immediately.
- **Calm Environment**: Keep the child in a quiet, shaded place.
- **No Solids**: Stick to liquids until the twitching stops.
- **Referral**: This child needs a blood test to check salt levels and likely an IV drip.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify hand twitching and confusion"] --> B["Immediately offer ORS or salted Lemon water"] --> C["Keep the child in a quiet and calm area"]
  C --> D["Check for any signs of a full seizure"]
  D --> E["Do NOT give plain water only; must have salt"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC for electrolyte assessment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Shaking/Twitching | Refer to CHC | Give ORS |
| Confusion/Dazed | Refer to CHC | Never treat at home |
| Extreme thirst | Refer to PHC | Provide ORS |
| Active, no shaking | Monitor at PHC | Home care with ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Twitching and confusion are signs of electrolyte imbalance. If left untreated, this can progress to a full coma.

Query: A baby has had diarrhea for 5 days. Today the baby's stools have a very bad smell and the baby is very floppy. Mother says she is giving 'honey water' only.
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Floppy body** (Muscle weakness)
- **Foul-smelling stools**
- **Lethargy**
- Sunken eyes

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (<1 year) | Any | Stop honey water, start breastfeeding/ORS, rush to CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Stop Honey**: Honey is not a treatment for diarrhea and can be dangerous for infants (Botulism risk).
- **Start ORS**: Switch immediately to ORS or salted Rice Kanji.
- **Breastfeeding**: Ask the mother to nurse the baby every few minutes.
- **Referral**: Floppiness is a sign of severe potassium loss. The baby needs hospital-grade rehydration.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify floppy muscle tone in baby"] --> B["Stop all non-medical liquids like honey water"] --> C["Start breastfeeding and give small spoons of ORS"]
  C --> D["Check if baby can still swallow properly"]
  D --> E["Monitor for any signs of breathing difficulty"]
  E --> F["Transport to Hospital for potassium and fluid therapy"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Floppy body | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Foul-smelling diarrhea | Refer to PHC | Start ORS |
| Honey water use | Refer to PHC | Switch to ORS |
| Crying, active baby | Monitor at PHC | Home care with ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Floppiness (hypotonia) is a sign of critical weakness. Prolonged diarrhea requires medical intervention beyond just ORS.

Query: How do I perform the 'Skin Pinch Test' correctly on a toddler to check for severe dehydration?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Skin stays peaked (folded)** for more than 2 seconds
- **Sunken eyes**
- **Drowsiness**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Children | Any | Perform test; if slow, give ORS and refer to PHC/CHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **The Pinch**: Locate the area between the belly button and the side of the stomach.
- **Method**: Use your thumb and index finger to pick up the skin and the fat under it. Do not just 'pinch' the surface.
- **Hold and Release**: Hold for a second and release. 
- **Watch**: If it goes back immediately, it's normal. If it takes a second, it's 'Some Dehydration'. If it takes >2 seconds, it is 'Severe Dehydration'.
- **Action**: If severe, rush the child to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Expose the child's abdomen"] --> B["Pinch the skin and subcutaneous tissue firmly"]
  B --> C["Release and count the seconds it takes to flatten"]
  C --> D["If >2 seconds, start intensive ORS and move child"]
  D --> E["Check eyes for sunken appearance during the count"]
  E --> F["Transport to CHC if pinch is slow or very slow"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Pinch takes >2 seconds | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Pinch takes 1-2 seconds | Refer to PHC | Give intensive ORS |
| Pinch goes back fast | Monitor at Home | Routine ORS |
| Sunken eyes + slow pinch | Emergency Referral | Never |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
The skin pinch test may be less reliable in very thin (malnourished) or very overweight children. Always look at the eyes and thirst too.

Query: A child has diarrhea and now his stomach looks very flat and his skin is cold. He is not crying even when we pinch him. What to do?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No reaction to pain** (Stupor/Coma)
- **Cold, pale skin**
- **Sunken eyes**
- Very weak pulse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Do NOT give liquids, keep warm, rush to hospital (108) |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Check Consciousness**: If the child does not react to a pinch, he is unconscious. 
- **Airway**: Keep the head tilted slightly back to make sure he can breathe.
- **No Choking**: Do **NOT** try to pour water or ORS into the mouth of an unconscious child. It will go into the lungs.
- **Immediate Referral**: This child is in deep shock. Only an IV can save his life.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Check for response to touch or voice"] --> B["If no response, do NOT put anything in the mouth"]
  B --> C["Check for breathing and feel for a pulse"]
  C --> D["Wrap child to maintain body heat"]
  D --> E["Call 108 or arrange fastest transport"]
  E --> F["Rush to CHC or District Hospital for IV fluids"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Unconscious/Unresponsive | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Cold skin | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Weak pulse | Emergency (108) | Never |
| Drowsy but can talk | Refer to CHC | Give sips of ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Failure to respond to pain is a sign of critical brain dehydration. This is a level-1 emergency.

Query: My 6-month-old baby has diarrhea and has stopped crying with tears. His mouth is dry like a bone. Can I give him some pomegranate juice?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **No tears when crying**
- **Bone-dry mouth**
- Sunken eyes
- Refusing to nurse

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Infant (6 months) | Any | Give ORS and breastmilk, do NOT give fruit juice, refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **No Fruit Juices**: Commercial or home juices like pomegranate have too much sugar, which can make diarrhea **worse**.
- **ORS/Breastmilk**: These are the only safe fluids for a 6-month-old.
- **Zinc**: Give 1/2 or 1 Zinc tablet (20mg) dissolved in milk.
- **Referral**: No tears and a dry mouth mean 'Some' to 'Severe' dehydration. Needs medical monitoring.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify lack of tears and dry mouth"] --> B["Stop all fruit juices or sugary drinks"] --> C["Prioritize breastfeeding and small sips of ORS"]
  C --> D["Dissolve Zinc tablet in breastmilk and give"]
  D --> E["Check for sunken soft spot on head"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for observation and treatment"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| No tears when crying | Refer to PHC | Increase breastfeeding |
| Dry mouth/tongue | Refer to PHC | Give ORS sips |
| Vomiting everything | Emergency Referral | Never |
| Playful, wet mouth | Monitor at Home | Home-ORS + Nursing |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Fruit juices can cause 'osmotic' diarrhea, pulling more water out of the baby's body. Use only ORS or breastmilk.

Query: A child is thirsty and drinking ORS, but the diarrhea is not stopping even after 2 days. The mother is losing patience. What should I tell her?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Diarrhea lasting >14 days** (Persistent)
- **Blood in stool**
- Weight loss
- Child becoming weak

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Continue ORS, give Zinc, and refer to PHC for possible antibiotics |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Counseling**: Tell the mother that ORS is not a 'stopper' for diarrhea; it is to keep the child alive while the body fights the infection. The diarrhea will stop on its own.
- **Zinc**: Ensure the child is taking Zinc daily for 14 days. This reduces the duration.
- **Feeding**: Ensure the child is eating soft, energy-rich food.
- **Referral**: If the child is getting weaker or the stools have blood.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Assess child's activity and thirst"] --> B["Explain to mother that ORS prevents death, not diarrhea"]
  B --> C["Ensure 14-day Zinc course is started and followed"]
  C --> D["Offer energy-dense foods like Banana or Dal-Khichdi"]
  D --> E["Check for blood in stool or weight loss"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC if condition lasts >3 days without improvement"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Diarrhea >3 days | Refer to PHC | Continue ORS |
| Child is weak/not active | Refer to PHC | Continue feeding |
| Blood in stool | Refer to CHC | Never treat at home |
| Drinking well, active | Monitor at Home | ORS + Zinc + Food |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Never give 'diarrhea-stopping' medicines (like Loperamide) to children. They can be very dangerous.

Query: A child has watery diarrhea and is very restless. He is constantly asking for water and crying. Is this severe?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Restlessness and irritability**
- **Drinking greedily/eagerly**
- Sunken eyes
- Skin pinch goes back slowly (1-2 seconds)

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Child (Any) | Any | Give ORS as much as the child wants, start Zinc, and refer to PHC |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Immediate ORS**: Give the child ORS immediately. He is telling you his body is thirsty.
- **Zinc**: Give the first dose of Zinc (20mg) now.
- **Natural Fluids**: Give salted lassi, coconut water, or lemon water.
- **Monitor**: If the child becomes sleepy or stops drinking, it has become 'Severe'. Move to the hospital.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Identify restlessness and extreme thirst"] --> B["Offer unlimited ORS and monitor intake"]
  B --> C["Start Zinc 14-day course immediately"]
  C --> D["Check for sunken eyes and skin pinch"]
  D --> E["If restlessness turns into lethargy"] --> F["Rush to CHC for professional rehydration"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Restless/Irritable | Refer to PHC | Give ORS |
| Drinking greedily | Refer to PHC | Give ORS |
| Skin pinch 1-2 sec | Refer to PHC | Give ORS |
| Active, normal thirst | Monitor at Home | Home-ORS + Zinc |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Restlessness and greedily drinking are signs of 'Some' dehydration. This can turn into 'Severe' dehydration very quickly if not treated.

Query: How do I explain to a village family what 'ORS' does for their dehydrated child in simple terms?
Answer:
## 🚨 Danger Signs
- **Dry mouth**
- **Sunken eyes**
- **No urine**

## 🏥 Actions for Age Group
| Age Group | Gender | What to do |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| All Ages | Any | Educate and demonstrate; give ORS to the child |

## 🩺 What To Do Now
- **Simple English**: Tell them ORS is 'Jeevan Rakshak Ghol' (Life-Saving Solution).
- **Salt-Sugar Balance**: Explain that diarrhea takes away salt and water. Water alone is like a car without fuel. Sugar is the fuel that helps the salt and water get into the body's 'engine'.
- **Demo**: Show them how to mix 1 packet in exactly 1 liter of clean water.
- **Spoon feeding**: Explain that even if the child is vomiting, small spoons of ORS will stay inside and save the child.

## 🔄 Triage Flowchart
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Demonstrate mixing 1 ORS packet in 1 liter water"] --> B["Explain that sugar helps salt enter the body faster"]
  B --> C["Show how to give small frequent spoons of the mix"]
  C --> D["Tell family that ORS prevents death from fluid loss"]
  D --> E["Explain that it does NOT stop the stools immediately"]
  E --> F["Refer to PHC for any signs of worsening weakness"]
```

## 🏥 Refer vs Home Care
| Symptom | When to Refer | When to Treat at Home |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Thirsty, drinking well | Monitor at PHC | Home ORS training |
| Refusing ORS | Refer to PHC | Use natural fluids |
| Child is very weak | Emergency Referral | Start ORS during transport |
| Passing urine | Monitor at Home | Continue ORS |

## ⚠️ Disclaimer
Counseling the family is as important as the medicine. A well-informed family will continue rehydration even at night.
